CurveBench consists of \textbf{756 images} of pairwise non-intersecting Jordan curves across easy, polygonal, topographic-inspired, maze-like, and dense counting configurations. Each image is annotated with a rooted tree encoding the containment relations between planar regions. We formulate the task as structured prediction: given an image, a model must recover the full rooted containment tree induced by the curves. Despite the visual simplicity of the task, the strongest evaluated model, Gemini 3.1 Pro, achieves only \textbf{71.1\%} tree-generation accuracy on CurveBench-Easy and \textbf{19.1\%} on CurveBench-Hard. We further demonstrate benchmark utility through RLVR-style fine-tuning of open-weight vision-language models. Our trained Qwen3-VL-8B model improves over \texttt{Qwen-3-VL-8B-Thinking} from \textbf{2.8\%} to \textbf{33.3\%} tree-generation accuracy on CurveBench-Easy, exceeding GPT-5.4 and Claude Opus 4.5 under our evaluation protocol. The remaining gap, especially on CurveBench-Hard, shows that exact topology-aware visual reasoning remains far from solved.

AI.tldr: CurveBench is a new benchmark for hierarchical topological reasoning from visual input, where models must recover rooted containment trees from images of non-intersecting Jordan curves; results show current models struggle, especially on hard configurations, with partial gains from vision-language fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: To quantify and foster progress in topology-aware visual reasoning by evaluating models on structured predictions of containment relations in simple visual scenes, highlighting gaps in current systems.
AI.method: CurveBench comprises 756 images across easy, polygonal, topographic-inspired, maze-like, and dense counting configurations, with annotations as rooted containment trees. The task is structured prediction: recover the full containment tree from the image. Baselines include Gemini 3.1 Pro; additional RLVR-style fine-tuning of open-weight vision-language models (e.g., Qwen3-VL-8B) is explored for performance gains.
AI.result: Gemini 3.1 Pro achieves 71.1% tree-generation accuracy on CurveBench-Easy and 19.1% on CurveBench-Hard. RLVR-style fine-tuning yields Qwen3-VL-8B-FT improving from 2.8% to 33.3% on CurveBench-Easy, surpassing GPT-5.4 and Claude Opus 4.5 under their protocol; substantial gap remains on Hard, indicating topology-aware visual reasoning is far from solved.
AI.conclusion: Topology-aware visual reasoning is challenging even on visually simple scenes. CurveBench serves as a diagnostic benchmark to measure progress; while guided fine-tuning yields meaningful gains, the hard configurations reveal substantial room for improvement.

title: EvolveMem:Self-Evolving Memory Architecture via AutoResearch for LLM Agents
summary: Long-term memory is essential for LLM agents that operate across multiple sessions, yet existing memory systems treat retrieval infrastructure as fixed: stored content evolves while scoring functions, fusion strategies, and answer-generation policies remain frozen at deployment. We argue that truly adaptive memory requires co-evolution at two levels: the stored knowledge and the retrieval mechanism that queries it. We present EvolveMem, a self-evolving memory architecture that exposes its full retrieval configuration as a structured action space optimized by an LLM-powered diagnosis module. In each evolution round, the module reads per-question failure logs, identifies root causes, and proposes targeted configuration adjustments; a guarded meta-analyzer applies them with automatic revert-on-regression and explore-on-stagnation safeguards. This closed-loop self-evolution realizes an AutoResearch process: the system autonomously conducts iterative research cycles on its own architecture, replacing manual configuration tuning. Starting from a minimal baseline, the process converges autonomously, discovering effective retrieval strategies including entirely new configuration dimensions not present in the original action space. On LoCoMo, EvolveMem outperforms the strongest baseline by 25.7% relative and achieves a 78.0% relative improvement over the minimal baseline. On MemBench, EvolveMem exceeds the strongest baseline by 18.9% relative. Evolved configurations transfer across benchmarks with positive rather than catastrophic transfer, indicating that the self-evolution process captures universal retrieval principles rather than benchmark-specific heuristics. Code is available at https://github.com/aiming-lab/SimpleMem.

AI.tldr: EvolveMem proposes a self-evolving memory system for LLM agents that co-evolves stored knowledge and its retrieval configuration via an LLM-driven diagnosis loop, using guarded auto-research to autonomously discover and apply retrieval strategies; achieves significant improvements on LoCoMo and MemBench and shows positive transfer across benchmarks; code released.
AI.motivation: Long-term memory for multi-session LLM agents is currently static in deployment, limiting adaptation to evolving tasks and environments. A truly adaptive memory should co-evolve both the stored content and the retrieval mechanism, enabling continual improvement of retrieval quality and policy over time.
AI.method: Introduce EvolveMem, a memory architecture whose retrieval configuration is an explicit action space. An LLM-powered diagnosis module analyzes per-question failure logs, identifies root causes, and proposes configuration adjustments. A guarded meta-analyzer automatically applies changes with revert-on-regression and explore-on-stagnation safeguards. The system runs iterative AutoResearch loops starting from a minimal baseline, discovering new configuration dimensions not present in the initial action space. Returned configurations are evaluated on benchmarks, and the process yields autonomously evolved retrieval strategies; cross-benchmark transfer is observed.
AI.result: On LoCoMo, EvolveMem outperforms the strongest baseline by 25.7% relative and achieves 78.0% relative improvement over the minimal baseline. On MemBench, it exceeds the strongest baseline by 18.9% relative. Evolved configurations transfer across benchmarks with positive transfer, suggesting the approach captures universal retrieval principles rather than benchmark-specific heuristics.
AI.conclusion: Self-evolution enables autonomous architecture optimization of retrieval systems for long-term memory in LLM agents. The AutoResearch loop yields convergent improvements and cross-benchmark generalization, and the authors provide code for reproduction.

title: Distill: Uncovering the True Intent behind Human-Robot Communication
summary: As robots become increasingly integrated into everyday environments, intuitive communication paradigms such as natural language and end-user programming have become indispensable for specifying autonomous robot behavior. However, these mechanisms are ineffective at fully capturing user intent: natural language is imprecise and ambiguous, whereas end-user programming can be overly specific. As a result, understanding what users truly mean when they interact with robots remains a central challenge for human-AI communication systems. To address this issue, we propose the Distill approach for human-robot communication interfaces. Given a task specification provided by the user, Distill (1) removes unnecessary steps; (2) generalizes the meaning behind individual steps; and (3) relaxes ordering constraints between steps. We implemented Distill on a web interface and, through a crowdsourcing study, demonstrated its ability to elicit and refine user intent from initial task specifications.

AI.tldr: Distill is a method to refine user task specifications for robots by removing unnecessary steps, generalizing the meaning of steps, and relaxing ordering, implemented via a web interface and validated with crowdsourcing to elicit/refine user intent.
AI.motivation: Natural language and end-user programming struggle to capture user intent precisely, leading to ambiguity in robot behavior. A distillation process can better align user specifications with actionable robot plans by abstracting and simplifying instructions.
AI.method: Introduce Distill: given a user task specification, it removes unnecessary steps, generalizes the meaning behind steps, and relaxes ordering constraints. Implemented as a web interface and evaluated through a crowdsourcing study to assess its ability to elicit and refine user intent from initial specifications.
AI.result: Demonstrated that Distill can elicit and refine user intent from initial task specifications in a crowdsourced setting, indicating improved alignment between user input and intended robot behavior.
AI.conclusion: Distill provides a practical interface for human-robot communication that reduces ambiguity and over-specification by distilling task specifications into more general, order-flexible representations, with validation via crowdsourcing.

title: Sheaf-Theoretic Transport and Obstruction for Detecting Scientific Theory Shift in AI Agents
summary: Scientific theory shift in AI agents requires more than fitting equations to data. An artificial scientific agent must detect whether an existing representational framework remains transportable into a new regime, or whether its language has become locally-to-globally obstructed and must be extended. This paper develops a finite sheaf-theoretic framework for detecting theory-shift candidates through transport and obstruction. Contexts are organized as a local-to-global structure in which source, overlap, target, and validation charts are fitted, restricted, and tested for gluing. Obstruction measures failure of coherence through residual fit, overlap incompatibility, constraint violation, limiting-relation failure, and representational cost. We evaluate the framework on a controlled transition-card benchmark designed to separate deformation within a source language from extension of that language. The main result is direct obstruction ranking: the intended deformation or extension is usually the lowest-obstruction candidate, and transition type is separated in the benchmark. A constellation kernel over the same signatures is included only as a secondary representational-similarity probe. The aim is not to reconstruct historical paradigm shifts or solve open-ended autonomous theory invention, but to isolate a finite diagnostic subproblem for AI agents: detecting when representational transport fails and extension becomes the coherent next move.

AI.tldr: Finite sheaf-theoretic diagnostic framework for AI theory-shift detection: uses transport vs extension, local-to-global contexts, and obstruction measures to rank deformation candidates; validated on a controlled benchmark; aims to isolate a practical diagnostic subproblem for representational transport failure and coherent extension.
AI.motivation: Enable AI agents to decide when a current representational framework remains transportable into a new regime or must be extended, moving beyond purely data-fitting toward principled theory shifts.
AI.method: Constructs a finite sheaf-theoretic framework with contexts organized as a local-to-global structure (source, overlap, target, validation charts) that are fitted, restricted, and tested for gluing. Defines obstruction measures (residual fit, overlap incompatibility, constraint violation, limiting-relation failure, representational cost). Evaluates on a transition-card benchmark designed to separate deformation within a source language from language extension; includes a constellation kernel as a secondary probe.
AI.result: Direct obstruction ranking tends to place the intended deformation or extension as the lowest obstruction candidate; transition type is separable in the benchmark. The framework provides a practical diagnostic for when transport fails and an extension is the coherent next move.
AI.conclusion: Isolates a finite diagnostic subproblem for AI agents: detecting transport failure and choosing a coherent extension; does not solve autonomous theory invention but provides a diagnostic tool with a measurable obstruction signal.

title: Venus-DeFakerOne: Unified Fake Image Detection & Localization
summary: In recent years, the rapid evolution of generative AI has fundamentally reshaped the paradigm of image forgery, breaking the traditional boundaries between document editing, natural image manipulation, DeepFake generation, and full-image AIGC synthesis. Despite this shift toward unified forgery generation, existing research in Fake Image Detection and Localization (FIDL) remains fragmented. This creates a mismatch between increasingly unified forgery generation mechanisms and the domain-specific detection paradigm. Bridging this mismatch poses two key challenges for FIDL: understanding cross-domain artifacts transfer and interference, and building a high-capacity unified foundation model for joint detection and localization. To address these challenges, we propose DeFakerOne, a data-centric, unified FIDL foundation model integrating InternVL2 and SAM2. DeFakerOne enables simultaneous image-level detection and pixel-level forgery localization across diverse scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DeFakerOne achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming baselines on 39 forgery detection benchmarks and 9 localization benchmarks. Furthermore, the model exhibits superior robustness against real-world perturbations and state-of-the-art generators such as GPT-Image-2. Finally, we provide a systematic analysis of data scaling laws, cross-domain artifacts transfer-interference patterns, the necessity of fine-grained supervision, and the original resolution artifacts preservation, highlighting the design principles for scalable, robust, and unified FIDL.

AI.tldr: Unified FIDL foundation model DeFakerOne integrates InternVL2 and SAM2 to enable image-level detection and pixel-level forgery localization across diverse forgery scenarios; achieves state-of-the-art on extensive benchmarks; demonstrates robustness and data-centric design with cross-domain insights.
AI.motivation: Current FIDL research is fragmented across domains as forgery techniques become increasingly unified. There is a need to understand cross-domain artifact transfer/interference and to build a high-capacity unified model capable of joint detection and localization.
AI.method: Data-centric integration of InternVL2 and SAM2 into DeFakerOne to enable simultaneous image-level detection and pixel-level localization across diverse forgery scenarios. Extensive benchmarking on 39 detection benchmarks and 9 localization benchmarks, with analyses of data scaling laws, cross-domain transfer-interference, fine-grained supervision, and preservation of original resolution artifacts.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on 39 forgery detection benchmarks and 9 localization benchmarks. Demonstrates robustness to real-world perturbations and to advanced generators such as GPT-Image-2. Provides systematic analyses of data scaling laws, cross-domain transfer-interference, supervision granularity, and artifact preservation.
AI.conclusion: Shows that a scalable, robust, unified FIDL foundation model is feasible and beneficial. Presents design principles for data-centric scaling, cross-domain artifact handling, and the importance of fine-grained supervision and original-resolution artifact preservation for unified forgery detection and localization.

title: EMA: Efficient Model Adaptation for Learning-based Systems
summary:
Machine learning (ML) is increasingly applied to optimize system performance in tasks such as resource management and network simulation. Unlike traditional ML tasks (e.g., image classification), networked systems often operate in heterogeneous, long-running, and dynamic environment states, where input conditions (e.g., network loads) and operational objectives can shift over time and across settings. Existing learning-based systems offer little support for adaptation, resulting in costly model training, extensive data collection, degraded system performance, and slow responsiveness.
  This paper presents EMA, the first model adaptation system supporting learning-based systems to adapt to evolving environments with minimal operational overhead. EMA takes a system-driven, data-centric approach that accommodates diverse system and model designs while addressing two key deployment challenges. First, it reduces expensive model training by introducing state transformers that align the input state of a new environment with previously similar states, allowing models to warm-start adaptation. Second, it addresses the often-overlooked yet costly process of data labeling--collecting ground truth for exploring and training on various system decisions--by prioritizing labeling high-utility data while balancing the tradeoff between training and labeling cost. Evaluations on eight representative learning-based systems show that EMA reduces adaptation costs (e.g., GPU training time) by 14.9-42.4% while improving system performance (e.g., network throughput) by 6.9-31.3%.

AI.tldr: EMA enables low-overhead model adaptation for learning-based networked systems by aligning new environments with prior states via state transformers and prioritizing high-utility data labeling to reduce training and labeling costs while improving performance.
AI.motivation: Networked ML systems operate in dynamic, heterogeneous environments; traditional ML approaches struggle to adapt efficiently, leading to costly retraining, data collection, degraded performance, and slow responsiveness.
AI.method: Proposes EMA, a system-driven, data-centric adaptation framework. Introduces state transformers to map input states of a new environment to previously similar states to warm-start adaptation, and a labeling policy that prioritizes high-utility data to balance training and labeling costs.
AI.result: Evaluations across eight representative learning-based systems show adaptation cost reductions in GPU training time of 14.9–42.4% and performance gains (e.g., network throughput) of 6.9–31.3%.
AI.conclusion: EMA enables efficient, adaptable learning-based systems across diverse settings with reduced overhead and improved performance.

title: Energy-Efficient Quadruped Locomotion with Compliant Feet
comment: 29 pages, 7 figures, supplemental videos link is mentioned in the paper
summary: Quadruped robots are often designed with rigid feet to simplify control and maintain stable contact during locomotion. While this approach is straightforward, it limits the ability of the legs to absorb impact forces and reuse stored elastic energy, leading to higher energy expenditure during locomotion. To explore whether compliant feet can provide an advantage, we integrate foot compliance into a reinforcement learning (RL) locomotion controller and study its effect on walking efficiency. In simulation, we train eight policies corresponding to eight different spring stiffness values and then cross-evaluate their performance by measuring mechanical energy consumed per meter traveled. In experiments done on a developed quadruped, the energy consumption for the intermediate stiffness spring is lower by ~ 17% when compared to a very stiff or a very flexible spring incorporated in the feet, with similar trends appearing in the simulation results. These results indicate that selecting an appropriate foot compliance can improve locomotion efficiency without destabilizing the robot during motion.

AI.tldr: RL-controlled quadruped with compliant feet shows that intermediate spring stiffness minimizes energy per meter; experiments report ~17% energy reduction vs extremes, with simulation trends aligning and no stability loss.
AI.motivation: Rigid feet simplify control but miss opportunities to absorb impacts and recycle elastic energy; introducing foot compliance could improve locomotion efficiency; findings support selecting an optimal stiffness to enhance energy efficiency without destabilization.
AI.method: Simulated eight RL policies across eight spring stiffness values; cross-evaluated performance by measuring energy per meter. Validated on a real quadruped with a compliant foot prototype, comparing energy consumption across stiffness values.
AI.result: Intermediate stiffness spring achieved ~17% lower energy per meter than very stiff or very flexible feet; observed similar trends between simulation and hardware experiments, indicating robustness of the finding.
AI.conclusion: Choosing an appropriate foot compliance improves locomotion efficiency without compromising stability; optimal stiffness balances energy storage/reuse and impact absorption.

title: DUET: Dual-Paradigm Adaptive Expert Triage with Single-cell Inductive Prior for Spatial Transcriptomics Prediction
summary: Inferring spatially resolved gene expression from histology images offers a cost-effective complement to spatial transcriptomics (ST). However, existing methods reduce this task to a simple morphology-to-expression mapping, where visual similarity does not guarantee molecular consistency. Meanwhile, single-cell data has amassed rich resources far surpassing the scale of ST data, yet it remains underexplored in vision-omics modeling. Furthermore, current approaches commit to a monolithic paradigm with bottlenecks, unable to balance expressive flexibility with biological fidelity. To bridge these gaps, we propose DUET, a novel dual-paradigm framework that synergizes parametric prediction and memory-based retrieval under cellular inductive priors. DUET implements a parallel regression-retrieval paradigm, adaptively reconciling the outputs of its complementary pathways. To mitigate aleatoric vision ambiguity, we incorporate large-scale single-cell references to impose molecular states as biological constraints for faithful learning. Building upon structural refinement, we further design a lightweight adapter to dynamically assign branch preference across spatial contexts to achieve optimal performance. Extensive experiments on three public datasets across varied gene scales demonstrate that DUET achieves SOTA performance, with consistent gains contributed by each proposed component. Code is available at https://github.com/Junchao-Zhu/DUET

AI.tldr: DUET combines parametric regression and memory-based retrieval with cellular priors to infer spatial gene expression from histology, leveraging large-scale single-cell references and an adaptive adapter to balance pathways, achieving state-of-the-art results on three datasets.
AI.motivation: Current morphology-to-expression mappings often rely solely on visual similarity, risking molecular inconsistency. The abundance of single-cell data remains underutilized in vision-omics. A rigid single-paradigm approach limits balancing expressive flexibility with biological fidelity.
AI.method: A parallel regression–retrieval framework that adaptively reconciles outputs from two complementary pathways. It incorporates cellular inductive priors from large single-cell references to constrain molecular states, uses a memory-based retrieval component, and adds a lightweight adapter to dynamically allocate branch preference across spatial contexts.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across three public datasets with varied gene scales; ablations show each proposed component (parallel pathways, single-cell priors, and the adapter) contributes reproducible gains.
AI.conclusion: DUET effectively integrates parametric prediction and memory-based retrieval under cellular priors to improve accuracy and robustness in image-based spatial gene expression inference; the approach balances expressive flexibility with biological fidelity and provides code for reproduction.

title: A Unified Geometric Framework for Weighted Contrastive Learning
summary: Contrastive learning (CL) aims to preserve relational structure between samples by learning representations that reflect a similarity graph. Yet, the geometry of the resulting embeddings remains poorly understood. Here we show that weighted InfoNCE objectives can be interpreted as Distance Geometry Problems, where the weighting scheme specifies the target geometry to be realized by the representation. This viewpoint yields exact characterizations of the optimal embeddings for several supervised and weakly supervised objectives. In supervised classification, both SupCon and Soft SupCon (a dense relaxation of it where pairs from distinct classes have small non-zero similarity) collapse samples within each class to a single prototype. However, while balanced SupCon recovers the classical regular simplex geometry, class imbalance breaks this symmetry: SupCon induces non-uniform inter-class similarities depending on class sizes, whereas Soft SupCon preserves a regular simplex geometry regardless of class imbalance. In continuous-label settings, our framework reveals a different failure mode: y-Aware CL generally cannot attain its entropic optimum unless the labels lie on a hypersphere, exposing a mismatch between Euclidean label weights and spherical latent similarity. By contrast, geometrically consistent choices such as Euclidean-Euclidean weighting or X-CLR admit unique optimal embeddings. Our results show that the choice of weighting scheme determines whether contrastive learning is geometrically realizable, degenerate, or inconsistent, providing a principled framework for designing contrastive objectives.

AI.tldr: Weighted InfoNCE objectives can be seen as distance-geometry problems; the chosen weighting specifies the target embedding geometry, yielding exact optimal embeddings for various supervised/weakly supervised CL objectives. Implications include collapse to class prototypes for SupCon, geometry changes under class imbalance, and mismatches in y-Aware CL unless labels lie on a hypersphere; certain weightings (Euclidean-Euclidean, X-CLR) give unique optima.
AI.motivation: To understand how the choice of weighting in contrastive learning objectives shapes the geometry of the learned embeddings, enabling principled design of objectives and clarifying failure modes.
AI.method: Reframe weighted InfoNCE as distance-geometry problems; derive exact optimal embeddings for supervised and weakly supervised CL objectives; analyze balanced vs. unbalanced SupCon, Soft SupCon, and continuous-label y-Aware CL; compare weightings and identify geometrically consistent choices; propose guidelines (e.g., Euclidean-Euclidean, X-CLR).
AI.result: Provided exact characterizations of optimal embeddings across objectives. In supervised classification, samples collapse to class prototypes; balanced SupCon recovers regular simplex geometry, while class imbalance induces non-uniform inter-class similarities in SupCon; Soft SupCon maintains a regular simplex despite imbalance. In continuous-label settings, y-Aware CL generally cannot reach its entropic optimum unless labels lie on a hypersphere. Geometric-consistent weightings (Euclidean-Euclidean, X-CLR) yield unique optimal embeddings.
AI.conclusion: The weighting scheme determines whether contrastive learning is geometrically realizable, degenerate, or inconsistent. This framework enables principled objective design by targeting specific geometric relations in the embedding space.

title: Before the Body Moves: Learning Anticipatory Joint Intent for Language-Conditioned Humanoid Control
summary: Natural language is an intuitive interface for humanoid robots, yet streaming whole-body control requires control representations that are executable now and anticipatory of future physical transitions. Existing language-conditioned humanoid systems typically generate kinematic references that a low-level tracker must repair reactively, or use latent/action policies whose outputs do not explicitly encode upcoming contact changes, support transfers, and balance preparation. We propose \textbf{DAJI} (\emph{Dynamics-Aligned Joint Intent}), a hierarchical framework that learns an anticipatory joint-intent interface between language generation and closed-loop control. DAJI-Act distills a future-aware teacher into a deployable diffusion action policy through student-driven rollouts, while DAJI-Flow autoregressively generates future intent chunks from language and intent history. Experiments show that DAJI achieves strong results in anticipatory latent learning, single-instruction generation, and streaming instruction following, reaching 94.42\% rollout success on HumanML3D-style generation and 0.152 subsequence FID on BABEL.

AI.tldr: DAJI proposes a hierarchical, language-conditioned control framework with two components—DAJI-Act (future-aware action policy distilled from a teacher via diffusion and rollouts) and DAJI-Flow (autoregressive generation of future intent chunks from language/history)—that achieves strong anticipatory control for humanoids, with 94.42% rollout success on HumanML3D-style tasks and 0.152 subsequence FID on BABEL.
AI.motivation: Streaming whole-body control for humanoid robots requires control representations that are executable now while anticipating future physical transitions; existing language-conditioned systems either output reactive kinematic references or latent policies that do not explicitly encode upcoming contact changes, transfers, or balance preparation.
AI.method: DAJI is a hierarchical framework comprising DAJI-Act and DAJI-Flow. DAJI-Act distills a future-aware teacher into a deployable diffusion action policy via student-driven rollouts. DAJI-Flow autoregressively generates future intent chunks from language and intent history.
AI.result: The approach yields strong performance in anticipatory latent learning, single-instruction generation, and streaming instruction following, achieving 94.42% rollout success on HumanML3D-style generation and 0.152 subsequence FID on BABEL.
AI.conclusion: DAJI effectively bridges language generation and closed-loop control by explicitly modeling future joint intent, enabling anticipatory, executable control for streaming language instructions in humanoid robots.

title: Enhanced and Efficient Reasoning in Large Learning Models
summary:
In current Large Language Models we can trust the production of smoothly flowing prose on the basis of the principles of machine learning. However, there is no comparably principled basis to justify trust in the content of the text produced. It appears to be conventional wisdom that addressing this issue by adding more principled reasoning is not computationally affordable.
  Here we propose a principled method of reasoning that is efficient enough to be practical for large language models. Further, the method allows the retention of much of the currently used software and hardware base. Our method for improving the functioning of large language models consists of a first stage of preprocessing that recodes the data to a Unary Relational Integracode that is more explicit about the relationships among the objects described in the text, followed as a second stage by a standard but possibly streamlined machine learning process that then also learns to predict these relationships.
  The method may be viewed as realizing a world model and applying beyond natural language, to vision and actions, for example, where the multiple properties of an object referred to in an input are brought together explicitly, rather than remaining distributed in the various references to it in the input. We articulate its advantages in terms of Robust Logic, a system for performing principled chaining on learned, and hence uncertain, information. We show that this recoding has the surprising and fortuitous property that, while succinct, it makes the task of learning a core subset of relational rules that hold in the world described in the training data polynomial time learnable in a defined sense, the polynomial depending on the complexity of the rule. This gives support for sound reasoning within each single call of the learned classifier as well as between multiple calls.

AI.tldr: Proposes an efficient, principled reasoning framework for LLMs by first converting input into a Unary Relational Integracode that explicitizes inter-object relations, then applying standard learning to predict these relations; framed by Robust Logic and a world-model concept, with polynomial-time learnability of a core relational-rule subset and applicability beyond NLP to vision and actions.
AI.motivation: LLMs currently produce fluent prose but lack a principled basis to trust content; improving principled reasoning is deemed costly, and there is a need for efficient, scalable methods that preserve existing software/hardware ecosystems.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: (1) preprocess data into a Unary Relational Integracode (URIC) to explicitly encode relationships among objects, (2) train a standard (potentially streamlined) ML model to predict these relations. The framework is connected to Robust Logic for principled chaining of uncertain information and is framed as a world-model that can extend to vision and actions by unifying object properties rather than distributing them across references.
AI.result: The URIC recoding enables learning a core subset of world-relational rules in polynomial time, with the polynomial's degree tied to rule complexity. This supports sound reasoning within single classifier calls and across multiple calls.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach delivers a principled, computationally efficient reasoning mechanism for LLMs with broad applicability and compatibility with existing infrastructure, underpinned by Robust Logic and a world-model perspective; it enables tractable learning of relational rules essential for trustworthy inference.

title: Bridging the Rural Healthcare Gap: A Cascaded Edge-Cloud Architecture for Automated Retinal Screening
summary: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is one of the leading causes of preventable blindness, yet rural regions often lack the specialists and infrastructure needed for early detection. Although cloud-based deep learning systems offer high accuracy, they face significant challenges in these settings due to high latency, limited bandwidth, and high data transmission costs. To address these challenges, we propose a two-tier edge-cloud cascade on the public APTOS 2019 Blindness Detection dataset. Tier 1 runs a lightweight MobileNetV3-small model on a local clinic device to perform a binary triage between Referable DR (Classes 2-4) and Non-referable DR (Classes 0-1). Tier 2 runs a RETFoundDINOv2 model in the cloud for ordinal severity grading, but only on the subset of images flagged as referable by Tier 1. On a stratified APTOS test split of 733 images, Tier 1 reaches 98.99% sensitivity and 84.37% specificity at a validation-tuned high-sensitivity threshold. The default cascade forwards 49.52% of test images to Tier 2, reducing cloud calls by 50.48% relative to using a cloud-based model for all images. In the deployed 4-class output space (Class 0-1 / Class 2 / Class 3 / Class 4), the cascade obtains 80.49% accuracy and 0.8167 quadratic weighted kappa; the cloud-only baseline obtains 80.76% accuracy and 0.8184 quadratic weighted kappa. On APTOS, the cascade cuts cloud use by about half with a modest drop in grading performance. Index Terms: Diabetic Retinopathy, Edge-Cloud Cascade, MobileNetV3-small, RETFound-DINOv2, Retinal Screening, tele-ophthalmology

AI.tldr: Edge-cloud cascade for diabetic retinopathy screening on APTOS 2019: on-device binary triage (Referable vs Non-referable) with MobileNetV3-small, cloud-based ordinal grading (RETFoundDINOv2) on images flagged as referable; achieves ~50% cloud reduction with minor drop in overall grading performance.
AI.motivation: Reduce latency, bandwidth, and data transmission costs in rural/low-bandwidth settings where cloud-only DL systems struggle, while maintaining accurate referral grading.
AI.method: Tier 1: on-device MobileNetV3-small performs binary triage (Referable DR vs Non-referable DR). Tier 2: cloud-based RETFoundDINOv2 performs ordinal severity grading on the subset of images flagged as referable by Tier 1. Evaluation uses a stratified APTOS test split of 733 images. Final deployment maps outputs to 4 classes (0-1, 2, 3, 4).
AI.result:
Tier 1 achieves 98.99% sensitivity and 84.37% specificity at a high-sensitivity threshold. The cascade forwards 49.52% of test images to Tier 2, reducing cloud calls by 50.48% compared to cloud-only.
In 4-class output, cascade accuracy is 80.49% and quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) 0.8167; cloud-only baseline yields 80.76% accuracy and QWK 0.8184.
AI.conclusion: The edge-cloud cascade substantially reduces cloud usage with only a modest decline in grading performance, demonstrating viability for resource-constrained tele-ophthalmology deployments; performance is close to cloud-only baselines on APTOS data.

title: Collider-Bench: Benchmarking AI Agents with Particle Physics Analysis Reproduction
comment: 23 pages | 9 figures | 4 tables | Code: https://github.com/dfaroughy/Collider-Bench | Task Corpus: https://huggingface.co/datasets/Dariusfar/ColliderBench
summary: Autonomous language-model agents are increasingly evaluated on long-horizon tool-use tasks, but existing benchmarks rarely capture the complexity and nuance of real scientific work. To address this gap, we introduce Collider-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating whether LLM agents can reproduce experimental analyses from the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) using only public papers and open scientific software. Such analyses are often difficult to reproduce because the public toolchain only approximates the software used internally by the experimental collaborations, while the published papers inevitably omit implementation details needed for a faithful reconstruction. Agents must therefore rely on physical reasoning, domain knowledge, and trial-and-error to fill these gaps. Each task requires the agent to turn a published analysis into an executable simulation-and-selection pipeline and submit predicted collision event yields in specified signal regions. These predictions are evaluated with standard histogram metrics that provide continuous fidelity scores without a hand-written rubric. We also report the computational cost incurred by each agent per task. Finally, we evaluate the codebase and full session trace using an LLM judge to catch qualitative failure modes such as fabrications, hallucinations and duplications. We release an initial set of tasks drawn from LHC searches, together with a containerized sandbox and event simulation tools. We evaluate across a capability ladder of general purpose coding agents. Our results show that on average no agent reliably beats the physicist-in-the-loop solution.

AI.tldr: Collider-Bench tests LLM agents' ability to reproduce LHC analyses from public papers/tools; results show no agent reliably beats a physicist-in-the-loop.
AI.motivation: Addresses reproducibility and long-horizon tool-use in scientific workflows by benchmarking real-world physics analyses beyond synthetic tasks.
AI.method: Introduce Collider-Bench; convert published LHC analyses into executable simulation-and-selection pipelines using public software; evaluate with histogram-based fidelity metrics; include containerized sandbox, event simulation, and LLM judge to detect fabrication; assess across multiple general-purpose coding agents; report computation cost per task; release initial task set.
AI.result: On average, no agent reliably beats the physicist-in-the-loop Baseline; provides infrastructure and evaluation framework; highlights current agent limitations.
AI.conclusion: Benchmarking is valuable for gauging current LLM capabilities in complex scientific analysis; current agents struggle with authentic physics analyses; future work should expand tasks, improve tool integration, and enhance evaluation measures.

title: Let Robots Feel Your Touch: Visuo-Tactile Cortical Alignment for Embodied Mirror Resonance
summary: Observing touch on another's body can elicit corresponding tactile sensations in the observer, a phenomenon termed mirror touch that supports empathy and social perception. This visuo-tactile resonance is thought to rely on structural correspondence between visual and somatosensory cortices, yet robotic systems lack computational frameworks that instantiate this principle. Here we demonstrate that cortical correspondence can be operationalized to endow robots with mirror touch. We introduce Mirror Touch Net, which imposes semantic, distributional and geometric alignment between visual and tactile representations through multi-level constraints, enabling prediction of millimetre-scale tactile signals across 1,140 taxels on a robotic hand from RGB images. Manifold analysis reveals that these constraints reshape visual representations into geometry consistent with the tactile manifold, reducing the complexity of cross-modal mapping. Extending this alignment framework to cross-domain observations of human hands enables tactile prediction and reflexive responses to observed human touch. Our results link a neural principle of visuo-tactile resonance to robotic perception, providing an explainable route towards anticipatory touch and empathic human-robot interaction. Code is available at https://github.com/fun0515/Mirror-Touch-Net.

AI.tldr: Mirror Touch Net enables robots to predict tactile signals (mm-scale across 1,140 taxels) from RGB images by enforcing semantic, distributional, and geometric alignment between visual and tactile representations, effectively implementing visuo-tactile resonance for robotic perception. It extends to cross-domain observation of human hands to trigger tactile predictions and reflexive responses, linking a neural principle to explainable empathic HRI.
AI.motivation: To operationalize neural visuo-tactile resonance in robotics, creating a computational framework that maps visual input to tactile expectations, enabling anticipatory touch and empathic interaction with humans and objects.
AI.method: Mirror Touch Net enforces multi-level alignment between visual and tactile modalities: semantic alignment (shared meaning constraints), distributional alignment (representation similarity across modalities), and geometric alignment (spatial structure consistency). It predicts tactile signals across 1,140 robotic-hand taxels from RGB input and uses manifold analysis to show the visual representation reorganizes toward a tactile-consistent manifold. The framework is extended to cross-domain (human hands) observations to enable tactile prediction and reflexive responses.
AI.result: The model can predict millimetre-scale tactile signals from RGB images across 1,140 taxels on a robotic hand. Manifold analysis demonstrates the alignment reshapes visuals to a tactile-consistent geometry, reducing mapping complexity. Cross-domain application to human hands enables tactile prediction and reflexive responses, supporting explainable anticipatory touch and empathic HRI.
AI.conclusion: The work ties a neural visuo-tactile resonance principle to robotic perception, offering an explainable route to anticipatory touch and improved human-robot interaction through structured cross-modal alignment. Code is released for reproducibility.

title: Model-Adaptive Tool Necessity Reveals the Knowing-Doing Gap in LLM Tool Use
summary: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly act as autonomous agents that must decide when to answer directly vs. when to invoke external tools. Prior work studying adaptive tool use has largely treated tool necessity as a model-agnostic property, annotated by human or LLM judge, and mostly cover cases where the answer is obvious (e.g., fetching the weather vs. paraphrasing text). However, tool necessity in the wild is more nuanced due to the divergence of capability boundaries across models: a problem solvable by a strong model on its own may still require tools for a weaker one. In this work, we introduce a model-adaptive definition of tool-necessity, grounded in each model's empirical performance. Following this definition, we compare the necessity against observed tool-call behavior across four models on arithmetic and factual QA dataset, and find substantial mismatches of 26.5-54.0% and 30.8-41.8%, respectively. To diagnose the failure, we decompose tool use into two stages: an internal cognition stage that reflects whether a model believes a tool is necessary, and an execution stage that determines whether the model actually makes a tool-call action. By probing the LLM hidden states, we find that both signals are often linearly decodable, yet their probe directions become nearly orthogonal in the late-layer, last-token regime that drives the next-token action. By tracing the trajectory of samples in the two-stage process, we further discover that the majority of mismatch is concentrated in the cognition-to-action transition, not in cognition itself. These results reveal a knowing-doing gap in LLM tool-use: improving tool-use reliability requires not only better recognition of when tools are needed, but also better translation of that recognition into action.

AI.tldr: Model-adaptive tool-necessity reveals a knowing-doing gap in LLM tool-use: there is a mismatch between when models think tools are needed and when they actually call them. The cognition and action signals are decoupled, with most mismatches arising at the cognition-to-action transition.
AI.motivation: To address the failure of model-agnostic notions of tool necessity, this work defines tool-necessity in a model-specific, data-driven way and investigates how model performance boundaries shape whether tools are needed and how they are used in practice.
AI.method: 1) Propose a model-adaptive definition of tool-necessity based on empirical performance. 2) Compare necessity to observed tool-use across four models on arithmetic and factual QA datasets. 3) Decompose tool use into cognition (whether a tool is deemed necessary) and execution (whether a tool-call is made). 4) Probe hidden states to assess decodability of these signals and analyze late-layer, last-token dynamics. 5) Trace samples to identify where mismatches arise.
AI.result: Substantial mismatches between predicted necessity and actual tool use: 26.5–54.0% (arithmetic) and 30.8–41.8% (factual QA). Both cognition and execution signals are linearly decodable, but their directions become nearly orthogonal in the late-layer, last-token regime. The majority of mismatch concentrates in the cognition-to-action transition rather than cognition itself.
AI.conclusion: There is a knowing-doing gap in LLM tool-use. Improving reliability requires better recognition of when tools are needed and more effective translation of that recognition into tool calls.

title: SToRe3D: Sparse Token Relevance in ViTs for Efficient Multi-View 3D Object Detection
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) enable strong multi-view 3D detection but are limited by high inference latency from dense token and query processing across multiple views and large 3D regions. Existing sparsity methods, designed mainly for 2D vision, prune or merge image tokens but do not extend to full-model sparsity or address 3D object queries. We introduce SToRe3D, a relevance-aligned sparsity framework that jointly selects 2D image tokens and 3D object queries while storing filtered features for reactivation. Mutual 2D-3D relevance heads allocate compute to driving-critical content and preserve other embeddings. Evaluated on nuScenes and our new nuScenes-Relevance benchmark, SToRe3D achieves up to 3x faster inference with marginal accuracy loss, establishing real-time large-scale ViT-based 3D detection while maintaining accuracy on planning-critical agents.

AI.tldr: SToRe3D introduces relevance-aligned sparsity for Vision Transformers in 3D detection by jointly pruning 2D image tokens and 3D object queries, with a reactivation mechanism, achieving up to 3x faster inference on nuScenes with minor accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Reduce inference latency of ViT-based 3D detectors by exploiting sparsity at both image token and object-query levels while preserving performance for planning-critical agents.
AI.method: Mutual 2D-3D relevance heads allocate compute to driving-critical content; select tokens and queries; store filtered features for reactivation; operate within a full-model sparsity framework; evaluate on nuScenes and a proposed nuScenes-Relevance benchmark.
AI.result: Up to 3x faster inference with marginal accuracy loss; enables real-time large-scale ViT-based 3D detection while maintaining accuracy for planning-critical agents.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of joint token- and query-sparsity in ViT-based 3D detection and introduces a relevance-guided benchmark, highlighting practical deployment gains and robustness for planning-critical scenarios.

title: WarmPrior: Straightening Flow-Matching Policies with Temporal Priors
summary: Generative policies based on diffusion and flow matching have become a dominant paradigm for visuomotor robotic control. We show that replacing the standard Gaussian source distribution with WarmPrior, a simple temporally grounded prior constructed from readily available recent action history, consistently improves success rates on robotic manipulation tasks. We trace this gain to markedly straighter probability paths, echoing the effect of optimal-transport couplings in Rectified Flow. Beyond standard behavior cloning, WarmPrior also reshapes the exploration distribution in prior-space reinforcement learning, improving both sample efficiency and final performance. Collectively, these results identify the source distribution as an important and underexplored design axis in generative robot control.

AI.tldr: Replacing the standard Gaussian source in diffusion/flow-based visuomotor policies with a temporally grounded WarmPrior (based on recent action history) yields consistent improvements in robotic manipulation success, attributed to straighter probability paths similar to optimal transport couplings; benefits extend to prior-space RL by improving sample efficiency and final performance.
AI.motivation: The source distribution in generative diffusion policies is an underexplored design axis that can shape trajectory distributions and exploration behavior, with potential gains in both control and learning efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce WarmPrior as a simple, temporally grounded prior constructed from recent action history; replace Gaussian source in diffusion/flow models; evaluate on robotic manipulation tasks; analyze probability path shapes; extend the approach to prior-space reinforcement learning to assess exploration and sample efficiency.
AI.result: WarmPrior consistently improves success rates in visuomotor manipulation tasks; probability paths are markedly straighter, echoing optimal-transport couplings; in RL, exploration in prior-space is improved, leading to better sample efficiency and final performance.
AI.conclusion: Source distribution is a critical and underexplored design choice for generative robot control; WarmPrior demonstrates that leveraging temporally grounded priors can yield meaningful gains in both control performance and learning efficiency by shaping the forward diffusion process.

title: Network-Aware Bilinear Tokenization for Brain Functional Connectivity Representation Learning
summary: Masked autoencoders (MAEs) have recently shown promise for self-supervised representation learning of resting-state brain functional connectivity (FC). However, a fundamental question remains unresolved: how should FC matrices be tokenized to align with the intrinsic modular organization of large-scale brain networks? Existing approaches typically adopt region-centric or graph-based schemes that treat FC as structurally homogeneous elements and overlook the large-scale network brain organization. We introduce NERVE (Network-Aware Representations of Brain Functional Connectivity via Bilinear Tokenization), a self-supervised learning framework that redefines FC tokenization by partitioning FC matrices into patches of intra- and inter-network connectivity blocks. Unlike image-based MAE, where fixed-size patches share a common tokenizer, FC patches defined by network pairs are heterogeneous in size and correspond to distinct functional roles. To resolve this problem, NERVE embeds FC patches through a novel structured bilinear factorization. This formulation preserves network identity and reduces parameter complexity from quadratic to linear scaling in the number of networks. We evaluate NERVE across three large-scale developmental cohorts (ABCD, PNC, and CCNP) for behavior and psychopathology prediction. Compared to structurally agnostic MAE variants and graph-based self-supervised baselines, the proposed network-aware formulation yields more stable and transferable representations, particularly in cross-cohort evaluation. Ablation studies confirm that the proposed bilinear network embedding and anatomically grounded parcellation are critical for performance. These findings highlight the importance of incorporating domain-specific structural priors into self-supervised learning for functional connectomics.

AI.tldr: NERVE proposes network-aware tokenization of functional connectivity using a structured bilinear factorization to partition FC into intra-/inter-network blocks, preserving network identity and reducing parameter complexity. It yields more stable and transferable self-supervised representations than standard MAEs, with strongest gains in cross-cohort transfer, supported by ablations on bilinear embedding and anatomically grounded parcellation.
AI.motivation: Conventional MAE-style tokenization treats FC as structurally homogeneous, failing to respect the brain's modular organization. Incorporating domain priors (network structure) should yield more meaningful, transferable representations for resting-state connectomics.
AI.method: Partition FC matrices into patches corresponding to intra- and inter-network connectivity blocks. Use a structured bilinear factorization to embed heterogeneous blocks while preserving network identity and achieving linear scaling in the number of networks. Employ anatomically grounded parcellation. Evaluate on ABCD, PNC, CCNP for behavior and psychopathology prediction; compare to structurally agnostic MAEs and graph-based SSL baselines; perform ablations.
AI.result: NERVE achieves more stable and transferable representations, particularly in cross-cohort settings. The bilinear network embedding and anatomically grounded parcellation are critical contributors, and the approach reduces parameter complexity from quadratic to linear in the number of networks.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating network-aware priors into self-supervised learning for functional connectomics improves representation quality and transferability, highlighting the value of domain-specific tokenization for resting-state FC analysis.

title: ProtoMedAgent: Multimodal Clinical Interpretability via Privacy-Aware Agentic Workflows
summary: While interpretable prototype networks offer compelling case-based reasoning for clinical diagnostics, their raw continuous outputs lack the semantic structure required for medical documentation. Bridging this gap via standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) routinely triggers ``retrieval sycophancy,'' where Large Language Models (LLMs) hallucinate post-hoc rationalizations to align with visual predictions. We introduce ProtoMedAgent, a framework that formalizes multimodal clinical reporting as an iterative, zero-gradient test-time optimization problem over a strict neuro-symbolic bottleneck. Operating on a frozen prototype backbone, we distill latent visual and tabular features into a discrete semantic memory. Online generation is strictly constrained by exact set-theoretic differentials and a reflective Scribe-Critic loop, mathematically precluding unsupported narrative claims. To safely bound data disclosure, we introduce a semantic privacy gate governed by $k$-anonymity and $\ell$-diversity. Evaluated on a 4,160-patient clinical cohort, ProtoMedAgent achieves 91.2\% Comparison Set Faithfulness where it fundamentally outperforms standard RAG (46.2\%). ProtoMedAgent additionally leverages a binding $\ell$-diversity phase transition to systematically reduce artifact-level membership inference risks by an absolute 9.8\%.

AI.tldr: ProtoMedAgent presents a neuro-symbolic, prototype-based framework for clinical reporting that eliminates post-hoc hallucinations by enforcing a strict, test-time optimization with discrete semantic memory and a Scribe-Critic loop, plus privacy controls; achieves high faithfulness and reduced privacy risk on a large clinical cohort.
AI.motivation: Interpretable prototype networks for clinical diagnostics produce outputs lacking semantic structure for medical documentation, and standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) often causes LLMs to hallucinate to align with predictions; there is a need for faithful, semantically structured, and privacy-preserving clinical reporting.
AI.method: Frozen prototype backbone; distill latent visual and tabular features into a discrete semantic memory; online generation constrained by exact set-theoretic differentials; reflective Scribe-Critic loop; zero-gradient test-time optimization; semantic privacy gate governed by k-anonymity and ell-diversity; -parameter data handling; phase-transition to reduce risks.
AI.result: On a 4,160-patient cohort, ProtoMedAgent achieves 91.2% Comparison Set Faithfulness (vs 46.2% for standard RAG). It also reduces artifact-level membership inference risk by an absolute 9.8% via an ell-diversity phase transition.
AI.conclusion: ProtoMedAgent delivers faithful, privacy-aware clinical reporting by enforcing a strict neuro-symbolic bottleneck and semantic memory, mitigating hallucinations and leakage risks while outperforming standard retrieval-augmented approaches.

title: Towards Resource-Efficient LLMs: End-to-End Energy Accounting of Distillation Pipelines
comment: Accepted to the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026). 11 pages, 6 figures
summary: The rise in deployment of large language models has driven a surge in GPU demand and datacenter scaling, raising concerns about electricity use, grid stress, and the impacts of modern AI workloads. Distillation is often promoted as one of the most effective paths to obtain cheaper, more efficient models, yet these claims rarely account for the full end-to-end energy and resource costs, including crucial teacher-side workloads such as data generation, logit caching, and evaluation. We present a comprehensive energy accounting framework that measures the complete computational cost of distillation pipelines via detailed stage-wise tracking of GPU device power consumption. In our experiments, we separate and log empirical energy use across distinct phases and systematically measure the energy and emissions of two common distillation methods: the classic logit-based knowledge distillation and synthetic-data supervised fine-tuning, constructing energy-quality Pareto frontiers that expose the previously ignored costs. From these measurements and analyses, we derive practical design rules for selecting distillation methods and hyperparameters under energy and budget constraints, and release an open-source measurement harness and accounting protocol to provide a standardized foundation for comparable, reproducible distillation research, explicitly accountable for complete pipeline energy impact.

AI.tldr: End-to-end energy accounting framework for LLM distillation, logging GPU power across pipeline stages, comparing logit-based KD and synthetic-data supervised fine-tuning, revealing energy-quality tradeoffs and providing design rules and open-source tooling.
AI.motivation: Rising GPU demand and electricity use from AI workloads, with distillation claims often ignoring teacher-side costs; need comprehensive, reproducible energy accounting to guide method and hyperparameter choices under energy constraints.
AI.method: Stage-wise measurement of GPU power across distillation pipelines, separating phases (data generation, logit caching, evaluation, training); compare two distillation methods (logit-based KD and synthetic-data supervised fine-tuning); build energy-quality Pareto frontiers; derive practical design rules; release open-source measurement harness and accounting protocol for reproducible end-to-end energy accounting.
AI.result: Quantified and separated energy use by pipeline stage; exposed additional costs in distillation not typically accounted for; produced energy-quality Pareto frontiers comparing KD and synthetic-data SFT; actionable design guidelines under energy and budget constraints; open-source harness and protocol for standardized evaluation.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end energy accounting is essential for fair evaluation of distillation methods; choose distillation strategy and hyperparameters with energy budgets in mind; promotes reproducible, energy-aware research through open-source tooling.

title: SeaVis: Modeling and Control of a Remotely Operated Towed Vehicle for Seabed Visualization and Mapping
summary: High-resolution seafloor mapping necessitates stable and precise positioning for underwater robots. This paper introduces a novel mathematical model for SeaVis remotely operated towed vehicles (ROTVs) and develops a gain-scheduled linear-quadratic regulator (LQR) for robust depth and attitude control. We validate the approach in a high-fidelity simulation, benchmarking the LQR against a conventional PID controller over a challenging seabed profile. The presented results demonstrate the LQR's superior performance, with significantly enhanced robustness to disturbances, greater control efficiency, and substantially reduced flap actuation. The gain scheduling also confirms the controller's effectiveness across the full operational velocity range. The complete simulation environment and controller are open-sourced.

AI.tldr: Gain-scheduled LQR for SeaVis ROTV depth and attitude control; outperforms PID in high-fidelity simulation; open-source.
AI.motivation: Need stable, precise positioning for high-resolution seafloor mapping; ROTV disturbances/uncertainties challenge conventional controllers.
AI.method: Develop a novel mathematical model for SeaVis ROTVs and design a gain-scheduled LQR for robust depth/attitude control; validate in high-fidelity simulation and compare to a PID baseline; open-source simulation environment and controller.
AI.result: LQR shows superior performance: enhanced disturbance robustness, higher control efficiency, and reduced flap actuation; effective across the full operational velocity range.
AI.conclusion: The proposed gain-scheduled LQR provides robust, efficient depth/attitude control for ROTVs in high-resolution seabed mapping, with open-source resources to enable replication and further work.

title: Bridging Legal Interpretation and Formal Logic: Faithfulness, Assumption, and the Future of AI Legal Reasoning
comment: 2 pages abstract accepted by Bloomberg LSLLAI 2026 Symposium
summary: The growing adoption of large language models in legal practice brings both significant promise and serious risk. Legal professionals stand to benefit from AI that can reason over contracts, draft documents, and analyze sources at scale, yet the high-stakes nature of legal work demands a level of rigor that current AI systems do not provide. The central problem is not simply that LLMs hallucinate facts and references; it is that they systematically draw inferences that go beyond what the source text actually supports, presenting assumption-laden conclusions as if they were logically grounded. This proposal presents a neuro-symbolic approach to legal AI that combines the expressive power of large language models with the rigor of formal verification, aiming to make AI-assisted legal reasoning both capable and trustworthy, thus reducing the burden of manual verification without sacrificing the accountability that legal practice demands.

AI.tldr: A neuro-symbolic framework combines LLMs with formal verification to improve legal AI reasoning, aiming for capable, trustworthy outputs and reduced manual verification burden.
AI.motivation: Legal practice requires rigorous, verifiable reasoning; current LLMs hallucinate and infer beyond source text, risking accountability and errors in high-stakes tasks.
AI.method: A neuro-symbolic approach that integrates the expressive power of large language models with formal verification to bound and validate legal inferences.
AI.result: Conceptual framework proposing rigorous, verifiable AI-assisted legal reasoning; aims to balance capability with accountability and reduce manual verification work.
AI.conclusion: If successful, the approach would reduce reliance on manual verification while maintaining legal accountability, enhancing trust in AI-assisted legal tasks.

title: PanoPlane: Plane-Aware Panoramic Completion for Sparse-View Indoor 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: We present PanoPlane, an approach for high-fidelity sparse-view indoor novel view synthesis that reconstructs closed room geometry via panoramic scene completion. Unlike perspective-based methods that generate training views from limited fields of view, PanoPlane leverages $360^{\circ}$ panoramic completion to condition the generative process on the full spatial layout. We propose Layout Anchored Attention Steering, a training-free mechanism that steers attention within the diffusion model's internal representation toward scene's detected planar surfaces at inference time. By directing each unobserved region's attention toward geometrically consistent observed content, our method replaces unconstrained hallucination with grounded surface extrapolation. The resulting panoramic completions provide supervision for 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling accurate novel-view synthesis across unobserved regions from as few as three input views. Experiments on Replica, ScanNet++, and Matterport3D demonstrate state-of-the-art novel view synthesis quality across 3, 6, and 9 input views, achieving up to $+17.8\%$ improvement in PSNR over the current state-of-the-art baseline without any training or fine-tuning of the diffusion model.

AI.tldr: PanoPlane introduces 360° panoramic completion to guide sparse-view indoor novel view synthesis, using a training-free Layout Anchored Attention Steering within a diffusion model to ground geometry and enable accurate 3D Gaussian Splatting-based rendering from as few as three views, achieving state-of-the-art PSNR without diffusion fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Conventional perspective-view synthesis from limited FOV struggles to infer complete room geometry; indoor scenes require coherent 3D layouts. Panoramic completion can provide full spatial context, enabling grounded extrapolation and better supervision for 3D representations.
AI.method: 1) Learn 360° panoramic room completion to capture full layout. 2) Introduce Layout Anchored Attention Steering, a training-free mechanism that steers diffusion-model attention toward detected planar surfaces during inference, aligning unobserved regions with observed content. 3) Use panoramic completions to supervise 3D Gaussian Splatting for novel-view synthesis across unobserved regions using as few as three input views.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art NVS quality on Replica, ScanNet++, and Matterport3D for 3, 6, and 9 input views, with up to +17.8% PSNR improvement over the current SOTA baseline without any training or fine-tuning of the diffusion model.
AI.conclusion: Panoramic completion with geometry-grounded attention steering enables grounded surface extrapolation and high-fidelity NVS from sparse views, reducing unconstrained hallucination and offering strong performance without diffusion-model training.

title: TabPFN-3: Technical Report
summary: Tabular data underpins most high-value prediction problems in science and industry, and TabPFN has driven the foundation model revolution for this modality. Designed with feedback from our users, TabPFN-3 builds on this foundation to scale state-of-the-art performance to datasets with 1M training rows and substantially reduce training and inference time. Pretrained exclusively on synthetic data from our prior, TabPFN-3 dramatically pushes the frontier of tabular prediction and brings substantial gains on time series, relational, and tabular-text data. On the standard tabular benchmark TabArena, a forward pass of TabPFN-3 outperforms all other models, including tuned and ensembled baselines, by a significant margin, and pareto-dominates the speed/performance frontier. On more diverse datasets, TabPFN-3 ranks first on datasets with many classes, and beats 8-hour-tuned gradient-boosted-tree baselines on datasets up to 1M training rows and 200 features. TabPFN-3 introduces test-time compute scaling to tabular foundation models. Our API offering TabPFN-3-Plus (Thinking) exploits this to beat all non-TabPFN models by over 200 Elo on TabArena, rising to 420 Elo on the largest data subset, and outperforms AutoGluon 1.5 extreme while being 10x faster, without using LLMs, real data, internet search or any other model besides TabPFN. TabPFN-3 extends the capabilities of our models, enabling SOTA prediction on relational data (new SOTA foundation model on RelBenchV1) and tabular-text data (SOTA on TabSTAR via TabPFN-3-Plus); and improves existing integrations: a specialized checkpoint, TabPFN-TS-3, ranks 2nd on the time-series benchmark fev-bench, and SHAP-value computation is up to 120x faster. TabPFN-3 achieves this performance while being up to 20x faster than TabPFN-2.5. In addition, a reduced KV cache and row-chunking scale to 1M rows on one H100 with fast inference speed.

AI.tldr: TabPFN-3 scales tabular foundation models to 1M training rows with faster inference/training, pretrained on synthetic data, achieving SOTA across tabular, relational, and tabular-text data; TabPFN-3-Plus (Thinking) beats non-TabPFN models on TabArena with large Elo gains; SHAP and inference speed improvements; 1M-row support via reduced KV cache and row-chunking.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable, fast, and broadly applicable tabular prediction using foundation-model-style approaches, addressing large datasets and diverse data modalities (time-series, relational, tabular-text).
AI.method: Scale architecture; synthetic-data pretraining from prior TabPFN; reduced KV cache; row-chunking; specialized checkpoints (TabPFN-TS-3); test-time compute scaling; API TabPFN-3-Plus; SHAP acceleration.
AI.result: TabPFN-3 achieves SOTA performance on TabArena, beating tuned/ensembled baselines; outperforms 8-hour-tuned GBDTs on datasets up to 1M rows and 200 features; TabPFN-3-Plus attains >200 Elo on TabArena and 420 Elo on the largest subset; 10x faster than AutoGluon 1.5 extreme; SHAP computation up to 120x faster; 20x speedup over TabPFN-2.5; scalable to 1M rows on a single H100 with reduced KV cache.
AI.conclusion: TabPFN-3 advances tabular foundation modeling by scalable pretraining and inference with broad applicability across modalities, delivering state-of-the-art results and practical speedups suitable for real-time deployment.

title: SR-Platform: An Agentic Pipeline for Natural Language-Driven Robot Simulation Environment Synthesis
summary: Generating robot simulation environments remains a major bottleneck in simulation-based robot learning. Constructing a training-ready MuJoCo scene typically requires expertise in 3D asset modeling, MJCF specification, spatial layout, collision avoidance, and robot-model integration. We present SR-Platform, a production-deployed agentic system that converts free-form natural language descriptions into executable, physically valid MuJoCo environments. SR-Platform decomposes scene synthesis into four stages: an LLM-based orchestrator that converts user intent into a structured scene plan; an asset forge that retrieves cached assets or generates new 3D geometry through LLM-to-CadQuery synthesis; a layout architect that assigns object poses and verifies industrial constraints; and a bridge layer that assembles the final MJCF scene and merges the selected robot model. The system is deployed as a nine-service Docker stack with WebSocket progress streaming, MinIO-backed mesh storage, Qdrant-based semantic asset retrieval, Redis job state, and InfluxDB telemetry. Using 30 days of production telemetry covering 611 successful LLM calls, SR-Platform generates five-object scenes with a median end-to-end latency of approximately 50 s, while cache-accelerated scenes complete in approximately 30-40 s. The asset forge shows an 11.3% first-attempt retry rate with automatic recovery, and cached asset retrieval removes per-object LLM calls for previously generated object types. These results show that agentic scene synthesis can reduce the manual effort required to create diverse robot training environments, enabling users to produce executable MuJoCo scenes from plain English prompts in under one minute.

AI.tldr: A production-deployed agentic system that translates plain-English prompts into executable MuJoCo environments via a four-stage pipeline (LLM orchestrator, asset forge, layout architect, bridge), achieving ~50 s end-to-end latency (30-40 s with caching) in production.
AI.motivation: Generating robot simulation environments is a bottleneck: it requires expertise in 3D asset modeling, MJCF specification, scene layout, collision avoidance, and robot-model integration. Automating end-to-end scene synthesis from natural language reduces manual effort and speeds up training.
AI.method: SR-Platform decomposes scene synthesis into four stages: (1) LLM-based orchestrator converts user intent into a structured scene plan; (2) asset forge retrieves cached assets or generates new 3D geometry via LLM-to-CadQuery synthesis; (3) layout architect assigns object poses and verifies industrial constraints; (4) bridge layer assembles the final MJCF scene and merges the selected robot model. It is deployed as a nine-service Docker stack with WebSocket progress streaming, MinIO-backed mesh storage, Qdrant-based semantic asset retrieval, Redis job state, and InfluxDB telemetry. Production telemetry covers 611 successful LLM calls over 30 days.
AI.result: SR-Platform generates five-object scenes with a median end-to-end latency of ~50 s; cache-accelerated scenes complete in ~30–40 s. Asset forge has an 11.3% first-attempt retry rate with automatic recovery. Cached asset retrieval eliminates per-object LLM calls for previously generated object types.
AI.conclusion: Agentic scene synthesis is a practical approach to reduce manual effort in creating diverse robot training environments, enabling users to produce executable MuJoCo scenes from plain English prompts in under one minute and demonstrating production viability and scalability.

title: SPIN: Structural LLM Planning via Iterative Navigation for Industrial Tasks
summary: Industrial LLM agent systems often separate planning from execution, yet LLM planners frequently produce structurally invalid or unnecessarily long workflows, leading to brittle failures and avoidable tool and API cost. We propose \texttt{SPIN}, a planning wrapper that combines validated Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) planning with prefix based execution control. \texttt{SPIN} enforces a strict DAG contract through \texttt{\_validate\_plan\_text} and repair prompting, producing executable plans before downstream execution, and then evaluates DAG prefixes incrementally to stop when the current prefix is sufficient to answer the query. On AssetOpsBench, across 261 scenarios, \texttt{SPIN} reduces executed tasks from 1061 to 623 and improves \emph{Accomplished} from 0.638 to 0.706, while reducing tool calls from 11.81 to 6.82 per run. On MCP Bench, the same wrapper improves planning, grounding, and dependency related scores for both GPT OSS1 and Llama 4 Maverick.

AI.tldr: SPIN is a planning wrapper that enforces DAG-valid executable plans and uses prefix-based execution to stop early, improving efficiency and success metrics in industrial LLM agent systems.
AI.motivation: LLM planners often generate structurally invalid or overly long workflows, causing brittleness and higher tool/API costs. A validated DAG-based abstraction with incremental execution promises robustness and efficiency.
AI.method: SPIN enforces a strict DAG contract via _validate_plan_text and repair prompting to produce executable plans prior to execution. It then incrementally evaluates DAG prefixes to stop once the prefix suffices to answer the query.
AI.result: On AssetOpsBench (261 scenarios): executed tasks reduced 1061→623; Accomplished improved 0.638→0.706; tool calls per run reduced 11.81→6.82. On MCP Bench: improved planning, grounding, and dependency-related scores for GPT OSS1 and Llama 4 Maverick.
AI.conclusion: SPIN provides safer, more efficient planning-execution by validating and repairing DAG plans and using prefix-based termination, yielding clear gains in task efficiency, success metrics, and tool usage across benchmarks.

title: TeDiO: Temporal Diagonal Optimization for Training-Free Coherent Video Diffusion
comment: CVPR'26 Workshop on Agentic AI for Visual Media
summary: Recent text-to-video diffusion transformers generate visually compelling frames, yet still struggle with temporal coherence, often producing flickering, drifting, or unstable motion. We show that these failures leave a clear imprint inside the model: incoherent videos consistently exhibit irregular, fragmented temporal diagonals in their intermediate self-attention maps, whereas stable motion corresponds to smooth, band-diagonal patterns. Building on this observation, we introduce TeDiO, a training-free, inference-time method that reinforces temporal consistency by regularizing these internal attention patterns. TeDiO estimates diagonal smoothness, identifies unstable regions, and performs lightweight latent updates that promote coherent frame-to-frame dynamics, without modifying model weights or using external motion supervision. Across multiple video diffusion models (e.g., Wan2.1, CogVideoX), TeDiO delivers markedly smoother motion while preserving per-frame visual quality, offering an efficient plug-and-play approach to improving dynamic realism in modern video generation systems.

AI.tldr: TeDiO is a training-free, inference-time method that regularizes temporal self-attention diagonals in text-to-video diffusion models to improve temporal coherence without changing weights or using external motion supervision.
AI.motivation: Temporal incoherence (flicker, drift) undermines realism in video diffusion; previous fixes require training or external motion cues, while a signal inside the model—self-attention diagonals—correlates with motion stability.
AI.method: Compute diagonal smoothness in intermediate self-attention maps during inference, identify unstable temporal regions, and apply lightweight latent updates to encourage smooth, coherent frame-to-frame dynamics. No weight updates or external supervision; compatible with multiple models (e.g., Wan2.1, CogVideoX).
AI.result: TeDiO yields markedly smoother temporal motion while preserving per-frame visual quality across several video diffusion backbones; demonstrates a plug-and-play route to improved dynamic realism without retraining.
