AI.conclusion: Temporal coherence in video diffusion can be enhanced via training-free, attention-guided latent refinements that regularize internal diagonals, enabling robust, model-agnostic improvements to motion realism.

title: Neural Fields for NV-Center Inverse Sensing
summary: Inverse problems in scientific sensing are often solved with either hand-designed regularizers or supervised networks trained on simulated labels, yet both can fail when the forward model is nonlinear, spectrally coupled, and physically delicate. We study this issue for noise sensing based on nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond, where a quantum sensor measures magnetic-noise spectra generated by sparse spin sources. We show that replacing a common scalar/coherent forward approximation with a tensor power-summed dipolar operator changes the inverse landscape and exposes a center-collapse failure mode in free-density optimization. We propose NeTMY, an amortization-free coordinate neural field coupled to the differentiable NV forward model, with annealed positional encoding, multiscale optimization, sparsity/gating, and spectrum-fidelity losses. Across sparse synthetic reconstructions generated by the corrected operator, NeTMY achieves the best localization and distributional metrics in the tested benchmark. Mechanism experiments show that NeTMY does not directly execute the raw density-space gradient; its parameterization smooths and redistributes updates, mitigating the center-collapse pathology. These results position NV quantum sensing as a useful testbed for physics-faithful neural inverse problems.

AI.tldr: Replacing a common scalar forward model with a tensor power-summed dipolar operator changes the inverse landscape, revealing a center-collapse failure in free-density optimization; NeTMY—a physics-faithful, amortization-free coordinate neural field tied to the NV forward model—achieves superior sparse reconstructions by smoothing updates and incorporating spectrum-fidelity losses.
AI.motivation: Inverse problems with nonlinear, spectrally coupled, delicate forward models (e.g., NV-based noise sensing) often fail with hand-designed regularizers or supervised learning. NV centers offer a physics-rich testbed to study and improve neural inverse methods under realistic forward models.
AI.method: Replace forward operator with a tensor power-summed dipolar operator. Propose NeTMY, an amortization-free coordinate neural field coupled to the differentiable NV forward model, featuring annealed positional encoding, multiscale optimization, sparsity/gating, and spectrum-fidelity losses.
AI.result: NeTMY achieves the best localization and distributional metrics on sparse synthetic reconstructions generated with the corrected operator. Mechanism studies show NeTMY’s parameterization smooths redistributes updates, mitigating center-collapse by not following raw density-space gradients.
AI.conclusion: NV quantum sensing is a valuable testbed for physics-faithful neural inverse problems; the proposed NeTMY framework demonstrates improved sparse reconstructions under a physically accurate forward model.

title: IntentVLA: Short-Horizon Intent Modeling for Aliased Robot Manipulation
comment: Code can be found in https://github.com/ZGC-EmbodyAI/IntentVLA
summary: Robot imitation data are often multimodal: similar visual-language observations may be followed by different action chunks because human demonstrators act with different short-horizon intents, task phases, or recent context. Existing frame-conditioned VLA policies infer each chunk from the current observation and instruction alone, so under partial observability they may resample different intents across adjacent replanning steps, leading to inter-chunk conflict and unstable execution. We introduce IntentVLA, a history-conditioned VLA framework that encodes recent visual observations into a compact short-horizon intent representation and uses it to condition chunk generation. We further introduce AliasBench, a 12-task ambiguity-aware benchmark on RoboTwin2 with matched training data and evaluation environments that isolate short-horizon observation aliasing. Across AliasBench, SimplerEnv, LIBERO, and RoboCasa, IntentVLA improves rollout stability and outperforms strong VLA baselines

AI.tldr: IntentVLA introduces a history-conditioned, short-horizon intent representation to stabilize action chunk generation in multimodal robot imitation; AliasBench provides a targeted benchmark to evaluate short-horizon observation aliasing; results show improved rollout stability and superior performance vs. baselines across multiple environments.
AI.motivation: Frame-conditioned visual-language action policies struggle under partial observability due to aliasing of short-horizon intents, leading to inter-chunk conflicts and unstable execution in robot imitation tasks.
AI.method: Develop IntentVLA, which encodes recent visual observations into a compact short-horizon intent representation and conditions chunk generation on this history-aware signal. Create AliasBench, a 12-task ambiguity-aware benchmark on RoboTwin2 with matched training data and evaluation environments to isolate short-horizon observation aliasing. Evaluate across AliasBench, SimplerEnv, LIBERO, and RoboCasa against strong VLA baselines.
AI.result: IntentVLA achieves improved rollout stability and outperforms strong VLA baselines across AliasBench, SimplerEnv, LIBERO, and RoboCasa.
AI.conclusion: History-conditioned intent representations effectively mitigate short-horizon observation aliasing, leading to more stable and reliable chunk-based policy execution in multimodal robot imitation scenarios; AliasBench provides a standardized testbed to study and quantify this challenge.

title: Bad Seeing or Bad Thinking? Rewarding Perception for Vision-Language Reasoning
summary: Achieving robust perception-reasoning synergy is a central goal for advanced Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Recent advancements have pursued this goal via architectural designs or agentic workflows. However, these approaches are often limited by static textual reasoning or complicated by the significant compute and engineering burden of external agentic complexity. Worse, this heavy investment does not yield proportional gains, often witnessing a "seesaw effect" on perception and reasoning. This motivates a fundamental rethinking of the true bottleneck. In this paper, we argue that the root cause of this trade-off is an ambiguity in modality credit assignment: when a VLM fails, is it due to flawed perception ("bad seeing") or flawed logic ("bad thinking")? To resolve this, we introduce a reinforcement learning framework that improves perception-reasoning synergy by reliably rewarding the perception fidelity. We explicitly decompose the generation process into interleaved perception and reasoning steps. This decoupling enables targeted supervision on perception. Crucially, we introduce Perception Verification (PV), leveraging a "blindfolded reasoning" proxy to reward perceptual fidelity independently of reasoning outcomes. Furthermore, to scale training across free-form VL tasks, we propose Structured Verbal Verification, which replaces high-variance LLM judging with structured algorithmic execution. These techniques are integrated into a Modality-Aware Credit Assignment (MoCA) mechanism, which routes rewards to the specific source of error -- either bad seeing or bad thinking -- enabling a single VLM to achieve simultaneous performance gains across a wide task spectrum.

AI.tldr: Root-cause bottleneck in vision-language models is modality credit assignment (perception vs. reasoning). The paper proposes a reinforcement learning framework with decoupled perception/reasoning, Perception Verification (PV), and Structured Verbal Verification, all under a Modality-Aware Credit Assignment (MoCA) to reliably reward perception fidelity and route errors, achieving broad performance gains across tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs struggle with a perception–reasoning trade-off: static textual reasoning or heavy external agents cause limited gains and a seesaw effect. The key problem is attributing failures to perception or thinking, which hampers targeted improvement.
AI.method: 1) Decompose generation into interleaved perception and reasoning steps. 2) Introduce Perception Verification (PV), a blindfolded reasoning proxy that rewards perceptual fidelity independently of reasoning outcomes. 3) Use Structured Verbal Verification to replace high-variance LLM judging with structured algorithmic execution, enabling scalable training for free-form VL tasks. 4) Integrate these into Modality-Aware Credit Assignment (MoCA) to route rewards to the faulty source (bad seeing vs bad thinking).
AI.result: The framework enables simultaneous performance gains across a wide task spectrum by reliably rewarding perception fidelity and clarifying error sources, improving perception–reasoning synergy without heavy external agentic components.
AI.conclusion: Attributing failures to perception or reasoning via PV, Structured Verbal Verification, and MoCA yields robust, scalable perception–reasoning synergy in VLMs, reducing the traditional seesaw and enabling broad task performance improvements.

title: Rethinking the Good Enough Embedding for Easy Few-Shot Learning
summary: The field of deep visual recognition is undergoing a paradigm shift toward universal representations. The Platonic Representation Hypothesis suggests that diverse architectures trained on massive datasets are converging toward a shared, "ideal" latent space. This again raises a critical question: is a "Good Embedding All You Need?" In this paper, we leverage this convergence to demonstrate that off-the-shelf embeddings are inherently "good enough" for complex tasks, rendering intensive task-specific fine-tuning unnecessary. We explore this hypothesis within the few-shot learning framework, proposing a straightforward, non-parametric pipeline that entirely bypasses backpropagation. By utilizing a k-Nearest Neighbor classifier on frozen DINOv2-L features, we conduct a layer-wise characterization to identify an optimal feature extraction. We further demonstrate that manifold refinement via PCA and ICA provides a beneficial regularizing effect. Our results across four major benchmarks demonstrate that our approach consistently surpasses sophisticated meta-learning algorithms, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: Non-parametric few-shot learning using frozen, universal embeddings; a kNN classifier on DINOv2-L features with PCA/ICA refinement, yielding state-of-the-art results across four benchmarks without backpropagation.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether diverse vision models converge to a universal latent space and whether fine-tuning is necessary for few-shot tasks; reduce training complexity by leveraging pre-trained embeddings.
AI.method: Layer-wise feature extraction analysis on frozen DINOv2-L representations; k-Nearest Neighbors classifier without backpropagation; manifold refinement via PCA and ICA; evaluation on four standard few-shot benchmarks.
AI.result: Across four benchmarks, the proposed pipeline consistently outperforms sophisticated meta-learning methods and achieves state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: Evidence supports the Platonic Representation Hypothesis: good-enough universal embeddings can drive strong few-shot performance with a simple, non-parametric approach; PCA/ICA aid regularization and generalization.

title: HodgeCover: Higher-Order Topological Coverage Drives Compression of Sparse Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers route tokens through a handful of experts, and learning-free compression of these layers reduces inference cost without retraining. A subtle obstruction blocks every existing compressor in this family: three experts can each be pairwise compatible yet form an irreducible cycle when merged together, so any score that ranks experts on pairwise signals is structurally blind to which triples are jointly mergeable. We show the obstruction is a precise mathematical object, the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian on a 2-complex whose vertices are experts, whose edges carry KL merge barriers, and whose faces carry triplet barriers; Hodge-decomposing the edge-barrier signal isolates the kernel exactly. We turn the diagnostic into a selection objective: HodgeCover greedily covers the harmonic-critical edges and triplet-critical triangles, and a hybrid variant of HodgeCover pairs it with off-the-shelf weight pruning on survivors. On three open-weight Sparse MoE backbones under aggressive expert reduction, HodgeCover matches state-of-the-art learning-free baselines on the expert-reduction axis, leads on the aggressive-compression frontier of the hybrid axis, and uniquely balances retained mass across all four Hodge components. These results show that exposing the harmonic kernel of a learned MoE structure changes which compressor wins at the regime that matters most.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals a fundamental obstruction in learning-free compression of sparse MoE layers: triple-wise interactions can form irreducible cycles that pairwise metrics miss. It introduces the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian to detect mergeable triplets and proposes HodgeCover to greedily cover harmonic-critical edges and triangles; a hybrid with weight pruning yields strong performance on multiple backbones, balancing mass across Hodge components.
AI.motivation: Overcome a structural blind spot in existing learning-free MoE compressions where three-expert interactions create irresolvable cycles. A topological diagnostic is needed to identify globally mergeable sets beyond pairwise compatibility.
AI.method: Construct a 2-complex with experts as vertices; edges carry KL merge barriers; faces carry triplet barriers. Compute the harmonic kernel via Hodge decomposition of the edge-barrier signal. Develop HodgeCover, a greedy algorithm that covers harmonic-critical edges and triangle barriers; propose a hybrid variant combining HodgeCover with standard weight pruning.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three open-weight Sparse MoE backbones under aggressive expert reduction shows HodgeCover matching state-of-the-art learning-free baselines for expert reduction, leading on the aggressive-compression frontier of the hybrid approach, and achieving balanced retained mass across all four Hodge components.
AI.conclusion: Exposing the harmonic kernel of a learned MoE structure shifts which compressor wins in the regime that matters, demonstrating the value of topological diagnostics for designing effective compression strategies in MoE models.

title: Exploring Bottlenecks in VLM-LLM Navigation: How 3D Scene Understanding Capability Impacts Zero-Shot VLN
comment: Accepted by ICRA Workshop MM-Spatial AI, Oral
summary: Zero-shot vision-and-language navigation (VLN) has gained significant attention due to its minimal data collection costs and inherent generalization. This paradigm is typically driven by the integration of pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and Large Language Models (LLMs), where VLMs construct 3D scene graphs while LLMs handle high-level reasoning and decision-making. However, a critical bottleneck exists in this system: current 3D perception models prioritize pixel-level accuracy, directly conflicting with the strict computational limits and real-time efficiency demanded by embodied navigation. To address this gap, this paper quantifies the actual impact of 3D scene understanding capability on VLN performance. Based on typical VLM-LLM frameworks, we propose statistical success rate (SR) upper bounds for two core subsystems: 1) the slow LLM planner, which relies on topological mapping semantics, and 2) the fast reactive navigator, which utilizes spatial coordinates and bounding boxes to execute LLM decisions. Evaluations using state-of-the-art 3D scene understanding models validate our proposed bounds and reveal a perception saturation phenomenon, indicating that improvements in perception accuracy beyond a certain threshold yield diminishing returns in navigation success. Our findings suggest that 3D scene understanding for VLN should pivot away from strict pixel-level precision, prioritizing instead navigation-relevant core vocabularies and accurate bounding box proportions.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot VLN performance saturates with perception accuracy; proposes statistical upper bounds (SR) for two subsystems (LLM planner and fast navigator) to quantify perception impact; finds diminishing returns beyond a perception threshold and advocates focusing on navigation-relevant vocabularies and bounding-box proportions rather than pixel-level precision.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch between high-precision 3D perception and the real-time, resource-constrained nature of VLN; quantify how perception quality translates to navigation success in VLM-LLM pipelines.
AI.method: Derive statistical success-rate upper bounds for two core VLN subsystems: (i) slow LLM planner using topological mapping semantics, and (ii) fast reactive navigator using spatial coordinates and bounding boxes. Validate these bounds using state-of-the-art 3D scene understanding models across VLN setups; examine the relationship between perception accuracy and navigation success.
AI.result: The proposed SR bounds are validated; a perception saturation phenomenon is observed where further improvements in perception accuracy yield diminishing returns in navigation success. Findings indicate that optimizing for navigation-relevant semantics and accurate bounding-box proportions is more beneficial than pursuing pixel-perfect 3D reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: VLN perception design should shift from pixel-level 3D accuracy to task-relevant information: focus on vocabularies and geometry that directly influence navigation decisions (e.g., correct bounding-box proportions, key semantic cues) to improve efficiency under real-time constraints.

title: MathAtlas: A Benchmark for Autoformalization in the Wild
summary: Current autoformalization benchmarks are largely focused on olympiad or undergraduate mathematics, while graduate and research-level mathematics remains underexplored. In this paper, we introduce MathAtlas, the first large-scale autoformalization benchmark of in the wild graduate-level mathematics, containing ~52k theorems, definitions, exercises, examples, and proofs extracted from 103 graduate mathematics textbooks. MathAtlas is enriched with a mathematical dependency graph containing ~178k relations, and is the first autoformalization benchmark to include such relations, facilitating evaluation and development of dependency-aware autoformalization systems. Our extensive experiments show that MathAtlas is high quality but extremely challenging: strong baselines achieve at most 9.8% correctness on theorem statements and 16.7% on definitions. Furthermore, we find performance of state-of-the-art models degrades substantially with dependency depth: on MA-Hard, a subset of 700 entities with the deepest dependency trees, the best model achieves only 2.6% correctness for autoformalization on this challenging dataset. We release MathAtlas to the community as a benchmark set for large-scale autoformalization of graduate-level mathematics in the wild.

AI.tldr: A large-scale, graduate-level autoformalization benchmark (MathAtlas) with ~52k statements from 103 textbooks and ~178k dependency relations; demonstrates high quality yet extreme difficulty, with low baseline accuracies and depth-sensitive performance; released for community use.
AI.motivation: Addresses a gap in existing autoformalization benchmarks focused on olympiad/undergrad math; aims to evaluate and drive dependency-aware autoformalization on real-world graduate mathematics.
AI.method: Construct dataset by mining 103 graduate math texts; extract 52k entities (theorems, definitions, exercises, examples, proofs); build a mathematical dependency graph with ~178k relations; define a hard subset MA-Hard (700 deepest dependency trees); run strong baselines to assess performance.
AI.result: Baseline models achieve up to 9.8% correctness on theorem statements and 16.7% on definitions; on MA-Hard subset, best model achieves 2.6% correctness; results indicate deterioration with dependency depth.
AI.conclusion: MathAtlas is high-quality yet extremely challenging, serving as a benchmark for large-scale autoformalization of graduate mathematics in the wild; will spur development of dependency-aware systems and further research in automated formalization.

title: You Only Landmark Once: Lightweight U-Net Face Super Resolution with YOLO-World Landmark Heatmaps
summary: Face image super-resolution aims to recover high-resolution facial images from severely degraded inputs. Under extreme upscaling factors, fine facial details are often lost, making accurate reconstruction challenging. Existing methods typically rely on heavy network architectures, adversarial training schemes, or separate alignment networks, increasing model complexity and computational cost. To address these issues, we propose a lightweight U-Net based-architecture designed to reconstructs $128{ \times }128$ facial images from severely degraded $16{ \times }16$ inputs, achieving an $8 \times $ magnification. A key contribution is a novel auxiliary-training-free supervision strategy that leverages heatmaps generated by YOLO-World, an open-vocabulary object detector, to localize key facial features such as eyes, nose, and mouth. These heatmaps are converted into spatial weights to form a heatmap-guided loss that emphasizes reconstruction errors in semantically important regions. Unlike prior methods that require dedicated landmark or alignment networks, our approach directly reuses detector outputs as supervision, maintaining an efficient training and inference pipeline. Experiments on the aligned CelebA dataset demonstrate that the proposed loss consistently improves quantitative metrics and produces sharper, more realistic reconstructions. Overall, our results show that lightweight networks can effectively exploit detection-driven priors for perceptually convincing extreme upscaling, without adversarial training or increased computational cost.

AI.tldr: Lightweight U-Net for extreme face super-resolution (8x) from 16x16 to 128x128, using heatmap-guided loss from YOLO-World detector as priors, without adversarial training or alignment networks.
AI.motivation: Reduce model complexity and computational cost in extreme upscaling, while leveraging semantic priors to preserve facial details.
AI.method: Proposes a small U-Net architecture; introduces an auxiliary supervision via heatmaps from an open-vocabulary detector (YOLO-World). Heatmaps converted to spatial weights to form heatmap-guided loss; no landmark/alignment networks, no adversarial training.
AI.result: On aligned CelebA, the approach yields improved quantitative metrics and sharper reconstructions; consistent improvements across metrics, highlighting the potential of detection-driven priors.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight networks can effectively exploit detection priors for perceptually convincing extreme upscaling, offering efficient training/inference without adversarial training.

title: Support Before Frequency in Discrete Diffusion
summary: Discrete diffusion models are increasingly competitive for language modeling, yet it remains unclear how their denoising objectives organize learning. Although these objectives target the full data distribution, we show that the exact reverse process induces a hierarchy between coarse support information and finer frequency information. For uniform and absorbing (a.k.a. masking) diffusion, we prove that, in the small-noise regime of the final denoising steps, each single-token reverse edit decomposes into a leading scale, determined by whether it moves toward the data support (e.g., grammatically valid sentences), and a finer coefficient, determining relative probabilities within the same scale. Thus, recovering validity structure only requires learning the correct order of magnitude of reverse probabilities, whereas recovering data frequencies requires coefficient-level estimation. The separation is mechanism-dependent: uniform diffusion exhibits a trichotomy into validity-improving, validity-preserving, and validity-worsening edits, while absorbing diffusion places its leading-order mass on validity-improving moves. Experiments on a masked language diffusion model and synthetic regular-language tasks support these predictions: support-localization emerges earlier than within-support frequency ranking, and the contrast between uniform and absorbing diffusion matches the predicted rate separation. Together, our results suggest that discrete diffusion models learn data support before data frequencies.

AI.tldr: Discrete diffusion models for language modeling exhibit a hierarchical organization in the reverse denoising process: a leading scale capturing data-support validity, and a finer coefficient capturing within-support frequencies. This separation depends on the diffusion type (uniform vs absorbing), with uniform diffusion showing a trichotomy of edits and absorbing diffusion mass on validity-improving moves. Empirical results on masked diffusion and synthetic tasks support earlier support-localization versus frequency ranking, indicating learning support before frequencies.
AI.motivation: Understand how denoising objectives in discrete diffusion models shape what the model learns about language: whether learning concentrates on the data support (valid sentences) before data frequencies (co-occurrence patterns), and how diffusion scheme (uniform vs absorbing) influences this order.
AI.method: Theoretically analyze the small-noise regime of the final denoising steps for uniform (random replacement with a token) and absorbing (masking) diffusion. Show that single-token reverse edits decompose into a leading scale (toward data support) and a finer probability coefficient (within the same scale). Prove a trichotomy for uniform diffusion and a leading-order mass on validity-improving moves for absorbing diffusion. Validate predictions with experiments on a masked language diffusion model and synthetic regular-language tasks.
AI.result: In the small-noise regime, reverse edits separate into a dominant scale and a subordinate frequency coefficient. Support-localization emerges earlier than within-support frequency ranking. Uniform diffusion yields a three-way classification of edits (validity-improving, preserving, worsening); absorbing diffusion concentrates mass on validity-improving moves. Experiments align with theory, indicating data-support learning precedes data-frequencies in discrete diffusion models.
AI.conclusion: Discrete diffusion models primarily learn the data-support structure before frequencies, with the diffusion mechanism shaping the rate and nature of this learning. This separation explains why such models may first capture grammatical validity before subtler frequency-based distinctions.

title: Learning Cross-Coupled and Regime Dependent Dynamics for Aerial Manipulation
summary: Accurate dynamics models are critical for aerial manipulators operating under complex tasks such as payload transport. However, modeling these systems remains fundamentally challenging due to strong quadrotor-manipulator coupling, delayed aerodynamic interactions, and regime-dependent dynamics variations arising from payload changes and manipulator reconfiguration. These effects produce residual dynamics that are simultaneously cross-coupled, history-dependent, and nonstationary, causing both analytical models and purely offline learned models to degrade during deployment. To address these challenges, we propose a structured encoder-decoder framework for adaptive residual dynamics learning in aerial manipulators. The proposed nonlinear latent encoder captures cross-variable coupling and temporal dependencies from state-input histories, while a lightweight linear latent decoder enables online adaptation under regime-dependent nonstationary dynamics. The linear-in-parameter decoder structure permits closed-form Bayesian adaptation together with consistency-driven covariance inflation, enabling rapid and stable adaptation to both transient and slowly varying dynamics changes while remaining compatible with real-time model predictive control (MPC). Experimental results on a real aerial manipulation platform demonstrate improved residual prediction accuracy, faster adaptation under changing operating conditions, and enhanced MPC-based trajectory tracking performance. These results highlight the importance of jointly modeling coupled temporal dynamics and deployment-time nonstationarity for reliable aerial manipulation.

AI.tldr: A structured encoder-decoder for adaptive residual dynamics in aerial manipulators, combining a nonlinear latent encoder for cross-variable coupling and temporal dependencies with a lightweight linear latent decoder that supports online Bayesian adaptation, yielding improved residual prediction and MPC-ready control under regime-dependent nonstationary dynamics.
AI.motivation: Aerial manipulators exhibit strong quadrotor-manipulator coupling, delayed aerodynamics, and regime-dependent, nonstationary dynamics due to payload changes and reconfiguration. These effects cause residual dynamics that are cross-coupled and history-dependent, degrading both analytic models and offline learned models during deployment.
AI.method: Use a nonlinear latent encoder to capture cross-variable coupling and temporal histories of state-input, paired with a lightweight linear latent decoder to enable online adaptation. The decoder is linear-in-parameters to allow closed-form Bayesian adaptation with covariance inflation, ensuring rapid, stable adaptation for transient and slowly varying dynamics, while staying compatible with real-time MPC.
AI.result: Experimental results on a real aerial manipulation platform show improved residual prediction accuracy, faster adaptation under changing operating conditions, and enhanced trajectory-tracking performance using MPC.
AI.conclusion: Modeling coupled temporal dynamics and deployment-time nonstationarity is crucial for reliable aerial manipulation; the proposed encoder-decoder framework effectively captures these effects, enabling robust residual modeling and online adaptation for MPC.

title: Know When To Fold 'Em: Token-Efficient LLM Synthetic Data Generation via Multi-Stage In-Flight Rejection
summary: While synthetic data generation with large language models (LLMs) is widely used in post-training pipelines, existing approaches typically generate full outputs before applying quality filters, leading to substantial token waste on samples that are ultimately discarded. To address this, we propose Multi-Stage In-Flight Rejection (MSIFR), a lightweight, training-free framework that detects and terminates low-quality generation trajectories at intermediate checkpoints before they reach full completion. MSIFR decomposes the generation process into sequential stages and applies fast rule-based validators to identify arithmetic inconsistencies, hallucination patterns, and formatting violations, enabling early rejection of faulty samples. We formalize in-flight rejection as a sequential decision process and show that any non-trivial discard policy reduces expected token consumption, with stage-wise savings increasing when rejection occurs earlier in the generation pipeline. We further demonstrate that conditional utility estimates form a martingale, ensuring that early, in-flight rejection does not bias the expected utility of retained samples. Across five instruction-tuned models and seven reasoning benchmarks, MSIFR reduces token consumption by 11%-77% as a standalone method, and up to 78.2% when combined with early-exit methods, while preserving or improving evaluation accuracy. These results confirm that MSIFR provides a practical mechanism for improving the efficiency of LLM-based synthetic data generation without additional training or architectural changes.

AI.tldr: MSIFR reduces token waste in LLM synthetic data generation via multi-stage, in-flight rejection using fast rule-based validators; achieves up to 78% token savings with accuracy retained.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiency in current LLM-driven synthetic data pipelines where full generations are created and discarded after post-hoc quality filters, causing high token waste.
AI.method: Decompose generation into sequential stages; apply lightweight, rule-based validators to detect arithmetic inconsistencies, hallucinations, and formatting violations; model in-flight rejection as a sequential decision process; prove discounting discarded trajectories reduces expected token use; show a martingale property for conditional utility estimates to avoid bias in retained samples.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across five instruction-tuned models and seven reasoning benchmarks shows 11–77% standalone token savings, up to 78.2% when combined with early-exit; preserves or improves evaluation accuracy.
AI.conclusion: MSIFR is a training-free, architecture-agnostic framework that improves efficiency of LLM-based synthetic data generation by early rejection of low-quality trajectories without harming performance.

title: CoReDiT: Spatial Coherence-Guided Token Pruning and Reconstruction for Efficient Diffusion Transformers
summary: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) deliver remarkable image and video generation quality but incur high computational cost, limiting scalability and on-device deployment. We introduce CoReDiT, a structured token pruning framework for DiTs across vision tasks. CoReDiT uses a linear-time spatial coherence score to estimate local redundancy in the latent token lattice and skips high coherence (redundant) tokens in self-attention. To maintain a dense representation and avoid visual discontinuities, we reconstruct skipped attention outputs via coherence-guided aggregation of spatially neighboring retained tokens. We further introduce a progressive, block-adaptive pruning schedule that increases pruning gradually and allocates larger budgets to blocks and denoising steps with higher redundancy. Across state-of-the-art diffusion backbones including PixArt-α and MagicDrive-V2, CoReDiT achieves up to 55% self-attention FLOPs reduction and inference speedups of 1.33x on cloud GPUs and 1.72x on mobile NPUs, while maintaining high visual quality. Notably, CoReDiT also increases on-device memory head-room, enabling higher-resolution generation.

AI.tldr: CoReDiT introduces structured, progressive token pruning for diffusion transformers to reduce self-attention cost while preserving visual quality; achieves up to 55% FLOPs reduction and speedups on GPUs and mobile NPUs, with increased on-device memory headroom.
AI.motivation: Diffusion Transformers deliver high-quality image/video generation but incur high computational cost, hindering scalability and on-device deployment. The authors propose a structured pruning framework (CoReDiT) to identify and skip redundant tokens in self-attention without sacrificing output quality, enabling faster and more memory-efficient inference across backbone models.
AI.method: Compute a linear-time spatial coherence score to estimate local redundancy in the latent token lattice and skip high-coherence (redundant) tokens in self-attention. Reconstruct skipped attention outputs via coherence-guided aggregation of neighboring retained tokens to preserve density and avoid visual discontinuities. Apply a progressive, block-adaptive pruning schedule that increases pruning gradually and allocates larger budgets to blocks and denoising steps with higher redundancy. Validate across diffusion backbones PixArt-α and MagicDrive-V2.
AI.result: Up to 55% self-attention FLOPs reduction and inference speedups of 1.33x on cloud GPUs and 1.72x on mobile NPUs, with maintenance of high visual quality. On-device memory headroom increases, enabling higher-resolution generation.
AI.conclusion: CoReDiT provides effective structured pruning for diffusion transformers, enabling faster, more memory-efficient inference while preserving quality. Demonstrates generality across backbones (PixArt-α, MagicDrive-V2) and is conducive to on-device deployment.

title: Dywave: Event-Aligned Dynamic Tokenization for Heterogeneous IoT Sensing Signal
summary: Internet of Things (IoT) systems continuously collect heterogeneous sensing signals from ubiquitous sensors to support intelligent applications such as human activity analysis, emotion monitoring, and environmental perception. These signals are inherently non-stationary and multi-scale, posing unique challenges for standard tokenization techniques. This paper proposes Dywave, a dynamic tokenization framework for IoT sensing signals that constructs compact input representations aligned with intrinsic temporal structures and underlying physical events. Dywave leverages wavelet-based hierarchical decomposition, identifies meaningful temporal boundaries corresponding to underlying semantic events, and adaptively compresses redundant intervals while preserving temporal coherence. Extensive evaluations on five real-world IoT sensing datasets across activity recognition, stress assessment, and nearby object detection demonstrate that Dywave outperforms state-of-the-art methods by up to 12% in accuracy, while improving computational efficiency by reducing input token lengths by up to 75% across mainstream sequence models. Moreover, Dywave exhibits improved robustness to domain shifts and varying sequence lengths.

AI.tldr: Dywave introduces a wavelet-based dynamic tokenization for IoT sensing that yields compact representations aligned with intrinsic temporal structure, improving accuracy (up to 12%) and reducing token length (up to 75%) across multiple IoT tasks and models.
AI.motivation: IoT sensing signals are inherently non-stationary and multi-scale, making standard tokenization ineffective at preserving semantic events and temporal coherence; a method that adaptively captures meaningful temporal boundaries and compresses redundant intervals is needed for robust, efficient downstream learning.
AI.method: Dywave employs hierarchical wavelet decomposition to capture multi-scale temporal structure, identifies semantic event boundaries aligned with underlying physical events, and adaptively compresses redundant intervals while preserving temporal coherence. It is evaluated on five real-world IoT datasets covering activity recognition, stress assessment, and nearby object detection, against state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.result: Dywave outperforms state-of-the-art methods by up to 12% in accuracy and reduces input token lengths by up to 75% across mainstream sequence models, with improved robustness to domain shifts and varying sequence lengths.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, wavelet-based tokenization effectively aligns inputs with intrinsic temporal structure and events in IoT sensing, yielding improved accuracy and efficiency. This approach is promising for broader IoT applications and could inspire further work on real-time deployment and cross-domain robustness.

title: CaMeRL: Collision-Aware and Memory-Enhanced Reinforcement Learning for UAV Navigation in Multi-Scale Obstacle Environments
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures. Submitted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
summary: In obstacle avoidance navigation of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), variations in obstacle scale have received strangely less attention than obstacle number or density. Existing methods typically extract purely geometric features from single-frame depth observations. Such representations tend to neglect small obstacles and lose spatial context under occlusions caused by large obstacles, leading to noticeable degradation in environments with multi-scale obstacles. To address this issue, we propose CaMeRL, a Collision-aware and Memory-enhanced Reinforcement Learning framework for UAV navigation. The collision-aware latent representation encodes risk-sensitive depth cues to preserve fine-grained obstacle structures, thereby improving sensitivity to small obstacles. The temporal memory module integrates observations across frames, mitigating partial observability caused by large-obstacle occlusions. We evaluate CaMeRL with multi-scale obstacles, including ultra-small and extra-large obstacle settings. Results show that CaMeRL outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across all scales, with success rate gains of 0.48 and 0.28 in the ultra-small and extra-large settings, respectively. More importantly, CaMeRL achieves reliable navigation in cluttered outdoor environments.

AI.tldr: CaMeRL: a collision-aware, memory-enhanced RL framework that improves multi-scale obstacle avoidance for UAVs, outperforming baselines on ultra-small and extra-large obstacle settings.
AI.motivation: Obstacle-scale variation and occlusions are under-addressed; single-frame, purely geometric features fail to preserve small obstacle details and lose context under occlusion.
AI.method: Propose CaMeRL with a collision-aware latent representation encoding risk-sensitive depth cues and a temporal memory module to fuse observations across frames within an RL navigation framework.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across obstacle scales; reported success-rate gains of 0.48 (ultra-small) and 0.28 (extra-large); demonstrates reliable navigation in cluttered outdoor environments.
AI.conclusion: CaMeRL improves small-obstacle sensitivity and occlusion robustness via collision-aware depth cues and temporal memory, highlighting the importance of multi-scale obstacle handling in UAV navigation.

title: SkillFlow: Flow-Driven Recursive Skill Evolution for Agentic Orchestration
summary: In recent years, a variety of powerful LLM-based agentic systems have been applied to automate complex tasks through task orchestration. However, existing orchestration methods still face key challenges, including strategy collapse under reward maximization, high gradient variance with opaque credit assignment, and unguided skill evolution whose decisions are typically made by directly prompting an LLM to judge rather than derived from principled training signals. To address these challenges, we propose SkillFlow, a flow-based framework that takes a trainable Supervisor as the agent and a structured environment with dynamic skill library and frozen executor, automating task orchestration through multi-turn interaction. SkillFlow employs Tempered Trajectory Balance (TTB), a regression-based flow-matching loss that samples trajectories proportional to reward, preserving diverse orchestration strategies rather than collapsing to a single mode. The same flow objective yields a jointly learned backward policy that provides transparent per-step credit assignment at zero additional inference cost. Building on these flow diagnostics, a recursive skill evolution mechanism determines when to evolve, what skills to create or prune, and where decision gaps lie -- closing the loop from training signal to autonomous capability growth. Experimental results on 14 datasets show that SkillFlow significantly outperforms baselines across question answering, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and real-world interactive decision making tasks. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SkillFlow-E850.

AI.tldr: SkillFlow introduces a flow-based framework for LLM-based task orchestration using a trainable Supervisor, tempered trajectory balance, a backward policy for credit assignment, and a recursive skill evolution mechanism, achieving strong performance on 14 datasets across QA, math reasoning, code generation, and interactive decisions.
AI.motivation: Address core challenges in LLM-based orchestration: strategy collapse under reward maximization, high gradient variance with opaque credit assignment, and unguided skill evolution that relies on direct prompting rather than principled training signals.
AI.method: A flow-based framework (SkillFlow) with a trainable Supervisor operating in a structured environment consisting of a dynamic skill library and frozen executor. It uses Tempered Trajectory Balance (TTB), a regression-based flow-matching loss that samples trajectories proportional to reward, to preserve diverse orchestration strategies. The approach yields a jointly learned backward policy for transparent per-step credit assignment at no extra inference cost. A recursive skill evolution mechanism decides when to evolve, what skills to create or prune, and where decision gaps lie, linking training signals to autonomous capability growth.
AI.result: Experimental results on 14 datasets show SkillFlow significantly outperforms baselines across question answering, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and real-world interactive decision making tasks.
AI.conclusion: Flow-based orchestration with a trainable Supervisor, principled credit assignment via the backward policy, and a recursive skill evolution loop yields robust, diverse, and scalable task orchestration for LLM agents; code is available.

title: Automatic Landmark-Based Segmentation of Human Subcortical Structures in MRI
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures. Accepted for presentation at the 48th Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society (EMBC 2026)
summary: Precise segmentation of brain structures in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is essential for reliable neuroimaging analysis, yet voxel-wise deep models often yield anatomically inconsistent results that diverge from expert-defined boundaries. In this research, we propose a landmark-guided 3D brain segmentation approach that explicitly mimics the manual segmentation protocol of the Harvard--Oxford Atlas. A Global-to-Local network automatically detects 16 landmarks representing key subcortical reference points. Then, a semantic segmentation model produces a coarse segmentation of 12 anatomical labels, each grouping multiple subcortical regions. Finally, a landmark-driven post-processing step separates these 12 labels into 26 distinct structures by enforcing local anatomical constraints. Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements in boundary accuracy. Overall, integrating learned landmarks aligns segmentations more closely with manual protocols.

AI.tldr: Landmark-guided 3D brain segmentation improves boundary accuracy by converting 12 coarse labels into 26 structures using 16 learned landmarks and a landmark-driven post-processing step, mimicking Harvard–Oxford protocol.
AI.motivation: To address anatomically inconsistent voxel-wise segmentations and better reflect expert manual boundaries by incorporating explicit landmarks that reflect the Harvard–Oxford atlas segmentation protocol.
AI.method: A Global-to-Local network detects 16 subcortical landmarks; a semantic 3D segmentation produces 12 coarse labels; a landmark-driven post-processing step enforces local anatomical constraints to split these into 26 structures.
AI.result: Experiments show consistent improvements in boundary accuracy and better alignment with manual protocol for subcortical segmentation.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating learned anatomical landmarks improves segmentation consistency with manual protocols and enhances boundary fidelity.

title: R2R2: Robust Representation for Intensive Experience Reuse via Redundancy Reduction in Self-Predictive Learning
comment: Accepted at the Forty-Third International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026). This is the camera-ready version
summary: For reinforcement learning in data-scarce domains like real-world robotics, intensive data reuse enhances efficiency but induces overfitting. While prior works focus on critic bias, representation-level instability in Self-Predictive Learning (SPL) under high Update-to-Data (UTD) regimes remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose Robust Representation via Redundancy Reduction (R2R2), a regularization method within SPL. We theoretically identify that standard zero-centering conflicts with SPL's spectral properties and design a non-centered objective accordingly. We verify R2R2 on SPL-native algorithms like TD7. Furthermore, to demonstrate its orthogonality to prior advancements, we extend the state-of-the-art SimbaV2, which originally lacks SPL, by integrating a tailored SPL module, termed SimbaV2-SPL. Experiments across 11 continuous control tasks confirm that R2R2 effectively mitigates overfitting; specifically, at a UTD ratio of 20, it improves TD7 by ~22% and provides additional gains on top of SimbaV2-SPL, which itself establishes a new state-of-the-art. The code can be found at: https://github.com/songsang7/R2R2

AI.tldr: Introduces R2R2, a non-centered redundancy-reduction regularization for Self-Predictive Learning (SPL) to curb overfitting under high Update-to-Data (UTD); extends SimbaV2 with SPL (SimbaV2-SPL); achieves state-of-the-art across 11 tasks; code at GitHub.
AI.motivation: In data-scarce domains like real-world robotics, heavy data reuse boosts sample efficiency but induces overfitting. SPL's representation-level instability under high UTD is underexplored; standard zero-centering conflicts with SPL's spectral properties.
AI.method: Propose Robust Representation via Redundancy Reduction (R2R2): a regularization term within SPL with a non-centered objective to align with SPL's spectral characteristics; verify on SPL-native algorithms like TD7; extend SimbaV2 with a tailored SPL module to form SimbaV2-SPL; experiments across 11 continuous-control tasks.
AI.result: R2R2 mitigates overfitting; at UTD=20, TD7 improves by ~22%; SimbaV2-SPL provides additional gains and achieves new state-of-the-art; code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: R2R2 addresses spectral-malignment in SPL by using a non-centered redundancy-reduction objective, improving stability under high data reuse; demonstrates compatibility and orthogonality with prior advances by integrating SPL into Sala SimbaV2 framework; broad potential for data-scarce RL; code release.

title: Learning Direct Control Policies with Flow Matching for Autonomous Driving
comment: 16 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables. Accepted at IEEE ITSC 2026
summary: We present a flow-matching planner for autonomous driving that directly outputs actionable control trajectories defined by acceleration and curvature profiles. The model is conditioned on a bird's-eye-view (BEV) raster of the surrounding scene and generates control sequences in a small number of Ordinary Differential Equations (ODE) integration steps, enabling low-latency inference suitable for real-time closed-loop re-planning. We train exclusively on urban scenarios (real urban city streets, intersections and roundabouts of the city of Parma, Italy) collected from a 2D traffic simulator with reactive agents, and evaluate in closed-loop on both in-distribution and markedly out-of-distribution environments, including multi-lane highways and unseen urban scenarios. Our results show that the model generalizes reliably to these unseen conditions, maintaining stable closed-loop control and successfully completing scenarios that differ substantially from the training distribution. We attribute this to the BEV representation, which provides a geometry-centric view of the scene that is inherently less sensitive to distributional shifts, and to the flow-matching formulation, which learns a smooth vector field that degrades gracefully under distribution shift. We provide video demonstrations of closed-loop behavior at https://marcelloceresini.github.io/DirectControlFlowMatching.

AI.tldr: A BEV-conditioned flow-matching planner outputs acceleration and curvature trajectories via a small number of ODE steps for real-time closed-loop autonomous driving, showing robust generalization to unseen in/out-of-distribution scenarios.
AI.motivation: Reduce latency and improve robustness of motion planning in AVs by producing direct control trajectories in a geometry-centric BEV frame, and by leveraging flow-matching to yield a smooth, generalizable vector field that degrades gracefully under distribution shift.
AI.method: Train a BEV-conditioned flow-matching planner on urban Parma data (2D simulator with reactive agents) that outputs control sequences defined by acceleration and curvature profiles, computed via a small number of ODE integration steps for low-latency inference; evaluate in closed-loop on both in-distribution and markedly out-of-distribution environments (multi-lane highways, unseen urban scenarios).
AI.result: The model generalizes reliably to unseen conditions, maintaining stable closed-loop control and completing scenarios that differ substantially from the training distribution.
AI.conclusion: BEV geometry-centric representation reduces sensitivity to distribution shifts; flow-matching learns a smooth vector field that degrades gracefully under shift; the approach enables low-latency, real-time re-planning as demonstrated in video demonstrations.

title: ChromaFlow: A Negative Ablation Study of Orchestration Overhead in Tool-Augmented Agent Evaluation
summary: Autonomous language-model agents increasingly combine planning, tool use, document processing, browsing, code execution, and verification loops. These capabilities make agent systems more useful, but they also introduce operational failure modes that are not visible from final accuracy alone. This report presents ChromaFlow, a tool-augmented autonomous reasoning framework built around planner-directed execution, specialized tool use, and telemetry-driven evaluation. We analyze ChromaFlow on GAIA 2023 Level-1 validation tasks under clean evaluation constraints. A frozen full Level-1 baseline achieved 29/53 correct answers, or 54.72%. A later recovery configuration with expanded orchestration achieved 27/53 correct answers, or 50.94%, while increasing tracebacks, timeout events, tool-failure mentions, token-line calls, and campaign-log cost estimates. Two randomized 20-task smoke evaluations produced 12/20 and 11/20 correct answers, showing that small diagnostic gains can be unstable across samples. The central result is therefore a negative ablation: more aggressive orchestration did not improve full-set performance and increased operational noise. The report argues that bounded planner escalation, deterministic extraction, evidence reconciliation, and explicit run gates should be treated as first-order requirements for reliable autonomous agent evaluation.

AI.tldr: Aggressive tool orchestration in ChromaFlow did not improve full-set performance and increased operational noise; bounded planner escalation and explicit evaluation gates are recommended for reliable autonomous-agent evaluation.
AI.motivation: Addresses failure modes in autonomous language-model agents that are not captured by final accuracy and argues for robust, telemetry-driven evaluation frameworks.
AI.method: Tool-augmented autonomous reasoning with planner-directed execution, specialized tool use, and telemetry-driven evaluation. Evaluation on GAIA 2023 Level-1 tasks under clean constraints with baseline and recovery configurations, plus two randomized smoke tasks.
AI.result: Baseline: 29/53 correct (54.72%). Recovery: 27/53 (50.94%). More orchestration increased tracebacks, timeout events, tool-failure mentions, token-line calls, and campaign-log costs without improving full-set accuracy. Two 20-task smoke evaluations: 12/20 and 11/20. Findings indicate a negative ablation: more aggressive orchestration did not help and added noise.
AI.conclusion: Advocate incorporating bounded planner escalation, deterministic extraction, evidence reconciliation, and explicit run gates as core requirements for reliable evaluation of autonomous agents.

title: Implicit spatial-frequency fusion of hyperspectral and lidar data via kolmogorov-arnold networks
summary:
Hyperspectral image (HSI) classification is challenging in complex scenes due to spectral ambiguity, spatial heterogeneity, and the strong coupling between material properties and geometric structures. Although LiDAR provides complementary elevation information, most HSI-LiDAR fusion methods rely on CNNs or MLPs with fixed activation functions and linear weights. These methods struggle to model structural discontinuities in LiDAR data, intricate spectral features of HSI, and their interactions. In addition, fusion of the two modalities in both spatial and frequency domains with LiDAR guidance remains underexplored.
  To address these issues, we propose the Implicit Frequency-Geometry Fusion Network (IFGNet), which leverages Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) with learnable spline-based functions to adaptively capture highly nonlinear relationships between hyperspectral and LiDAR features. Furthermore, IFGNet introduces a LiDAR-guided implicit aggregation module in both spatial and frequency domains, enhancing geometry-aware spatial representations while capturing global structural patterns.
  Experiments on the Houston 2013 and MUUFL benchmarks demonstrate that IFGNet consistently outperforms existing fusion methods in overall accuracy, average accuracy, and Cohen's Kappa, while maintaining an efficient architecture.

AI.tldr: IFGNet uses Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) with learnable spline activations and a LiDAR-guided implicit aggregation module in both spatial and frequency domains to fuse HSI and LiDAR features, addressing nonlinear relationships and geometric structure; shows improved OA, AA, and Cohen's Kappa on Houston 2013 and MUUFL benchmarks with efficient architecture.
AI.motivation: Address spectral ambiguity, spatial heterogeneity, and the coupling between material properties and geometry in HSI-LiDAR fusion. Overcome limitations of fixed-activation CNN/MLP approaches and underexplored spatial-frequency fusion guided by LiDAR.
AI.method: Employ Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) with spline-based learnable activations to model highly nonlinear HSI-LiDAR interactions. Introduce a LiDAR-guided implicit aggregation module operating in both spatial and frequency domains to produce geometry-aware, global representations.
AI.result: IFGNet consistently improves overall accuracy, average accuracy, and Cohen's Kappa compared to existing fusion methods on Houston 2013 and MUUFL benchmarks while maintaining a compact/efficient architecture.
AI.conclusion: KAN-based nonlinear fusion with LiDAR-guided implicit aggregation in dual domains effectively captures complex HSI-LiDAR relationships and geometry, yielding superior fusion performance with efficient computation.

title: Self-Pruned Key-Value Attention: Learning When to Write by Predicting Future Utility
summary:
Under modern test-time compute and agentic paradigms, language models process ever-longer sequences. Efficient text generation with transformer architectures is increasingly constrained by the Key-Value cache memory footprint and bandwidth. To address this limitation, we introduce Self-Pruned Key-Value Attention (SP-KV), a mechanism designed to predict future KV utility in order to reduce the size of the long-term KV cache. This strategy operates at a fine granularity: a lightweight utility predictor scores each key-value pair, and while recent KVs are always available via a local window, older pairs are written in the cache and used in global attention only if their predicted utility surpasses a given threshold. The LLM and the utility predictor are trained jointly end-to-end exclusively through next-token prediction loss, and are adapted from pretrained LLM checkpoints.
  Rather than enforcing a fixed compression ratio, SP-KV performs dynamic sparsification: the mechanism adapts to the input and typically reduces the KV cache size by a factor of $3$ to $10\times$, longer sequences often being more compressible. This leads to vast improvements in memory usage and decoding speed, with little to no degradation of validation loss nor performance on a broad set of downstream tasks. Beyond serving as an effective KV-cache reduction mechanism, our method reveals structured layer- and head-specific sparsity patterns that we can use to guide the design of hybrid local-global attention architectures.

AI.tldr: SP-KV introduces a lightweight, end-to-end trainable predictor that sparsifies the transformer KV-cache by dynamically filtering older keys/values based on predicted utility, reducing memory and decode time by 3–10x with minimal accuracy loss, and revealing layer-/head-specific sparsity to guide hybrid attention designs.
AI.motivation: Efficient text generation with long input sequences is bottlenecked by the memory and bandwidth required for storing and attending to a long KV cache in transformers. A dynamic, data-adaptive mechanism is needed to trim unused KV entries without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: A self-pruning KV attention mechanism. A lightweight utility predictor assigns a score to each key-value pair. Recent KVs are always available in a local window; older KVs are cached but only used in global attention if their predicted utility exceeds a threshold. The LLM and predictor are trained jointly end-to-end from next-token prediction loss, starting from pretrained checkpoints. Unlike fixed compression, the method sparsifies dynamically and adapts to the input, typically achieving 3–10× KV-cache reduction.
AI.result: Dynamic sparsification yields substantial memory and decoding speed improvements with little to no degradation on validation loss or downstream tasks. It also uncovers structured sparsity patterns that vary by layer and attention head, offering guidance for designing hybrid local-global attention architectures.
AI.conclusion: SP-KV provides an effective, end-to-end trainable mechanism for KV-cache reduction under long-sequence regimes, enabling larger-scale or faster generation while preserving performance, and offering insights into architecture design via observed sparsity patterns.

title: Chrono-Gymnasium: An Open-Source, Gymnasium-Compatible Distributed Simulation Framework
summary: High-fidelity physics simulation is essential for closing the sim-to-real gap in robotics and complex mechanical systems. However, the computational overhead of high-fidelity engines often limits their use in data-intensive tasks like Reinforcement Learning (RL) and global optimization. We introduce Chrono-Gymnasium, a distributed computing framework that scales the high-fidelity multi-body dynamics of Project Chrono across large-scale computing clusters. Built upon the Ray framework, Chrono-Gymnasium provides a standardized Gymnasium interface, enabling seamless integration with modern machine learning libraries while providing built-in synchronization and messaging primitives for distributed execution. We demonstrate the framework's capabilities through two distinct case studies: (1) the training of an RL agent for autonomous robotic navigation in complex terrains, and (2) the Bayesian Optimization of a planetary lander's design parameters to ensure landing stability. Our results show that Chrono-Gymnasium reduces wall-clock time for high-fidelity simulations without sacrificing physical accuracy, offering a scalable path for the design and control of complex robotic systems.

AI.tldr: A distributed, high-fidelity physics-simulation framework (Chrono-Gymnasium) scales Project Chrono across clusters via Ray, exposing a Gymnasium interface to enable ML workflows, with built-in synchronization for distributed execution; demonstrated on RL navigation and planetary-lander design optimization.
AI.motivation: Address the computational overhead of high-fidelity simulations and close the sim-to-real gap in data-intensive tasks (e.g., RL and global optimization) by enabling scalable, distributed physics.
AI.method: Integrates Project Chrono physics with Ray for distributed execution and a Gymnasium-compatible interface; provides synchronization/messaging primitives; validated through two case studies: RL-based autonomous navigation in complex terrains and Bayesian optimization of lander design parameters for stable landing.
AI.result: Demonstrates reduced wall-clock time for high-fidelity simulations without compromising physical fidelity; shows scalability and practical utility for robotics control and design optimization.
AI.conclusion: Chrono-Gymnasium offers a scalable path to leverage high-fidelity physics in data-driven robotics, enabling faster iterations in learning and optimization while preserving accuracy.

title: Modeling Bounded Rationality in Drug Shortage Pharmacists Using Attention-Guided Dynamic Decomposition
comment: Accepted at CogSci 2026. 6 pages plus references, 1 figure, 2 tables
summary: Hospital pharmacists make high-stakes decisions to mitigate drug shortages under uncertainty, time pressure, and patient risk. Interviews revealed that pharmacists focus attention on a small subset of drugs, limiting cognitive effort to the most urgent cases. Motivated by these findings, we formalize a bounded-rational, attention-guided decision framework that dynamically decomposes drugs into a subset for high-cost reasoning and a complementary subset for low-cost monitoring. We develop two agents: an Expert Agent that applies attention weights derived from pharmacist interviews, and a Learner Agent that adapts attention allocation over time through experience. Across simulated scenarios spanning short to long horizons, we show that attention-guided planning supports stable decision-making without complete state reasoning. These results suggest that a primary decision is not what action to take, but where to allocate cognitive effort, and that attention-guided, satisficing strategies can reduce problem complexity while maintaining stable performance.

AI.tldr: An attention-guided bounded-rational framework for hospital pharmacy decisions that decomposes drugs into high-cost reasoning and low-cost monitoring subsets; implements an Expert Agent with fixed attention from interviews and a Learner Agent that adapts attention; simulations show attention-focused planning yields stable decisions under uncertainty with reduced full-state reasoning.
AI.motivation: Pharmacists face high-stakes, uncertain drug shortage decisions and reportedly focus attention on a small set of urgent drugs. The work formalizes and tests whether bounded rationality and attention allocation can reduce cognitive load while preserving decision quality.
AI.method: Develop a bounded-rational, attention-guided decision framework that dynamically partitions drugs into high-cost and low-cost subsets. Implement an Expert Agent using interview-derived attention weights and a Learner Agent that learns/adjusts attention over time. Evaluate across simulated scenarios with short to long horizons to assess stability and performance without full state reasoning.
AI.result: Attention-guided planning produced stable decision-making without requiring complete state reasoning, suggesting that focusing cognitive effort on select drugs can maintain performance while reducing problem complexity.
AI.conclusion: The primary decision is where to allocate cognitive effort rather than what action to take. Attention-guided satisficing strategies can reduce problem complexity and sustain stable performance in high-stakes pharmacy decisions.

title: Generative Deep Learning for Computational Destaining and Restaining of Unregistered Digital Pathology Images
summary: Conditional generative adversarial networks (cGANs) have enabled high-fidelity computational staining and destaining of hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) in digital pathology whole-slide images (WSI). However, their ability to generalize to out-of-distribution WSI across institutions without retraining remains insufficiently characterized. Previously developed cGAN models trained on 102 registered prostate core biopsy WSIs from Brigham and Women's Hospital were evaluated on 82 spatially unregistered WSIs acquired at Stanford University. To mitigate domain shift without retraining, a preprocessing pipeline consisting of histogram-based stain normalization for H&E-stained WSIs and channel-wise intensity calibration for unstained WSIs was developed. Because image registration was intentionally omitted for real-world deployment conditions, the reported quantitative results are conservative lower bounds reflecting both model performance and limited spatial alignment. Under these conditions, virtual destaining achieved a Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) of 0.854, structural similarity index measure (SSIM) of 0.699, and peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) of 18.41 dB. H&E restaining from computationally destained outputs outperformed direct staining from ground-truth unstained inputs across all metrics (PCC: 0.798 vs. 0.715; SSIM: 0.756 vs. 0.718; PSNR: 20.08 vs. 18.51 dB), suggesting that preprocessing quality may be more limiting than model capacity. Qualitative pathological review indicated preservation of benign glandular structures while showing that malignant glands were often rendered with vessel-like morphologies. These findings support the feasibility of applying cGAN-based computational H&E staining and destaining generative models to external WSI datasets using preprocessing-based adaptation alone while defining specific morphological targets for future domain adaptation.

AI.tldr: cGAN-based computational H&E staining/destaining shows moderate generalization to an external WSI dataset without retraining when aided by a preprocessing normalization pipeline; destaining/outcome can exceed direct staining in several metrics, but gland morphologies in malignant tissue may be distorted, and results are conservative due to lack of image registration.
AI.motivation: Assess how well pretrained cGAN models for H&E staining/destaining generalize across institutions and whether preprocessing-based domain adaptation suffices to reduce performance loss without retraining.
AI.method: Evaluate a cGAN trained on 102 registered Brigham and Women's Hospital prostate WSIs on 82 spatially unregistered Stanford WSIs. Apply preprocessing: histogram-based stain normalization for H&E WSIs and channel-wise intensity calibration for unstained WSIs. Do not use image registration. Compare destained outputs to ground-truth H&E and to directly stained outputs from unstained inputs using PCC, SSIM, PSNR. Provide qualitative pathology review.
AI.result: Destaining: PCC 0.854, SSIM 0.699, PSNR 18.41 dB. Restaining from destained outputs outperforms direct staining (PCC 0.798 vs 0.715; SSIM 0.756 vs 0.718; PSNR 20.08 vs 18.51 dB). Results are conservative lower bounds due to lack of registration; preprocessing quality may limit performance more than model capacity. Benign glands are well-preserved; malignant glands often resemble vessel-like morphologies after processing.
AI.conclusion: Preprocessing-based domain adaptation enables external deployment of cGAN-based staining/destaining without retraining, but attention should be given to morphology-targeted domain adaptation and potential artifacts in malignant regions; future work should quantify and mitigate these morphological distortions and explore registration-inclusive alignments to raise performance bounds.

title: Reliability-Gated Source Anchoring for Continual Test-Time Adaptation
summary: Continual test-time adaptation (CTTA) updates a pretrained model online on an unlabeled, non-stationary stream while anchoring it to a frozen source checkpoint. This anchor is useful only when the source remains reliable. On CCC-Hard, however, a ResNet-50 source falls to approximately $1.3\%$ top-$1$ accuracy, while existing source-anchored CTTA methods continue applying the same anchor strength. We call this failure mode blind anchoring and propose RMemSafe, a reliability-gated extension of ROID that uses the frozen source's normalized predictive entropy to attenuate all explicit source-coupled uses in the objective. When the source posterior approaches uniformity, the gate closes: the source anchor and agreement filter vanish, and the objective reduces to a source-agnostic fallback comprising ROID's base losses plus marginal calibration. Combined with ASR, RMemSafe achieves the lowest error on $8$ of $9$ matched-split continual-corruption cells and is the best reset-based method on all $9$, improving ROID+ASR by $1.05$~pp on ResNet-50 and $0.48$~pp on ViT-B/16. A controlled source-degradation sweep shows a $1.13{\times}$ shallower harm slope than ROID+ASR, consistent with the graceful-decay prediction. The entropy gate detects high-entropy source collapse, not confidently wrong low-entropy sources; this scope is explicitly evaluated and discussed.

AI.tldr: RMemSafe extends ROID with a reliability-gated mechanism that uses the source's predictive entropy to down-weight or remove source-coupled objectives when the frozen source becomes unreliable, yielding source-agnostic fallback behavior and improved CTTA performance.
AI.motivation: Continual test-time adaptation relies on a fixed, frozen source. When the source deteriorates (blind anchoring), existing CTTA methods degrade as they keep applying the same anchor strength. A mechanism to detect and down-weight reliance on a failing source is needed.
AI.method: Introduce RMemSafe, a reliability-gated extension of ROID. It uses the frozen source's normalized predictive entropy as a gate to attenuate all explicit source-coupled terms. When entropy is high (source posterior near uniform), the anchor and agreement terms are disabled, leaving a source-agnostic loss (ROID base losses plus marginal calibration). Combined with ASR, evaluated on continual-corruption benchmarks and across ResNet-50 and ViT-B/16.
AI.result: RMemSafe achieves state-of-the-art performance on 8 of 9 matched-split continual-corruption cells and is the best reset-based method on all 9. It improves ROID+ASR by 1.05 percentage points on ResNet-50 and 0.48 pp on ViT-B/16. A controlled degradation sweep shows a 1.13× shallower harm slope compared with ROID+ASR, aligning with graceful-decay predictions. The entropy gate detects high-entropy source collapse (not confidently wrong low-entropy cases) and is validated in evaluation.
AI.conclusion: An entropy-based reliability gate can effectively suppress harmful source anchoring in CTTA, enabling graceful degradation and robust performance under source degradation while preserving strong results when the source is reliable.

title: FU-MPC: Frontier- and Uncertainty-Aware Model Predictive Control for Efficient and Accurate UAV Exploration with Motorized LiDAR
summary: Efficient UAV exploration in unknown environments requires rapid coverage expansion while maintaining accurate and reliable localization, since safe navigation in complex scenes depends on consistent mapping and pose estimation. However, for conventional LiDAR-equipped UAVs, the observable region is tightly coupled with the UAV pose and motion. Expanding coverage often requires additional translational or rotational maneuvers, which can reduce exploration efficiency and increase the risk of localization degradation in geometrically challenging environments. Motorized rotating LiDARs provide a promising solution by actively adjusting the sensor viewing direction without changing the UAV motion, thereby introducing an additional sensing degree of freedom. Nevertheless, existing exploration systems rarely exploit this scanning freedom as an explicit decision variable linked to both exploration progress and localization quality. To address this gap, we develop a UAV platform equipped with an independently actuated rotating LiDAR and propose a hierarchical exploration framework. The global planner organizes frontiers into representative viewpoints and sequences them using topology-aware transition costs. Built upon this planner, FU-MPC serves as a local receding-horizon scan controller that optimizes LiDAR rotation along the predicted flight trajectory. The controller jointly considers frontier-aware exploration utility and direction-dependent localization uncertainty, while lightweight surrogate evaluation enables real-time onboard execution. Experiments in complex environments demonstrate that the proposed system improves exploration efficiency while maintaining robust localization performance compared with fixed-pattern scanning and uncertainty-only baselines. The project page can be found at https://kafeiyin00.github.io/FU-MPC/.

AI.tldr: A rotating LiDAR-enabled UAV with hierarchical planning improves exploration efficiency and localization robustness by decoupling sensing from motion: a global frontier-based planner with topology-aware sequencing guides the flight, while a local FU-MPC controller optimizes LiDAR rotation along the trajectory. Real-time onboard performance outperforms fixed-pattern scanning and uncertainty-only approaches.
AI.motivation: For LiDAR-equipped UAVs, the observable region is tightly coupled to pose and motion, making rapid exploration and robust localization difficult in complex environments. An independently actuated rotating LiDAR introduces an additional sensing degree of freedom to improve coverage and localization without extra UAV motion.
AI.method: Develop a UAV platform with an independently actuated rotating LiDAR and a hierarchical exploration framework: (1) a global planner that groups frontiers into representative viewpoints and sequences them using topology-aware transition costs, and (2) a local receding-horizon scan controller (FU-MPC) that optimizes LiDAR rotation along the predicted flight path, balancing frontier-aware exploration utility with direction-dependent localization uncertainty. Real-time onboard execution via lightweight surrogate evaluation.
AI.result: Experiments in complex environments show improved exploration efficiency and robust localization performance compared with fixed-pattern scanning and uncertainty-only baselines.
AI.conclusion: Active LiDAR scanning via an independently actuated rotating LiDAR provides an effective extra sensing degree of freedom, enabling faster, more reliable UAV exploration with maintained localization, demonstrated by onboard real-time implementation and comparative experiments.

title: ClawForge: Generating Executable Interactive Benchmarks for Command-Line Agents
summary: Interactive agent benchmarks face a tension between scalable construction and realistic workflow evaluation. Hand-authored tasks are expensive to extend and revise, while static prompt evaluation misses failures that only appear when agents operate over persistent state. Existing interactive benchmarks have advanced agent evaluation significantly, but most initialize tasks from clean state and do not systematically test how agents handle pre-existing partial, stale, or conflicting artifacts. We present \textbf{ClawForge}, a generator-backed benchmark framework for executable command-line workflows under state conflict. The framework compiles scenario templates, grounded slots, initialized state, reference trajectories, and validators into reproducible task specifications, and evaluates agents step by step over persistent workflow surfaces using normalized end state and observable side effects rather than exact trajectory matching. We instantiate this framework as the ClawForge-Bench (17 scenarios, 6 ability categories).
