Results across seven frontier models show that the best model reaches only 45.3% strict accuracy, wrong-state replacement remains below 17\% for all models, and the widest model separation (17% to 90%) is driven by whether agents inspect existing state before acting. Partial-credit and step-efficiency analyses further reveal that many failures are near-miss closures rather than early breakdowns, and that models exhibit qualitatively different failure styles under state conflict.

AI.tldr: ClawForge introduces a generator-backed benchmark for executable CLI workflows under state conflict, enabling stepwise evaluation with persistent state and partial-credit metrics.
AI.motivation: Addresses the tension between scalable benchmark construction and realistic workflow evaluation by testing agents on pre-existing partial, stale, or conflicting artifacts in persistent environments.
AI.method: Generator-backed framework that compiles scenario templates, grounded slots, initialized state, reference trajectories, and validators into reproducible task specifications; evaluates agents step by step over persistent workflow surfaces using normalized end state and observable side effects rather than exact trajectory matching; instantiated as ClawForge-Bench with 17 scenarios across 6 ability categories; evaluation across seven frontier models.
AI.result: Best model reaches 45.3% strict accuracy; wrong-state replacement remains below 17% for all models; widest model separation (17% to 90%) is driven by whether agents inspect existing state before acting; partial-credit and step-efficiency analyses indicate many failures are near-miss closures and reveal qualitatively different failure styles under state conflict.
AI.conclusion: ClawForge provides a scalable, reproducible benchmark for evaluating agent behavior under state conflict, emphasizing state-awareness and offering fine-grained metrics (partial credit, step efficiency) to diagnose failure modes and guide future benchmark design.

title: Towards Real-Time Autonomous Navigation: Transformer-Based Catheter Tip Tracking in Fluoroscopy
comment: Harry Robertshaw and Yanghe Hao contributed equally to this work. Published in the International Journal of Computer Assisted Radiology and Surgery
summary: Purpose: Mechanical thrombectomy (MT) improves stroke outcomes, but is limited by a lack of local treatment access. Widespread distribution of reinforcement learning (RL)-based robotic systems can be used to alleviate this challenge through autonomous navigation, but current RL methods require live device tip coordinate tracking to function. This paper aims to develop and evaluate a real-time catheter tip tracking pipeline under fluoroscopy, addressing challenges such as low contrast, noise, and device occlusion. Methods: A multi-threaded pipeline was designed, incorporating frame reading, preprocessing, inference, and post-processing. Deep learning segmentation models, including U-Net, U-Net+Transformer, and SegFormer, were trained and benchmarked using two-class and three-class formulations. Post-processing involved two-step component filtering, one-pixel medial skeletonization, and greedy arc-length path following with contour fall-back. Results: On manually-labeled moderate complexity fluoroscopic video data, the two-class SegFormer achieved a mean absolute error of 4.44 mm, outperforming U-Net (4.60 mm), U-Net+Transformer (6.20 mm) and all three-class models (5.19-7.74 mm). On segmentation benchmarks, the system exceeded state-of-the-art CathAction results with improvements of up to +5% in Dice scores for three-segmentation. Conclusion: The results demonstrate that the proposed multi-threaded tracking framework maintains stable performance under challenging imaging conditions, outperforming prior benchmarks, while providing a reliable and efficient foundation for RL-based autonomous MT navigation.

AI.tldr: Real-time catheter tip tracking in fluoroscopy using multi-threaded DL segmentation; two-class SegFormer achieves lowest MAE (4.44 mm) and outperforms CathAction Dice by up to 5%; supports RL-based autonomous MT navigation.
AI.motivation: Enable autonomous, RL-driven MT navigation by providing reliable real-time catheter tip localization under challenging fluoroscopic imaging to expand treatment access.
AI.method: Multi-threaded pipeline (frame read, preprocess, inference, post-process). Evaluated DL segmentation models (U-Net, U-Net+Transformer, SegFormer) in two-class and three-class formulations; post-processing includes two-step component filtering, 1-pixel medial skeletonization, and greedy arc-length path following with contour fall-back.
AI.result: Two-class SegFormer MAE 4.44 mm (best among tested); U-Net 4.60 mm; U-Net+Transformer 6.20 mm; three-class models 5.19-7.74 mm. On segmentation benchmarks, +up to 5% Dice over CathAction for three-segmentation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-threaded tracking framework yields stable, improved catheter-tip localization under challenging fluoroscopy, providing a robust foundation for RL-based autonomous MT navigation.

title: Behavioral Data-Driven Optimal Trajectory Generation for Rotary Cranes
summary: With the growth of the construction industry and the global shortage of skilled labor, the automation of crane control has become increasingly important for safe and efficient operations. A central challenge in automatic crane control is the reduction of load oscillations during motion, which is primarily addressed through appropriate slewing trajectories. In this context, classical model-based control methods rely on accurate dynamical models and expert tuning, and often struggle to meet safety and precision requirements, while many learning-based approaches require large data sets and significant computational resources. This paper proposes a behavioral data-driven framework for generating open-loop slewing trajectories for rotary cranes that suppress load sway while reducing operation time and energy consumption. The approach builds on Willems' fundamental lemma and its generalizations, to bypass explicit system modeling and operate directly on measured input-output data. A practical workflow is presented in this paper to reduce the need for expert knowledge. Despite the underactuated nature of the crane dynamics, the method identifies a nonparametric representation of the system behavior and generates smooth, optimal trajectories using limited data and convex optimization. The proposed trajectory generation method is validated on a laboratory crane setup and compared against an established model-based approach, achieving up to 35% reduction in load sway, 43% reduction in tracking error, and 50% reduction in travel time.

AI.tldr: Data-driven open-loop slewing trajectory generation for rotary cranes using Willems' lemma; achieves significant reductions in load sway, tracking error, and travel time; validated on a lab crane.
AI.motivation: Address the need for safe, efficient crane control without relying on accurate models or extensive data; mitigate dependence on expert tuning in underactuated crane dynamics.
AI.method: Nonparametric system behavior identification using Willems' fundamental lemma from measured input-output data, followed by convex optimization to generate smooth, optimal open-loop trajectories for slewing with sway suppression.
AI.result: Laboratory crane experiments show up to 35% reduction in load sway, 43% reduction in tracking error, and 50% reduction in travel time compared to a model-based approach.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability of data-driven trajectory generation for rotary cranes, reducing required expert knowledge; promising for practice with caveats about open-loop robustness and data requirements.

title: Distribution-Aware Algorithm Design with LLM Agents
summary:
We study learning when the learned object is executable solver code rather than a predictor. In this setting, correctness is not enough: two solvers may both return valid solutions on the deployment distribution while differing substantially in runtime. Given samples from an unknown task distribution, the learner returns code evaluated on fresh instances by both solution quality and execution time. Our central abstraction is a \emph{solver hint}: reusable structure inferred from samples and compiled into specialized solver code. We prove that the empirically fastest sample-consistent solver from a fixed library generalizes in both correctness and runtime, and that statistically identifiable hints can be recovered and compiled from polynomially many samples.
  Empirically, we instantiate the framework with LLM code agents on \(21\) structured combinatorial-optimization target distributions across seven problem classes. The synthesized solvers reach mean normalized quality \(0.971\), improve by \(+0.224\) over the average heuristic pool and by \(+0.098\) over the highest-quality heuristic, and are \(336.9\times\), \(342.8\times\), and \(16.1\times\) faster than the quality-best heuristic, Gurobi, and the selected time-limited exact backend, respectively. On released PACE 2025 Dominating Set private instances, the synthesized solver is valid on all \(100\) graphs and runs about two orders of magnitude faster than top competition solvers, with a moderate quality gap. Inspection shows that many gains come from changing the computational scale: replacing ambient exponential search or general-purpose optimization with compiled distribution-specific computation.

AI.tldr: Synthesize executable solvers by learning solver hints from samples; the fastest sample-consistent solver from a fixed library generalizes in both correctness and runtime, and hints can be recovered from polynomially many samples. Empirically, LLM-based solvers across many combinatorial-optimization tasks achieve large quality and dramatic runtime gains by compiling distribution-specific solver code, outperforming heuristics, Gurobi, and exact backends, and scaling to PACE Dominating Set instances.
AI.motivation: In many settings, not only solution correctness but also execution time matters. Learned objects are executable solver programs, so the optimization objective must include runtime. This motivates developing a framework to infer reusable solver structures (solver hints) from data and compile them into specialized solvers that depend on the task distribution.
AI.method: Introduce solver hints as reusable structures inferred from samples and compiled into specialized solver code. Prove that the empirically fastest sample-consistent solver from a fixed library generalizes in both correctness and runtime, and that statistically identifiable hints can be recovered from polynomially many samples. Empirically instantiate with LLM code agents on 21 structured combinatorial-optimization distributions across seven problem classes; evaluate on fresh instances with metrics on solution quality and execution time; compare against a pool of heuristics, Gurobi, and a selected time-limited exact backend.
AI.result: Solvers yield mean normalized quality 0.971; improve by +0.224 over average heuristic pool and by +0.098 over best heuristic. They are 336.9x, 342.8x, and 16.1x faster than the quality-best heuristic, Gurobi, and the time-limited exact backend, respectively. On PACE 2025 Dominating Set private instances, the synthesized solver is valid on all 100 graphs and runs ~100x faster than top competitors, with a moderate quality gap. Gains largely stem from replacing ambient exponential search or generic optimization with compiled, distribution-specific computation.
AI.conclusion: Solver hints enable generalizable, highly efficient executable solvers learned from data. The framework combines theoretical guarantees (generalization of fastest sample-consistent solver and identifiability of hints) with strong empirical performance across diverse problem classes, suggesting a viable path to automatically synthesize high-performance solvers tailored to task distributions; future work could extend to broader distributions, robustness, and interpretability of the inferred hints.

title: Image Restoration via Diffusion Models with Dynamic Resolution
summary: Diffusion models (DMs) have exhibited remarkable efficacy in various image restoration tasks. However, existing approaches typically operate within the high-dimensional pixel space, resulting in high computational overhead. While methods based on latent DMs seek to alleviate this issue by utilizing the compressed latent space of a variational autoencoder, they require repeated encoder-decoder inference. This introduces significant additional computational burdens, often resulting in runtime performance that is even inferior to that of their pixel-space counterparts. To mitigate the computational inefficiency, this work proposes projecting data into lower-dimensional subspaces using dynamic resolution DMs to accelerate the inference process. We first fine-tune pre-trained DMs for dynamic resolution priors and adapt DPS and DAPS, which are two widely used pixel-space methods for general image restoration tasks, into the proposed framework, yielding methods we refer to as SubDPS and SubDAPS, respectively. Given the favorable inference speed and reconstruction fidelity of SubDAPS, we introduce an enhanced variant termed SubDAPS++ to further boost both reconstruction efficiency and quality. Empirical evaluations across diverse image datasets and various restoration tasks demonstrate that the proposed methods outperform recent DM-based approaches in the majority of experimental scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/StarNextDay/SubDAPS.git.

AI.tldr: A dynamic-resolution, subspace approach for diffusion-model-based image restoration achieves faster inference with competitive or superior reconstruction quality by projecting data into lower-dimensional subspaces and adapting existing pixel-space restoration methods (DPS/DAPS) into SubDPS/SubDAPS, with SubDAPS++ offering further improvements.
AI.motivation: To reduce the computational burden of diffusion-model-based image restoration, which is high in pixel space, and improve efficiency without sacrificing quality, addressing the inefficiency of latent-DMs that rely on encoder-decoder cycles.
AI.method: Fine-tune pre-trained diffusion models for dynamic-resolution priors and adapt two pixel-space methods, DPS and DAPS, into a subspace framework, yielding SubDPS and SubDAPS. Introduce SubDAPS++ as an enhanced variant to boost speed and quality.
AI.result: Empirical results across diverse datasets and restoration tasks show that SubDPS/SubDAPS (especially SubDAPS and SubDAPS++) outperform recent DM-based approaches in most scenarios, indicating improved inference speed and reconstruction fidelity. Code is released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic-resolution subspace diffusion models offer a practical and effective solution for fast, high-quality image restoration, with SubDAPS++ providing a notable performance edge over existing DM-based methods.

title: SurF: A Generative Model for Multivariate Irregular Time Series Forecasting
summary: Irregularly sampled multivariate event streams remain a stubbornly difficult modality for generative modeling: tokenization-based approaches break down when inter-event intervals vary by orders of magnitude, and neural temporal point processes are bottlenecked by window-level numerical quadrature. We (i) propose SurF, a generative model that uses the Time Rescaling Theorem (TRT) as a learnable bijection between event sequences and i.i.d.\ unit-rate exponential noise, enabling a single model to be trained across heterogeneous event-stream datasets; (ii) three efficient parameterizations of the cumulative intensity that scale to long sequences; and (iii) a Transformer-based encoder for multi-dataset pretraining. On six real-world benchmarks, SurF achieves the best reported time RMSE on Earthquake, Retweet, and Taobao, and is within trial-level noise of the strongest specialist on the remaining three. Under a strict leave-one-out protocol, the held-out checkpoint beats every classical and neural-autoregressive baseline on 5/6 datasets and beats every baseline on Amazon and Earthquake, an initial step toward foundation models over asynchronous event streams.

AI.tldr: SurF presents a unified generative model for irregular multivariate event streams by using the Time Rescaling Theorem as a learnable bijection to i.i.d. exponential noise, with scalable intensity parameterizations and Transformer-based pretraining across datasets; it achieves state-of-the-art time RMSE on multiple benchmarks and shows strong cross-dataset generalization under leave-one-out evaluation.
AI.motivation: Irregularly sampled event streams challenge generative modeling, as tokenization-based methods fail with wide inter-event intervals and numerical quadrature limits neural temporal point processes. A single model capable of handling heterogeneous, long sequences across datasets is highly desirable.
AI.method: Introduce SurF, a model that uses the Time Rescaling Theorem as a learnable bijection from event sequences to i.i.d. unit-rate exponential noise. Provide three efficient parameterizations of the cumulative intensity to scale to long sequences, and employ a Transformer-based encoder for multi-dataset pretraining.
AI.result: SurF achieves the best reported time RMSE on Earthquake, Retweet, and Taobao across six real-world benchmarks; on the remaining three, it remains within trial-level noise of the strongest specialist. In a strict leave-one-out setting, the held-out checkpoint beats all classical and neural-autoregressive baselines on 5/6 datasets and beats every baseline on Amazon and Earthquake, indicating promising steps toward foundation-model-like behavior for asynchronous event streams.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates strong cross-dataset generalization and efficiency for irregular event streams, offering a compelling path toward foundation models in this modality, though further analysis on scalability, diversity, and long-horizon generation would be valuable.

title: A Prototyping Framework for Distributed Control of Multi-Robot Systems
summary: This paper presents a prototyping framework for distributed control of multi-robot systems, aimed at bridging theory and practical testing of distributed optimization algorithms. Using the Single Program, Multiple Data (SPMD) paradigm, the framework emulates distributed control on a single computer, with each core running the same algorithm using local states and neighbour-to-neighbour communication. We demonstrate the framework on a four-quadrotor position-swapping task using a non-cooperative game-theoretic distributed algorithm. Computational time and trajectory data are compared across the supported dynamics levels: a point-mass model, a high-fidelity quadrotor model, and an experimental hardware testbed using Crazyflie quadcopters. The results show that the framework provides a low-cost and accessible approach for validating distributed algorithms.

AI.tldr: A SPDM-based prototyping framework emulates distributed control for multi-robot systems on a single computer, enabling rapid testing of distributed optimization algorithms across models and hardware; demonstrated on a four-quadrotor position-swapping task with a non-cooperative game-theoretic algorithm; low-cost validation across point-mass, high-fidelity quadrotor, and Crazyflie hardware.
AI.motivation: Bridge theory and practice in distributed multi-robot optimization by providing an accessible, low-cost testing environment that spans simulation and hardware-like testing.
AI.method: Use Single Program, Multiple Data (SPMD) on a multi-core computer to run the same distributed algorithm with local states and neighbor-to-neighbor communication; apply to a four-quadrotor position-swapping task using a non-cooperative game-theoretic distributed algorithm; compare performance (computational time and trajectories) across three dynamics levels: point-mass model, high-fidelity quadrotor model, and a hardware testbed with Crazyflie quadcopters.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of the framework; provides a low-cost, accessible approach for validating distributed algorithms; yields comparative data (time and trajectories) across dynamics levels, highlighting practical testing capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The SPDM-based prototyping framework effectively bridges theory and practice in distributed multi-robot control by enabling rapid, cost-efficient validation of distributed optimization algorithms across models and hardware-like environments.

title: Agentic Systems as Boosting Weak Reasoning Models
summary: Can a committee of weak reasoning-model calls reach the performance of much stronger models? We study verifier-backed committee search as inference-time boosting for reasoning language models. The mechanism is not simply that ``more agents help'': samples expose latent correct solutions, while critics and comparators must recover them without access to the hidden verifier. We formalize this view by separating proposal coverage, local identifiability, progress, and diversity. We prove that coverage can be amplified by repeated sampling, but cannot by itself create useful critics or comparators; reliable amplification requires an additional local soundness signal, such as execution, proof checking, type checking, tests, or constraint solving. We give rank-based bounds showing when local selection errors compose into reliable trajectories, and characterize the proposer-side ceiling: oracle best-of-\(k\) converges only to the mass of task slices on which the proposal system assigns nonzero useful probability. Empirically, on SWE-bench Verified, a single \texttt{GPT-5.4 nano} proposal solves \(67.0\%\) of tasks. Using the same nano model, our critic--comparator orchestration reaches \(76.4\%\) with \(k=8\) proposals, matching the standalone performance of \texttt{Gemini 3 Pro} and \texttt{Claude Opus 4.5} Thinking and approaching the \(79.0\%\) oracle best-of-\(8\) upper bound. Thus, many correct patches are already present in weak-model proposal pools; the main challenge is selecting them. The remaining failures are mostly proposal-coverage failures, indicating shared blind spots that stronger selection alone cannot close.

AI.tldr: A verifier-backed committee of weak reasoning-models can approach strong models in performance; coverage amplification requires local soundness signals; empirical results show substantial gains on SWE-bench Verified with critic-comparator orchestration.
AI.motivation: Investigate inference-time boosting for reasoning LMs and disentangle coverage, identifiability, progress, diversity; determine when multiple weak agents can match stronger models.
AI.method: Formal framework separating components; analyze amplification via repeated sampling; prove that coverage alone isn't enough; introduce local soundness signals (execution, proof checking, tests, constraint solving); derive rank-based bounds; provide oracle-best-of-k ceiling; run experiments on SWE-bench Verified with GPT-5.4 nano and critic-comparator with k=8.
AI.result: Coverage can be amplified by sampling; local soundness signals are necessary for reliable improvement; empirical gains: single nano 67.0%; with k=8 proposals, 76.4%, on par with Gemini 3 Pro and Claude Opus 4.5 and close to oracle best-of-8 79.0%; main limitation is proposal coverage; failures mainly from coverage gaps.
AI.conclusion: Many correct patches reside in weak-model proposal pools; selection is the key challenge; stronger selection alone cannot close blind spots; combining weak proposals with verifiers approaches stronger models; future work should enhance local signals, expand coverage, and refine bounds.

title: AttnGen: Attention-Guided Saliency Learning for Interpretable Genomic Sequence Classification
summary: Deep neural networks have achieved strong performance in genomic sequence classification; however, relating their predictions to biologically meaningful sequence patterns remains challenging. In this work, we present AttnGen, an attention-guided training framework that embeds interpretability directly into the optimization process. AttnGen computes nucleotide-level importance scores using an attention mechanism and progressively suppresses low-contribution positions during training. This encourages the model to focus its predictions on a compact set of informative regions while reducing reliance on noisy sequence elements. We evaluate AttnGen on the standardized demo_human_or_worm benchmark, a binary classification task over 200-nucleotide sequences. With moderate masking, AttnGen achieves a validation accuracy of 96.73%, outperforming a conventional CNN baseline with 95.83% accuracy, while also exhibiting faster convergence and improved training stability. To assess whether the learned importance scores reflect functionally relevant signal, we conduct perturbation-based analysis by removing high-saliency nucleotides. This causes accuracy to drop from 96.9% to near chance level on a 3,000-sequence evaluation set, indicating that the model relies on a relatively small subset of informative positions. Our analysis shows that masking 10--20% of positions provides the most favorable trade-off between predictive performance and interpretability. These results suggest that attention-guided masking not only improves classification performance but also reshapes how models distribute importance across sequence positions. Although this study focuses on short genomic sequences, the proposed approach may extend to more complex interpretable sequence modeling settings.

AI.tldr: AttnGen is an attention-guided training framework that integrates interpretability into genomic sequence classification by progressively masking low-contribution positions based on nucleotide-level attention, achieving higher accuracy and faster convergence than a CNN on a 200-nt benchmark while showing that a small subset of positions drives predictions.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of linking model predictions to biologically meaningful sequence patterns by embedding interpretability directly into the training objective, reducing reliance on noisy elements and highlighting compact informative regions.
AI.method: AttnGen trains a neural network with an attention mechanism to assign nucleotide-level importance scores. During training, low-contribution positions are progressively masked to force the model to rely on a compact set of informative regions. The model is evaluated on the demo_human_or_worm benchmark (binary classification of 200-nt sequences). Perturbation analysis removes high-saliency nucleotides to assess functional relevance, and a 10–20% masking regime is identified as providing the best accuracy-interpretability trade-off.
AI.result: AttnGen achieves 96.73% validation accuracy with moderate masking, outperforming a CNN baseline at 95.83%, and shows faster convergence and improved stability. Perturbation by removing high-saliency nucleotides drops accuracy from ~96.9% to near chance on a 3,000-sequence set, indicating reliance on a small informative subset. Masking 10–20% of positions offers the best balance between performance and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: Attention-guided masking not only boosts classification performance but also reshapes how the model distributes importance across sequence positions. The approach enhances interpretability without sacrificing accuracy and may extend to more complex interpretable sequence modeling tasks beyond short genomic sequences.

title: SOCC-ICP: Semantics-Assisted Odometry based on Occupancy Grids and ICP
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, Accepted May 2026 for publication in IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L)
summary: Reliable pose estimation in previously unseen environments is a fundamental capability of autonomous systems. Existing LiDAR odometry methods typically employ point-, surfel-, or NDT-based map representations, which are distinct from the semantic occupancy grids commonly used for downstream tasks such as motion planning. We introduce SOCC-ICP, a semantics-assisted odometry framework that jointly performs Semantic OCCupancy grid mapping and LiDAR scan alignment. Each map voxel encodes geometric and semantic statistics, enabling adaptive point-to-point or point-to-plane ICP based on local planarity. Further, the occupancy grid naturally filters dynamic objects through raycasting-based free-space updates. Across diverse evaluation scenarios, SOCC-ICP achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art LiDAR odometry and remains robust in geometrically degenerate environments, even in the absence of semantic cues. When semantic labels are available, integrating them into map construction, downsampling, and correspondence weighting yields further accuracy gains. By unifying odometry and semantic occupancy grid mapping within a single representation, SOCC-ICP eliminates redundant map structures and directly provides a map suitable for downstream robotic applications.

AI.tldr: SOCC-ICP unifies semantic occupancy grid mapping with LiDAR odometry, using voxel-level geometry+semantic statistics to drive adaptive ICP; it filters dynamics via raycasting and yields competitive, robust performance, with extra gains when semantics are available, and a single map suitable for downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional LiDAR odometry relies on geometric maps (points, surfaces, NDT) that are separate from downstream semantic planning grids; integrating semantics into a compact, unified map can improve robustness to dynamics and provide immediate utility for planning and perception.
AI.method: Joint semantic occupancy grid mapping and scan alignment. Each voxel stores geometric and semantic statistics. ICP is adaptive: point-to-point or point-to-plane based on local planarity. Raycasting-based free-space updates suppress dynamic points. If semantics are available, use labels to influence map construction, downsampling, and correspondence weighting. The representation is unified for odometry and mapping.
AI.result: SOCC-ICP achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art LiDAR odometry across varied tests; robust in geometrically degenerate environments even without semantics; semantic cues yield additional accuracy gains when available.
AI.conclusion: A single, semantics-aware occupancy grid effectively replaces multiple map structures, delivering odometry and a ready-to-use map for downstream robotic tasks; dynamics can be mitigated via raycasting; semantic information further improves accuracy.

title: Unsteady Metrics and Benchmarking Cultures of AI Model Builders
summary: The primary way to establish and compare competencies in foundation and generative AI models has shifted from peer-reviewed literature to press releases and company blog posts, where model builders highlight results on selected benchmarks. These artifacts now largely define the state of the art for researchers and the public. Despite their prominence, which benchmarks model builders choose to highlight, and what they communicate through this selection, is underexamined. To investigate, we introduce and open-source Benchmarking-Cultures-25, a dataset of 231 benchmarks highlighted across 139 model releases in 2025 from 11 major AI builders, alongside an interactive tool to explore the data. Our analysis reveals a fragmented evaluation landscape with limited cross-model comparability: 63.2% of highlighted benchmarks are used by a single builder, and 38.5% appear in just one release. Few achieve widespread use (e.g., GPQA Diamond, LiveCodeBench, AIME 2025). Moreover, benchmarks are attributed different competencies by different builders, depending on their narrative. To disentangle these conflicting presentations, we develop a unified taxonomy mapping diverging terminology to a shared framework of measured signals based on what benchmark authors claim to measure. "General knowledge application" is the second most popular, yet vaguely defined, category. Qualitative analysis shows many such benchmarks deemphasize construct validity, instead framing results as indicators of progress toward AGI. Their authors claim to measure knowledge or reasoning broadly, yet mostly evaluate STEM subjects (especially math). We argue that highlighted benchmarks function less as standardized measurement tools and more as flexible narrative devices prioritizing market positioning over scientific evaluation. Data: https://hf.co/datasets/matybohacek/benchmarking-cultures-25; tool: https://bench-cultures.net.

AI.tldr: A dataset (Benchmarking-Cultures-25) analyzes how AI benchmarks are highlighted in 2025 model releases, revealing fragmentation and narrative-driven evaluation; a unified taxonomy and open tools are provided to scrutinize benchmark culture.
AI.motivation: To understand what benchmarks builders highlight, how these selections influence perceived progress, and to provide reproducible resources for analyzing benchmarking culture.
AI.method: Collect and analyze 231 benchmarks highlighted across 139 2025 model releases from 11 major AI builders; develop an open, cross-cutting taxonomy mapping divergent terminology to shared signals; provide an interactive exploration tool and data.
AI.result: Found fragmentation: 63.2% of highlighted benchmarks appear in only a single builder, 38.5% in a single release; few benchmarks achieve broad adoption (e.g., GPQA Diamond, LiveCodeBench, AIME 2025). Different builders assign different competencies to the same benchmarks; the taxonomy links terms to measured signals. Many benchmarks emphasize ‘general knowledge application’ but lack construct validity and are framed as progress toward AGI, mostly evaluating STEM/math. Overall, highlighted benchmarks function more as narrative devices than standardized measures.
AI.conclusion: The benchmarking landscape is heterogeneous and narrative-driven; the open dataset and taxonomy enable scrutiny and cross-model comparability improvements, highlighting the need for more standardized, scientifically grounded evaluation in AI benchmarking.

title: Diagnosing and Correcting Concept Omission in Multimodal Diffusion Transformers
summary: Multimodal Diffusion Transformers (MM-DiTs) have achieved remarkable progress in text-to-image generation, yet they frequently suffer from concept omission, where specified objects or attributes fail to emerge in the generated image. By performing linear probing on text tokens, we demonstrate that text embeddings can distinguish a characteristic `omission signal' representing the absence of target concepts. Leveraging this insight, we propose Omission Signal Intervention (OSI), which amplifies the omission signal to actively catalyze the generation of missing concepts. Comprehensive experiments on FLUX.1-Dev and SD3.5-Medium demonstrate that OSI significantly alleviates concept omission even in extreme scenarios.

AI.tldr: Introduce Omission Signal Intervention (OSI) to mitigate concept omission in multimodal diffusion transformers by amplifying an 'omission' signal discovered via linear probing of text embeddings; achieves notable improvements on FLUX.1-Dev and SD3.5-Medium.
AI.motivation: Concept omission (missing specified objects/attributes) is a key limitation of multimodal diffusion models like MM-DiTs. The authors identify a dedicated omission signal in text embeddings via linear probing and aim to actively manipulate generation to include missing concepts.
AI.method: Perform linear probing on text token embeddings to identify a characteristic omission signal representing absence of target concepts. Propose Omission Signal Intervention (OSI), which amplifies this signal during diffusion to boost generation of missing concepts.
AI.result: Experiments on FLUX.1-Dev and SD3.5-Medium show that OSI significantly reduces concept omission, performing well even in extreme scenarios.
AI.conclusion: OSI provides an effective mechanism to address concept omission in MM-DiTs by leveraging and amplifying an embedding-level omission signal; suggests a general approach for prompting fidelity improvements in multimodal generative models.

title: Fair and Calibrated Toxicity Detection with Robust Training and Abstention
summary:
Fairness in toxicity classification involves three integrated axes: ranking, calibration, and abstention. Training-time interventions and post-hoc safety mechanisms cannot be evaluated independently because the former determines the efficacy of the latter. We compare Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM), instance-level reweighting, and Group DRO across these axes, combined with temperature scaling, confidence-based abstention, and per-identity threshold optimization. Evaluation uses subgroup AUC, BPSN/BNSP AUC, error gaps, and per-subgroup Expected Calibration Error (ECE) with bootstrap CIs ($n = 1000$).
  We report four findings. (1) Calibration disparity is a hidden fairness violation. ERM has near-perfect aggregate calibration ($0.013$) but is significantly miscalibrated across all identity subgroups ($+0.029$ to $+0.134$). (2) Training interventions reshape rather than eliminate disparity. Reweighted ERM improves ranking (BPSN AUC $+0.06$ to $+0.12$) but worsens the calibration-fairness gap by up to $+0.232$. Group DRO eliminates calibration disparity but only by becoming uniformly miscalibrated globally (ECE $0.118$). (3) Post-hoc methods inherit training failure modes. Temperature scaling fails because miscalibration is non-uniform. Confidence-based abstention works under ERM but breaks under DRO, where the risk-coverage curve rises with deferral. (4) Abstention itself is unfair. Confidence-based deferral helps background content far more than identity-mentioning content. We argue that SRAI fairness requires a multi-axis framework: methods that differ only in aggregate ranking can differ sharply in failure modes that determine real-world harm.

AI.tldr: Calibration disparity is a hidden fairness violation in toxicity classification; training-time interventions and post-hoc safety mechanisms interact in complex, axis-specific ways across ranking, calibration, and abstention.
AI.motivation: To understand how three fairness axes (ranking, calibration, abstention) interact and whether common training-time interventions and post-hoc protections yield consistent fairness across identity subgroups.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of ERM, instance-level reweighting, and Group DRO across ranking and calibration axes, combined with temperature scaling, confidence-based abstention, and per-identity threshold optimization; evaluation via subgroup AUC, BPSN/BNSP AUC, error gaps, and per-subgroup ECE with bootstrap CIs (n=1000).
AI.result: 1) Calibration disparity is a hidden fairness violation: ERM has near-perfect aggregate calibration (ECE ≈0.013) but substantial subgroup miscalibration (+0.029 to +0.134). 2) Training interventions reshape rather than erase disparity: Reweighting improves ranking (BPSN AUC +0.06 to +0.12) but worsens calibration fairness gap by up to +0.232; Group DRO removes calibration disparity but yields uniform global miscalibration (ECE ≈0.118). 3) Post-hoc methods inherit training failures: Temperature scaling fails due to non-uniform miscalibration; confidence-based abstention helps under ERM but breaks under DRO (risk-coverage curve rises with deferral). 4) Abstention itself is unfair: deferral benefits background content more than identity-mentioning content.
AI.conclusion: Fairness in SRAI requires a multi-axis framework; methods that align on aggregate ranking can diverge dramatically in failure modes that drive real-world harm. Evaluation should jointly consider ranking, calibration across subgroups, and abstention behavior to assess true societal impact.

title: CLOVER: Closed-Loop Value Estimation \& Ranking for End-to-End Autonomous Driving Planning
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving planners are commonly trained by imitating a single logged trajectory, yet evaluated by rule-based planning metrics that measure safety, feasibility, progress, and comfort. This creates a training--evaluation mismatch: trajectories close to the logged path may violate planning rules, while alternatives farther from the demonstration can remain valid and high-scoring. The mismatch is especially limiting for proposal-selection planners, whose performance depends on candidate-set coverage and scorer ranking quality. We propose CLOVER, a Closed-LOop Value Estimation and Ranking framework for end-to-end autonomous driving planning. CLOVER follows a lightweight generator--scorer formulation: a generator produces diverse candidate trajectories, and a scorer predicts planning-metric sub-scores to rank them at inference time. To expand proposal support beyond single-trajectory imitation, CLOVER constructs evaluator-filtered pseudo-expert trajectories and trains the generator with set-level coverage supervision. It then performs conservative closed-loop self-distillation: the scorer is fitted to true evaluator sub-scores on generated proposals, while the generator is refined toward teacher-selected top-$k$ and vector-Pareto targets with stability regularization. We analyze when an imperfect scorer can improve the generator, showing that scorer-mediated refinement is reliable when scorer-selected targets are enriched under the true evaluator and updates remain conservative. On NAVSIM, CLOVER achieves 94.5 PDMS and 90.4 EPDMS, establishing a new state of the art. On the more challenging NavHard split, it obtains 48.3 EPDMS, matching the strongest reported result. On supplementary nuScenes open-loop evaluation, CLOVER achieves the lowest L2 error and collision rate among compared methods. Code data will be released at https://github.com/WilliamXuanYu/CLOVER.

AI.tldr: CLOVER is a Closed-Loop Value Estimation and Ranking framework for end-to-end autonomous driving planning that uses a generator-scorer setup, evaluator-filtered pseudo-experts, and closed-loop self-distillation to improve candidate coverage and scoring, achieving state-of-the-art results on NAVSIM and competitive performance on NavHard and nuScenes.
AI.motivation: There is a training-evaluation mismatch in end-to-end driving planners: policies trained to imitate a single logged trajectory may be evaluated with rule-based metrics that require valid but different trajectories; this misalignment is acute for proposal-selection planners reliant on candidate coverage and scorer quality.
AI.method: A generator produces diverse candidate trajectories; a scorer predicts planning-metric sub-scores to rank proposals. Evaluator-filtered pseudo-expert trajectories augment training; set-level coverage supervision guides the generator. Closed-loop self-distillation updates: the scorer learns from true evaluator sub-scores on generated proposals; the generator is steered toward teacher-selected top-k and vector-Pareto targets with stability regularization. The paper analyzes when an imperfect scorer can help, showing reliability under enriched targets and conservative updates.
AI.result: On NAVSIM, CLOVER achieves 94.5 PDMS and 90.4 EPDMS (state of the art). On NavHard, it reaches 48.3 EPDMS (matching the strongest reported result). In supplementary nuScenes open-loop tests, CLOVER shows the lowest L2 error and collision rate among competing methods.
AI.conclusion: CLOVER effectively closes the training-evaluation gap for end-to-end driving planners by coupling diverse proposal generation with learned, conservative ranking; it delivers state-of-the-art or competitive results across benchmarks and provides practical benefits for proposal selection through improved coverage and ranking.

title: The Evaluation Trap: Benchmark Design as Theoretical Commitment
summary: Every AI benchmark operationalizes theoretical assumptions about the capability it claims to assess. When assumptions function as unexamined commitments, benchmarks stabilize the dominant paradigm by narrowing what counts as progress. Over time, narrow evaluation reorganizes capability concepts: architectures and definitions are selected for benchmark legibility until evaluation ceases to track an independent object and instead produces a version of the target defined by its own operational assumptions. The result is a trap: evaluation frameworks treat self-reinforcing assessments as valid, both creating and obscuring structural limits on what the current paradigm can accomplish. We introduce Epistematics, a methodology for deriving evaluation criteria directly from technical capability claims and auditing whether proposed benchmarks can discriminate the claimed capability from proxy behaviors. The contribution is meta-evaluative: an audit procedure, a failure mode taxonomy, and benchmark-design criteria for evaluating capability-evaluation coherence. We demonstrate the procedure through a worked audit of Dupoux et al. (2026), a proposal that revises the dominant paradigm's theoretical assumptions at the architectural level while reproducing them in its evaluation criteria, thereby entrenching the constraint it seeks to overcome in a form the evaluation cannot detect.

AI.tldr: Epistematics provides a meta-evaluative audit framework to ensure AI benchmarks measure true capabilities rather than proxies, introducing an audit procedure, a failure-mode taxonomy, and benchmark-design criteria; demonstrated via a worked audit of Dupoux et al. (2026) that embeds the dominant paradigm's assumptions in evaluation.
AI.motivation: Benchmarks encode unexamined commitments about capabilities, which can stabilize a dominant paradigm and collapse the object of evaluation into its own criteria. There is a need for an explicit audit to expose when benchmarks cannot discriminate the target capability from proxy behaviors.
AI.method: Propose Epistematics: a meta-evaluative methodology comprising an audit procedure, a taxonomy of failure modes, and benchmark-design criteria. Apply it in a worked audit to a concrete proposal (Dupoux et al. 2026) to show how evaluation criteria can entrench architectural assumptions unless they are scrutinized.
AI.result: A structured meta-evaluative framework, including an auditable procedure, a taxonomy of failure modes, and design guidelines, plus a demonstrated application showing how current proposals can reproduce dominant-evaluation criteria and thus fail to detect the target capability.
AI.conclusion: Epistematics enables capability-evaluation coherence by surfacing misalignments between claimed capabilities and how they are evaluated, helping to avoid self-reinforcing evaluation traps and improving interpretability of benchmark results.

title: CreFlow: Corrective Reflow for Sparse-Reward Embodied Video Diffusion RL
summary: Video generation models trained on heterogeneous data with likelihood-surrogate objectives can produce visually plausible rollouts that violate physical constraints in embodied manipulation. Although reinforcement-learning post-training offers a natural route to adapting VGMs, existing video-RL rewards often reduce each rollout to a low-level visual metric, whereas manipulation video evaluation requires logic-based verification of whether the rollout satisfies a compositional task specification. To fill this gap, we introduce a compositional constraint-based reward model for post-training embodied video generation models, which automatically formulates task requirements as a composition of Linear Temporal Logic constraints, providing faithful rewards and localized error information in generated videos. To achieve effective improvement in high-dimensional video generation using these reward signals, we further propose CreFlow, a novel online RL framework with two key designs: i) a credit-aware NFT loss that confines the RL update to reward-relevant regions, preventing perturbations to unrelated regions during post-training; and ii) a corrective reflow loss that leverages within-group positive samples as an explicit estimate of the correction direction, stabilizing and accelerating training. Experiments show that CreFlow yields reward judgments better aligned with human and simulator success labels than existing methods and improves downstream execution success by 23.8 percentage points across eight bimanual manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: Proposes CreFlow: a compositional constraint-based reward model for post-training embodied video generation, using Linear Temporal Logic constraints to align rewards with task specifications, plus two RL innovations (credit-aware NFT loss and corrective reflow loss), achieving superior reward alignment and a 23.8-point improvement in manipulation success across eight tasks.
AI.motivation: Video generation models trained on heterogeneous data struggle to satisfy physical constraints; RL post-training needs faithful, compositional rewards rather than simplistic visual metrics.
AI.method: Formulate task specifications as Linear Temporal Logic constraints to derive rewards; implement online RL framework CreFlow with two losses: credit-aware NFT loss targeting reward-relevant regions, and corrective reflow loss leveraging within-group positives to estimate correction direction.
AI.result: CreFlow yields reward judgments more aligned with human/simulator success labels than baselines and improves downstream execution success by 23.8 percentage points across eight bimanual manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Compositional, logic-guided rewards with region-aware updates and corrective stabilization improve embodied video generation performance and reliability in manipulation tasks.

title: Rethinking Layer Relevance in Large Language Models Beyond Cosine Similarity
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language processing. Understanding their internal mechanisms is crucial for developing more interpretable and optimized architectures. Mechanistic interpretability has led to the development of various methods for assessing layer relevance, with cosine similarity being a widely used tool in the field. On this work, we demonstrate that cosine similarity is a poor proxy for the actual performance degradation caused by layer removal. Our theoretical analysis shows that a layer can exhibit an arbitrarily low cosine similarity score while still being crucial to the model's performance. On the other hand, empirical evidence from a range of LLMs confirms that the correlation between cosine similarity and actual performance degradation is often weak or moderate, leading to misleading interpretations of a transformer's internal mechanisms. We propose a more robust metric for assessing layer relevance: the actual drop in model accuracy resulting from the removal of a layer. Even though it is a computationally costly metric, this approach offers a more accurate picture of layer importance, allowing for more informed pruning strategies and lightweight models. Our findings have significant implications for the development of interpretable LLMs and highlight the need to move beyond cosine similarity in assessing layer relevance.

AI.tldr: Cosine similarity is an unreliable proxy for layer importance in LLMs; measuring the actual accuracy drop from layer removal provides a more accurate but costlier metric for pruning and interpretability.
AI.motivation: To improve mechanistic interpretability and pruning in LLMs by correctly identifying layer relevance, avoiding misleading interpretations from cosine similarity.
AI.method: Theoretically analyze whether a layer can have arbitrarily low cosine similarity yet be crucial; empirically evaluate cosine similarity vs. actual performance drop across diverse LLMs; advocate using actual accuracy drop as the relevance metric despite higher computational cost.
AI.result: Cosine similarity shows weak to moderate correlation with performance degradation; many layers with low cosine similarity can be highly impactful, while some important layers do not show large cosine changes. The proposed metric—actual accuracy drop upon layer removal—provides a more reliable measure of layer importance.
AI.conclusion: Move beyond cosine similarity for layer relevance; adopt actual accuracy drop as the robust metric for interpretability and pruning, accepting higher computational cost for more accurate model understanding and potentially lighter models.

title: CoCo-InEKF: State Estimation with Learned Contact Covariances in Dynamic, Contact-Rich Scenarios
summary: Robust state estimation for highly dynamic motion of legged robots remains challenging, especially in dynamic, contact-rich scenarios. Traditional approaches often rely on binary contact states that fail to capture the nuances of partial contact or directional slippage. This paper presents CoCo-InEKF, a differentiable invariant extended Kalman filter that utilizes continuous contact velocity covariances instead of binary contact states. These learned covariances allow the method to dynamically modulate contact confidence, accounting for more nuanced conditions ranging from firm contact to directional slippage or no contact. To predict these covariances for a set of predefined contact candidate points, we employ a lightweight neural network trained end-to-end using a state-error loss. This approach eliminates the need for heuristic ground-truth contact labels. In addition, we propose an automated contact candidate selection procedure and demonstrate that our method is insensitive to their exact placement. Experiments on a bipedal robot demonstrate a superior accuracy-efficiency tradeoff for linear velocity estimation, as well as improved filter consistency compared to baseline methods. This enables the robust execution of challenging motions, including dancing and complex ground interactions -- both in simulation and in the real world.

AI.tldr: CoCo-InEKF introduces a differentiable invariant EKF for legged robots that uses continuous contact velocity covariances learned by a lightweight neural network, replacing binary contact states to better handle partial contact and slippage. It trains without ground-truth contact labels and automatically selects contact candidates. Empirical results on a biped show improved velocity estimation accuracy and filter consistency, enabling robust dynamic motions in simulation and on hardware.
AI.motivation: Binary contact state representations inadequately capture nuanced contact conditions (partial contact, directional slippage) in dynamic legged locomotion. A continuous, learned contact uncertainty can improve robustness and estimation performance.
AI.method: Extend an invariant EKF (InEKF) with differentiable, learned contact velocity covariances. Use a lightweight neural network to predict these covariances for predefined contact candidate points using a state-error loss. Include an automated contact candidate selection procedure and measure insensitivity to candidate placement.
AI.result: The approach yields superior accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs for linear velocity estimation and improved filter consistency compared to baselines on a bipedal robot, under dynamic motions (dancing, complex ground interactions) in both simulation and real-world tests.
AI.conclusion: Learning continuous contact uncertainties within a differentiable EKF framework enhances robustness and consistency of state estimation in contact-rich, dynamic locomotion, without requiring ground-truth contact labels.

title: Grounded Continuation: A Linear-Time Runtime Verifier for LLM Conversations
summary: In long conversations, an LLM can produce a next utterance that sounds plausible but rests on premises the conversation has already abandoned. Context-manipulation attacks against deployed agents now actively exploit this gap. We close it with a runtime verifier that maintains an explicit dependency graph: an LLM classifies each turn into one of 8 update operations drawn from four formalisms (dynamic epistemic logic, abductive reasoning, awareness logic, argumentation), and a symbolic engine records which claims depend on which evidence. Checking whether a continuation is supported reduces to a graph walk; retraction propagates through the same graph to flag exactly the conclusions that lose support, with linear per-turn cost and a formal conflict-free guarantee. On LongMemEval-KU oracle (n=78), the verifier reaches 89.7% accuracy vs. 88.5% for the LLM-only baseline (+1.3pp) and 87.2% for a transcript-RAG baseline matched on retrieval budget (+2.6pp); wins among disagreements are correct abstentions where the baseline confabulates. On LoCoMo's 60 official QA items the verifier is competitive with retrieval-augmented baselines. Beyond external benchmarks, we construct two multi-agent scenarios and a 50-item grounding test: on the 15-item stale-premise subset, the verifier reaches 100% accuracy vs. 93.3% (+6.7pp). These instantiate a soundness-faithfulness decomposition: the structural check is sound by construction, and per-deployment LLM extraction faithfulness is the empirical question we measure across four LLM families. The retraction check plateaus at microseconds while history-replay grows linearly with conversation length.

AI.tldr: A runtime verifier for LLM conversations maintains a dependency graph to ensure continuations are supported by evidence, using updates from four formalisms and a symbolic engine; it achieves sound, efficient verification with competitive accuracy on standard benchmarks and demonstrates a soundness-faithfulness decomposition.
AI.motivation: Long conversations expose gaps between what the LLM uses to justify a turn and the evidence or premises previously established in the dialogue. Context-manipulation attacks exploit this gap; a runtime verifier is needed to ensure that each continuation is properly supported by the conversation history.
AI.method: classify each turn into one of 8 update operations drawn from four formalisms (dynamic epistemic logic, abductive reasoning, awareness logic, argumentation); a symbolic engine records dependencies between claims and evidence; check a continuation by graph-walking the dependency graph; retraction propagates through the graph to flag losing support; ensure a formal, conflict-free guarantee; per-turn cost is linear.
AI.result: On LongMemEval-KU (n=78), verifier accuracy is 89.7% versus 88.5% for LLM-only baseline (+1.3pp) and 87.2% for a transcript-RAG baseline (+2.6pp); wins among disagreements are correct abstentions where the baseline confabulates. On LoCoMo’s 60 QA items, verifier is competitive with retrieval-augmented baselines. In multi-agent and grounding tests (50 items, 15-item stale-premise subset), the verifier achieves 100% vs 93.3% (+6.7pp). Retraction check runs in microseconds; history-replay grows linearly with conversation length.
AI.conclusion: The approach establishes a soundness-faithfulness decomposition: the structural check is sound by construction, while faithfulness depends on LLM extraction in deployment, measured across four LLM families. The retraction mechanism is very fast; history replay scales with dialogue length, enabling efficient runtime verification of long conversations.

title: KVPO: ODE-Native GRPO for Autoregressive Video Alignment via KV Semantic Exploration
summary: Aligning streaming autoregressive (AR) video generators with human preferences is challenging. Existing reinforcement learning methods predominantly rely on noise-based exploration and SDE-based surrogate policies that are mismatched to the deterministic ODE dynamics of distilled AR models, and tend to perturb low-level appearance rather than the high-level semantic storyline progression critical for long-horizon coherence. To address these limitations, we present KVPO, an ODE-native online Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) framework for aligning streaming video generators. For diversity exploration, KVPO introduces a causal-semantic exploration paradigm that relocates the source of variation from stochastic noise to the historical KV cache. By stochastically routing historical KV entries, it constructs semantically diverse generation branches that remain strictly on the data manifold. For policy modeling, KVPO introduces a velocity-field surrogate policy based on Trajectory Velocity Energy (TVE), which quantifies branch likelihood in flow-matching velocity space and yields a reward-weighted contrastive objective fully consistent with the native ODE formulation. Experiments on multiple distilled AR video generators demonstrate consistent gains in visual quality, motion quality, and text-video alignment across both single-prompt short-video and multi-prompt long-video settings.

AI.tldr: KVPO offers an ODE-native online policy optimization for streaming autoregressive video generators, using causal-semantic exploration via a historical KV cache and a velocity-field surrogate policy (Trajectory Velocity Energy) to achieve data-manifold-consistent, semantically diverse generation and improved text-video alignment; demonstrates gains in short-video and long-video tasks.
AI.motivation: Align streaming AR video generators with human preferences; existing RL methods rely on noise-based exploration and SDE surrogates that mismatch the deterministic ODE dynamics of distilled AR models and tend to perturb low-level appearance rather than high-level storyline progression essential for long-horizon coherence.
AI.method: KVPO introduces an online Group Relative Policy Optimization framework with two key components: (1) causal-semantic exploration that routes historical KV cache entries to create diverse generation branches on the data manifold without noise, and (2) a velocity-field surrogate policy based on Trajectory Velocity Energy (TVE) that measures branch likelihood in flow-matching velocity space and yields a reward-weighted contrastive objective fully aligned with the native ODE formulation.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple distilled AR video generators show consistent improvements in visual quality, motion quality, and text-video alignment for both single-prompt short-video and multi-prompt long-video settings.
AI.conclusion: KVPO provides an effective ODE-native RL framework for streaming AR video generation, achieving improved alignment with human preferences through data-manifold-constrained exploration and velocity-based policy objectives, validated by gains across short- and long-video tasks.

title: Mini-JEPA Foundation Model Fleet Enables Agentic Hydrologic Intelligence
summary: Geospatial foundation models compress multispectral observations into dense embeddings increasingly used in natural-language environmental reasoning systems. A single planetary-scale model, e.g. Google AlphaEarth, handles broad characterization well but may compromise on specialized hydrologic signals. Such generalist models are also often inaccessible, expensive, and require large-scale compute. We propose Mini-JEPAs: a fleet of small sensor-specialized Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) foundation models consulted by a routing agent for specialized questions. We pretrained five 22M-parameter Mini-JEPAs sharing an identical Vision Transformer backbone, JEPA recipe, and 64-d output space, using Sentinel-2 optical, Sentinel-1 SAR, MODIS thermal, multi-temporal Sentinel-2 phenology, and a topography-soil stack. Each Mini-JEPA reconstructs the variable matched to its sensor, with cross-validated $R^2$ reaching 0.97 for elevation, 0.97 for temperature, and 0.81 for precipitation. The five manifolds differ in geometric structure, with global participation ratios from 8.9 to 20.2 and local intrinsic dimensionalities from 2.3 to 9.0. Joint topography-soil and phenology models add predictive value beyond AlphaEarth alone for soil moisture, aridity, and precipitation ($ΔR^2$ up to 0.031). A router LLM reads per-modality references and selects appropriate sensors with a perfect hit rate over a curated question set. In paired LLM-as-Judge evaluation, dual retrieval over AlphaEarth and the routed fleet outperforms AlphaEarth alone on physics-matched questions (Cohen's $d = 1.10$, $p = 0.031$). Locally-trained Mini-JEPAs can be operationalized for hydrologic intelligence with modest compute.

AI.tldr: Mini-JEPAs: a fleet of small, sensor-specific JEPA models (22M params each) that route questions to the most relevant modalities, delivering improved hydrologic inference over a single large geospatial foundation model, with high sensor-wise reconstructions and cross-modal gains, accessible with modest compute.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of a planetary-scale generalist geospatial foundation model (cost, accessibility, compute, and possible dilution of specialized hydrologic signals) by deploying small, sensor-specialized models guided by a routing system.
AI.method: Pretrain five 22M-parameter Mini-JEPAs with identical Vision Transformer backbone, JEPA recipe, and 64-d output. Each is trained to reconstruct the variable corresponding to its sensor using multispectral data: Sentinel-2 optical, Sentinel-1 SAR, MODIS thermal, multi-temporal Sentinel-2 phenology, and a topography–soil stack. Evaluate cross-validated R^2 for target variables (elevation, temperature, precipitation). Characterize geometry (global participation ratios 8.9–20.2; local intrinsic dimensionalities 2.3–9.0). Build joint topography–soil and phenology models for added predictive value (soil moisture, aridity, precipitation). Implement a routing LLM that reads per-modality references and selects sensors; assess with LLM-as-Judge via dual retrieval against AlphaEarth; report Cohen’s d and p-values. Demonstrate feasibility of locally-trained Mini-JEPAs under modest compute.
AI.result: Cross-validated R^2: elevation 0.97; temperature 0.97; precipitation 0.81. Five manifolds show varying geometry (global participation 8.9–20.2; local intrinsic dimensionality 2.3–9.0). Joint models (topography–soil and phenology) yield additional predictive value over AlphaEarth (ΔR^2 up to 0.031 for soil moisture, aridity, precipitation). Router LLM achieves perfect hit rate in sensor selection. Paired LLM-as-Judge with dual retrieval (AlphaEarth + routed fleet) outperforms AlphaEarth alone on physics-matched questions (Cohen’s d = 1.10, p = 0.031). Local Mini-JEPAs are feasible with modest compute.
AI.conclusion: Sensor-specialized Mini-JEPAs with a routing mechanism can outperform a general geospatial foundation model on physics-aligned hydrologic tasks and are practical to deploy with limited compute.

title: Pelican-Unified 1.0: A Unified Embodied Intelligence Model for Understanding, Reasoning, Imagination and Action
summary:
We present Pelican-Unified 1.0, the first embodied foundation model trained according to the principle of unification. Pelican-Unified 1.0 uses a single VLM as a unified understanding module, mapping scenes, instructions, visual contexts, and action histories into a shared semantic space. The same VLM also serves as a unified reasoning module, autoregressively producing task-, action-, and future-oriented chains of thought in a single forward pass and projecting the final hidden state into a dense latent variable. A Unified Future Generator (UFG) then conditions on this latent variable and jointly generates future videos and future actions through two modality-specific output heads within the same denoising process. The language, video, and action losses are all backpropagated into the shared representation, enabling the model to jointly optimize understanding, reasoning, imagination, and action during training, rather than training three isolated expert systems.
  Experiments demonstrate that unification does not imply compromise. With a single checkpoint, Pelican-Unified 1.0 achieves strong performance across all three capabilities: 64.7 on eight VLM benchmarks, the best among comparable-scale models; 66.03 on WorldArena, ranking first; and 93.5 on RoboTwin, the second-best average among compared action methods. These results show that the unified paradigm succeeds in preserving specialist strength while bringing understanding, reasoning, imagination, and action into one model.

AI.tldr: Pelican-Unified 1.0 presents an embodied foundation model that unifies perception, reasoning, imagination, and action within a single VLM-based architecture, achieving state-of-the-art or near-state performance across vision-language tasks, world-arena-like benchmarks, and robotic action benchmarks without sacrificing specialization.
AI.motivation: Address fragmentation by unifying understanding, reasoning, imagination, and action into one coherent model using a shared representation that generalizes across modalities and tasks.
AI.method: Employ a single Visual-Language Model (VLM) as both understanding and reasoning module, autoregressively generating task-, action-, and future-oriented chains of thought in a single forward pass, projecting the final hidden state to a dense latent variable. A Unified Future Generator (UFG) uses this latent variable to jointly generate future videos and actions via two modality-specific heads within a denoising process. All language, video, and action losses backpropagate into the shared representation to jointly optimize understanding, reasoning, imagination, and action.
AI.result: Single checkpoint achieves strong cross-domain performance: 64.7 on eight VLM benchmarks (best among comparable-scale models), 66.03 on WorldArena (ranked first), and 93.5 on RoboTwin (second-best average among action methods). Demonstrates that unification can preserve specialist strengths while enabling integrated capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Unification can preserve specialist performance while enabling a single model to perform perception, reasoning, imagination, and action cohesively, suggesting a viable path toward embodied, unified AI systems.

title: SimPersona: Learning Discrete Buyer Personas from Raw Clickstreams for Grounded E-Commerce Agents
summary: LLM-based web agents can navigate live storefronts, yet they often collapse to a single "average buyer" policy, failing to capture the heterogeneous and distributional nature of real buyer populations. Existing personalization methods rely on hand-crafted prompt-based personas that are brittle, difficult to scale, context-inefficient, and unable to faithfully represent population-level behavior. We introduce SimPersona, a novel framework that learns discrete buyer types from historical traffic and exposes them to LLM-based web agents as compact persona tokens. Given raw clickstreams, a behavior-aware VQ-VAE induces a discrete buyer-type space that captures the statistical structure of real buyer behavior and merchant-specific buyer population distributions. To provide behavior-specific guidance to LLM-based web agents, SimPersona maps each learned buyer type to a dedicated persona token in the LLM agent vocabulary and fine-tunes the agent with these tokens on real browsing traces. At inference, each synthetic buyer is assigned to a learned buyer type with a single encoder forward pass, requiring no retraining or store-specific prompt engineering. For population-level simulation, SimPersona samples buyer types from each merchant's empirical distribution over the learned VQ-VAE codebook and instantiates agents with the corresponding persona tokens, preserving merchant-specific buyer population distributions. Evaluated on $8.37$M buyers across $42$ held-out live storefronts, SimPersona achieves $78\%$ conversion-rate alignment with real buyers, exhibits interpretable behavioral variation across buyer types, and outperforms a baseline with $8\times$ more parameters on goal-oriented shopping tasks. We further release an open-source data pipeline that converts raw e-commerce event logs into buyer representations and agent-training traces.

AI.tldr: SimPersona uses a behavior-aware VQ-VAE to extract discrete buyer types from raw e-commerce clickstreams, mapping them to LLM persona tokens to guide agents. It enables population-aware, scalable, and interpretable LLM-based web agents, achieving strong alignment with real buyers and outperforming larger baselines. A new open-source data pipeline is released.
AI.motivation: Address the heterogeneity and distributional nature of real buyer populations, and overcome brittle, prompt-based personas that fail to capture population-level behavior in LLM-driven storefront agents.
AI.method: Train a behavior-aware VQ-VAE on clickstreams to learn a discrete buyer-type space; map each type to a dedicated LLM persona token; fine-tune the LLM agent with these tokens on real traces; at inference, assign buyers to types via a single encoder pass; for population simulation, sample buyer types from each merchant's empirical distribution and instantiate agents with corresponding tokens.
AI.result: On 8.37M buyers across 42 held-out storefronts, achieved 78% conversion-rate alignment with real buyers; displayed interpretable behavioral variation across types; outperformed a larger-baseline by 8x parameters on goal-oriented shopping tasks.
AI.conclusion: SimPersona enables scalable, population-aware LLM-based web agents with interpretable buyer-type control and a reusable data pipeline, improving alignment with real buyer behavior and offering practical advantages over larger, less targeted baselines.

title: ICED: Concept-level Machine Unlearning via Interpretable Concept Decomposition
summary: Machine unlearning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) is typically performed at the image or instance level, making it difficult to precisely remove target knowledge without affecting unrelated semantics. This issue is especially pronounced since a single image often contains multiple entangled concepts, including both target concepts to be forgotten and contextual information that should be preserved. In this paper, we propose an interpretable concept-level unlearning framework for VLMs, which constructs a compact task-specific concept vocabulary from the forgetting set using a multimodal large language model. In addition to modality alignment, visual representations are decomposed into sparse, nonnegative combinations of semantic concepts, providing an explicit interface for fine-grained knowledge manipulation. Based on this decomposition, our method formulates unlearning as concept-level optimization, where target concepts are selectively suppressed while intra-instance non-target semantics and global cross-modal knowledge are preserved. Extensive experiments across both in-domain and out-of-domain forgetting settings demonstrate that our method enables more comprehensive target forgetting, better preserves non-target knowledge within the same image, and maintains competitive model utility compared with existing VLM unlearning methods.

AI.tldr: A concept-level unlearning framework for Vision-Language Models that decomposes visuals into a sparse, nonnegative combination of semantic concepts and unlearns target concepts via concept-level optimization, improving forgetting precision while preserving non-target and cross-modal knowledge.
AI.motivation: Image- or instance-level unlearning entangles target concepts with contextual content; there is a need for fine-grained control to forget specific concepts without harming unrelated semantics.
AI.method: From forgetting set, build a compact task-specific concept vocabulary using a multimodal LLM; decompose visual representations into sparse nonnegative combinations of these semantic concepts; perform concept-level optimization to suppress target concepts while preserving intra-instance non-target semantics and global cross-modal knowledge.
AI.result: Experiments in both in-domain and out-of-domain forgetting show more comprehensive target forgetting, better preservation of non-target knowledge within the same image, and competitive model utility against existing VLM unlearning methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides interpretable, fine-grained control for unlearning in VLMs and improves forgetting precision without sacrificing utility.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Tool-Calling Agents in Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR)
summary: Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) is the dominant standard for interoperable exchange of healthcare data. In FHIR, electronic health records form a directed graph of resources. Answering clinically meaningful questions over FHIR requires agents to perform multi-step reasoning, filtering, and aggregation across multiple resource types. Prior work shows that even tool-augmented LLM agents (retrieval, code execution, multi-turn planning) often select the wrong resources or violate traversal constraints. We study this problem in the context of FHIR-AgentBench, a benchmark for realistic question answering over real-world hospital data, and frame reasoning on FHIR as a sequential decision-making problem over a queryable structured graph. We implement a multi-turn CodeAct agent and post-train it with reinforcement learning using a custom harness and tools. A LLM Judge provides execution-grounded rewards. Compared to prompt-based, closed-model baselines, RL post-training improves performance while enforcing data-integrity constraints. Empirically, our approach improves answer correctness from 50% (o4-mini) to 77% on FHIR-AgentBench using a smaller and cheaper Qwen3-8B model. We present an end-to-end post-training pipeline (environment building, harness construction, model training and custom evaluation) that reliably improves multi-turn reasoning over structured clinical graphs.

AI.tldr: RL post-training of a CodeAct agent with a judge-based reward improves multi-turn reasoning over FHIR resource graphs, boosting QA accuracy from 50% to 77% on FHIR-AgentBench using a 8B-model.
AI.motivation: Address incorrect resource selection and traversal-constraint violations in tool-augmented LLMs for QA over FHIR graphs; enable data-integrity-aware reasoning over real-world hospital data.
AI.method: Frame FHIR reasoning as sequential decision-making on a graph of resources; implement a multi-turn CodeAct agent; apply RL post-training using a custom environment harness and tools; incorporate an LLM Judge providing execution-grounded rewards; compare to prompt-based baselines; evaluation on the FHIR-AgentBench benchmark.
AI.result: Accuracy improved from 50% to 77% on FHIR-AgentBench; achieved with a smaller Qwen3-8B model; demonstrates effective RL post-training and judge-based rewards; provides an end-to-end pipeline for environment, harness, training, and evaluation.
AI.conclusion: RL post-training with execution-grounded rewards improves multi-turn reasoning and enforces data-integrity constraints in FHIR QA over realistic clinical graphs; supports a scalable, cost-efficient end-to-end pipeline.

title: Hand-in-the-Loop: Improving Dexterous VLA via Seamless Interventional Correction
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are prone to compounding errors in dexterous manipulation, where high-dimensional action spaces and contact-rich dynamics amplify small policy deviations over long horizons. While Interactive Imitation Learning (IIL) can refine policies through human takeover data, applying it to high-degree-of-freedom (DoF) robotic hands remains challenging due to a command mismatch between human teleoperation and policy execution at the takeover moment, which causes abrupt robot-hand configuration changes, or "gesture jumps".
