We present Hand-in-the-Loop (HandITL), a seamless human-in-the-loop intervention method that blends human corrective intent with autonomous policy execution to avoid gesture jumps during bimanual dexterous manipulation. Compared with direct teleoperation takeover, HandITL reduces takeover jitter by 99.8% and preserves robust post-takeover manipulation, reducing grasp failures by 87.5% and mean completion time by 19.1%. We validate HandITL on tasks requiring bimanual coordination, tool use, and fine-grained long-horizon manipulation. When used to collect intervention data for policy refinement, HandITL yields policies that outperform those trained with standard teleoperation data by 19% on average across three long-horizon dexterous tasks.

AI.tldr: HandITL blends human corrective intent with autonomous policy execution to prevent gesture jumps during bimanual dexterous manipulation, improving takeover stability and data quality for policy refinement.
AI.motivation: IIL for dexterous manipulation is hindered by command mismatch at takeover, causing abrupt robot-hand configuration changes (gesture jumps) and degraded performance. Reducing takeover jitter and improving post-takeover manipulation is essential; evaluating HandITL on bimanual, tool-use, long-horizon tasks to validate robustness and learning gains.
AI.method: Introduce Hand-in-the-Loop (HandITL), a seamless human-in-the-loop intervention that merges human corrective intent with autonomous policy execution at takeover to align commands with policy execution and avoid gesture jumps. Compare to direct teleoperation takeover. Use tasks with bimanual coordination, tool use, and fine-grained long-horizon manipulation. Quantify takeover jitter, grasp failures, and completion time; also collect HandITL intervention data to train/refine policies for learning under IIL.
AI.result: HandITL reduces takeover jitter by 99.8%, reduces grasp failures by 87.5%, and shortens mean completion time by 19.1% compared with direct teleoperation takeover. On three long-horizon dexterous tasks, policies learned from HandITL intervention data outperform those trained on standard teleoperation data by 19% on average.
AI.conclusion: HandITL effectively mitigates gesture jumps, enabling robust post-takeover manipulation and improving data quality for policy learning. The approach demonstrates strong gains on bimanual manipulation tasks and offers a practical pathway for collecting high-quality data for IIL-based policy refinement.

title: ASH: Agents that Self-Hone via Embodied Learning
summary: Long-horizon embodied tasks remain a fundamental challenge in AI, as current methods rely on hand-engineered rewards or action-labeled demonstrations, neither of which scales. We introduce ASH, an agentic system that learns an embodied policy from unlabeled, noisy internet video, without reward shaping or expert annotation. ASH follows a self-improvement loop; when it gets stuck, ASH learns an Inverse Dynamics Model (IDM) from its own trajectories, and uses its IDM to extract supervision from relevant internet video. ASH uses unsupervised learning to identify key moments from large-scale internet video and retains them as long-term memory -- allowing it to tackle long-horizon problems. We evaluate ASH on two complementary environments demanding multi-hour planning: Pokemon Emerald, a turn-based RPG, and The Legend of Zelda: The Minish Cap, a real-time action-adventure game. In both games, behavioral cloning, retrieval-augmented and zero-shot foundation-model baselines plateau, while ASH sustains progression across our 8-hour evaluation. ASH reaches an average of $11.2/12$ milestones in Pokemon Emerald and $9.9/12$ in Legend of Zelda, while the strongest baseline gets stuck in both environments at an average of $6.5/12$ and $6.0/12$ milestones, respectively. We demonstrate that self-improving agents are a scalable recipe for long-horizon embodied learning.

AI.tldr: ASH is a self-improving embodied agent that learns from unlabeled internet video without rewards or annotations, using a loop where an Inverse Dynamics Model informs supervision from video; it uses unsupervised key moment discovery and long-term memory to tackle long-horizon tasks, outperforming baselines on two games.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon embodied tasks suffer from reliance on hand-engineered rewards or labeled demonstrations, which do not scale. A scalable, reward-free, self-improving approach is needed to progress in complex tasks over extended timescales.
AI.method: ASH deploys a self-improvement loop: when learning stalls, it trains an Inverse Dynamics Model (IDM) from its own trajectories and uses IDM-derived signals to mine supervision from relevant internet video. It also uses unsupervised learning to identify key moments in large-scale internet video and stores them in long-term memory for retrieval-augmented policy learning; evaluation is on Pokemon Emerald and The Minish Cap Zelda in long-horizon settings.
AI.result: In both games, ASH achieves higher progression, averaging 11.2/12 milestones in Pokemon Emerald and 9.9/12 in Zelda, while strong baselines plateau around 6.0–6.5 milestones. This demonstrates robust long-horizon learning and self-improvement.
AI.conclusion: Self-improving agents can be a scalable strategy for long-horizon embodied learning without rewards or human annotations, leveraging IDM-based supervision, unsupervised moment discovery, and long-term memory.

title: CoRDS: Coreset-based Representative and Diverse Selection for Streaming Video Understanding
summary: Streaming video understanding with large vision-language models (VLMs) requires a compact memory that can support future reasoning over an ever-growing visual history. A common solution is to compress the key-value (KV) cache, but existing streaming methods typically rely on local token-wise heuristics, such as recency, temporal redundancy, or saliency, which do not explicitly optimize whether the retained cache is representative of the accumulated history. We propose to view KV-cache compression as a coreset selection problem: rather than scoring tokens independently for retention, we select a small subset that covers the geometry of the accumulated visual cache. Our method operates in a joint KV representation and introduces a bicriteria objective that balances coverage in key and value spaces, preserving both retrieval structure and output-relevant information. To encourage a more diverse retained subset, we further introduce an orthogonality-driven diversity criterion that favors candidates contributing new directions beyond the current selection, and connect this criterion to log-determinant subset selection. Across four open-source VLMs and five long-video and streaming-video benchmarks, our method improves over heuristic streaming compression baselines under a fixed cache budget. These results highlight that representative coreset selection offers a more effective principle, than token-wise pruning, for memory-constrained streaming video understanding.

AI.tldr: Coreset-based KV-cache compression for streaming vision-language models improves memory-efficiency by selecting a representative subset of the visual history that covers the geometry of the accumulated cache, using a bicriteria coverage objective and an orthogonality-driven diversity criterion linked to log-determinant subset selection, outperforming heuristic token-wise pruning across multiple models and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current streaming VLMs rely on local, token-wise heuristics (recency, redundancy, saliency) for memory compression, which may not preserve the global structure of the accumulated visual history. There is a need for a memory strategy that explicitly optimizes representativeness of the history to support future reasoning.
AI.method: Joint KV representation with a bicriteria objective balancing coverage in key and value spaces for the selected coreset; an orthogonality-driven diversity criterion to encourage new directions beyond current selection; theoretical connection to log-determinant subset selection; evaluation across four open-source VLMs and five long-video/streaming benchmarks with fixed cache budgets.
AI.result: The proposed coreset-based method yields improvements over heuristic streaming compression baselines under the same cache budget across multiple models and benchmarks, demonstrating that representative coreset selection more effectively preserves memory-relevant information than token-wise pruning.
AI.conclusion: Representative coreset selection provides a principled and effective memory compression mechanism for streaming video understanding with large VLMs, outperforming traditional token-wise heuristics under memory constraints.

title: bde: A Python Package for Bayesian Deep Ensembles via MILE
summary: bde is a user-friendly Python package for Bayesian Deep Ensembles with a particular focus on tabular data. Built on an efficient JAX implementation of the sampling-based inference method Microcanonical Langevin Ensembles (MILE), it provides scikit-learn compatible estimators for fast training, efficient Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling, and uncertainty quantification in both regression and classification tasks.

AI.tldr: A Python package (bde) for Bayesian Deep Ensembles on tabular data, using Microcanonical Langevin Ensembles (MILE) implemented in JAX, with scikit-learn compatible estimators for fast training and uncertainty quantification in regression and classification.
AI.motivation: Provide a user-friendly, scalable solution for Bayesian ensembling on tabular data with robust uncertainty quantification, integrating into the Python data-science stack.
AI.method: Implement Microcanonical Langevin Ensembles (MILE) as a sampling-based inference method in JAX; expose scikit-learn compatible estimators to enable fast training, efficient MCMC sampling, and uncertainty quantification for regression and classification tasks.
AI.result: Demonstrates efficient training and scalable Markov chain Monte Carlo sampling for Bayesian deep ensembles on tabular data, enabling practical uncertainty quantification.
AI.conclusion: bde offers a practical, scikit-learn–friendly framework for Bayesian deep ensembles on tabular data, combining JAX-based performance with robust uncertainty quantification.

title: MetaAgent-X : Breaking the Ceiling of Automatic Multi-Agent Systems via End-to-End Reinforcement Learning
summary: Automatic multi-agent systems aim to instantiate agent workflows without relying on manually designed or fixed orchestration. However, existing automatic MAS approaches remain only partially adaptive: they either perform training-free test-time search or optimize the meta-level designer while keeping downstream execution agents frozen, which creating a frozen-executor ceiling and leaving the end-to-end training of self-designing and self-executing agentic models unexplored. To address this, we introduce MetaAgent-X, an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes automatic MAS design and execution. MetaAgent-X enables script-based MAS generation, execution rollout collection, and credit assignment for both designer and executor trajectories. To support stable and scalable optimization, we propose Executor Designer Hierarchical Rollout and Stagewise Co-evolution to improve training stability and expose the dynamics of designer-executor co-evolution. MetaAgent-X consistently outperforms existing automatic MAS baselines, achieving up to 21.7% gains. Comprehensive ablations show that both designer and executor improve throughout training, and that effective automatic MAS learning follows a stagewise co-evolution process. These results establish end-to-end trainable automatic MAS as a practical paradigm for building self-designing and self-executing agentic models.

AI.tldr: Proposes MetaAgent-X, an end-to-end RL framework that jointly optimizes automatic MAS design and execution, using script-based MAS generation, hierarchical rollout, and stagewise co-evolution, achieving up to 21.7% gains over baselines.
AI.motivation: Overcome the frozen-executor ceiling and the lack of end-to-end training by enabling simultaneous optimization of MAS design and execution in an automatic, self-designing/self-executing agent framework.
AI.method: 1) Script-based MAS generation 2) Execution rollout collection 3) Credit assignment for both designer and executor trajectories 4) Executor Designer Hierarchical Rollout to stabilize training 5) Stagewise Co-evolution to expose co-evolution dynamics between designer and executor.
AI.result: MetaAgent-X consistently outperforms existing automatic MAS baselines, with gains up to 21.7%; ablations show both designer and executor improve during training and that learning follows stagewise co-evolution.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end trainable automatic MAS is a practical paradigm for building self-designing and self-executing agentic models.

title: TurboVGGT: Fast Visual Geometry Reconstruction with Adaptive Alternating Attention
summary: Recent feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods, such as visual geometry transformers, have substantially advanced the traditional per-scene optimization paradigm by enabling effective multi-view reconstruction in a single forward pass. However, most existing methods struggle to achieve a balance between reconstruction quality and computational efficiency, which limits their scalability and efficiency. Although some efficient visual geometry transformers have recently emerged, they typically use the same sparsity ratio across layers and frames and lack mechanisms to adaptively learn representative tokens to capture global relationships, leading to suboptimal performance. In this work, we propose TurboVGGT, a novel approach that employs an efficient visual geometry transformer with adaptive alternating attention for fast multi-view 3D reconstruction. Specifically, TurboVGGT employs an end-to-end trainable framework with adaptive sparse global attention guided by adaptive sparsity selection to capture global relationships across frames and frame attention to aggregate local details within each frame. In the adaptive sparse global attention, TurboVGGT adaptively learns representative tokens with varying sparsity levels for global geometry modeling, considering that token importance varies across frames, attention layers operate tokens at different levels of abstraction, and global dependencies rely on structurally informative regions. Extensive experiments on multiple 3D reconstruction benchmarks demonstrate that TurboVGGT achieves fast multi-view reconstruction while maintaining competitive reconstruction quality compared with state-of-the-art methods. Project page: https://turbovggt.github.io/.

AI.tldr: An end-to-end adaptive sparse attention transformer (TurboVGGT) for fast, high-quality multi-view 3D reconstruction, achieving speedups via adaptive token sparsification while preserving global geometry modeling.
AI.motivation: Existing feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods struggle to balance reconstruction quality and efficiency. Many efficient visual geometry transformers use fixed sparsity and uniform token treatment, failing to adapt to frame-specific importance or global dependencies.
AI.method: Proposes TurboVGGT with adaptive sparse global attention guided by adaptive sparsity selection and frame-level attention. It learns representative tokens with varying sparsity across frames and abstraction levels, enabling global relationships to be captured efficiently; combines global adaptive attention with per-frame local attention in an end-to-end trainable framework.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple 3D reconstruction benchmarks show that TurboVGGT delivers fast multi-view reconstruction while maintaining competitive reconstruction quality compared with state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive alternating attention and adaptive sparsity enable efficient, scalable multi-view 3D reconstruction without sacrificing accuracy; the approach balances speed and quality and offers a practical solution for large-scale settings.

title: GenCircuit-RL: Reinforcement Learning from Hierarchical Verification for Genetic Circuit Design
summary: Genetic circuit design remains a laborious, expert-driven process despite decades of progress in synthetic biology. We study this problem through code generation: models produce Python code in pysbol3 to construct genetic circuits in the Synthetic Biology Open Language (SBOL), a formal representation that supports automated verification. We introduce GenCircuit-RL, a reinforcement learning framework built around hierarchical verification rewards that decompose correctness into five levels, from code execution to task-specific topological checks, and a four-stage curriculum that shifts optimization pressure from code generation to functional reasoning. We also introduce SynBio-Reason, a benchmark of 4,753 circuits spanning six canonical circuit types and nine tasks from code repair to de novo design, with held-out biological parts for out-of-distribution evaluation. Hierarchical verification improves task success on functional reasoning tasks by 14 to 16 percentage points over binary rewards, and curriculum learning is required for strong design performance. The resulting models generate topologically correct circuits, generalize to novel biological parts, and rediscover canonical designs from the synthetic biology literature.

AI.tldr: The work proposes GenCircuit-RL and SynBio-Reason to automate genetic circuit design via AI. It uses hierarchical verification rewards and curriculum learning to improve functional reasoning in SBOL-based code generation, achieving topologically correct designs and generalization to new parts, and rediscovering canonical designs.
AI.motivation: Genetic circuit design remains labor-intensive and expert-driven; automating code-generation for SBOL-based representations requires robust verification to ensure functional correctness and generalizability, including out-of-distribution parts.
AI.method: GenCircuit-RL is an RL framework with hierarchical verification rewards decomposed into five levels (code execution to task-specific topology checks) and a four-stage curriculum shifting optimization pressure toward functional reasoning. SynBio-Reason is a benchmark of 4,753 circuits across six canonical types and nine tasks, with held-out parts for out-of-distribution evaluation.
AI.result: Hierarchical verification improves task success on functional reasoning tasks by 14–16 percentage points over binary rewards. Curriculum learning is required for strong design performance. Models generate topologically correct circuits, generalize to novel parts, and rediscover canonical designs from the literature.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical rewards plus curriculum learning enable robust, generalizable AI-driven genetic circuit design in SBOL, with a new OOD benchmark (SynBio-Reason) facilitating evaluation of out-of-distribution generalization.

title: D2-CDIG: Controlled Diffusion Remote Sensing Image Generation with Dual Priors of DEM and Cloud-Fog
summary: Remote sensing image generation provides a reliable data foundation for remote sensing large models and downstream tasks. However, existing controllable remote sensing image generation methods typically rely on traditional techniques such as segmentation and edge detection, which do not fully leverage terrain or atmospheric conditions. As a result, the generated images often lack accuracy and naturalness when dealing with complex terrains and atmospheric phenomena. In this paper, we propose a novel remote sensing image generation framework, D2-CDIG, which integrates diffusion models with a dual-prior control mechanism. By incorporating both Digital Elevation Model (DEM) and cloud-fog information as dual prior knowledge, D2-CDIG precisely controls ground features and atmospheric phenomena within the generated images. Specifically, D2-CDIG decouples the terrain and atmospheric generation processes through independent control of ground and atmospheric branches. Additionally, a refined cloud-fog slider is introduced to flexibly adjust cloud thickness and distribution. During training, ground and atmospheric control signals are injected in layers to ensure a seamless transition within the images. Compared to traditional methods based on segmentation or edge detection, D2-CDIG shows significant improvements in image quality, detail richness, and realism. D2-CDIG offers a flexible and precise solution for remote sensing image generation, providing high-quality data for training large remote sensing models and downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces D2-CDIG, a diffusion-based remote sensing image generator with dual priors (DEM and cloud-fog) to control ground and atmospheric features separately, improving realism and detail over segmentation-based methods.
AI.motivation: Existing RS image generation relies on segmentation/edge techniques that poorly capture complex terrain and atmospheric phenomena, leading to less accurate and natural results; there is a need for a controllable generative framework that leverages terrain and atmospheric priors for realistic RS imagery.
AI.method: Uses diffusion models with a dual-prior control mechanism (ground and atmospheric branches) guided by DEM and cloud-fog information. Ground and atmosphere are decoupled into separate control paths; a refined cloud-fog slider adjusts thickness and distribution; training injects ground and atmospheric controls at multiple layers to ensure seamless transitions.
AI.result: Reported improvements in image quality, detail richness, and realism compared with traditional segmentation/edge-based methods; the framework provides flexible and precise RS image generation for training large RS models and downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: D2-CDIG offers a flexible, precise framework for remote sensing image generation by integrating dual terrain-atmosphere priors into diffusion models, enabling accurate ground and atmospheric rendering and better data for model training.

title: Uncovering Trajectory and Topological Signatures in Multimodal Pediatric Sleep Embeddings
summary: While generative models have shown promise in pediatric sleep analysis, the latent structure of their multimodal embeddings remains poorly understood. This work investigates session-wide diagnostic information contained in the sequences of 30-second pediatric PSG epochs embedded by a multimodal masked autoencoder. We test whether augmenting embeddings with PHATE-derived per-epoch coordinates and whole-night movement descriptors, persistent homology summaries of the embedding cloud, and EHR yields task-relevant signals. Simple linear and MLP models, chosen for interpretability rather than state-of-the-art performance, show that geometric, topological, and clinical features each provide complementary gains. For binary predictions, feature importance is task-dependent, and more expressive late-fusion models generally perform better, with AUPRC improving from 0.26 to 0.34 for desaturation, 0.31 to 0.48 for EEG arousal, 0.09 to 0.22 for hypopnea, and 0.05 to 0.14 for apnea. We also report Brier score and Expected Calibration Error, where the full fusion model yields the best calibration across all four binary tasks. Our study reveals that latent geometry/topology and EHR offer complementary, interpretable signals beyond embeddings, improving calibration and robustness under extreme imbalance.

AI.tldr: Multimodal pediatric PSG embeddings augmented with geometry/topology features and EHR improve task performance and calibration in sleep disorder detection.
AI.motivation: To elucidate the latent structure of multimodal embeddings in pediatric sleep analysis and extract complementary, interpretable signals beyond embeddings, particularly under severe class imbalance.
AI.method: Embed 30-s segments of pediatric PSG with a multimodal masked autoencoder; augment embeddings with PHATE-derived per-epoch coordinates, whole-night movement descriptors, persistent-homology summaries of the embedding cloud, and EHR data; train simple linear and MLP models (prioritizing interpretability) and compare fusion strategies (early/late/full) across four binary tasks: desaturation, EEG arousal, hypopnea, apnea.
AI.result: Geometric, topological, and clinical features provide complementary gains to embeddings; late-fusion models yield stronger task performance; AUPRC improvements: desaturation 0.26→0.34, EEG arousal 0.31→0.48, hypopnea 0.09→0.22, apnea 0.05→0.14; full fusion achieves the best calibration (lower Brier, ECE) across all tasks; robustness under extreme class imbalance improved.
AI.conclusion: Latent geometry/topology features and EHR offer complementary, interpretable signals that enhance calibration and robustness beyond embedding alone, advancing pediatric sleep disorder assessment with multimodal representations.

title: Fusion-fission forecasts when AI will shift to undesirable behavior
summary: The key problem facing ChatGPT-like AI's use across society is that its behavior can shift, unnoticed, from desirable to undesirable -- encouraging self-harm, extremist acts, financial losses, or costly medical and military mistakes -- and no one can yet predict when. Shifts persist in even the newest AI models despite remarkable progress in AI modeling, post-training alignment and safeguards. Here we show that a vector generalization of fusion-fission group dynamics observed in living and active-matter systems drives -- and can forecast -- future shifts in the AI's behavior. The shift condition, which is also derivable mathematically, results from group-level competition between the conversation-so-far (C) and the desirable (B) and undesirable (D) basin dynamics which can be estimated in advance for a given application. It is neither model-specific nor driven by stochastic sampling. We validate it across six independent tests, including: 90 percent correct across seven AI models spanning two orders of magnitude in parameter count (124M-12B); production-scale persistence across ten frontier chatbots; and a priori time-stamped prediction eleven months before the Stanford 'Delusional Spirals' corpus appeared, and independently confirmed by that corpus of 207,443 human-AI exchanges. Because it sits architecturally below the current safety stack, the same formula provides a real-time warning signal that current alignment does not supply, portable across current and future ChatGPT-like AI architectures and instantiable in application domains where competing response classes can be defined.

AI.tldr: A vectorized, model-agnostic application of fusion-fission group dynamics predicts and forecasts shifts in ChatGPT-like AI behavior, providing a real-time warning signal below the safety stack that is transferable across architectures and domains.
AI.motivation: Address unpredictable shifts in AI behavior (from desirable to undesirable) despite alignment efforts and safeguards; to provide a portable, architecture-agnostic forecasting signal that works across model scales and applications.
AI.method: Develop a vector generalization of fusion-fission group dynamics. Model dynamics as competition among three basins: conversation-so-far (C), desirable (B), and undesirable (D). Derive a shift condition from group-level interactions; estimate B and D in advance for a given domain; show the condition is not model-specific nor driven by stochastic sampling; validate across six tests including cross-model accuracy (124M–12B), production-scale chatbots, and a priori forecasts (11 months before the Stanford 'Delusional Spirals' corpus) corroborated by 207,443 human–AI exchanges.
AI.result: Reportedly 90% correct across seven AI models spanning two orders of magnitude in parameter count; observed persistence across ten frontier chatbots; provided a priori time-stamped prediction preceding the Delusional Spirals corpus by 11 months, with independent confirmation from the corpus of 207,443 exchanges.
AI.conclusion: The shift-condition operates beneath current safety stacks and yields a portable, real-time warning signal applicable to current and future ChatGPT-like architectures, across domains where competing response classes can be defined.

title: InsightTok: Improving Text and Face Fidelity in Discrete Tokenization for Autoregressive Image Generation
comment: Code and checkpoints are available at https://github.com/LeapLabTHU/InsightTok
summary: Text and faces are among the most perceptually salient and practically important patterns in visual generation, yet they remain challenging for autoregressive generators built on discrete tokenization. A central bottleneck is the tokenizer: aggressive downsampling and quantization often discard the fine-grained structures needed to preserve readable glyphs and distinctive facial features. We attribute this gap to standard discrete-tokenizer objectives being weakly aligned with text legibility and facial fidelity, as these objectives typically optimize generic reconstruction while compressing diverse content uniformly. To address this, we propose InsightTok, a simple yet effective discrete visual tokenization framework that enhances text and face fidelity through localized, content-aware perceptual losses. With a compact 16k codebook and a 16x downsampling rate, InsightTok significantly outperforms prior tokenizers in text and face reconstruction without compromising general reconstruction quality. These gains consistently transfer to autoregressive image generation in InsightAR, producing images with clearer text and more faithful facial details. Overall, our results highlight the potential of specialized supervision in tokenizer training for advancing discrete image generation.

AI.tldr: Introduces InsightTok, a content-aware discrete tokenizer with perceptual losses to improve text readability and facial fidelity in tokenized imagery, yielding a 16k codebook with 16x downsampling and improved text/face reconstruction; gains translate to autoregressive generation (InsightAR).
AI.motivation: Discrete tokenizers for text and faces suffer from over-aggressive downsampling and quantization, which butcher fine-grained structures essential for legible text and realistic facial details. Standard objectives optimize generic reconstruction, poorly aligning with text legibility and facial fidelity.
AI.method: Train a discrete visual tokenizer (InsightTok) with localized, content-aware perceptual losses to emphasize text and face regions. Use a compact 16k codebook and 16x downsampling. Integrate into an autoregressive generation pipeline (InsightAR) to demonstrate improved text and facial fidelity in generated images.
AI.result: Tokenizer outperforms prior tokenizers on text and face reconstruction without sacrificing general reconstruction quality. The improvements transfer to autoregressive generation, yielding clearer text and more faithful facial details in generated images.
AI.conclusion: Specialized, content-aware supervision in tokenizer training can significantly advance discrete image generation, particularly for text and facial fidelity, and readily transfer to autoregressive synthesis.

title: Finite Sample Bounds for Learning with Score Matching
summary: Learning of continuous exponential family distributions with unbounded support remains an important area of research for both theory and applications in high-dimensional statistics. In recent years, score matching has become a widely used method for learning exponential families with continuous variables due to its computational ease when compared against maximum likelihood estimation. However, theoretical understanding of the statistical properties of score matching is still lacking. In this work, we provide a non-asymptotic sample complexity analysis for learning the structure of exponential families of polynomials with score matching. The derived sample bounds show a polynomial dependence on the model dimension. These bounds are the first of its kind, as all prior work has shown only asymptotic bounds on the sample complexity.

AI.tldr: Non-asymptotic sample complexity bounds for learning structural properties of continuous exponential family distributions with polynomial sufficient statistics using score matching; bounds scale polynomially with model dimension; first finite-sample results in this setting.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in theoretical understanding of score matching for continuous exponential families with unbounded support; exploit computational advantages of score matching over MLE; provide finite-sample guarantees.
AI.method: Finite-sample (non-asymptotic) analysis of score matching for learning exponential families of polynomials; derive dimension-dependent polynomial sample complexity bounds; compare to prior asymptotic results.
AI.result: Established non-asymptotic, polynomial-in-dimension sample complexity bounds for structure learning with score matching in polynomial exponential families; first such finite-sample guarantees in this context.
AI.conclusion: Presents the first finite-sample guarantees for score matching in polynomial exponential families, offering new insights into statistical properties and guiding data requirements in high-dimensional settings.

title: Systematic Discovery of Semantic Attacks in Online Map Construction through Conditional Diffusion
summary: Autonomous vehicles depend on online HD map construction to perceive lane boundaries, dividers, and pedestrian crossings -- safety-critical road elements that directly govern motion planning. While existing pixel perturbation attacks can disrupt the mapping, they can be neutralized by standard adversarial defenses. We present MIRAGE, a framework for systematic discovery of semantic attacks that bypass adversarial defenses and degrade mapping predictions by finding plausible environmental variation (e.g. shadows, wet roads). MIRAGE exploits the latent manifold of real-world data learned by diffusion models, and searches for semantically mutated scenes neighboring the ground truth with the same road topology yet mislead the mapping predictions. We evaluate MIRAGE on nuScenes and demonstrate two attacks: (1) boundary removal, suppressing 57.7% of detections and corrupting 96% of planned trajectories; and (2) boundary injection, the only method that successfully injects fictitious boundaries, while pixel PGD and AdvPatch fail entirely. Both attacks remain potent under various adversarial defenses. We use two independent VLM judges to quantify realism, where MIRAGE passes as realistic 80--84% of the time (vs. 97--99% for clean nuScenes), while AdvPatch only 0--9%. Our findings expose a categorical gap in current adversarial defenses: semantic-level perturbations that manifest as legitimate environmental variation are substantially harder to mitigate than pixel-level perturbations.

AI.tldr: MIRAGE uses diffusion-model latent-space search to craft semantically plausible scene variations that bypass defenses and degrade HD-map predictions, enabling boundary removal/injection attacks that outperform pixel-level perturbations on nuScenes.
AI.motivation: Current defenses counter pixel-level perturbations but not semantic, real-world scene changes. HD map accuracy for lane boundaries and other road elements is safety-critical; thus robustness to realistic environmental variation is essential.
AI.method: Leverages diffusion models to explore semantically mutated scenes with the same road topology. Searches for nearby samples that mislead HD-map predictions while preserving topology. Evaluates two attacks (boundary removal and boundary injection) on nuScenes, comparing against pixel PGD and AdvPatch. Uses two independent vision-language models to quantify realism.
AI.result: Boundary removal: suppresses 57.7% of detections and corrupts 96% of planned trajectories. Boundary injection: only method to inject fictitious boundaries; pixel-based attacks fail. Defenses remain ineffective against these semantic perturbations. Realism judged by two VLMs: MIRAGE 80–84% realistic vs 97–99% for clean data; AdvPatch 0–9%. Overall, a categorical gap in defenses: semantic perturbations resembling legitimate environmental variation are harder to mitigate than pixel perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Semantic-level perturbations discovered via diffusion-based latent-space search constitute a distinct threat class unmet by current adversarial defenses. Robust safety-critical perception systems require defenses that address plausible real-world variations, potentially through semantic-aware training, detection, or model-internal robustness against topology-preserving perturbations.

title: Good to Go: The LOOP Skill Engine That Hits 99% Success and Slashes Token Usage by 99% via One-Shot Recording and Deterministic Replay
summary: Deploying AI agents for repetitive periodic tasks exposes a critical tension: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer unmatched flexibility in tool orchestration, yet their inherent stochasticity causes unpredictable failures, and repeated invocations incur prohibitive token costs. We present the LOOP SKILL ENGINE, a system that achieves a combined 99% success rate and 99% token reduction for periodic agent tasks through a one-shot recording, deterministic replay paradigm. On its first run, the agent executes the task with full LLM reasoning while the system transparently intercepts and records the complete tool-call trajectory. A greedy length-descending template extraction algorithm then converts this recording into a parameterized, branch-free Loop Skill -- a deterministic execution plan that captures the task's functional intent while parameterizing time-dependent and result-dependent variables. All subsequent executions bypass the LLM entirely: the engine resolves template variables against real-time values and replays the tool sequence deterministically. We prove two theorems: (1) Replay Determinism -- the step sequence of a validated Loop Skill is invariant across all future executions; (2) Write Safety -- concurrent access to persistent configuration is serialized through reentrant locks and atomic file replacement. Across a benchmark of periodic agent tasks spanning intervals from 5 minutes to 24 hours, the Loop Skill Engine reduces monthly token consumption by 93.3%--99.98% and cuts execution latency by 8.7x while eliminating output non-determinism. A multi-layer degradation strategy guarantees that tasks never stall. We release the engine as part of the buddyMe open-source agent framework.

AI.tldr: One-shot recording converts LLM-driven task execution into a deterministic, parameterized Loop Skill that deterministically replays future runs, dramatically reducing tokens and latency while maintaining high success.
AI.motivation: Address the inefficiency and non-determinism of using LLMs for periodic tasks by capturing a single optimal tool-call trajectory and replaying it deterministically.
AI.method: On the first run, the agent uses LLM reasoning and records the complete tool-call trajectory. A greedy length-descending template extraction converts this recording into a parameterized, branch-free Loop Skill. Subsequent runs bypass the LLM and deterministically replay tool sequences by substituting real-time values into template variables. Theorems: Replay Determinism (execution order invariant) and Write Safety (serialized config access). A multi-layer degradation strategy prevents stalls. Implemented as open-source within buddyMe.
AI.result: Reported 99% success rate and 99% token reduction; 93.3%–99.98% monthly token reduction; 8.7x latency improvement; elimination of output non-determinism.
AI.conclusion: Loop Skill Engine effectively converts flexible LLM workflows into fast, predictable, and cost-efficient periodic tasks, and is released as open-source (buddyMe).

title: IG-Diff: Complex Night Scene Restoration with Illumination-Guided Diffusion Model
summary: In nighttime circumstances, it is challenging for individuals and machines to perceive their surroundings. While prevailing image restoration methods adeptly handle singular forms of degradation, they falter when confronted with intricate nocturnal scenes, such as the concurrent presence of weather and low-light conditions. Compounding this challenge, the lack of paired data that encapsulates the coexistence of low-light situations and other forms of degradation hinders the development of a comprehensive end-to-end solution. In this work, we contribute complex nighttime scene datasets that simulate both illumination degradation and other forms of deterioration. To address the complexity of night degradation, we propose an integration of an illumination-guided module embedded in the diffusion model to guide the illumination restoration process. Our model can preserve texture fidelity while contending with the adversities posed by various degradation in low-light scenarios.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-model-based nighttime restoration with an illumination-guided module trained on complex degraded night scenes to jointly restore illumination and suppress other degradations while preserving texture.
AI.motivation: Addresses limitations of single-degradation restoration in night scenes, the scarcity of paired data for combined night degradations, and the need for end-to-end solutions for complex nocturnal environments.
AI.method: Create complex nighttime scene datasets simulating illumination degradation and other degradations; integrate an illumination-guided module into a diffusion model to steer illumination restoration; optimize to preserve texture fidelity under multi-degradation.
AI.result: The model preserves texture fidelity and robustness to multiple degradation types in low-light conditions; the abstract indicates feasibility of joint restoration under complex nocturnal data, though specific quantitative metrics are not provided.
AI.conclusion: The work contributes datasets and a diffusion-based restoration framework with an illumination-guided component to advance end-to-end restoration of complex nighttime scenes.

title: CSI-JEPA: Towards Foundation Representations for Ubiquitous Sensing with Minimal Supervision
summary: Channel state information (CSI) provides a widely available sensing modality for human and environment perception, but existing CSI sensing models usually rely on task-specific supervised training and require substantial labeled data for each task, device, user, or environment. This limits their scalability in practical deployments where unlabeled CSI is abundant but labeled data is costly to collect. In this paper, we present CSI-JEPA, a self-supervised predictive representation learning framework for label-efficient, multi-task Wi-Fi sensing. CSI-JEPA learns reusable temporal-spectral representations from unlabeled CSI samples by predicting latent features of masked channel regions from visible context. To better match the physical structure of CSI, CSI-JEPA tokenizes channel-response amplitude windows along the time and subcarrier dimensions. It then introduces a channel variation-aware masking strategy that samples predictive targets from regions with stronger local temporal and subcarrier-domain variations. After pretraining, the encoder is frozen and used as a backbone, with lightweight task-specific adapters added for downstream sensing tasks. We evaluate CSI-JEPA on seven real-world Wi-Fi sensing tasks spanning diverse objectives and deployment settings. The results show that CSI-JEPA improves downstream sensing performance over competitive baselines, achieving up to 10.64 percentage points mean accuracy gain over state-of-the-art supervised Transformer and matched-budget label savings of up to 98.0%.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised, multi-task Wi-Fi sensing framework (CSI-JEPA) that learns transferable temporal-spectral representations by predicting latent features of masked CSI regions from visible context; achieves strong label-efficient performance across seven real-world tasks, including substantial accuracy gains and near-max label savings.
AI.motivation: Current CSI sensing models rely on task-specific supervised training requiring large labeled datasets. Labeled data is costly and not scalable across tasks, devices, environments, and users. Unlabeled CSI is abundant, motivating label-efficient, multi-task learning.
AI.method: Self-supervised predictive representation learning (CSI-JEPA). Tokenizes channel-response amplitude windows along time and subcarrier dimensions to reflect CSI structure. Introduces a channel variation-aware masking strategy that selects predictive targets from regions with strong local temporal and spectral variations. Pretrains an encoder to predict latent features of masked regions from context; after pretraining, freezes the encoder and attaches lightweight task-specific adapters for downstream sensing tasks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on seven real-world Wi-Fi sensing tasks shows CSI-JEPA outperforms competitive baselines. It achieves up to 10.64 percentage points improvement in mean accuracy over state-of-the-art supervised Transformer and affords matched-budget label savings of up to 98.0%.
AI.conclusion: CSI-JEPA provides scalable, label-efficient Wi-Fi sensing by learning reusable, predictive representations that transfer across tasks. The approach reduces labeling requirements while delivering strong performance across diverse deployment settings.

title: SceneFunRI: Reasoning the Invisible for Task-Driven Functional Object Localization
summary: In real-world scenes, target objects may reside in regions that are not visible. While humans can often infer the locations of occluded objects from context and commonsense knowledge, this capability remains a major challenge for vision-language models (VLMs). To address this gap, we introduce SceneFunRI, a benchmark for Reasoning the Invisible. Based on the SceneFun3D dataset, SceneFunRI formulates the task as a 2D spatial reasoning problem via a semi-automatic pipeline and comprises 855 instances. It requires models to infer the locations of invisible functional objects from task instructions and commonsense reasoning. The strongest baseline model (Gemini 3 Flash) only achieves an CAcc@75 of 15.20, an mIoU of 0.74, and a Dist of 28.65. We group our prompting analysis into three categories: Strong Instruction Prompting, Reasoning-based Prompting, and Spatial Process of Elimination (SPoE). These findings indicate that invisible-region reasoning remains an unstable capability in current VLMs, motivating future work on models that more tightly integrate task intent, commonsense priors, spatial grounding, and uncertainty-aware search.

AI.tldr: A benchmark (SceneFunRI) tests 2D spatial reasoning for invisible/occluded objects in real scenes, built from SceneFun3D, with 855 instances. It measures how well vision-language models infer occluded object locations from instructions and commonsense. Current top models perform poorly (CAcc@75 15.20, mIoU 0.74, Dist 28.65). Prompting analyses show three directions (Strong Instruction Prompting, Reasoning-based Prompting, SPoE). Conclusion: invisible-region reasoning is unstable; future work should better integrate task intent, priors, spatial grounding, and uncertainty-aware search.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in VLMs' ability to infer invisible/occluded objects in real scenes, leveraging commonsense and spatial reasoning to improve grounding and task performance.
AI.method: Formulates as a 2D spatial reasoning task via a semi-automatic pipeline using the SceneFun3D dataset to create 855 instances; evaluates VLMs with different prompting strategies across three categories (Strong Instruction Prompting, Reasoning-based Prompting, SPoE).
AI.result: Gemini 3 Flash baseline achieves CAcc@75=15.20, mIoU=0.74, Dist=28.65, indicating weak performance on invisible-region reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Invisible-region reasoning remains challenging for current VLMs; future work should integrate task intent, commonsense priors, spatial grounding, and uncertainty-aware search.

title: Hypergraph Enterprise Agentic Reasoner over Heterogeneous Business Systems
summary: Applying Large Language Models (LLMs) to heterogeneous enterprise systems is hindered by hallucinations and failures in multi-hop, n-ary reasoning. Existing paradigms (e.g., GraphRAG, NL2SQL) lack the semantic grounding and auditable execution required for these complex environments. We introduce HEAR, an enterprise agentic reasoner built on a Stratified Hypergraph Ontology. Its base Graph Layer virtualizes provenance-aware data interfaces, while the Hyperedge Layer encodes n-ary business rules and procedural protocols. Operating an evidence-driven reasoning loop, HEAR dynamically orchestrates ontology tools for structured multi-hop analysis without requiring LLM retraining. Evaluations on supply-chain tasks, including order fulfillment blockage root cause analysis (RCA), show HEAR achieves up to 94.7% accuracy. Crucially, HEAR demonstrates adaptive efficiency: utilizing procedural hyperedges to minimize token costs, while leveraging topological exploration for rigorous correctness on complex queries. By matching proprietary model performance with open-weight backbones and automating manual diagnostics, HEAR establishes a scalable, auditable foundation for enterprise intelligence.

AI.tldr: HEAR is an ontology-based enterprise agent that uses a stratified hypergraph to ground data and rules, enabling evidence-driven, auditable multi-hop reasoning without LLM retraining; it achieves high accuracy on supply-chain tasks and reduces token costs via procedural hyperedges.
AI.motivation: Tackle hallucinations and ungrounded, multi-hop reasoning in heterogeneous enterprise systems while providing auditable execution, addressing gaps in GraphRAG/NL2SQL-like paradigms.
AI.method: Introduce a Stratified Hypergraph Ontology with a Graph Layer for provenance-aware data interfaces and a Hyperedge Layer for encoding n-ary business rules and procedural protocols. Operate an evidence-driven reasoning loop that dynamically orchestrates ontology tools for structured multi-hop analysis without requiring LLM retraining.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on supply-chain tasks (e.g., order fulfillment blockage RCA) showing up to 94.7% accuracy; adaptive efficiency by using procedural hyperedges to reduce token costs and topological exploration to ensure correctness on complex queries; performance matches proprietary-model levels with open-weight backbones and automates manual diagnostics.
AI.conclusion: HEAR provides a scalable, auditable foundation for enterprise intelligence by combining provenance-grounded data interfaces with structured, rule-based reasoning, enabling robust, cost-efficient enterprise AI without retraining LLMs.

title: AnyBand-Diff: A Unified Remote Sensing Image Generation and Band Repair Framework with Spectral Priors
summary: Existing diffusion models have made significant progress in generating realistic images. However, their direct adaptation to remote sensing imagery often disregards intrinsic physical laws. This oversight frequently leads to spectral distortion and radiometric inconsistency, severely limiting the scientific utility of generated data. To address this issue, this paper introduces AnyBand-Diff, a novel spectral-prior-guided diffusion framework tailored for robust spectral reconstruction. Specifically, we design a Masked Conditional Diffusion backbone integrated with a dual stochastic masking strategy, empowering the model to recover complete spectral information from arbitrary band subsets. Subsequently, to ensure radiometric fidelity, a Physics-Guided Sampling mechanism is proposed, leveraging gradients from a differentiable physical model to explicitly steer the denoising trajectory toward the manifold of physically plausible solutions. Furthermore, a Multi-Scale Physical Loss is formulated to enforce rigorous constraints across pixel, region, and global levels in a joint manner. Extensive experiments confirm the effectiveness of AnyBand-Diff in generating reliable imagery and achieving accurate spectral reconstruction, contributing to the advancement of physics-aware generative methods for Earth observation.

AI.tldr: AnyBand-Diff is a physics-informed diffusion framework for robust spectral reconstruction in remote sensing, combining a masked diffusion backbone, a physics-guided sampling mechanism, and a multi-scale physical loss to ensure radiometric fidelity and spectral completeness.
AI.motivation: Standard diffusion models struggle to preserve intrinsic physical laws in remote sensing data, causing spectral distortion and radiometric inconsistency that undermine scientific use.
AI.method: Introduce a Masked Conditional Diffusion backbone with a dual stochastic masking strategy to recover full spectral information from arbitrary band subsets; implement a Physics-Guided Sampling mechanism that uses gradients from a differentiable physical model to steer denoising toward physically plausible solutions; formulate a Multi-Scale Physical Loss enforcing pixel-, region-, and global-level physics constraints.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that AnyBand-Diff produces reliable imagery and achieves accurate spectral reconstruction, validating physics-aware diffusion for Earth observation.
AI.conclusion: AnyBand-Diff advances physics-aware generative methods for Earth observation by enforcing spectral and radiometric fidelity through spectral priors, physics-guided sampling, and multi-scale physical constraints.

title: LLMs Know When They Know, but Do Not Act on It: A Metacognitive Harness for Test-time Scaling
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often expose useful signals of self-monitoring: before solving a problem, they can estimate whether they are likely to succeed, and after solving it, they can judge whether their answer is likely to be correct. However, these signals are typically measured or elicited in isolation, rather than used to control inference. In this work, we ask whether LLMs possess latent metacognitive ability that can be turned into effective test-time control. Inspired by the Nelson--Narens theory from cognitive psychology, we propose a metacognitive harness that separates monitoring from reasoning. For each problem, the model first reports a pre-solve feeling-of-knowing (FOK) signal; after each solve attempt, it reports a post-solve judgment-of-learning (JOL) signal. Rather than treating these signals as passive confidence estimates, the harness turns them into an explicit control interface for reasoning: it decides when to trust the current solution, when to retry with compact metacognitive feedback, and when to pass multiple attempts to a final aggregator. Across text, code, and multimodal reasoning benchmarks, our harness substantially improves a fixed Claude Sonnet-4.6 base model without parameter updates or benchmark-specific fine-tuning. On the evaluated public benchmark snapshots, it raises pooled accuracy from 48.3 to 56.9 and exceeds the strongest listed leaderboard entries on the three primary evaluation settings: HLE-Verified, LiveCodeBench v6, and R-Bench-V. These results suggest that strong LLMs may already possess useful metacognitive ability, but require an explicit control harness to act on it during reasoning.

AI.tldr: A metacognitive harness turns LLM self-monitoring signals (pre-solve FOK and post-solve JOL) into an explicit control interface for reasoning, improving accuracy without parameter updates across text, code, and multimodal benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLMs show self-monitoring signals but these are usually measured in isolation. Harnessing latent metacognition to actively control inference could improve reasoning without fine-tuning.
AI.method: For each problem, the model reports a pre-solve feeling-of-knowing (FOK) signal, then after a solve attempt reports a post-solve judgment-of-learning (JOL) signal. These signals are used to decide when to trust a solution, when to retry with compact metacognitive feedback, or to pass multiple attempts to a final aggregator. This metacognitive control is evaluated on text, code, and multimodal benchmarks using a fixed Claude Sonnet-4.6 model with no parameter updates or task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.result: The harness substantially improves base model performance, raising pooled accuracy from 48.3 to 56.9 and surpassing strongest leaderboard entries on HLE-Verified, LiveCodeBench v6, and R-Bench-V benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Strong LLMs possess latent metacognitive ability; explicit control harnessing it can significantly improve reasoning performance without retraining.

title: EARL: Towards a Unified Analysis-Guided Reinforcement Learning Framework for Egocentric Interaction Reasoning and Pixel Grounding
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026. Project page: https://github.com/yuggiehk/EARL
summary: Understanding human--environment interactions from egocentric vision is essential for assistive robotics and embodied intelligent agents, yet existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) still struggle with accurate interaction reasoning and fine-grained pixel grounding. To this end, this paper introduces EARL, an Egocentric Analysis-guided Reinforcement Learning framework that explicitly transfers coarse interaction semantics to query-oriented answering and grounding. Specifically, EARL adopts a two-stage parsing framework including coarse-grained interpretation and fine-grained response. The first stage holistically interprets egocentric interactions and generates a structured textual description. The second stage produces the textual answer and pixel-level mask in response to the user query. To bridge the two stages, we extract a global interaction descriptor as a semantic prior, which is integrated via a novel Analysis-guided Feature Synthesizer (AFS) for query-oriented reasoning. To optimize heterogeneous outputs, including textual answers, bounding boxes, and grounding masks, we design a multi-faceted reward function and train the response stage with GRPO. Experiments on Ego-IRGBench show that EARL achieves 65.48% cIoU for pixel grounding, outperforming previous RL-based methods by 8.37%, while OOD grounding results on EgoHOS indicate strong transferability to unseen egocentric grounding scenarios.

AI.tldr: Two-stage EARL framework for egocentric analysis: coarse interaction parsing guides a response-oriented stage that jointly outputs text and pixel masks, using an Analysis-guided Feature Synthesizer and GRPO-trained RL, achieving strong in-domain and OOD grounding results.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal LLMs struggle with precise interaction reasoning and fine-grained pixel grounding in egocentric vision; aseparate coarse-to-fine parsing with a semantic prior can improve query-driven answers and grounding.
AI.method: A two-stage parsing framework: Stage 1 coarse interpretation producing a structured textual description of egocentric interactions; Stage 2 generates textual answers and pixel-level masks in response to user queries. A global interaction descriptor serves as a semantic prior, integrated via a novel Analysis-guided Feature Synthesizer (AFS) for query-oriented reasoning. The models are optimized with a multi-faceted reward function and trained the response stage using GRPO.
AI.result: On Ego-IRGBench, EARL achieves 65.48% cIoU for pixel grounding, outperforming prior RL-based methods by 8.37%. OOD grounding results on EgoHOS indicate strong transferability to unseen egocentric grounding scenarios.
AI.conclusion: EARL effectively bridges coarse interaction semantics and fine-grained grounding/response, delivering strong in-domain grounding and robust OOD transfer for egocentric reasoning tasks.

title: Heuristic Pathologies and Further Variance Reduction via Uncertainty Propagation in the AIVAT Family of Techniques
summary:
How should an agent's performance in a multiagent environment be evaluated when there is a limited sample size or a high cost of running a trial? The AIVAT family of variance reduction techniques was proposed to address this challenge by introducing unbiased low-variance estimators of agents' expected payoffs. An important component of AIVAT is a heuristic value function that discriminates between potentially low- and high-value counterfactual histories. A notable gap in the literature is that there is little to no constraint or guideline on how the heuristic value function should be chosen or how uncertainty in its output should be handled.
  In our first contribution, we parameterize the heuristic value function to highlight AIVAT's potential vulnerabilities: a) the sample variance can be set pathologically low by directly applying gradient descent on the sample variance, and b) one can p-hack to draw a desired statistical conclusion via gradient descent/ascent on the test statistic. The main takeaway is that the heuristic value function should be fixed prior to observing the evaluation data! In our second contribution, we show how the heuristic uncertainty can be propagated to quantify the uncertainty of AIVAT estimates. It is then possible to further reduce the variance using inverse-variance weighted averaging, but AIVAT's unbiasedness guarantee may have to be sacrificed. In our experiments, we use a dataset of 10,000 poker hands to demonstrate our heuristic pathology and uncertainty results, with the latter yielding a 43.0% reduction in the number of samples (poker hands) needed to draw statistical conclusions.

AI.tldr: AIVAT's heuristic value function can be manipulated to artificially lower variance or bias evaluation; fix the heuristic a priori to avoid data snooping; propagate heuristic uncertainty to allow inverse-variance variance reduction, though at the cost of unbiasedness; experiments on 10k poker hands show up to 43% fewer samples needed for statistical conclusions.
AI.motivation: Evaluate agent performance under limited samples or high trial cost using unbiased, low-variance payoffs; address the lack of guidelines for choosing the heuristic value function and handling its uncertainty in AIVAT.
AI.method: (1) Parameterize the heuristic value function to reveal vulnerabilities: gradient-descent on sample variance to reduce it; gradient-based p-hacking to steer test statistics. (2) Propose fixing the heuristic a priori. (3) Propagate heuristic uncertainty to quantify uncertainty of AIVAT; explore inverse-variance weighting to reduce variance, accepting possible loss of unbiasedness.
AI.result: Demonstrated vulnerabilities of the heuristic function to manipulation; proposed a priori fixing of the heuristic; established a framework to propagate heuristic uncertainty and reduce variance further using inverse-variance weighting with trade-offs; empirical validation on 10,000 poker hands showing a 43% reduction in required sample size for statistical conclusions.
AI.conclusion: Heuristic choice should be fixed before observing data; propagating heuristic uncertainty is feasible and can reduce variance but may compromise unbiasedness; practical guidance for AIVAT use and need for robust heuristic design.

title: Learning with Semantic Priors: Stabilizing Point-Supervised Infrared Small Target Detection via Hierarchical Knowledge Distillation
summary: Single-frame Infrared Small Target Detection (ISTD) aims to localize weak targets under heavy background clutter, yet dense pixel-wise annotations are expensive. Point supervision with online label evolution reduces annotation cost; however, lightweight CNN detectors often lack sufficient semantics, leading to noisy pseudo-masks and unstable optimization. To address this, we propose a hierarchical VFM-driven knowledge distillation framework that uses a frozen Vision Foundation Model (VFM) during training. We formulate point-supervised learning as a bilevel optimization process: the inner loop adapts a VFM-embedded teacher on reweighted training samples, while the outer loop transfers validation-guided knowledge to a lightweight student to mitigate pseudo-label noise and training-set bias. We further introduce Semantic-Conditioned Affine Modulation (SCAM) to inject VFM semantics into CNN features at multiple layers. In addition, a dynamic collaborative learning strategy with cluster-level sample reweighting enhances robustness to imperfect pseudo-masks. Experiments on diverse challenging cases across multiple ISTD backbones demonstrate consistent improvements in detection accuracy and training stability. Our code is available at https://github.com/yuanhang-yao/semantic-prior.

AI.tldr: A bilevel, VFM-guided knowledge distillation framework for point-supervised ISTD, featuring SCAM and dynamic sample reweighting to improve detection accuracy and training stability with a frozen Vision Foundation Model.
AI.motivation: Reduce annotation cost for ISTD by using point supervision; address pseudo-label noise and limited semantics in lightweight CNNs by injecting VFM knowledge and a robust distillation strategy.
AI.method: Inner loop: adapt a VFM-embedded teacher on reweighted training samples; outer loop: transfer validation-guided knowledge to a lightweight student to mitigate pseudo-label noise and training-set bias. Semantic-Conditioned Affine Modulation (SCAM) injects VFM semantics into CNN features across multiple layers. Dynamic collaborative learning with cluster-level sample reweighting enhances robustness to imperfect pseudo-masks.
AI.result: Empirical results across diverse ISTD backbones show consistent improvements in detection accuracy and training stability; code available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of VFM-driven distillation and semantic conditioning for weakly supervised ISTD; scalable approach with potential applicability to other weakly supervised detection tasks; invites further work on efficiency and cross-modality generalization.

title: How to Scale Mixture-of-Experts: From muP to the Maximally Scale-Stable Parameterization
summary: Recent frontier large language models predominantly rely on Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures. Despite empirical progress, there is still no principled understanding of how hyperparameters should scale with network width $N$, expert width $N_e$, number of experts $M$, sparsity $K$, and depth $L$ to ensure both stability and optimal performance at scale. We take a principled step toward resolving this gap by analyzing three different scaling regimes: (I) co-scaling $N\asymp N_e$, (II) co-scaling $N\asymp M\asymp K$, and (III) full proportional scaling of $N, N_e, M$, and $K$. For each regime, we develop a novel Dynamical Mean Field Theory (DMFT) description of the limiting training dynamics of MoEs that provides a formal foundation for our analysis. Within this framework, we derive the unique parameterization for SGD and Adam satisfying all maximal-update ($μ$) desiderata. We then show that the resulting $μ$P prescription does not reliably induce monotonic improvement with scale or robust learning-rate transfer. We trace these pathologies to scale-dependent observables in the aggregation dynamics, which motivates a refined set of desiderata that we term maximal scale stability. Guided by this principle, we derive a Maximally Scale-Stable Parameterization (MSSP) for both SGD and Adam in all three scaling regimes, and characterize the corresponding limiting dynamics - qualitatively distinct from the $μ$P limit - through a separate DMFT analysis. Experiments verify that MSSP robustly recovers learning rate transfer and monotonic improvement with scale across regimes. Combined with existing depth-scaling theory, these results provide a complete scaling prescription for MoE architectures as a function of width, depth, expert width, and number of experts.

AI.tldr: The abstract proposes a principled Maximally Scale-Stable Parameterization (MSSP) for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) networks, derived via Dynamical Mean Field Theory (DMFT) across three scaling regimes, addressing limitations of the prior μ parameterization and yielding a complete scaling prescription that harmonizes width, expert width, number of experts, sparsity, and depth with stable, scalable learning.
AI.motivation: To establish principled scaling laws for MoE models at scale, ensuring stability and monotonic performance gains, and to overcome observed failures of existing scale-invariant SGD/Adam parameterizations (μP) under scaling.
AI.method: Three scaling regimes are analyzed with DMFT: (I) N ~ Ne, (II) N ~ M ~ K, (III) full proportional scaling of N, Ne, M, K. The authors derive a unique SGD/Adam parameterization satisfying maximal-update (μ) desiderata, reveal its scale-pathologies, and then formulate MSSP under a refined principle of maximal scale stability. Limiting dynamics are characterized by a separate DMFT analysis. Empirical experiments validate that MSSP preserves learning-rate transfer and monotonically improves with scale across regimes.
AI.result: The μP parameterization fails to guarantee monotonic improvement or robust learning-rate transfer under scaling due to scale-dependent aggregation dynamics. MSSP resolves these issues, yielding scale-stable training dynamics and consistent transfer across all three regimes. The framework provides a complete scaling prescription when combined with existing depth-scaling theory.
AI.conclusion: MSSP offers a robust, universal scaling recipe for MoEs that accounts for width, depth, Ne, M, and K, overcoming μP’s deficiencies. It enables monotonic scale-up benefits and stable learning-rate transfer, and complements depth-scaling results to deliver a full, principled MoE scaling guide.

title: Slot-MPC: Goal-Conditioned Model Predictive Control with Object-Centric Representations
summary: Predictive world models enable agents to model scene dynamics and reason about the consequences of their actions. Inspired by human perception, object-centric world models capture scene dynamics using object-level representations, which can be used for downstream applications such as action planning. However, most object-centric world models and reinforcement learning (RL) approaches learn reactive policies that are fixed at inference time, limiting generalization to novel situations. We propose Slot-MPC, an object-centric world modeling framework that enables planning through Model Predictive Control (MPC). Slot-MPC leverages vision encoders to learn slot-based representations, which encode individual objects in the scene, and uses these structured representations to learn an action-conditioned object-centric dynamics model. At inference time, the learned dynamics model enables action planning via MPC, allowing agents to adapt to previously unseen situations. Since the learned world model is differentiable, we can use gradient-based MPC to directly optimize actions, which is computationally more efficient than relying on gradient-free, sampling-based MPC methods. Experiments on simulated robotic manipulation tasks show that Slot-MPC improves both task performance and planning efficiency compared to non-object-centric world model baselines. In the considered offline setting with limited state-action coverage, we find that gradient-based MPC performs better than gradient-free, sampling-based MPC. Our results demonstrate that explicitly structured, object-centric representations provide a strong inductive bias for controllable and generalizable decision-making. Code and additional results are available at https://slot-mpc.github.io.

AI.tldr: Slot-MPC introduces an object-centric world model enabling differentiable MPC planning, yielding improved manipulation performance and planning efficiency, with strong generalization to unseen situations and advantages over non-object-centric baselines and gradient-free MPC in offline settings.
AI.motivation: Address limited generalization and reliance on reactive policies in existing object-centric world models by enabling planning with a differentiable, object-centric dynamics model that can be optimized via gradient-based MPC.
AI.method: Learn slot-based object representations from vision encoders, construct an action-conditioned object-centric dynamics model, and perform planning via gradient-based Model Predictive Control. Compare against non-object-centric baselines and gradient-free MPC, especially in offline settings.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on simulated robotic manipulation tasks shows Slot-MPC achieves higher task performance and faster planning than non-object-centric baselines; gradient-based MPC outperforms gradient-free MPC in offline/limited state-action coverage settings; code and results are provided.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly structured, object-centric representations provide a strong inductive bias that improves controllability and generalization of decision-making; differentiable world models enable efficient planning.

title: Agentic AI Ecosystems in Higher Education: A Perspective on AI Agents to Emerging Inclusive, Agentic Multi-Agent AI Framework for Learning, Teaching and Institutional Intelligence
summary: Integration of artificial intelligent (AI) agents in higher education is transforming teaching, learning and administrative processes. Although existing AI agents effectively support individual tasks, their implementation remains fragmented and inefficient for handling the complexity of educational institutions. This highlights a significant research gap: the lack of integrated eco-system-level agentic multi-agent AI platform capable of coordinated planning, reasoning, and adaptive decision-making across multiple educational functions. This paper presents a forward-looking perspective on agentic multi-agent AI platform in higher education, consisting interconnected autonomous, goal driven agents that support learning, teaching, and institutional operations. It addresses timely and critical questions: Can agentic AI represent the next generation of intelligent systems in tertiary education? Can they collectively support seamless coordinated operations across teaching, learning and administrative support? To what extent can such systems foster inclusive and equitable learning for diverse learners with special educational needs? To ground this perspective, a thematic analysis of existing literature identifies four dominant themes: task-specific fragmented AI tools, the transition from single-agent to multi-agent systems, limited cross-functional integration, and insufficient focus on inclusivity and accessibility. Findings reveal a clear gap between current AI implementations and the needs of holistic, learner-centered educational ecosystem. The paper synthesizes challenges and outlines future research directions for scalable human-aligned, and inclusive agentic AI platform. The significant contribution is the incorporation of inclusive learning perspectives, highlighting how coordinated agentic multi-agent platform can support diverse learners through adaptive, multimodal interventions.

AI.tldr: An integrated, agentic multi-agent AI platform is needed in higher education to coordinate learning, teaching, and administration, addressing fragmented AI tools and inclusivity gaps.
AI.motivation: Current AI in higher education is fragmented and task-specific; there is a need for an ecosystem-level, coordinated AI platform that can plan, reason, and adapt across functions to support learners, educators, and administrators, including diverse learners with special needs.
AI.method: The paper employs thematic analysis of existing literature to identify four dominant themes (task-specific tools, single-agent to multi-agent transition, cross-functional integration, inclusivity). It synthesizes findings and outlines future research directions.
AI.result: Identifies a gap between current AI implementations and the desired holistic, learner-centered ecosystem; proposes a forward-looking, interconnected, autonomous, goal-driven multi-agent framework; emphasizes inclusive, multimodal interventions for diverse learners.
AI.conclusion: Advocates scalable, human-aligned, inclusive agentic AI platforms for higher education and outlines directions for research to realize coordinated operations across teaching, learning, and administration.

title: Delta Forcing: Trust Region Steering for Interactive Autoregressive Video Generation
summary: Interactive real-time autoregressive video generation is essential for applications such as content creation and world modeling, where visual content must adapt to dynamically evolving event conditions. A fundamental challenge lies in balancing reactivity and stability: models must respond promptly to new events while maintaining temporal coherence over long horizons. Existing approaches distill bidirectional models into autoregressive generators and further adapt them via streaming long tuning, yet often exhibit persistent drift after condition changes. We identify the cause as conditional bias, where the teacher may provide condition-aligned but trajectory-agnostic guidance, biasing generation toward locally valid yet globally inconsistent modes. Inspired by Trust Region Policy Optimization, we propose Delta Forcing, a simple yet effective framework that constrains unreliable teacher supervision within an adaptive trust region. Specifically, Delta Forcing estimates transition consistency from the latent delta between teacher and generator trajectories, and uses it to balance teacher supervision with a monotonic continuity objective. This suppress unreliable teacher-induced shifts while preserving responsiveness to new events. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Delta Forcing significantly improves consistency while maintaining event reactivity.

AI.tldr: Delta Forcing introduces an adaptive trust-region framework to curb conditional bias in autoregressive video generation by regulating teacher supervision using the latent delta between teacher and generator trajectories, thereby improving long-horizon temporal coherence without sacrificing event reactivity.
AI.motivation: Real-time autoregressive video generation must be both responsive to new events and temporally coherent over long horizons. Existing distillation-plus-tuning approaches suffer from conditional bias leading to drift when conditions change.
AI.method: Propose Delta Forcing, inspired by Trust Region Policy Optimization. It constrains unreliable teacher supervision within an adaptive trust region by estimating transition consistency from the latent delta between teacher and generator trajectories and balancing this with a monotonic continuity objective.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show improved consistency over long horizons while maintaining responsiveness to evolving events.
AI.conclusion: Delta Forcing offers a simple, effective mechanism to suppress unreliable teacher-induced shifts and preserve reactivity, enhancing streaming autoregressive video generation where long-horizon coherence is critical.

title: Towards Fine-Grained and Verifiable Concept Bottleneck Models
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) offer interpretable alternatives to black-box predictors by introducing human-relatable concepts before the final output. However, existing CBMs struggle to verify whether predicted concepts correspond to the correct visual evidence, limiting their reliability. We propose a fine-grained CBM framework that grounds each concept in localized visual evidence, enabling direct inspection of where and how concepts are encoded.
