This design allows users to interpret predictions and verify that the model learns intended concepts rather than spurious correlations. Experiments on medical imaging benchmarks show that our learned concept space is information-complete and achieves predictive performance comparable to standard CBMs, while substantially improving transparency. Unlike post-hoc attribution methods, our framework validates both the presence and correctness of concept representations, bridging interpretability with verifiability. Our approach enhances the trustworthiness of CBMs and establishes a principled mechanism for human-model interaction at the concept level, paving the way toward more reliable and clinically actionable concept-based learning systems.

AI.tldr: A fine-grained, ground-tracked Concept Bottleneck Model (CBM) that anchors each concept to localized visual evidence, enabling verifiable interpretation without sacrificing predictive performance in medical imaging.
AI.motivation: CBMs are interpretable but often fragile: predicted concepts may not align with actual visual evidence, undermining trust. There is a need to verify not only the presence of concepts but their correct grounding in the image to ensure reliable, clinically actionable decisions.
AI.method: Develop a fine-grained CBM framework that grounds each concept in localized visual evidence. The model produces concept representations linked to specific image regions, enabling inspection of where and how each concept is encoded. This yields an information-complete concept space and maintains predictive performance comparable to standard CBMs, while enhancing transparency and verifiability.
AI.result: On medical imaging benchmarks, the proposed framework achieves predictive performance on par with standard CBMs and renders the concept space information-complete. It substantially improves transparency by validating both the presence and correct grounding of concepts, rather than relying on post-hoc explanations.
AI.conclusion: Grounding concepts in localized evidence bridges interpretability and verifiability, increasing trust and enabling principled human–model interaction at the concept level. This approach advances reliable, clinically actionable concept-based learning systems.

title: Evo-Depth: A Lightweight Depth-Enhanced Vision-Language-Action Model
summary: Vision-Language-Action models have emerged as a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation by unifying perception, language grounding, and action generation. However, they often struggle in scenarios requiring precise spatial understanding, as current VLA models primarily rely on 2D visual representations that lack depth information and detailed spatial relationships. While recent approaches incorporate explicit 3D inputs such as depth maps or point clouds to address this issue, they often increase system complexity, require additional sensors, and remain vulnerable to sensing noise and reconstruction errors. Another line of work explores implicit 3D-aware spatial modeling directly from RGB observations without extra sensors, but it often relies on large geometry foundation models, resulting in higher training and deployment costs. To address these challenges, we propose Evo-Depth, a lightweight depth-enhanced VLA framework that enhances spatially grounded manipulation without relying on additional sensing hardware or compromising deployment efficiency. Evo-Depth employs a lightweight Implicit Depth Encoding Module to extract compact depth features from multi-view RGB images. These features are incorporated into vision-language representations through a Spatial Enhancement Module via depth-aware modulation, enabling efficient spatial-semantic enhancement. A Progressive Alignment Training strategy is further introduced to align the resulting depth-enhanced representations with downstream action learning. With only 0.9B parameters, Evo-Depth achieves superior performance across four simulation benchmarks. In real-world experiments, Evo-Depth attains the highest average success rate while also exhibiting the smallest model size, lowest GPU memory usage, and highest inference frequency among compared methods.

AI.tldr: A lightweight depth-enhanced Vision-Language-Action framework (Evo-Depth) for robotic manipulation that uses implicit depth encoding and depth-aware modulation to improve spatial understanding with 0.9B parameters, achieving state-of-the-art results in simulations and real-world efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of current VLA models that rely on 2D visuals and lack depth; avoid the complexity and noise of explicit 3D inputs or costly geometry foundation models; enable accurate spatial grounding and action in robotics with minimal hardware and compute.
AI.method: 1) Implicit Depth Encoding Module to extract compact depth features from multi-view RGB images. 2) Spatial Enhancement Module performing depth-aware modulation to fuse depth features into vision-language representations. 3) Progressive Alignment Training to align depth-enhanced representations with downstream action learning.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines across four simulation benchmarks. Real-world experiments show the highest average success rate, the smallest model size, lowest GPU memory usage, and highest inference frequency among compared methods.
AI.conclusion: Evo-Depth delivers depth-aware spatial grounding for manipulation with a lightweight, efficient architecture, reducing hardware and computation costs while improving performance.

title: Parallelizing Counterfactual Regret Minimization
comment: This paper contains and extends ideas that were originally in arxiv:2408.14778
summary: Parallelization has played an instrumental role in the field of artificial intelligence (AI), drastically reducing the time taken to train and evaluate large AI models. In contrast to its impact in the broader field of AI, applying parallelization to computational game solving is relatively unexplored, despite its great potential. In this paper, we parallelize the family of counterfactual regret minimization (CFR) algorithms, which were central to important breakthroughs for solving large imperfect-information games. We present a generalized parallelization framework, reframing CFR as a series of linear algebra operations. Then, existing techniques for parallelizing linear algebra operations can be applied to accelerate CFR. We also describe how our technique can be applied to other tabular members of the CFR family of algorithms, including the state-of-the-art, such as CFR+, discounted CFR, and predictive variants of CFR. Experimentally, we show that our CFR implementation on a GPU is up to four orders of magnitude faster than Google DeepMind OpenSpiel's CFR implementations on a CPU.

AI.tldr: A GPU-accelerated framework for CFR: reframes CFR as linear algebra to enable parallelization, applicable to CFR, CFR+, discounted CFR, and predictive CFR; reports large speedups over CPU baseline OpenSpiel CFR.
AI.motivation: CFR is central to solving large imperfect-information games, but parallelization is underexplored; enabling GPU-based linear-algebra acceleration could dramatically speed up training and evaluation.
AI.method: Generalized framework that rewrites CFR as a sequence of linear-algebra operations; leverages existing GPU/parallel linear-algebra techniques; extends to tabular CFR variants (CFR+, discounted CFR, predictive CFR).
AI.result: Experimental GPU implementation achieves up to four orders of magnitude speedup over OpenSpiel's CPU CFR implementations.
AI.conclusion: Parallel CFR framed as linear algebra is effective and scalable to CFR variants; enables faster AI game solving and broader applicability, with considerations needed for convergence, memory, and reproducibility across hardware.

title: Dual-Latent Collaborative Decoding for Fidelity-Perception Balanced Image Compression
summary: Learned image compression (LIC) increasingly requires reconstructions that balance distortion fidelity and perceptual realism across a wide range of bitrates. However, most existing methods still rely on a single compressed latent representation to simultaneously carry structural details, semantic cues, and perceptual priors, requiring the same latent representation to serve multiple, potentially conflicting roles. This tension becomes evident across different latent paradigms: scalar-quantized (SQ) continuous latents provide rate-scalable fidelity but tend to lose perceptual details at low rates, while vector-quantized (VQ) discrete tokens preserve compact semantic cues but suffer from limited structural fidelity and bitrate scalability. To address this issue, we propose Mixture of Decoder Experts (MoDE), a dual-latent collaborative decoding framework that decomposes reconstruction responsibilities across complementary latent paradigms. Specifically, MoDE treats the SQ branch as a fidelity-oriented expert and the VQ branch as a perception-oriented expert, and coordinates them through two decoder-side modules: Expert-Specific Enhancement (ESE), which preserves branch-specific expert references, and Cross-Expert Modulation (CEM), which enables selective complementary transfer during reconstruction. The resulting framework supports selective cross-latent collaboration under a shared dual-stream bitstream and enables both fidelity-anchored and perception-anchored decoding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MoDE achieves a more favorable fidelity-perception balance than representative distortion-oriented, perception-oriented, generative, and dual-latent baselines across a wide bitrate range, highlighting decoder-side expert collaboration as an effective design for wide-range fidelity-perception balanced LIC.

AI.tldr: MoDE introduces a dual-latent image compression framework that separates fidelity and perceptual aspects into a scalar-quantized (fidelity) and a vector-quantized (perception) latent, with decoder-side modules (ESE and CEM) enabling selective cross-latent collaboration for improved fidelity-perception balance across bitrate ranges.
AI.motivation: Single latent representations struggle to simultaneously satisfy fidelity, semantic cues, and perceptual realism at varying bitrates. A dual-latent approach can distribute these roles to dedicated latent types, but requires effective coordination to avoid conflicting demands.
AI.method: Mixture of Decoder Experts (MoDE) uses two parallel latent streams: SQ (fidelity-oriented) and VQ (perception-oriented). Decoder-side modules—Expert-Specific Enhancement (ESE) preserves branch-specific references, and Cross-Expert Modulation (CEM) enables selective transfer between experts during reconstruction, enabling both fidelity-anchored and perception-anchored decoding under a shared dual-stream bitstream.
AI.result: MoDE achieves a more favorable fidelity-perception balance than a range of baselines (distortion-oriented, perception-oriented, generative, and dual-latent) across a wide bitrate range, demonstrating the efficacy of decoder-side expert collaboration for wide-range LIC.
AI.conclusion: Decoder-side collaboration between complementary latent paradigms is effective for balancing fidelity and perceptual realism in learned image compression, enabling flexible decoding strategies without sacrificing bitstream compatibility.

title: PreFT: Prefill-only finetuning for efficient inference
summary: Large language models can now be personalised efficiently at scale using parameter efficient finetuning methods (PEFTs), but serving user-specific PEFTs harms throughput, even with specialised kernels and memory management techniques. This is because, theoretically and empirically, a mismatch exists between prefill (processing a large number of tokens at once) and decode (generating a single token autoregressively): the latter has far lower throughput when serving multiple adapters. Rather than optimising performance relative to parameter count, for efficient multi-adapter serving, we instead ought to optimise performance relative to serving throughput. We therefore propose PreFT (Prefill-only Finetuning), wherein we only apply the adapter to prefill tokens and discard it afterwards. PreFT significantly increases throughput with minimal effect on performance. We develop and release an efficient implementation of two prefill-only PEFTs, LoRA and ReFT, on the vLLM inference engine. We first show that serving multi-user PreFTs is more efficient than traditional PEFTs ($1.9\times$ the throughput when serving $512$ adapters on Llama 3.1 70B). Then, we compare the performance of prefill-only vs. all-token adapters on a variety of supervised finetuning and reinforcement learning tasks with LMs at varying scales. On SFT, we observe that the evaluation loss of PreFTs is higher than PEFTs, but can be compensated by increasing rank with nearly no reduction in throughput. On RL, we consistently find that PreFTs approach parity with standard PEFTs. Together, this work validates prefill-only adaptation of LLMs as a more favourable accuracy-throughput tradeoff than existing PEFTs for personalised serving.

AI.tldr: Prefill-only Finetuning (PreFT) applies adapters only during the prefill stage of autoregressive LM generation, discarding them later to boost multi-adapter serving throughput with minimal performance loss. Implemented as LoRA and ReFT on vLLM, achieving significant throughput gains and favorable accuracy-throughput tradeoffs across tasks.
AI.motivation: PEFTs enable personalization at scale but suffer throughput penalties when serving many adapters due to mismatch between prefill (bulk processing) and decode (token-by-token generation). A throughput-centric optimization is needed for multi-adapter serving.
AI.method: Propose PreFT: apply adapters only to prefill tokens and discard afterwards. Develop efficient implementations of two prefill-only PEFTs, LoRA and ReFT, on the vLLM inference engine. Evaluate serving throughput with multi-user setups (e.g., 512 adapters on Llama 3.1 70B) and compare against traditional PEFTs. Assess performance on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL) tasks across LM scales.
AI.result: Prefill-only adapters substantially increase throughput (≈1.9× with 512 adapters on Llama 3.1 70B) compared with full-token adapters. SFT shows higher evaluation loss with PreFT but can be mitigated by increasing adapter rank with negligible throughput loss. In RL tasks, PreFT approaches parity with standard PEFTs. Overall, PreFT offers a more favorable accuracy-throughput tradeoff for multi-adapter personalized serving; the paper provides efficient implementations of LoRA and ReFT on vLLM.
AI.conclusion: Prefill-only adaptation is a more efficient strategy for multi-adapter personalized serving than traditional PEFTs, delivering higher throughput with minimal (or tolerable) performance sacrifice, and enabling scalable, personalized LLM deployment.

title: Articraft: An Agentic System for Scalable Articulated 3D Asset Generation
summary: A bottleneck in learning to understand articulated 3D objects is the lack of large and diverse datasets. In this paper, we propose to leverage large language models (LLMs) to close this gap and generate articulated assets at scale. We reduce the problem of generating an articulated 3D asset to that of writing a program that builds it. We then introduce a new agentic system, Articraft, that writes such programs automatically. We design a programmatic interface and harness to help the LLM do so effectively. The LLM writes code against a domain-specific SDK for defining parts, composing geometry, specifying joints, and writing tests to validate the resulting assets. The harness exposes a restricted workspace and interface to the LLM, validates the resulting assets, and returns structured feedback. In this way, the LLM is not distracted by details such as authoring a URDF file or managing a complex software environment. We show that this produces higher-quality assets than both state-of-the-art articulated-asset generators and general-purpose coding agents. Using Articraft, we build Articraft-10K, a curated dataset of over 10K articulated assets spanning 245 categories, and show its utility both for training models of articulated assets and in downstream applications such as robotics simulation and virtual reality.

AI.tldr: Proposes Articraft, an LLM-driven system that programmatically generates articulated 3D assets via a domain-specific SDK and a validation harness, enabling scalable asset creation and a 10K-artifact dataset (Articraft-10K) for robotics and VR.
AI.motivation: Addresses the data bottleneck for articulated 3D objects by leveraging LLMs to generate diverse, high-quality assets at scale, overcoming the limitations of manual creation and traditional asset generators.
AI.method: An LLM writes code using a domain-specific SDK to define parts, geometry, joints, and tests. A restricted harness validates assets and provides structured feedback, eliminating the need to handle URDF files or complex environments. The pipeline produces articulated assets automatically.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art articulated-asset generators and general coding agents in asset quality. Produces Articraft-10K, a curated dataset of over 10,000 articulated assets across 245 categories, useful for training models and downstream tasks like robotics simulation and VR.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that programmatic, LLM-guided asset generation with a validation harness can scale articulated 3D data creation, improving quality and applicability for robotics, simulation, and VR workflows.

title: Precise Verification of Transformers through ReLU-Catalyzed Abstraction Refinement
comment: 32 pages, 6 figures, the full version of the paper accepted by CAV 2026
summary: Formal verification of transformers has become increasingly important due to their widespread deployment in safety-critical applications. Compared to classic neural networks, the inferences of transformers involve highly complex computations, such as dot products in self-attention layers, rendering their verification extremely difficult. Existing approaches explored over-approximation methods by constructing convex constraints to bound the output ranges of transformers, which can achieve high efficiency. However, they may sacrifice verification precision, and consequently introduce significant approximation error that leads to frequent occurrences of false alarms. In this paper, we propose a transformer verification approach that can achieve improved precision. At the core of our approach is a novel usage of ReLU, by which we represent a precise but non-linear bound for dot products such that we can further exploit the rich body of literature for convex relaxation of ReLU to derive precise bounds. We extend two classic approaches to the context of transformers, a rule-based one and an optimization-based one, resulting in two new frameworks for efficient and precise verification. We evaluate our approaches on different model architectures and robustness properties derived from two datasets about sentiment analysis, and compare with the state-of-the-art baseline approach. Compared to the baseline, our approach can achieve significant precision improvement for most of the verification tasks with acceptable compromise of efficiency, which demonstrates the effectiveness of our approach.

AI.tldr: Proposes two new transformer verification frameworks that achieve higher precision by using a ReLU-based bound for dot products, extending a rule-based and an optimization-based approach; demonstrates precision gains with acceptable efficiency on sentiment-analysis datasets.
AI.motivation: Formal verification of transformers is essential for safety-critical applications. Existing convex-relaxation-based over-approximation methods are efficient but can be imprecise, leading to false alarms. A more precise verification method for transformers is needed.
AI.method: Introduce a novel ReLU-based representation to derive precise nonlinear bounds for dot-product computations in self-attention. Leverage the convex-relaxation literature for ReLU to obtain tight bounds. Adapt two classic verification paradigms—rule-based and optimization-based—to transformers, creating two complementary frameworks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple model architectures and sentiment-analysis datasets shows the proposed methods achieve significant precision improvements over a state-of-the-art baseline, with an acceptable trade-off in verification efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The ReLU-based bound and the two extended verification frameworks substantially improve the precision of transformer verification, validating the approach's effectiveness for robust, reliable model analysis.

title: Analogical Trajectory Transfer
summary: We study analogical trajectory transfer, where the goal is to translate motion trajectories in one 3D environment to a semantically analogous location in another. Such a capacity would enable machines to perform analogical spatial reasoning, with applications in AR/VR co-presence, content creation, and robotics. However, even semantically similar scenes can still differ substantially in object placement, scale, and layout, so naively matching semantics leads to collisions or geometric distortions. Furthermore, finding where each trajectory point should transfer to has a large search space, as the mapping must preserve semantics and functionality without tearing the trajectory apart or causing collisions. Our key insight is to decompose the problem into spatially segregated subproblems and merge their solutions to produce semantically consistent and spatially coherent transfers. Specifically, we partition scenes into object-centric clusters and estimate cross-scene mappings via hierarchical smooth map prediction, using 3D foundation model features that encode contextual information from object and open-space arrangements. We then combinatorially assemble the per-cluster maps into an initial transfer and refine the result to remove collisions and distortions, yielding a spatially coherent trajectory. Our method does not require training, attains a fast runtime around 0.6 seconds, and outperforms baselines based on LLMs, VLMs, and scene graph matching. We further showcase applications in virtual co-presence, multi-trajectory transfer, camera transfer, and human-to-robot motion transfer, which indicates the broad applicability of our work to AR/VR and robotics.

AI.tldr: A training-free method for translating motion trajectories between semantically similar 3D scenes by decomposing scenes into object-centric clusters and predicting cross-scene mappings via hierarchical smooth maps using 3D foundation-model features; merges per-cluster maps into a coherent trajectory; achieves ~0.6s runtime and outperforms LLM/VLM/scene-graph baselines; showcases AR/VR and robotics applications.
AI.motivation: Enable analogical spatial reasoning by transferring trajectories across environments that share semantics but differ in object placement, scale, and layout; naive semantic matching can cause collisions and distortions; the search space for mappings is large due to semantic/functionality preservation constraints.
AI.method: Partition scenes into object-centric clusters; estimate cross-scene mappings with hierarchical smooth map prediction; leverage 3D foundation-model features encoding object and open-space context; combinatorially assemble per-cluster maps into an initial transfer; refine to remove collisions and distortions, yielding a spatially coherent trajectory; no training required; runtime around 0.6 s.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines based on LLMs, VLMs, and scene-graph matching; demonstrates applications in virtual co-presence, multi-trajectory transfer, camera transfer, and human-to-robot motion transfer.
AI.conclusion: Spatially coherent, semantically consistent trajectory transfers can be achieved without training by decomposing the problem and leveraging context-rich 3D features; the approach is fast and broadly applicable to AR/VR and robotics.

title: Diagnosing Training Inference Mismatch in LLM Reinforcement Learning
summary: Modern LLM RL systems separate rollout generation from policy optimization. These two stages are expected to produce token probabilities that match exactly. However, implementation differences can make them assign different values to the same sequence under the same model weights, inducing Training-Inference Mismatch (TIM). TIM is difficult to inspect because it is entangled with off-policy drift and common stabilization mechanisms. In this work, we isolate TIM in a zero-mismatch diagnostic setting (VeXact), and show that small token-level numerical disagreements can independently cause training collapse. We further show that TIM changes the effective optimization problem, and identify a set of remedies that could mitigate TIM. Our results suggest that TIM is not benign numerical noise, but a systems-level perturbation that should be treated as a first-order factor in analyzing LLM RL stability.

AI.tldr: Introduces VeXact, a zero-mismatch diagnostic to isolate Training-Inference Mismatch (TIM) in LLM RL, showing that small token-level numerical disagreements can cause training collapse, TIM alters the optimization objective, and proposing remedies; TIM should be treated as a first-order factor in RL stability analyses.
AI.motivation: In modern LLM RL pipelines, rollout generation and policy optimization can produce token probabilities that diverge due to implementation differences. This TIM is intertwined with off-policy drift and stabilizing heuristics, making it hard to diagnose. A clear diagnostic for TIM is needed to understand and mitigate its impact on stability and performance.
AI.method: Develop VeXact, a zero-mismatch diagnostic setting to exactly align rollout and policy probabilities and isolate TIM. Analyze how minute token-level numerical disagreements affect training dynamics, quantify their impact on optimization, and identify remediation strategies to mitigate TIM.
AI.result: TIM is not mere numerical noise; even tiny token-level disagreements can trigger training collapse and qualitatively change the optimization objective. A set of remedies can mitigate TIM, and TIM should be treated as a first-order systemic perturbation in LLM RL stability analysis.
AI.conclusion: TIM constitutes a fundamental, systems-level perturbation in RL for LLMs. It should be accounted for in both analysis and design, rather than treated as incidental numerical error.

title: Semantic Feature Segmentation for Interpretable Predictive Maintenance in Complex Systems
comment: 18 pages, 7 figures. Under review at Neural Computing and Applications. Keywords: semantic segmentation, change point detection, fault anticipation
summary: Predictive maintenance in complex systems is often complicated by the heterogeneity and redundancy of monitored variables,which can obscure fault-relevant information and reduce model interpretability. This work proposes a semantic feature segmentation framework that decomposes the monitored feature space into a canonical component,expected to retain the dominant predictive information, and a residual component containing structurally peripheral signals. The segmentation is defined through domain informed criteria and sets up monitoring variables into functional groups reflecting operational mechanisms such as throughput,latency,pressure,network activity,and structural state. To evaluate the effectiveness of this decomposition, we adopt a predictive perspective in which expected predictive risk is used as an operational proxy for task-relevant information. Experimental results obtained through time-aware cross-validation show that the canonical space consistently achieves lower predictive risk than the residual space across multiple temporal configurations, indicating that the semantic segmentation concentrates the most relevant information for fault anticipation. In addition, the canonical segments exhibit significantly stronger intra-segment coherence than inter-segment dependence, and this structural organization remains stable after redundancy reduction. When compared with the full feature space and with a Principal Component Analysis (PCA) representation, the canonical space carries out comparable predictive performance and furthermore preserves the semantic meaning of the original variables. These findings suggest that semantic feature segmentation provides an interpretable and information-preserving decomposition of monitoring signals, enabling competitive predictive performance without sacrificing the operational interpretability required in predictive maintenance applications.

AI.tldr: Semantic feature segmentation separates monitored data into a canonical (dominant predictive) component and a residual (peripheral) component; domain-informed grouping into functional segments; canonical space shows lower predictive risk and higher intra-segment coherence, comparable to using full features or PCA while preserving semantics for interpretable predictive maintenance.
AI.motivation: Address heterogeneity/redundancy in monitoring variables that reduce interpretability and predictive performance; achieve interpretable, information-preserving decomposition without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: Define a semantic segmentation that partitions features into a canonical component and residual component using domain-informed criteria; group features into functional segments (throughput, latency, pressure, network activity, structural state); evaluate predictive risk via time-aware cross-validation; compare canonical space to full feature space and PCA; assess intra-segment coherence and robustness after redundancy reduction.
AI.result: Canonical space yields lower predictive risk than residual across temporal configurations. Canonical segments show stronger intra-segment coherence than inter-segment dependence and remain stable after redundancy reduction. Predictive performance of canonical space is comparable to full feature space and PCA, while preserving semantic meaning of original variables.
AI.conclusion: Semantic feature segmentation provides an interpretable, information-preserving decomposition that achieves competitive predictive performance with better interpretability for predictive maintenance; the canonical space concentrates the most relevant information for fault anticipation and maintains semantic interpretability of monitoring signals.

title: DT-Transformer: A Foundation Model for Disease Trajectory Prediction on a Real-world Health System
summary: Accurate disease trajectory prediction is critical for early intervention, resource allocation, and improving long-term outcomes. While electronic health records (EHRs) provide a rich longitudinal view of patient health in clinical environments, models trained on curated research cohorts may not reflect routine deployment settings, and those trained on single-hospital datasets capture only fragments of each patient's trajectory. This highlights the importance of leveraging large, multi-hospital health systems for training and validation to better reflect real-world clinical complexity. In this work, we develop DT-Transformer, a foundation model trained on 57.1M structured EHR entries over 1.7M patients from Mass General Brigham (MGB), spanning 11 hospitals and a broad network of outpatient clinics. DT-Transformer achieves strong discrimination in both held-out and prospective validation settings. Next-event prediction achieves a median age- and sex-stratified AUC of 0.871 across 896 disease categories, with all categories exceeding AUC 0.5. These results support health system-scale training as a path toward foundation models suited to real-world clinical forecasting.

AI.tldr: DT-Transformer is a foundation model trained on a large multi-hospital EHR dataset (57.1M entries, 1.7M patients across 11 hospitals) achieving strong next-event prediction with median age/sex-stratified AUC 0.871 across 896 disease categories; supports health-system scale training for real-world clinical forecasting.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between curated research cohorts and routine deployment by leveraging broad, multi-hospital data to capture complete patient trajectories and heterogeneity.
AI.method: Train a transformer-based foundation model (DT-Transformer) on structured EHR data from Mass General Brigham (11 hospitals + outpatient clinics), 57.1M entries, 1.7M patients. Evaluate with held-out and prospective validation; perform next-event prediction across 896 disease categories; report stratified AUC.
AI.result: Median AUC 0.871 in age-/sex-stratified analysis; all categories AUC > 0.5; demonstrates strong discrimination and generalizability across disease categories and real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: Health-system-scale training produces foundation models suited to real-world clinical forecasting; supports deployment in diverse clinical contexts.

title: Are Agents Ready to Teach? A Multi-Stage Benchmark for Real-World Teaching Workflows
summary: Language agents are increasingly deployed in complex professional workflows, with tutoring emerging as a particularly high-stakes capability that remains largely unmeasured in existing benchmarks. Effective tutor agents require more than producing correct answers or executing accurate tool calls: a robust tutor must diagnose learner state, adapt support over time, make pedagogically justified decisions grounded in educational evidence, and execute interventions within realistic learning-management systems. We introduce EduAgentBench, a source-grounded benchmark for holistically evaluating tutor agents across the full scope of teaching work. It contains 150 quality-controlled tasks across three capability surfaces: professional pedagogical judgment, situated multi-turn tutoring, and Canvas-style teaching workflow completion. Tasks are constructed through a pedagogical-insight-driven pipeline and evaluated with complementary verification signals and human review. Across a comprehensive evaluation of frontier models, our findings reveal that current models are generally capable of bounded pedagogical judgment, but still fall short of professional teaching standards in situated tutoring and autonomous teaching-workflow execution. To our knowledge, EduAgentBench is the first theory-grounded and realistic benchmark for evaluating the holistic teaching capability of tutor agents, providing a measurement foundation for developing future tutor agents that can support realistic teaching work.

AI.tldr: EduAgentBench is a source-grounded benchmark for holistic evaluation of tutor agents, spanning three capability surfaces—professional pedagogical judgment, situated multi-turn tutoring, and Canvas-style teaching workflow completion—across 150 tasks. Frontier models show bounded pedagogical judgment but fall short on situated tutoring and autonomous workflow execution.
AI.motivation: Assess the full scope of teaching work performed by AI tutor agents in realistic, high-stakes educational settings, addressing the gap in existing benchmarks that focus on narrow abilities.
AI.method: A pedagogical-insight-driven pipeline constructs 150 high-quality tasks evaluated via complementary verification signals and human review. Tasks cover three surfaces: professional pedagogical judgment, situated multi-turn tutoring, and LMS-like workflow completion. Evaluation is performed on frontier models to establish baselines.
AI.result: Current frontier models exhibit bounded pedagogical judgment but fail to meet professional teaching standards in situated tutoring and autonomous teaching-workflow execution; EduAgentBench provides a theory-grounded, realistic measurement framework for holistic teaching capability.
AI.conclusion: EduAgentBench is the first theory-grounded benchmark enabling evaluation of the holistic teaching capability of tutor agents, offering a foundation for developing future tutors that can support realistic teaching work.

title: SceneForge: Structured World Supervision from 3D Interventions
summary: Many multimodal learning tasks require supervision that remains consistent across edits, viewpoints, and scene-level interventions. However, such supervision is difficult to obtain from observation-level datasets, which do not expose the underlying scene state or how changes propagate through it. We present SceneForge, an intervention-driven framework that generates structured supervision from editable 3D world states. SceneForge represents each scene as a persistent world with semantic, geometric, and physical dependencies. By applying explicit interventions (e.g., object removal or camera variation) and propagating their effects through scene dependencies, SceneForge renders supervision that remains consistent with object structure and scene-level effects. This produces aligned outputs including counterfactual observations, multi-view observations, and effect-aware signals such as shadows and reflections, all derived from a shared world state rather than post hoc image-space processing. We instantiate SceneForge using Infinigen and Blender to construct a licensing-clean indoor supervision resource with a large number of counterfactual pairs and aligned annotations from over 2K scenes, covering both diverse single-view and registered multi-view settings. Under matched training budgets, incorporating SceneForge supervision improves both object removal and scene removal performance across multiple benchmarks in both quantitative and qualitative evaluation. These results indicate that modeling supervision as structured state transitions in editable worlds provides a practical and scalable foundation for intervention-consistent multimodal learning.

AI.tldr: SceneForge introduces an intervention-driven, editable-3D-world framework to generate consistent supervision for multimodal learning, yielding counterfactuals, multi-view data, and effect-aware signals, demonstrated with Infinigen/Blender on 2K indoor scenes, improving object/scene removal tasks.
AI.motivation: Observation-level datasets lack explicit underlying scene state and propagation of changes; maintaining supervision that remains consistent across edits/viewpoints requires structured, stateful supervision rooted in editable worlds.
AI.method: Represent each scene as a persistent world with semantic, geometric, and physical dependencies. Apply explicit interventions (e.g., object removal, camera variation) and propagate effects through dependencies to render supervision aligned with object structure and scene-level effects. Generate outputs such as counterfactual observations, multi-view observations, and effect-aware signals (shadows/reflections) from a shared world state. Implemented using Infinigen and Blender to produce licensing-clean indoor supervision data from ~2K scenes, covering single-view and registered multi-view settings.
AI.result: Under matched training budgets, SceneForge supervision improves object removal and scene removal performance across multiple benchmarks, with both quantitative and qualitative gains.
AI.conclusion: Modeling supervision as structured state transitions in editable worlds is a practical and scalable foundation for intervention-consistent multimodal learning.

title: AudioMosaic: Contrastive Masked Audio Representation Learning
summary: Audio self-supervised learning (SSL) aims to learn general-purpose representations from large-scale unlabeled audio data. While recent advances have been driven mainly by generative reconstruction objectives, contrastive approaches remain less explored, partly due to the difficulty of designing effective audio augmentations and the large batch sizes required for contrastive pre-training. We introduce \textbf{AudioMosaic}, a contrastive learning-based audio encoder for general audio understanding. During pre-training, AudioMosaic constructs positive pairs by applying structured time-frequency masking to spectrogram patches, which reduces memory usage and enables efficient large-batch training. Compared with generative approaches, the AudioMosaic encoder learns more discriminative utterance-level representations that demonstrate strong transferability across datasets, domains, and acoustic conditions. Extensive experiments show that AudioMosaic achieves state-of-the-art performance on several standard audio benchmarks under both linear probing and fine-tuning. We further show that integrating the pretrained AudioMosaic encoder into audio-language models improves performance on audio-language tasks. The code is publicly available in our \href{https://github.com/HanxunH/AudioMosaic}{GitHub repository}.

AI.tldr: AudioMosaic is a contrastive self-supervised learning (SSL) method for general audio understanding that forms positive pairs through structured time-frequency masking of spectrogram patches, enabling efficient large-batch training and strong transferability across datasets; it achieves state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks and improves audio-language tasks when integrated into cross-modal models; code is public.
AI.motivation: Contrastive audio SSL faces challenges in augmentation design and the need for large batch sizes; there is a demand for discriminative, utterance-level representations that transfer well across datasets, domains, and acoustic conditions.
AI.method: During pre-training, AudioMosaic applies structured time-frequency masking to spectrogram patches to create positive pairs for a contrastive objective. This approach reduces memory usage and enables efficient large-batch training. The learned encoder yields robust utterance-level representations and can be integrated into audio-language models.
AI.result: AudioMosaic achieves state-of-the-art performance on several standard audio benchmarks under both linear probing and fine-tuning. It demonstrates strong transferability across datasets, domains, and acoustic conditions. Integrating the pretrained encoder into audio-language models improves performance on audio-language tasks. Code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The work shows that a carefully designed contrastive SSL with structured masking for spectrogram patches can surpass generative approaches in learning transferable, discriminative audio representations and can benefit cross-modal audio-language tasks.

title: CrystalReasoner: Reasoning and RL for Property-Conditioned Crystal Structure Generation
summary: Generative modeling has emerged as a promising approach for crystal structure discovery. However, existing LLM-based generative models struggle with low-level atomic precision, while diffusion-based methods fall short in integrating high-level scientific knowledge. As a result, generated structures are often invalid, unstable, or do not possess desirable properties. To address this gap, we propose CrystalReasoner (\method), an end-to-end LLM framework that generates crystal structures from natural language instructions through reasoning and alignment. \method introduces physical priors as thinking tokens, which include crystallographic symmetry, local coordination environments and predicted physical properties before generating atomic coordinates. This bridges the gap between natural language and 3D structures. \method then employs reinforcement learning (RL) with a multi-objective, dense reward function to align generation with physical validity, chemical consistency, and thermodynamic stability. For property-conditioned tasks, we design task-specific reward functions and train specialized models for discrete constraints (e.g., space group) and continuous properties (e.g., elasticity, thermal expansion). Empirical results demonstrate that compared to prior works and baselines without thinking traces or RL, \method obtains better performance on diverse metrics, triples S.U.N. ratio, and achieves better performance for property conditioned generation. \method also exhibits adaptive reasoning, increasing reasoning lengths as the number of atoms increases. Our work demonstrates the potential of leveraging thinking traces and RL for generating valid, stable, and property-conditioned crystal structures. Please see our work at https://crystalreasoner.github.io/ .

AI.tldr: CrystalReasoner is an end-to-end LLM framework that generates crystal structures from natural language by incorporating thinking traces (physical priors) and reinforcement learning to ensure physical validity, chemical consistency, and thermodynamic stability, with task-specific rewards for discrete constraints and continuous properties.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between natural language instructions and 3D crystal coordinates; existing LLM-based generators lack atomic-level precision and diffusion-based methods struggle to encode high-level scientific knowledge.
AI.method: Use thinking tokens capturing crystallographic symmetry, local coordination environments, and predicted properties before generating atomic coordinates. Apply reinforcement learning with a multi-objective dense reward to align output with physical validity, chemical consistency, and thermodynamic stability. For property-conditioned tasks, employ task-specific rewards and train specialized models for discrete constraints (space group) and continuous properties (elasticity, thermal expansion). Demonstrate adaptive reasoning with increasing reasoning length as atom count grows.
AI.result: Claims improved performance over baselines lacking thinking traces or RL across multiple metrics, including S.U.N. ratio; better support for property-conditioned generation; adaptive reasoning behavior; substantive evidence via empirical evaluation and an online resource.
AI.conclusion: Thinking traces combined with RL can enable generation of valid, stable, and property-conditioned crystal structures from natural language, suggesting a promising direction for integrating domain knowledge with generative models in materials science.

title: DermAgent: A Self-Reflective Agentic System for Dermatological Image Analysis with Multi-Tool Reasoning and Traceable Decision-Making
summary: Dermatological diagnosis requires integrating fine-grained visual perception with expert clinical knowledge. Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) facilitate interactive medical image analysis, their application in dermatology is hindered by insufficient domain-specific grounding and hallucinations. To address these issues, we propose DermAgent, a collaborative multi-tool agent that orchestrates seven specialized vision and language modules within a Plan-Execute-Reflect framework. DermAgent delivers stepwise, traceable diagnostic reasoning through three core components. First, it employs complementary visual perception tools for comprehensive morphological description, dermoscopic concept annotation, and disease diagnosis. Second, to overcome the lack of domain prior, a dual-modality retrieval module anchors every prediction in external evidence by cross-referencing 413,210 diagnosed image cases and 3,199 clinical guideline chunks. To further mitigate hallucinations, a deterministic critic module conducts strict post-hoc auditing via confidence, coverage, and conflict gates, automatically detecting inter-source disagreements to trigger targeted self-correction. Extensive experiments on five dermatology benchmarks demonstrate that DermAgent consistently outperforms state-of-the-art MLLMs and medical agent baselines across zero-shot fine-grained disease diagnosis, concept annotation, and clinical captioning tasks, exceeding GPT-4o by 17.6% in skin disease diagnostic accuracy and 3.15% in captioning ROUGE-L. Our code is available at https://github.com/YizeezLiu/DermAgent.

AI.tldr: DermAgent is a collaborative multi-tool agent for dermatology that orchestrates seven vision-language modules within a Plan-Execute-Reflect framework to provide stepwise, traceable diagnostic reasoning. It combines comprehensive visual perception, dual-modality external evidence retrieval from a large diagnosed-image and guideline corpus, and a deterministic post-hoc critic to mitigate hallucinations. Empirically, it surpasses state-of-the-art MLLMs and medical baselines on five dermatology benchmarks, notably beating GPT-4o by 17.6% in skin-disease diagnostic accuracy and 3.15% in captioning ROUGE-L; code is released.
AI.motivation: To address two prominent limitations of Multimodal Large Language Models in dermatology: (1) insufficient domain grounding for fine-grained visual and clinical reasoning, and (2) hallucinations due to reliance on internal priors. The work aims to provide interpretable, evidence-grounded, and robust dermatology reasoning by modularizing perception, grounding, and auditing within a Plan-Execute-Reflect loop.
AI.method: DermAgent is a collaborative multi-tool agent operating seven specialized vision-language modules within a Plan-Execute-Reflect framework. Perception submodules perform morphological description, dermoscopic concept annotation, and disease diagnosis. A dual-modality retrieval module anchors predictions by querying 413,210 diagnosed image cases and 3,199 clinical guideline chunks. A deterministic critic module conducts post-hoc auditing using confidence, coverage, and conflict gates to detect inter-source disagreements and trigger targeted self-correction. Evaluation spans five dermatology benchmarks focusing on zero-shot fine-grained disease diagnosis, concept annotation, and clinical captioning; codebase available.
AI.result: DermAgent consistently outperforms state-of-the-art MLLMs and medical agent baselines across all targeted tasks, with a 17.6% improvement in skin disease diagnostic accuracy over GPT-4o and a 3.15% gain in captioning ROUGE-L. The architecture demonstrates strong grounding and reduced hallucinations through external evidence and deterministic auditing.
AI.conclusion: The Plan-Execute-Reflect architecture with modular perception, external evidence retrieval, and post-hoc auditing yields traceable, evidence-grounded dermatology reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple tasks and mitigating hallucinations. The approach offers a scalable blueprint for reliable medical multimodal reasoning, with code publicly available.

title: Quantum Advantage in Multi Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: We present an empirical evaluation of quantum entanglement in agent coordination within quantum multi agent reinforcement learning (QMARL). While QMARL has attracted growing interest recently, most prior work evaluates quantum policies without provable baselines, making it impossible to rigorously distinguish quantum advantage from algorithmic coincidence. We address this directly by evaluating a decentralized QMARL framework with variational quantum circuit (VQC) actors with shared entangled states. In the CHSH game, which has a mathematically proven classical performance ceiling of 0.75 win rate, we show that entangled QMARL agents approach the Tsirelson limit of 0.854, providing clear evidence of their quantum advantage. We show that unentangled quantum circuits match the classical baseline, confirming that entanglement and not the quantum circuit itself is the active coordination mechanism. We also explore the effect of specific entanglement structures, as some Bell states enable coordination gains while others actively harm performance. On cooperative navigation (CoopNav), QMARL without entanglement achieves $\sim2\times$ improvement in success rate over classical MAA2C ($\sim$0.85 versus $\sim$0.40), with the hybrid configuration, quantum actor paired with a classical centralised critic, outperforming both fully classical and fully quantum solutions. We present our experimental analysis and discuss future work.

AI.tldr: Entanglement drives quantum advantage in QMARL; CHSH results near Tsirelson bound; unentangled circuits mimic classical baselines; entanglement structure critically affects performance; hybrid quantum-classical CoopNav yields best performance.
AI.motivation: Provide provable baselines to separate quantum advantage from algorithmic artifacts and quantify the role of entanglement in coordinating agents.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation of decentralized QMARL with variational quantum circuit actors using shared entangled states; compare entangled vs unentangled circuits in CHSH game and CoopNav; test different Bell-state entanglement structures; benchmarks against classical MAA2C; include a hybrid quantum actor with classical centralized critic.
AI.result: In CHSH, entangled QMARL approaches Tsirelson limit 0.854 (vs classical 0.75). Unentangled circuits reproduce classical baseline. Some Bell states improve coordination; others harm it. In CoopNav, unentangled QMARL achieves ~0.85 vs classical ~0.40 (≈2x), while the hybrid quantum-classical configuration outperforms both fully classical and fully quantum solutions.
AI.conclusion: The study provides clear evidence that entanglement is the key active coordination mechanism in QMARL; entanglement structure critically affects performance; hybrid architectures may offer superior performance; establishes rigorous baselines and directions for architecture design and future work.

title: Herculean: An Agentic Benchmark for Financial Intelligence
summary: As AI agents improve, the central question is no longer whether they can solve isolated well-defined financial tasks, but whether they can reliably carry out financial professional work. Existing financial benchmarks offer only a partial view of this ability, as they primarily evaluate static competencies such as question answering, retrieval, summarization, and classification. We introduce Herculean, the first skilled benchmark for agentic financial intelligence spanning four representative workflows, including Trading, Hedging, Market Insights, and Auditing. Each workflow is instantiated as a standardized MCP-based skill environment with its own tools, interaction dynamics, constraints, and success criteria, enabling consistent end-to-end assessment of heterogeneous agent systems. Across frontier agents, we find agents perform relatively well on Trading and Market Insights, but struggle substantially on Hedging and Auditing, where long-horizon coordination, state consistency, and structured verification are critical. Overall, our results point to a key gap in current agents in turning financial reasoning into dependable workflow execution in high-stakes financial workflows.

AI.tldr: Introduces Herculean, the first skilled benchmark for agentic financial intelligence spanning four workflows; frontier agents perform well in Trading and Market Insights but struggle in Hedging and Auditing, highlighting gaps in long-horizon coordination and verification.
AI.motivation: To move beyond static financial benchmarks and evaluate end-to-end, dependable workflow execution by autonomous agents in high-stakes finance.
AI.method: Standardized MCP-based skill environments for four workflows (Trading, Hedging, Market Insights, Auditing) with dedicated tools, interaction dynamics, constraints, and success criteria; cross-agent evaluation.
AI.result: Across frontier agents, strong performance on Trading and Market Insights; substantial struggle on Hedging and Auditing due to needs for long-horizon coordination, state consistency, and structured verification.
AI.conclusion: There is a notable gap in turning financial reasoning into dependable, end-to-end workflow execution; future work should strengthen long-horizon planning, state management, and verification within agent architectures and benchmarks.

title: Think When Needed: Adaptive Reasoning-Driven Multimodal Embeddings with a Dual-LoRA Architecture
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have emerged as a powerful backbone for multimodal embeddings. Recent methods introduce chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning into the embedding pipeline to improve retrieval quality, but remain costly in both model size and inference cost. They typically employ separate reasoner and embedder with substantial parameter overhead, and generate CoT indiscriminately for every input. However, we observe that for simple inputs, discriminative embeddings already perform well, and redundant reasoning can even mislead the model, degrading performance. To address these limitations, we propose Think When Needed (TWN), a unified multimodal embedding framework with adaptive reasoning. TWN introduces a dual-LoRA architecture that attaches reasoning and embedding adapters to a shared frozen backbone, detaching gradients at their interface to mitigate gradient conflicts introduced by joint optimization while keeping parameters close to a single model. Building on this, an adaptive think mechanism uses a self-supervised routing gate to decide per input whether to generate CoT, skipping unnecessary reasoning to reduce inference overhead and even improve retrieval quality. We further explore embedding-guided RL to optimize CoT quality beyond supervised training. On the 78 tasks of MMEB-V2, TWN achieves state-of-the-art embedding quality while being substantially more efficient than existing generative methods, requiring only 3-5% additional parameters relative to the backbone and up to 50% fewer reasoning tokens compared to the full generative mode.

AI.tldr: Think When Needed (TWN): a unified adaptive multimodal embedding framework with dual-LoRA adapters on a frozen backbone and a self-supervised routing gate that decides per input whether to generate chain-of-thought (CoT). It achieves state-of-the-art embedding quality on MMEB-V2 with substantially fewer parameters and up to 50% fewer reasoning tokens than full generative methods.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiencies in existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) that couple large reasoners with embedders, incurring high parameter counts and inference costs due to unconditional CoT generation for all inputs.
AI.method: Attach reasoning and embedding adapters via a dual-LoRA scheme to a shared frozen backbone; detach gradients at the interface to avoid gradient conflicts; introduce a self-supervised routing gate for adaptive CoT generation; optionally apply embedding-guided reinforcement learning to improve CoT quality.
AI.result: On 78 tasks of MMEB-V2, TWN achieves state-of-the-art embedding quality while being substantially more efficient than existing generative methods, requiring only 3-5% additional parameters relative to the backbone and up to 50% fewer reasoning tokens than the full generative mode.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, selective reasoning within a unified, parameter-efficient framework yields strong embedding performance and reduced inference cost, supported by gradient-conflict mitigation via interface gradient detachment and reinforced by optional embedding-guided RL.

authors: Francisco Nattero Santiago Mauricio Barron Bucolo
title: Active Learners as Efficient PRP Rerankers
summary: Pairwise Ranking Prompting (PRP) elicits pairwise preference judgments from an LLM, which are then aggregated into a ranking, usually via classical sorting algorithms. However, judgments are noisy, order-sensitive, and sometimes intransitive, so sorting assumptions do not match the setting. Because sorting aims to recover a full permutation, truncating it to meet a call budget does not produce a dependable top-K. We thus reframe PRP reranking as active learning from noisy pairwise comparisons and show that active rankers are drop-in replacements that improve NDCG@10 per call in the call-constrained regime. Our noise-robust framework also introduces a randomized-direction oracle that uses a single LLM call per pair. This approach converts systematic position bias into zero-mean noise, enabling unbiased aggregate ranking without the cost of bidirectional calls.

AI.tldr: Active rankers for pairwise ranking prompting (PRP) improve top-K quality under call-constrained LLM usage by reframing PRP as active learning from noisy comparisons and using a single-call randomized-direction oracle to achieve unbiased rankings with fewer calls.
AI.motivation: LLM-generated pairwise judgments are noisy, order-sensitive, and sometimes intransitive; relying on full permutation sorting under strict call budgets is unreliable. A robust, cost-efficient top-K ranking method is needed.
AI.method: Introduce active rankers as drop-in replacements for sorting in PRP, recasting PRP as active learning from noisy pairwise comparisons. Develop a noise-robust framework with a randomized-direction oracle that uses a single LLM call per pair, converting systematic position bias into zero-mean noise.
AI.result: Active rankers yield improved NDCG@10 per call in the call-constrained regime compared to standard sorting-based PRP. The randomized-direction oracle enables unbiased aggregate rankings with only one LLM call per pair, reducing cost.
AI.conclusion: A noise-robust, active-learning-based PRP framework with a single-call randomized-direction oracle provides reliable top-K ranking under call constraints, mitigating bias and noise inherent in LLM pairwise judgments.

title: Uncovering the Representation Geometry of Minimal Cores in Overcomplete Reasoning Traces
summary: Language models often generate long chain-of-thought traces, but it remains unclear how much of this reasoning is necessary for preserving the final prediction. We study this through the lens of overcomplete reasoning traces: generated traces that contain more intermediate steps than are needed to support the model's answer. We define the minimal core as the smallest subset of steps that preserves either the final answer or predictive distribution, and introduce metrics for compression ratio, redundancy mass, step necessity, and necessity concentration. Across six deliberative reasoning benchmarks spanning arithmetic, competition mathematics, expert scientific reasoning, and commonsense multi-hop QA, we find substantial overcompleteness: on average, 46% of steps are removable under greedy minimal-core extraction while preserving the original answer in 86% of cases. We also find that predictive support is concentrated: the top three steps account for 65% of measured necessity mass on average. Beyond compression, minimal cores expose a cleaner geometry of reasoning: compared with full traces, they improve correct-incorrect trace separation by 11 points, reduce estimated intrinsic dimensionality by 34%, and transfer across model families with 85% off-diagonal answer retention. Theoretically, we establish existence of minimal sufficient subsets, local irreducibility guarantees for greedy elimination, and certificates of overcompleteness and sparse necessity. Together, these results suggest that full reasoning traces are often verbose and overcomplete, while minimal cores isolate the effective support underlying language-model predictions.

AI.tldr: Overcomplete reasoning traces are common in language models; minimal cores can preserve predictions while removing most steps, revealing the essential support for decisions.
AI.motivation: Quantify and understand redundancy in chain-of-thought traces to determine if full reasoning is necessary for correct predictions and how much can be compressed.
AI.method: Define a minimal core and metrics (compression ratio, redundancy mass, step necessity, necessity concentration). Evaluate six deliberative benchmarks (arithmetic, contest mathematics, expert scientific reasoning, commonsense multi-hop QA). Use greedy extraction to obtain minimal cores, analyze predictive support, trace geometry, and cross-model transfer. Provide theoretical results: existence of minimal sufficient subsets, local irreducibility guarantees, and certificates of overcompleteness and sparse necessity.
AI.result: Huge overcompleteness observed: on average 46% of steps removable while preserving the original answer in 86% of cases. Predictive support is concentrated: top three steps account for ~65% of necessity mass. Minimal cores yield cleaner trace geometry: improve correct-incorrect trace separation by 11 points, reduce intrinsic dimensionality by 34%, and retain 85% of answers across model families when transferring. Theoretically, demonstrate existence of minimal sufficient subsets, local irreducibility under greedy elimination, and certificates of overcompleteness and sparse necessity.
AI.conclusion: Full reasoning traces tend to be verbose and overcomplete; extracting minimal cores exposes the effective support behind predictions and improves interpretability and cross-model robustness.

title: ClickRemoval: An Interactive Open-Source Tool for Object Removal in Diffusion Models
summary: Existing object removal tools often rely on manual masks or text prompts, making precise removal difficult for non-expert users in complex scenes and often leading to incomplete removal or unnatural background completion. To address this issue, we present ClickRemoval, an open-source interactive object removal tool built on pretrained Stable Diffusion models and driven solely by user clicks. Without additional training, hand-drawn masks, or text descriptions, ClickRemoval localizes target objects and restores the background through self-attention modulation during denoising. Experiments show that ClickRemoval achieves competitive results across quantitative metrics and user studies. We release a complete software package at https://github.com/zld-make/ClickRemoval under the Apache-2.0 license.

AI.tldr: A click-driven, open-source object removal tool built on pretrained Stable Diffusion that localizes targets via user clicks and restores background through self-attention modulation during denoising, without extra training or masks.
AI.motivation: Make precise object removal accessible to non-experts in complex scenes, reducing incomplete removals and unnatural background completion.
AI.method: Interactive localization using user clicks; leverages pretrained Stable Diffusion; performs background restoration by self-attention modulation during denoising; no additional training, hand-drawn masks, or text prompts required.
AI.result: Competitive performance on standard quantitative metrics and favorable user studies; software released under Apache-2.0 at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Offers a practical, user-friendly solution for object removal that bypasses costly per-image annotations and training, with open-source availability enabling further development and adoption.

title: Paraphrasing Attack Resilience of Various AI-Generated Text Detection Methods
summary: The recent large-scale emergence of LLMs has left an open space for dealing with their consequences, such as plagiarism or the spread of false information on the Internet. Coupling this with the rise of AI detector bypassing tools, reliable machine-generated text detection is in increasingly high demand. We investigate the paraphrasing attack resilience of various machine-generated text detection methods, evaluating three approaches: fine-tuned RoBERTa, Binoculars, and text feature analysis, along with their ensembles using Random Forest classifiers. We discovered that Binoculars-inclusive ensembles yield the strongest results, but they also suffer the most significant losses during attacks. In this paper, we present the dichotomy of performance versus resilience in the world of AI text detection, which complicates the current perception of reliability among state-of-the-art techniques.

AI.tldr: A study assesses robustness of machine-generated text detectors against paraphrasing attacks; finds Binoculars-based ensembles most accurate but least robust.
AI.motivation: With widespread LLM usage leading to plagiarism and misinformation, there is a need to assess not just detection accuracy but resilience to adversarial paraphrasing.
AI.method: Evaluate three detection approaches—fine-tuned RoBERTa, Binoculars, and text feature analysis—and their ensembles using Random Forest classifiers, under paraphrasing attacks; compare performance and attack resilience.
AI.result: Binoculars-inclusive ensembles provide the strongest detection performance yet suffer the largest performance declines under paraphrasing attacks, revealing a strong performance-resilience trade-off.
AI.conclusion: There is a performance-vs-resilience dichotomy in AI text detection; reliability of state-of-the-art detectors is challenged by paraphrasing attacks, indicating a need for robust, attack-aware detection design and evaluation.

title: Nexus : An Agentic Framework for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Time series forecasting is not just numerical extrapolation, but often requires reasoning with unstructured contextual data such as news or events. While specialized Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) excel at forecasting based on numerical patterns, they remain unaware to real-world textual signals. Conversely, while LLMs are emerging as zero-shot forecasters, their performance remains uneven across domains and contextual grounding. To bridge this gap, we introduce Nexus, a multi-agent forecasting framework that decomposes prediction into specialized stages: isolating macro-level and micro-level temporal fluctuations, and integrating contextual information when available before synthesizing a final forecast. This decomposition enables Nexus to adapt from seasonal signals to volatile, event-driven information without relying on external statistical anchors or monolithic prompting. We show that current-generation LLMs possess substantially stronger intrinsic forecasting ability than previously recognized, depending critically on how numerical and contextual reasoning are organized. Evaluated on data strictly succeeding LLM knowledge cutoffs spanning Zillow real estate metrics and volatile stock market equities, Nexus consistently matches or outperforms state-of-the-art TSFMs and strong LLM baselines. Beyond numerical accuracy, Nexus produces high-quality reasoning traces that explicitly show the fundamental drivers behind each forecast. Our results establish that real-world forecasting is an agentic reasoning problem extending well beyond only sequence modeling.

AI.tldr: Nexus is a multi-agent forecasting framework that combines TSFMs and LLMs by decomposing forecasts into macro-level, micro-level signals and contextual information, then synthesizing a final forecast, yielding strong accuracy and interpretable reasoning traces on post-cutoff data.
AI.motivation: Real-world forecasting requires leveraging unstructured contextual data (news/events) alongside numerical patterns. TSFMs lack textual grounding, while LLMs struggle with domain grounding and integration; a hybrid, agentic approach could unify strengths without external anchors.
AI.method: Nexus decomposes prediction into stages: (1) isolate macro-level and micro-level temporal fluctuations, (2) integrate contextual information when available, (3) synthesize a final forecast. It uses current-generation LLMs and an agentic, staged reasoning process rather than monolithic prompts to ground numerical and contextual reasoning.
AI.result: On data that post-dates typical LLM knowledge cutoffs (Zillow real estate metrics and volatile stock equities), Nexus matches or exceeds state-of-the-art TSFMs and strong LLM baselines, and provides high-quality reasoning traces that reveal the drivers of each forecast.
AI.conclusion: Forecasting in the real world is an agentic reasoning problem that extends beyond sequence modeling. Proper organization of numerical and contextual reasoning enables LLMs to perform competitively when integrated in a multi-stage framework like Nexus.

title: Real2Sim in HOI: Toward Physically Plausible HOI Reconstruction from Monocular Videos
summary: Recovering 4D human-object interaction (HOI) from monocular video is a key step toward scalable 3D content creation, embodied AI, and simulation-based learning. Recent methods can reconstruct temporally coherent human and object trajectories, but these trajectories often remain visual artifacts while failing to preserve stable contact, functional manipulation, or physical plausibility when used as reference motions for humanoid-object simulation. This reveals a fundamental interaction gap: HOI reconstruction should not stop at tracking a human and an object, but should recover the relation that makes their motion a coherent interaction. We introduce $\textbf{HA-HOI}$, a framework for reconstructing physically plausible 4D HOI animation from in-the-wild monocular videos. Instead of treating the human and object as independent entities in an ambiguous monocular 3D space, we propose a $\textit{human-first, object-follow}$ formulation. The human motion is recovered as the interaction anchor, and the object is reconstructed, aligned, and refined relative to the human action. The resulting kinematic trajectory is then projected into a physics-based humanoid-object simulation, where it acts as a teacher trajectory for stable physical rollout. Across benchmark and in-the-wild videos, $\textbf{HA-HOI}$ improves human-object alignment, contact consistency, temporal stability, and simulation readiness over prior monocular HOI reconstruction methods. By moving beyond visually plausible trajectory recovery toward physically grounded interaction animation, our work takes a step toward turning general monocular HOI videos into scalable demonstrations for humanoid-object behavior. Project page: https://knoxzhao.github.io/real2sim_in_HOI/

AI.tldr: HA-HOI proposes a human-first, object-follow 4D HOI reconstruction from monocular video that yields physically plausible interaction trajectories and uses them as teacher trajectories in a physics-based humanoid-object simulator.
AI.motivation: Current monocular HOI reconstructions produce visually plausible yet physically unstable trajectories that fail to preserve stable contact, functional manipulation, or physical plausibility for humanoid-object simulation. A coherent interaction model is needed to bridge perception and physics-based control.
AI.method: Adopt a human-first, object-follow formulation: recover human motion as the interaction anchor, then reconstruct, align, and refine the object relative to the human action; project the resulting kinematic trajectory into a physics-based humanoid-object simulation where it serves as a teacher trajectory for stable physical rollout.
AI.result: HA-HOI achieves improved human-object alignment, contact consistency, temporal stability, and simulation readiness compared with prior monocular HOI methods across benchmark and in-the-wild videos.
AI.conclusion: Moving from visually plausible trajectory recovery to physically grounded interaction animation, HA-HOI enables scalable monocular-to-sim workflows for humanoid-object behavior demonstrations.

title: Latency-Quality Routing for Functionally Equivalent Tools in LLM Agents
summary: Tool-augmented LLM agents increasingly access the same tool type through multiple functionally equivalent providers, such as web-search APIs, retrievers, or LLM backends exposed behind a shared interface. This creates a provider-routing problem under runtime load: the router must choose among providers that differ in latency, reliability, and answer quality, often without gold labels at deployment time. We introduce LQM-ContextRoute, a contextual bandit router for same-function tool providers. Its key design is latency-quality matching: instead of letting low latency offset poor answers in an additive reward, the router ranks providers by expected answer quality per service cycle. It combines this capacity-aware score with query-specific quality estimation and LLM-as-judge feedback, allowing it to adapt online to both load changes and provider-quality differences. On the main web-search load benchmark, LQM-ContextRoute improves F1 by +2.18 pp over SW-UCB while staying on the latency-quality frontier. In a high-heterogeneity StrategyQA setting, LQM-ContextRoute avoids additive-reward collapse and improves accuracy by up to +18 pp over SW-UCB; on heterogeneous retriever pools, it improves NDCG by +2.91--+3.22 pp over SW-UCB. These results show that same-function tool routing benefits from treating latency as service capacity, especially when runtime pressure and provider-quality heterogeneity coexist.

AI.tldr: Proposes LQM-ContextRoute, a context-aware contextual bandit router for selecting among same-function tool providers under runtime load. It uses latency-quality matching and LLM feedback to rank providers by expected quality per service cycle, improving accuracy while staying latency-conscious across web-search, StrategyQA, and retriever pooling tasks.
AI.motivation: To solve provider-routing under runtime load where multiple functionally equivalent tools vary in latency, reliability, and answer quality, often without gold labels at deployment.
AI.method: A capacity-aware contextual bandit router that ranks same-function providers by expected answer quality per service cycle. It combines a latency-quality matching score with query-specific quality estimation and LLM-as-judge feedback to adapt online to load changes and provider quality differences.
AI.result: On main web-search load benchmark, +2.18 F1 over SW-UCB while staying on the latency-quality frontier. In StrategyQA with high heterogeneity, avoids additive-reward collapse and improves accuracy by up to +18 percentage points over SW-UCB. For heterogeneous retriever pools, NDCG improves by +2.91 to +3.22 points over SW-UCB.
AI.conclusion: Treating latency as service capacity and integrating it with quality-aware, online adaptation yields robust gains in same-function tool routing under load and provider heterogeneity.

title: Learning to Build the Environment: Self-Evolving Reasoning RL via Verifiable Environment Synthesis
summary: We pursue a vision for self-improving language models in which the model does not merely generate problems or traces to imitate, but constructs the environments that train it. In zero-data reasoning RL, this reframes self-improvement from a data-generation loop into an environment-construction loop, where each artifact is a reusable executable object that samples instances, computes references, and scores responses. Whether this vision sustains improvement hinges on a single property: the environments must exhibit stable solve--verify asymmetry, the model must be able to write an oracle once that it cannot reliably execute in natural language on fresh instances. This asymmetry takes two complementary forms. Some tasks are algorithmically hard to reason through but trivial as code: a dynamic program or graph traversal, compiled once, yields unboundedly many calibrated instances. Others are intrinsically hard to solve but easy to verify, like planted subset-sum or constraint satisfaction. Both create a durable gap between proposing and solving that the policy cannot close by gaming the verifier, and it is this gap that keeps reward informative as the learner improves. We instantiate this view in EvoEnv, a single-policy generator, solver method that synthesizes Python environments from ten seeds and admits them only after staged validation, semantic self-review, solver-relative difficulty calibration, and novelty checks. The strongest evidence comes from the already-strong regime: on Qwen3-4B-Thinking, fixed public-data RLVR and fixed hand-crafted environment RLVR reduce the average, while EvoEnv improves it from 72.4 to 74.8, a relative gain of 3.3%.
