Utilizing this pipeline, we construct a large-scale dataset, named LiWi-100k, with over 100,000 high-quality layered in-the-wild images. Second, we present a novel framework that jointly improves photometric fidelity and alpha boundary accuracy. Specifically, shadow-guided learning explicitly models the illumination effects, and degradation-restoration objective provides boundary-correction supervision by recovering clean foreground image from degraded one. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art (SoTA) performance in natural image decomposition, outperforming existing models in RGB L1 and Alpha IoU metrics. We will soon release our code and dataset.

AI.tldr: Proposes an Agent-driven Data Decomposition (ADD) pipeline to synthesize large-scale layered natural images and a shadow-guided, boundary-aware decomposition framework that improves photometric fidelity and alpha boundaries, achieving SoTA on natural image decomposition (RGB L1, Alpha IoU) with LiWi-100k dataset.
AI.motivation: Layered data for natural images is underexplored due to scalability and modeling of object interactions (illumination, boundaries). A scalable data synthesis pipeline and a boundary-aware decomposition method are needed to enable fine-grained editing in real-world images.
AI.method: 1) Agent-driven Data Decomposition (ADD) to synthesize layered data without manual intervention, yielding LiWi-100k (100k+ layered in-the-wild images). 2) A joint framework with shadow-guided learning to model illumination effects and a degradation-restoration objective to supervise boundary correction by recovering a clean foreground from degraded input.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in natural image decomposition, outperforming existing models on RGB L1 and Alpha IoU metrics. LiWi-100k dataset introduced; code and dataset will be released.
AI.conclusion: The approach advances natural image decomposition by combining scalable synthetic layered data with a boundary-aware, photometrically faithful decomposition method, enabling improved editing of real-world images and broader applications.

title: Minimal-Intervention KV Retention: A Design-Space Study and a Diversity-Penalty Survivor
comment: 12 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/libophd/minimal-kv-retention
summary: KV-cache compression at small budgets is a crowded design space spanning cache representation, head-wise routing, compression cadence, decoding behavior, and within-budget scoring. We study seven mechanisms across these five families under matched mean cache on long-form mathematical reasoning (MATH-500~\cite{hendrycks2021math}) with two distilled-reasoning models (Qwen-7B and Llama-8B variants of DeepSeek-R1-Distill~\cite{deepseek2025r1}) at budgets $b \in \{64, 128\}$. All seven were rejected. We then propose $α$, a one-function modification to the TriAttention~\cite{mao2026triattention} retention scorer that replaces argmax-top-$k$ with greedy facility-location-inspired selection under a V-space redundancy penalty controlled by a single weight $λ$. A pre-registered protocol tunes $λ$ on a frozen development split and confirms on a disjoint held-out split; with $λ= 0.5$, $α$ clears Bonferroni on two of the four (model, budget) cells (Qwen $b{=}128$ and Llama $b{=}64$), no cell is significantly negative, and the pre-registered Branch~A triggers. The finding is asymmetric: a minimal scoring modification beat heavier structural redesigns in this regime, and the combined matched-memory, sympy-graded, held-out confirmation protocol is the evidence standard that made the asymmetry visible.

AI.tldr: A minimal one-function modification to the TriAttention retention scorer (α) outperforms heavier KV-cache redesigns under tight memory budgets (64/128) on MATH-500, validated by a pre-registered, held-out protocol; two model-budget cells show significant gains under Bonferroni, revealing an asymmetric advantage for scoring tweaks over structural changes.
AI.motivation: KV-cache compression at small budgets is a crowded design space spanning cache representation, head-wise routing, compression cadence, decoding behavior, and within-budget scoring. The study seeks to identify whether minor, well-validated scoring adjustments can outperform broader architectural redesigns under matched memory usage.
AI.method: Evaluate seven mechanisms across five families under matched memory on MATH-500 with two distilled-reasoning models (Qwen-7B and Llama-8B variants of DeepSeek-R1-Distill) at budgets b in {64,128}. Propose α, a one-function modification to the TriAttention retention scorer, replacing argmax-top-k with greedy facility-location-inspired selection under a V-space redundancy penalty controlled by a single weight λ. Use a pre-registered protocol that tunes λ on a frozen development split and confirms on a disjoint held-out split. Claim significance via a Bonferroni-corrected test across four (model, budget) cells.
AI.result: All seven baseline mechanisms were rejected. With α at λ=0.5, Bonferroni-corrected significance is achieved in two of four cells (Qwen at 128 and Llama at 64); no cell is significantly negative, and the pre-registered Branch A triggers.
AI.conclusion: A minimal scoring modification can outperform heavier architectural redesigns in constrained-memory regimes. The combination of matched-memory, sympy-graded evaluation, and held-out confirmation constitutes a robust evidence standard that revealed this asymmetry.

title: OmniDrop: Layer-wise Token Pruning for Omni-modal LLMs via Query-Guidance
summary: Omni-modal large language models have demonstrated remarkable potential in holistic multimodal understanding; however, the token explosion caused by high-resolution audio and video inputs remains a critical bottleneck for real-time applications and long-form reasoning. Existing omni-modal token compression methods typically prune tokens at the input embedding level, relying on audio-video similarity or temporal co-occurrence as proxies for semantic relevance. In practice, such assumptions are often unreliable. To address this limitation, we propose OmniDrop, a training-free, layer-wise token pruning framework that progressively prunes audiovisual tokens within the LLM decoder layers rather than at the input-level, allowing early layers to preserve sufficient omni-modal information fusion before aggressively removing tokens in deeper layers. We further utilize text queries as guidance for modality-agnostic and task-adaptive token pruning. We also introduce a temporal diversity score that encourages balanced token survival to preserve global temporal context. Experimental results across various audiovisual benchmarks demonstrate that OmniDrop outperforms all baselines by up to 3.58 points while reducing prefill latency by up to 40% and memory usage by up to 14.7%.

AI.tldr: OmniDrop proposes a training-free, layer-wise token pruning approach for omni-modal LLMs that prunes audiovisual tokens during decoding guided by text queries and a temporal diversity score, enabling strong latency and memory reductions with competitive accuracy gains across audiovisual benchmarks.
AI.motivation: High-resolution audio/video causes token explosion in omni-modal LLMs, hindering real-time and long-form reasoning. Input-level pruning based on audio-video similarity is unreliable, motivating a method that preserves multi-modal fusion in early layers while aggressively pruning later.
AI.method: Progressive, layer-wise token pruning inside the LLM decoder layers (not input embedding). Uses text queries as guidance for modality-agnostic, task-adaptive pruning. Introduces a temporal diversity score to encourage balanced token survival and preserve global temporal context. Training-free workflow.
AI.result: Across audiovisual benchmarks, OmniDrop outperforms baselines by up to 3.58 points and reduces prefill latency by up to 40% and memory usage by up to 14.7%.
AI.conclusion: A lightweight, effective solution to omni-modal token explosion, enabling faster and more memory-efficient processing while maintaining or improving performance. Potential areas for further validation include robustness across tasks, ablation studies, and generalization to broader modality combinations.

title: SpectraFlow: Unifying Structural Pretraining and Frequency Adaptation for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Medical image segmentation remains challenging in low-data regimes, where scarce annotations often yield poor generalization and ambiguous boundaries with missing fine structures. Recent self-supervised pretraining has improved transferability, but it often exhibits a texture bias. In contrast, accurate segmentation is inherently geometry-aware and depends on both topological consistency and precise boundary preservation. To address this problem, we propose a two-stage framework that couples structure-aware encoder pretraining with boundary-oriented decoding. In Stage-1, we aim to learn structure-aware representations for downstream segmentation in low-data regimes. To this end, we propose Mixed-Domain MeanFlow Pretraining, which aligns images and binary masks in a shared latent space through latent transport regression, where masks act as conditional structural guidance rather than prediction targets, making the pretraining task-agnostic. To further improve training stability under scarce supervision, we incorporate a lightweight Dispersive Loss to prevent representation collapse. In Stage-2, we fine-tune the pretrained encoder with a lightweight decoder that combines Direct Attentional Fusion for adaptive cross-scale gating and Frequency-Directional Dynamic Convolution for high-frequency boundary refinement under appearance variation. Experiments on ISIC-2016, Kvasir-SEG, and GlaS demonstrate consistent gains over state-of-the-art methods, with improved robustness in low-data settings and sharper boundary delineation.

AI.tldr: Two-stage framework for medical image segmentation in low-data regimes: Stage-1 structure-aware encoder pretraining with Mixed-Domain MeanFlow Pretraining and Dispersive Loss; Stage-2 a lightweight decoder with Direct Attentional Fusion and Frequency-Directional Dynamic Convolution for boundary refinement. Demonstrates gains on ISIC-2016, Kvasir-SEG, GlaS, with improved robustness and sharper boundaries.
AI.motivation: Medical image segmentation struggles in low-data settings due to scarce annotations, leading to poor generalization and ambiguous boundaries. Self-supervised pretraining helps transferability but suffers texture bias; accurate segmentation requires geometry-aware representations and precise boundary preservation.
AI.method: Stage-1: Mixed-Domain MeanFlow Pretraining that aligns images and binary masks in a shared latent space via latent transport regression; masks provide conditional structural guidance, not direct targets; light-weight Dispersive Loss to prevent representation collapse. Stage-2: lightweight decoder with Direct Attentional Fusion for adaptive cross-scale gating and Frequency-Directional Dynamic Convolution for high-frequency boundary refinement under appearance variation.
AI.result: Experiments on ISIC-2016, Kvasir-SEG, and GlaS show consistent gains over state-of-the-art methods, with improved robustness in low-data regimes and sharper boundary delineation.
AI.conclusion: A two-stage framework effectively couples structure-aware encoder pretraining with boundary-oriented decoding, yielding improved segmentation performance and boundary accuracy in low-data settings.

title: Policy Optimization in Hybrid Discrete-Continuous Action Spaces via Mixed Gradients
summary: We study reinforcement learning in hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces, such as settings where the discrete component selects a regime (or index) and the continuous component optimizes within it -- a structure common in robotics, control, and operations problems. Standard model-free policy gradient methods rely on score-function (SF) estimators and suffer from severe credit-assignment issues in high-dimensional settings, leading to poor gradient quality. On the other hand, differentiable simulation largely sidesteps these issues by backpropagating through a simulator, but the presence of discrete actions or non-smooth dynamics yields biased or uninformative gradients. To address this, we propose Hybrid Policy Optimization (HPO), which backpropagates through the simulator wherever smoothness permits, using a mixed gradient estimator that combines pathwise and SF gradients while maintaining unbiasedness. We also show how problems with action discontinuities can be reformulated in hybrid form, further broadening its applicability. Empirically, HPO substantially outperforms PPO on inventory control and switched linear-quadratic regulator problems, with performance gaps increasing as the continuous action dimension grows. Finally, we characterize the structure of the mixed gradient, showing that its cross term -- which captures how continuous actions influence future discrete decisions -- becomes negligible near a discrete best response, thereby enabling approximate decentralized updates of the continuous and discrete components and reducing variance near optimality. All resources are available at github.com/MatiasAlvo/hybrid-rl.

AI.tldr: Introduces Hybrid Policy Optimization (HPO) for hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces, combining pathwise (gradient through differentiable simulator) and score-function estimators to provide an unbiased mixed gradient, with improved performance over PPO on inventory control and switched LQR tasks, and a theoretical decomposition showing the cross-term vanishes near a discrete best response, enabling near-decentralized updates.
AI.motivation: Address credit-assignment and gradient quality challenges in hybrid action settings by leveraging differentiable simulation where possible while retaining unbiasedness when discreteness or non-smooth dynamics are present.
AI.method: HPO backpropagates through the simulator in smooth regions and combines pathwise and SF gradients into a unified, unbiased mixed gradient. It also reformulates problems with action discontinuities into a hybrid form and analyzes the gradient structure, showing the cross-term becomes negligible near a discrete BR; empirical evaluation on inventory control and switched LQR compares against PPO; code is released.
AI.result: Empirically, HPO substantially outperforms PPO on the tested tasks, with larger gains as the continuous action dimension grows. Theoretical analysis shows the cross-term in the mixed gradient diminishes near a discrete best response, supporting approximate decentralized updates and variance reduction near optima.
AI.conclusion: HPO provides a principled, unbiased gradient framework for hybrid action problems by exploiting smooth dynamics via differentiable simulation while properly handling discrete decisions, with strong empirical gains and a clear path to scalable, decentralized updates; applicability is broad and supported by the accompanying codebase.

title: From Table to Cell: Attention for Better Reasoning with TABALIGN
summary: Multi-step LLM reasoning over structured tables fails because planning and execution share no explicit cell-grounding contract. Existing methods constrain the planner to a left-to-right factorization at odds with table permutation invariance, and score intermediate states by generated content alone, overlooking cell grounding. We conduct a pilot study showing that diffusion language models (DLMs) produce more human-aligned and permutation-stable cell attention on tables than autoregressive models, with a 40.2% median reduction in attention-AUROC variability under row reordering. Motivated by this, we propose TABALIGN, a planned table reasoning framework that operationalizes the contract. TABALIGN pairs a masked DLM planner, whose bidirectional denoising emits plan steps as binary cell masks, with TABATTN, a lightweight verifier trained on 1,600 human-verified attention standards to score each step by its attention overlap with the plan-designated mask. Across eight benchmarks covering table question answering and fact verification, TABALIGN improves average accuracy by 15.76 percentage points over the strongest open-source baseline at comparable 8B-class scale, with a matched-backbone ablation attributing 2.87 percentage points of this gain to the DLM planner over an AR planner on a fixed reasoner. Cleaner DLM plans also accelerate downstream reasoning execution by 44.64%.

AI.tldr: TABALIGN is a planned table reasoning framework using a masked diffusion LLM planner and a cell-attention verifier (TABATTN). It improves multi-step table reasoning by enforcing a table-grounded plan via binary cell masks, achieving stronger accuracy and permutation-stability than autoregressive planners, with substantial downstream speedups.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of explicit cell-grounding contracts in multi-step LLM table reasoning, and to overcome permutation sensitivity from left-to-right planners that ignore cell-level grounding.
AI.method: A masked DLM planner emits plan steps as binary cell masks through bidirectional denoising. TABATTN is a lightweight verifier trained on 1,600 human-verified attention standards to score each step by the overlap of its attention with the designated mask. The system is evaluated on eight benchmarks spanning table QA and fact verification; ablations compare DLM planner vs AR planner on fixed reasoners.
AI.result: TABALIGN improves average accuracy by 15.76 percentage points over the strongest open-source baseline at comparable 8B-class scale. A matched-backbone ablation attributes 2.87 percentage points of this gain to the DLM planner over an AR planner. Cleaner DLM plans accelerate downstream reasoning by 44.64%.
AI.conclusion: TABALIGN provides a better contract for planner-executor interaction in table reasoning; diffusion LLMs deliver more stable, human-aligned cell grounding, yielding substantial accuracy gains and faster downstream execution.

title: Bridging Brain and Semantics: A Hierarchical Framework for Semantically Enhanced fMRI-to-Video Reconstruction
summary: Reconstructing dynamic visual experiences as videos from functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) is pivotal for advancing the understanding of neural processes. However, current fMRI-to-video reconstruction methods are hindered by a semantic gap between noisy fMRI signals and the rich content of videos, stemming from a reliance on incomplete semantic embeddings that neither capture video-specific cues (e.g., actions) nor integrate prior knowledge. To this end, we draw inspiration from the dual-pathway processing mechanism in human brain and introduce CineNeuron, a novel hierarchical framework for semantically enhanced video reconstruction from fMRI signals with two synergistic stages. First, a bottom-up semantic enrichment stage maps fMRI signals to a rich embedding space that comprehensively captures textual semantics, image contents, action concepts, and object categories. Second, a top-down memory integration stage utilizes the proposed Mixture-of-Memories method to dynamically select relevant "memories" from previously seen data and fuse them with the fMRI embedding to refine the video reconstruction. Extensive experimental results on two fMRI-to-video benchmarks demonstrate that CineNeuron surpasses state-of-the-art methods across various metrics.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical CineNeuron framework reconstructs dynamic videos from fMRI using bottom-up semantic enrichment and top-down memory integration (Mixture-of-Memories), achieving state-of-the-art results on two benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To bridge the semantic gap between noisy fMRI signals and rich video content by leveraging comprehensive semantic embeddings and memory-augmented reconstruction, inspired by dual-pathway brain processing.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: (1) bottom-up semantic enrichment that maps fMRI to a rich embedding space capturing text semantics, image content, actions, and object categories; (2) top-down memory integration via Mixture-of-Memories that dynamically selects relevant memories from prior data and fuses them with the fMRI embedding to refine video reconstructions.
AI.result: CineNeuron demonstrates superior performance over state-of-the-art methods on two fMRI-to-video benchmarks across multiple metrics.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively narrows the semantic gap and improves video reconstruction by combining richer semantic embeddings with memory-augmented fusion, illustrating the value of dual-pathway processing in fMRI-to-video reconstruction.

title: Language-Induced Priors for Domain Adaptation
summary: Domain adaptation faces a fundamental paradox in the cold-start regime. When target data is scarce, statistical methods fail to distinguish relevant source domains from irrelevant ones, which often leads to negative transfer. In this paper, we address this challenge by leveraging expert textual descriptions of the target domain, a resource that is often available but overlooked. We propose a probabilistic framework that translates these semantic descriptions into a choice model, namely a Language-Induced Prior (LIP), that learns the preferences from a pretrained Large Language Model (LLM). The LIP is then integrated into an Expectation-Maximization algorithm to identify source relevance. Methodologically, this framework is compatible with any parametric model where a likelihood is available. It allows the LIP to guide the selection of sources when target signals are weak, while gradually refining these choices as samples accumulate. Theoretically, we prove that the estimator roughly matches an oracle cold-start MSE under a correct prior, while remaining asymptotically consistent regardless of the quality of the LIP. Empirically, we validated the framework on a descriptive (Gaussian estimation), a predictive (C-MAPSS dataset), and a prescriptive task (MuJoCo hopper).

AI.tldr: Language-Induced Prior (LIP) translates expert textual descriptions of the target domain into a prior over source-domain relevance, learned from a pretrained LLM and integrated into EM to select sources in cold-start domain adaptation; proven to be oracle-like under a correct prior and asymptotically consistent, with empirical validation on Gaussian estimation, C-MAPSS, and MuJoCo Hopper.
AI.motivation: In the cold-start regime, scarce target data makes it hard to distinguish relevant from irrelevant source domains, causing negative transfer. Expert textual descriptions of the target domain provide a rich semantic signal that can guide source selection.
AI.method: Define a probabilistic framework that converts semantic target-domain descriptions into a Language-Induced Prior (LIP) learned from a pretrained Large Language Model. Integrate LIP into an Expectation-Maximization algorithm to identify source relevance. The framework is model-agnostic (any parametric likelihood) and allows LIP-guided source selection, refined as target samples accumulate.
AI.result: Theoretical: the estimator matches an oracle cold-start MSE when the prior is correct, and remains asymptotically consistent regardless of LIP quality. Empirical: validated on descriptive (Gaussian estimation), predictive (C-MAPSS), and prescriptive (MuJoCo Hopper) tasks.
AI.conclusion: Semantic priors derived from expert textual descriptions can effectively guide source selection in cold-start domain adaptation, yielding theoretical guarantees and practical improvements across tasks, with broad compatibility to likelihood-based models.

title: Med-DisSeg: Dispersion-Driven Representation Learning for Fine-Grained Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Accurate medical image segmentation is fundamental to precision medicine, yet robust delineation remains challenging under heterogeneous appearances, ambiguous boundaries, and large anatomical variability. Similar intensity and texture patterns between targets and surrounding tissues often lead to blurred activations and unreliable separation. We attribute these failures to representation collapse during encoding and insufficient fine grained multi scale decoding. To address these issues, we propose Med DisSeg, a dispersion driven medical image segmentation framework that jointly improves representation learning and anatomical delineation. Med DisSeg combines a lightweight Dispersive Loss with adaptive attention for fine grained structure segmentation. The Dispersive Loss enlarges inter sample margins by treating in batch hidden representations as negative pairs, producing well dispersed and boundary aware embeddings with negligible overhead. Based on these enhanced representations, the encoder strengthens structure sensitive responses, while the decoder performs adaptive multi scale calibration to preserve complementary local texture and global shape information. Extensive experiments on five datasets spanning three imaging modalities demonstrate consistent state of the art performance. Moreover, Med DisSeg achieves competitive results on multi organ CT segmentation, supporting its robustness and cross task applicability.

AI.tldr: Med DisSeg introduces a dispersive loss with adaptive attention to improve representation learning and multi-scale decoding for robust medical image segmentation, achieving state-of-the-art on five datasets across three modalities and competitive results on multi-organ CT segmentation.
AI.motivation: Accurate medical image segmentation is hampered by heterogeneous appearances, ambiguous boundaries, and large anatomical variability. Similar intensity/texture patterns between targets and surrounding tissues lead to blurred activations and unreliable separation. Current encoding/decoding suffers from representation collapse and insufficient fine-grained multi-scale decoding.
AI.method: A lightweight Dispersive Loss that treats in-batch hidden representations as negative pairs to enlarge inter-sample margins, yielding dispersed, boundary-aware embeddings. Adaptive attention guides fine-grained structure segmentation. The encoder emphasizes structure-sensitive responses, while the decoder performs adaptive multi-scale calibration to preserve local textures and global shapes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five datasets spanning three imaging modalities show consistent state-of-the-art performance. Med DisSeg also achieves competitive results on multi-organ CT segmentation, indicating robustness and cross-task applicability.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively enhances representation learning and delineation through a simple, efficient dispersive loss and adaptive decoding, enabling robust segmentation across modalities and tasks with broad applicability; potential for further ablations and efficiency considerations.

title: Matrix-Space Reinforcement Learning for Reusing Local Transition Geometry
summary: Compositional generalization in sequential decision-making requires identifying which parts of prior rollouts remain useful for new tasks. Existing methods reuse skills or predictive models, but often overlook rich local transition geometry and dynamics. We propose Matrix-Space Reinforcement Learning (MSRL), a geometric abstraction that represents trajectory segments through positive semidefinite matrix descriptors aggregating first- and second-order statistics of lifted one-step transitions. These descriptors expose shared hidden structure, support algebraic composition in an abstract matrix space, and reveal opportunities for transfer. We prove that the descriptor is well defined up to coordinate gauge, complete for the induced low-order additive signal class, additive under valid segment composition, and minimally sufficient among admissible additive descriptors. We further show that conditioning value functions on the trajectory-segment matrix yields a first-order smooth approximation of action values, enabling source-learned matrix-to-value mappings to bootstrap learning in new tasks. MSRL is plug-in compatible with standard model-free and model-based methods, while obstruction filtering rejects implausible compositions. Empirically, MSRL achieves the best average finite-budget target AUC of 0.73, outperforming MSRL from scratch (0.65), TD-MPC-PT+FT (0.63), and TD-MPC (0.57).

AI.tldr: MSRL presents a PSD-matrix descriptor framework for trajectory segments in reinforcement learning, enabling compositional generalization and transfer through algebra in matrix space, with a first-order value approximation and competitive finite-budget performance.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches often reuse skills or predictive models but ignore rich local transition geometry. A geometric, compositional descriptor is proposed to reveal shared structure and facilitate transfer across tasks.
AI.method: Define positive semidefinite (PSD) matrix descriptors aggregating first- and second-order statistics of lifted one-step transitions to capture local dynamics. Use algebraic composition in matrix space, with coordinate gauge invariance. Prove properties: well-defined up to gauge, complete for low-order additive signals, additive under valid segment composition, minimally sufficient among admissible additive descriptors. Condition value functions on the trajectory-segment matrix to obtain a first-order smooth approximation of action values, enabling source-learned matrix-to-value mappings to bootstrap learning. MSRL is plug-in compatible with standard model-free and model-based methods, and includes obstruction filtering to reject implausible compositions.
AI.result: The descriptor is well-defined up to coordinate gauge, complete for the induced low-order additive signal class, additive under valid segment composition, and minimally sufficient among admissible additive descriptors. Conditioning value functions on the trajectory-segment matrix yields a first-order smooth approximation of action values. Empirically, MSRL achieves the best average finite-budget target AUC of 0.73, outperforming MSRL from scratch (0.65), TD-MPC-PT+FT (0.63), and TD-MPC (0.57).
AI.conclusion: MSRL offers a principled geometric abstraction for compositional generalization in RL, enabling transfer via matrix-to-value mappings and robust composition with obstruction filtering; it improves finite-budget performance and is compatible with existing RL methods.

title: Deepchecks: Evaluating Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) augmented with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) techniques are revolutionizing applications across multiple domains, such as healthcare, finance, and customer service. Despite their potential, evaluating RAG systems remains a complex challenge due to the stochastic nature of generated outputs and the intricate interplay between retrieval and generation components. This paper introduces Deepchecks, a comprehensive framework tailored for evaluating RAG applications. Deepchecks' evaluation framework addresses RAG applications evaluation through a multi-faceted approach, root cause analysis and production monitoring. By ensuring alignment with application-specific requirements, Deepchecks framework provides a robust foundation for assessing reliability, relevance, and user satisfaction in RAG systems.

AI.tldr: Deepchecks introduces a framework for evaluating RAG systems focusing on reliability, relevance, and user satisfaction, incorporating multi-faceted evaluation, root-cause analysis, and production monitoring to align with application-specific requirements.
AI.motivation: RAG systems' stochastic outputs and complex retrieval-generation coupling make evaluation challenging; there is a need for robust, production-aware evaluation tools.
AI.method: Proposes Deepchecks, a comprehensive evaluation framework for RAG applications that provides a multi-faceted evaluation suite, root-cause analysis, and production monitoring, with emphasis on alignment to application-specific requirements.
AI.result: The abstract presents the framework concept; no empirical results or benchmarks are reported.
AI.conclusion: Deepchecks offers a robust foundation for assessing reliability, relevance, and user satisfaction in RAG systems and supports production-grade deployment.

title: A Picture is Worth a Thousand Words? An Empirical Study of Aggregation Strategies for Visual Financial Document Retrieval
summary: Visual RAG has offered an alternative to traditional RAG. It treats documents as images and uses vision encoders to obtain vision patch tokens. However, hundreds of patch tokens per document create retrieval and storage challenges in a vector database. Practical deployment requires aggregating them into a single vector. This raises a critical question: does single-vector aggregation lose key information in financial documents? We develop a diagnostic benchmark using financial documents where changes in single digits can lead to significant semantic shifts. Our experiments show that single-vector aggregation collapses different documents with almost identical vectors. Metrics show that the patch level detects semantic changes, and confirm that aggregation obscures these details. We identify global texture dominance as the root cause. Our findings are consistent across model scales, retrieval-optimized embeddings, and multiple mitigation strategies, highlighting significant risks for single-vector visual document retrieval in financial applications.

AI.tldr: Single-vector aggregation in visual RAG for financial documents loses digit-level semantic changes; patch-level signals remain discriminative whereas aggregation collapses distinct documents, driven by global texture dominance; robust across scales and mitigations, indicating serious risk for single-vector retrieval.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether aggregating hundreds of vision patch tokens into a single vector preserves essential semantic information in financial documents, where small digit changes can alter meaning and decisions.
AI.method: Construct a diagnostic benchmark with financial documents highlighting digit-level semantic shifts. Compare patch-level representations to single-vector aggregation. Test across model scales, retrieval-optimized embeddings, and mitigation strategies. Analyze cause, notably global texture dominance, and report robustness of findings across conditions.
AI.result: Single-vector aggregation collapses different documents into nearly identical vectors; patch-level representations detect semantic changes. Aggregation obscures these details. Global texture dominance identified as the root cause. Findings hold across model scales, retrieval-optimized embeddings, and multiple mitigation strategies.
AI.conclusion: Single-vector visual document retrieval poses significant risks for financial applications. Multi-vector or patch-aware aggregation approaches are recommended to preserve critical digit-level semantics, with ongoing evaluation across model scales and embeddings.

title: Beyond Binary: Reframing GUI Critique as Continuous Semantic Alignment
comment: 28 pages including appendix. Code and BBBench benchmark to be released
summary: Test-Time Scaling (TTS), which samples multiple candidate actions and ranks them via a Critic Model, has emerged as a promising paradigm for generalist GUI agents. Its efficacy thus hinges on the critic's fine-grained ranking ability. However, existing GUI critic models uniformly adopt binary classification. Our motivational analysis of these models exposes a severe entanglement: scores for valid actions and plausible-but-invalid distractors become indistinguishable. We attribute this failure to two structural defects: Affordance Collapse--the hierarchical affordance space is compressed into 0/1 labels; and Noise Sensitivity--binary objectives overfit to noisy decision boundaries. To resolve this, we introduce BBCritic (Beyond-Binary Critic), a paradigm shift grounded in the Functional Equivalence Hypothesis. Through two-stage contrastive learning, BBCritic aligns instructions and actions in a shared Affordance Space, recovering the hierarchical structure that binary supervision flattens. We also present BBBench (Beyond-Binary Bench), the first GUI critic benchmark that pairs a dense action space with a hierarchical four-level taxonomy, enabling fine-grained ranking evaluation. Experimental results show that BBCritic-3B, trained without any extra annotation, outperforms 7B-parameter SOTA binary models. It demonstrates strong zero-shot transferability across platforms and tasks, supporting our methodological view: GUI critique is fundamentally a metric-learning problem, not a classification one.

AI.tldr: BBCritic uses two-stage contrastive learning to move GUI critic from binary classification to metric learning, recovering a hierarchical affordance space and enabling fine-grained ranking; introduces BBBench; BBCritic-3B outperforms 7B binary SOTA and shows strong zero-shot transfer.
AI.motivation: Existing GUI critics rely on binary labels, suffering Affordance Collapse (flattened hierarchical affordances) and Noise Sensitivity (overfitting to noisy decision boundaries). A metric-learning approach can preserve and utilize the hierarchical affordance structure for finer action ranking.
AI.method: Two-stage contrastive learning under the Functional Equivalence Hypothesis to align instructions and actions in a shared Affordance Space; leverages a dense action space with a four-level hierarchical taxonomy (BBBench); builds BBCritic-3B with no extra annotation.
AI.result: BBCritic-3B surpasses 7B-state-of-the-art binary models; demonstrates strong zero-shot transfer across platforms and tasks; supports view that GUI critique is a metric-learning problem rather than pure classification.
AI.conclusion: Recasting GUI critique as metric learning with a hierarchical affordance taxonomy resolves Affordance Collapse and Noise Sensitivity, enabling fine-grained ranking and better generalization; the BBBench benchmark is a crucial resource for evaluating such models.

title: Learning Scenario Reduction for Two-Stage Robust Optimization with Discrete Uncertainty
summary: Two-Stage Robust Optimization (2RO) with discrete uncertainty is challenging, often rendering exact solutions prohibitive. Scenario reduction alleviates this issue by selecting a small, representative subset of scenarios to enable tractable computation. However, existing methods are largely problem-agnostic, operating solely on the uncertainty set without consulting the feasible region or recourse structure. In this paper, we introduce PRISE, a problem-driven sequential lookahead heuristic that constructs reduced scenario sets by evaluating the marginal impact of each scenario. While PRISE yields high-quality scenario subsets, each selection step requires solving multiple subproblems, making it computationally expensive at scale. To address this, we propose NeurPRISE, a neural surrogate model built on a GNN-Transformer backbone that encodes the per-scenario structure via graph convolution and captures cross-scenario interactions through attention. NeurPRISE is trained via imitation learning with a gain-aware ranking objective, which distills marginal gain information from PRISE into a learned scoring function for scenario ranking and selection. Extensive results on three 2RO problems show that NeurPRISE consistently achieves competitive regret relative to comprehensive methods, maintains strong calability with varying numbers of scenarios, and delivers 7-200x speedup over PRISE. NeurPRISE also exhibits strong zero-shot generalization, effectively handling instances with larger problem scales (up to 5x), more scenarios (up to 4x), and distribution shifts.

AI.tldr: PRISE is a problem-driven lookahead method for scenario reduction in 2-stage robust optimization; NeurPRISE is a GNN-Transformer surrogate that imitates PRISE to enable fast, scalable, and generalizable scenario selection, achieving large speedups with competitive quality.
AI.motivation: Discretely uncertain 2RO is computationally hard; existing scenario reduction is largely problem-agnostic and ignores the recourse structure and cross-scenario interactions; a problem-driven, scalable reduction is needed.
AI.method: PRISE constructs reduced scenario sets via sequential lookahead evaluating the marginal impact of each scenario. NeurPRISE trains a neural surrogate (GNN-Transformer) to predict per-scenario rankings, using imitation learning with a gain-aware ranking objective to distill PRISE's marginal gains.
AI.result: Three 2RO problems show NeurPRISE achieves competitive regret relative to full methods, scales well with more scenarios, and delivers 7–200x speedups over PRISE; exhibits strong zero-shot generalization to larger instances, more scenarios, and distribution shifts.
AI.conclusion: A successful problem-driven, neural-guided approach to scenario reduction for discrete-uncertainty 2RO, delivering substantial speedups and robust generalization while preserving solution quality.

title: FedStain: Modeling Higher-Order Stain Statistics for Federated Domain Generalization in Computational Pathology
summary: Robust whole-slide image (WSI) analysis under strict data-governance remains challenging due to substantial cross-institutional stain heterogeneity. Domain generalization (DG) mitigates these shifts but typically requires centralized data, conflicting with privacy regulations. Federated learning (FedL) provides a decentralized alternative; however, existing FedL and federated DG (FedDG) approaches rely almost exclusively on low-order statistics, assuming Gaussian-like stain distributions. In contrast, real-world staining processes often produce asymmetric, heavy-tailed color distributions due to biochemical diffusion and scanner nonlinearity. Consequently, current methods fail to model the higher-order, non-Gaussian characteristics dominating real-world stain variability. To address this, we propose FedStain, a stain-aware FedDG framework explicitly incorporating higher-order stain moments--skewness and kurtosis--as compact statistical descriptors exchanged during federated optimization. These descriptors require no pixel-level data transmission, preserving strict privacy and communication efficiency, while enabling the global model to capture stain variability missed by low-order statistics. FedStain also employs a contrastive, cross-site parameter aggregation strategy to promote stain-invariant representations without relaxing data constraints. Extensive experiments on Camelyon17 and our new MvMidog-Fed benchmark show FedStain yields consistent improvements, outperforming state-of-the-art FedL, DG, and FedDG baselines by up to +3.9% absolute accuracy. To our knowledge, FedStain is the first FedDG approach to explicitly model higher-order stain statistics, enabling robust cross-institutional deployment in computational pathology.

AI.tldr: FedStain introduces higher-order stain statistics to FedDG for WSI domain generalization, preserving privacy; improves cross-institution robustness.
AI.motivation: Stain color distributions in histopathology are non-Gaussian and heavy-tailed; current FedDG relies on low-order stats, failing to capture higher-order variability across institutions.
AI.method: FedStain exchanges skewness and kurtosis descriptors during federated optimization, with a contrastive cross-site parameter aggregation strategy to promote stain-invariant representations; no pixel-level data transfer.
AI.result: On Camelyon17 and the MvMidog-Fed benchmark, FedStain yields up to +3.9% absolute accuracy improvement over SOTA FedL, DG, and FedDG baselines; first FedDG approach to explicitly model higher-order stain statistics.
AI.conclusion: Higher-order stain statistics enable robust cross-institutional deployment in computational pathology by capturing non-Gaussian stain variability without compromising privacy.

title: Guided Diffusion Sampling for Precipitation Forecast Interventions
summary: Extreme precipitation causes severe societal and economic damage, and weather control has long been discussed as a potential mitigation strategy. However, to the best of our knowledge, perturbation-based interventions for weather control using data-driven weather forecasting models have not yet been explored. While adversarial attacks also generate perturbations that alter forecasts, they aim to exploit model artifacts and do not account for physical plausibility. In this paper, we propose a gradient-based guidance framework for precipitation-reduction interventions through diffusion sampling in diffusion-based weather forecasting models. Instead of directly perturbing atmospheric states, our method steers the diffusion sampling trajectory, enabling precipitation reduction while maintaining consistency with the atmospheric distribution. To assess physical plausibility, we evaluate from three perspectives: (i) vertical and variable-wise perturbation profiles, (ii) latent-space trajectory deviation, and (iii) cross-model transferability. Experiments on extreme precipitation events from WeatherBench2 demonstrate that our method achieves effective precipitation reduction while yielding more physically plausible interventions than adversarial perturbations.

AI.tldr: Gradient-guided diffusion-sampling to reduce precipitation in diffusion-based forecasts, achieving effective, physically plausible interventions and outperforming adversarial perturbations on WeatherBench2 extreme events.
AI.motivation: Mitigate extreme precipitation damage by exploring data-driven weather-control interventions. Existing perturbation/adversarial attacks lack physical plausibility and do not enforce consistency with atmospheric physics, leaving a gap for interventions that are both effective and physically plausible.
AI.method: Introduce a gradient-based guidance framework that steers the diffusion sampling trajectory in diffusion-based weather forecasting models to reduce precipitation. Interventions perturb the sampling process rather than atmospheric states, preserving consistency with the atmospheric distribution. Evaluate plausibility along three axes: vertical/variable-wise perturbation profiles, latent-space trajectory deviation, and cross-model transferability, using extreme precipitation events from WeatherBench2.
AI.result: The method achieves effective precipitation reduction and yields more physically plausible interventions than adversarial perturbations, with demonstrated cross-model transferability across WeatherBench2 extreme events.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-guided sampling offers a viable, physically consistent approach for precipitation-reduction interventions in data-driven forecasts, showing promise for weather-control-like applications and highlighting a path for further improvements in plausibility and robustness.

title: When Robots Do the Chores: A Benchmark and Agent for Long-Horizon Household Task Execution
summary: Long-horizon household tasks demand robust high-level planning and sustained reasoning capabilities, which are largely overlooked by existing embodied AI benchmarks that emphasize short-horizon navigation or manipulation and rely on fixed task categories. We introduce LongAct, a benchmark designed to evaluate planning-level autonomy in long-horizon household tasks specified through free-form instructions. By abstracting away embodiment-specific low-level control, LongAct isolates high-level cognitive capabilities such as instruction understanding, dependency management, memory maintenance, and adaptive planning. We further propose HoloMind, a VLM-driven agent with a DAG-based long-horizon hierarchical planner, a Multimodal Spatial Memory for persistent world modeling, an Episodic Memory for experience reuse, and a global Critic for reflective supervision. Experiments with GPT-5 and Qwen3-VL models show that HoloMind substantially improves long-horizon performance while reducing reliance on model scale. Even top models achieve only 59% goal completion and 16% full-task success, underscoring the difficulty of LongAct and the need for stronger long-horizon planning in embodied agents.

AI.tldr: LongAct benchmarks long-horizon household task planning with free-form instructions; HoloMind is a VLM-driven agent featuring a DAG-based long-horizon planner, Multimodal Spatial Memory, Episodic Memory, and a global Critic; results show limited success of top models, highlighting the need for stronger long-horizon planning.
AI.motivation: Addresses a gap in embodied AI benchmarks that emphasize short-horizon navigation/manipulation and fixed tasks; aims to isolate high-level cognitive capabilities (instruction understanding, dependency management, memory, adaptive planning) by abstracting away low-level control.
AI.method: Introduce LongAct benchmark for planning-level autonomy in long-horizon tasks; propose HoloMind architecture with a DAG-based long-horizon hierarchical planner, Multimodal Spatial Memory for persistent world modeling, Episodic Memory for experience reuse, and a global Critic for reflective supervision; evaluate with GPT-5 and Qwen3-VL models on goal completion and full-task success.
AI.result: HoloMind substantially improves long-horizon performance and reduces reliance on model scale; however, even top models achieve only 59% goal completion and 16% full-task success, underscoring the difficulty of long-horizon planning in embodied contexts.
AI.conclusion: Long-horizon planning remains challenging; the proposed benchmark and architecture demonstrate gains but reveal substantial gaps relative to real-world expectations, indicating a need for stronger planning, memory integration, and perhaps embodied feedback to achieve robust long-horizon autonomy.

title: TOPOS: High-Fidelity and Efficient Industry-Grade 3D Head Generation
summary: High-fidelity 3D head generation plays a crucial role in the film, animation and video game industries. In industrial pipelines, studios typically enforce a fixed reference topology across all head assets, as such a clean and uniform topology is a prerequisite for production-level rigging, skinning and animation. In this paper, we present TOPOS, a framework tailored for single image conditioned 3D head generation that jointly recovers geometry and appearance under such an industry-standard topology. In contrast to general 3D generative models which produce triangle meshes with inconsistent topology and numerous vertices, hindering semantic correspondence and asset-level reuse, TOPOS generates head meshes with a fixed, studio-style topology, enabling consistent vertex-level correspondence across all generated heads. To model heads under this unified topology, we proposed a novel variational autoencoder structure, termed TOPOS-VAE. Inspired by multi-model large language models (MLLMs), our TOPOS-VAE leverages the Perceiver Resampler to convert input pointclouds sampled from head meshes of diverse topologies into the target reference topology. Building upon TOPOS-VAE's structured latent space, we train a rectified flow transformer, TOPOS-DiT, to efficiently generate high-fidelity head meshes from a single image. We further present TOPOS-Texture, an end-to-end module that produces relightable UV texture maps from the same portrait image via fine-tuning a multimodal image generative model. The generated textures are spatially aligned with the underlying mesh geometry and faithfully preserve high-frequency appearance details. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TOPOS achieves state-of-the-art performance on 3D head generation, surpassing both classical face reconstruction methods and general 3D object generative models, highlighting its effectiveness for digital human creation.

AI.tldr: TOPOS introduces a production-oriented 3D head generation framework with a fixed studio-style topology, combining TOPOS-VAE (Perceiver Resampler-based topology mapping) and TOPOS-DiT (rectified flow transformer) to generate high-quality geometry from a single image, plus TOPOS-Texture for relightable textures, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: In film/animation pipelines, a fixed reference topology is required for rigging, skinning, and asset reuse. General 3D generators produce meshes with inconsistent topology, hindering semantic correspondence and production pipelines. A unified topology enables vertex-level correspondence across assets.
AI.method: 1) TOPOS-VAE: a topology-conditioned VAE using a Perceiver Resampler to map diverse input topologies (point clouds) to a fixed studio topology. 2) TOPOS-DiT: a rectified flow transformer that leverages the structured latent space to generate high-fidelity head meshes from a single image. 3) TOPOS-Texture: an end-to-end module that fine-tunes a multimodal image generative model to produce relightable UV textures aligned with the mesh. 4) Enforce a consistent topology across generated heads for asset reuse and correspondence.
AI.result: The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in 3D head generation, surpassing classical face reconstruction methods and general 3D object generative models, and demonstrates strong suitability for production-ready digital humans with consistent topology and high-frequency appearance details.
AI.conclusion: TOPOS delivers a unified, production-oriented pipeline for single-image conditioned 3D head generation with a fixed topology, yielding consistent vertex-level correspondence, high-fidelity geometry and relightable textures, suitable for rigging, animation, and asset reuse.

title: Dynamic Latent Routing
summary: We investigate the temporal concatenation of sub-policies in Markov Decision Processes (MDP) with time-varying reward functions. We introduce General Dijkstra Search (GDS), and prove that globally optimal goal-reaching policies can be recovered through temporal composition of intermediate optimal sub-policies. Motivated by the "search, select, update" principle underlying GDS, we propose Dynamic Latent Routing (DLR), a language-model post-training method that jointly learns discrete latent codes, routing policies, and model parameters through dynamic search in a single training stage. In low-data fine-tuning settings, DLR matches or outperforms supervised fine-tuning across four datasets and six models, achieving a mean gain of +6.6 percentage points, while prior discrete-latent baselines consistently underperform SFT. Mechanistic analyses and targeted code ablations show that DLR learns structured routing behaviors with distinct causal roles.

AI.tldr: GDS enables globally optimal goal-reaching by temporally composing optimal sub-policies in MDPs with time-varying rewards; DLR is a language-model post-training method that jointly learns discrete latent codes, routing policies, and model parameters via dynamic search, yielding strong low-data fine-tuning gains (+6.6pp) and interpretable routing behaviors.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of optimal policy composition across temporally varying reward landscapes, and improve data-efficiency and interpretability in language-model fine-tuning through discrete latent routing.
AI.method: Introduce General Dijkstra Search (GDS) with theoretical optimality guarantees; propose Dynamic Latent Routing (DLR), a one-stage post-training framework that jointly learns discrete latent codes, routing policies, and model parameters through dynamic search; validate via mechanistic analyses and ablations.
AI.result: Theoretical result: globally optimal goal-reaching policies can be recovered by temporally composing intermediate optimal sub-policies under time-varying rewards. Empirical result: in low-data fine-tuning across four datasets and six models, DLR matches or surpasses supervised fine-tuning, with a mean gain of +6.6 percentage points; discrete-latent baselines underperform SFT. Mechanistic analyses and targeted code ablations indicate DLR learns structured routing with distinct causal roles.
AI.conclusion: Temporal policy composition via GDS and the DLR post-training framework offer data-efficient, interpretable routing solutions for LM fine-tuning and MDPs with time-varying rewards, highlighting the value of discrete latent routing and dynamic search.

title: Cattle Trade: A Multi-Agent Benchmark for LLM Bluffing, Bidding, and Bargaining
summary: We introduce \textsc{Cattle Trade, a multi-agent benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) as agents in strategic reasoning under imperfect information, adversarial interaction, and resource constraints. The benchmark combines auctions, hidden-offer trade challenges (TCs), bargaining, bluffing, opponent modeling, and resource allocation within a single long-horizon game lasting 50--60 turns. Unlike prior agent benchmarks that test these abilities in isolation, \textsc{Cattle Trade} evaluates whether agents integrate them across a competitive, multi-agent economic game with conflicting incentives. The benchmark logs every bid, TC offer, counteroffer, and card selection, enabling behavioural analysis beyond final scores or win rates. We evaluate seven cost-efficient language models and three deterministic code agents across 242 games. Strategic coherence, in particular spending efficiency, resource discipline, and phase-adaptive bidding, is associated with rank more strongly than spending volume or any single subskill. Two heuristic code agents outperform most tested LLMs, and behavioural traces surface recurring LLM failure modes including overbidding, self-bidding, bankrupt TC initiation, and weak opponent-state adaptation. Evaluating agentic competence requires benchmarks that test the joint deployment of multiple capabilities in multi-agent environments with conflicting incentives, uncertainty, and economic dynamics.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent benchmark, Cattle Trade, evaluates LLMs as strategic agents in a long-horizon, imperfect-information economic game combining auctions, bargaining, bluffing, and resource allocation. It logs rich behavioral traces to study integration of multiple capabilities, revealing that heuristic code agents outperformed many LLMs and that strategic coherence predicted performance better than raw spending.
AI.motivation: To address the limitation of existing benchmarks that test isolated subskills, the authors argue for evaluating agentic competence via joint deployment of multiple strategic capabilities in interactive, uncertain, and incentive-rich multi-agent settings.
AI.method: Introduce Cattle Trade, a 50–60 turn long-horizon multi-agent game combining auctions, hidden-offer trade challenges, bargaining, bluffing, opponent modeling, and resource allocation. Log every bid, offering, counteroffer, and choice. Evaluate seven cost-efficient LLMs and three deterministic code agents across 242 games.
AI.result: Two heuristic code agents outperform most tested LLMs. LLMs reveal recurring failure modes (overbidding, self-bidding, bankrupt TC initiation, poor opponent adaptation). Strategic coherence and phase-adaptive bidding correlate with higher rank more than total spending. The benchmark enables rich behavioral analysis beyond final scores.
AI.conclusion: Effective evaluation of agentic competence requires joint testing of multiple capabilities in multi-agent, uncertain, competitive economics. LLMs currently struggle to integrate strategic reasoning with other capabilities in such environments; benchmarks like Cattle Trade are essential to drive development of more capable, coordinated agents.

title: VMU-Diff: A Coarse-to-fine Multi-source Data Fusion Framework for Precipitation Nowcasting
summary: Precipitation nowcasting is a vital spatio-temporal prediction task for meteorological applications but faces challenges due to the chaotic property of precipitation systems. Existing methods predominantly rely on single-source radar data to build either deterministic or probabilistic models for extrapolation. However, the single deterministic model suffers from blurring due to MSE convergence. The single probabilistic model, typically represented by diffusion models, can generate fine details but suffers from spurious artifacts that compromise accuracy and computational inefficiency. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel coarse-to-fine Vision Mamba Unet and residual Diffusion (VMU-Diff) based precipitation nowcasting framework. It realizes precipitation nowcasting through a two-stage process, i.e., a deterministic model-based coarse stage to predict global motion trends and a probabilistic model-based fine stage to generate fine prediction details. In the coarse prediction stage, rather than single-source radar data, both radar and multi-band satellite data are taken as input. A spatial-temporal attention block and several Vision mamba state-space blocks realize multi-source data fusion, and predict the future echo global dynamics. The fine-grained stage is realized by a spatio-temporal refine generator based on residual conditional diffusion models. It first obtains spatio-temporal residual features based on coarse prediction and ground truth, and further reconstructs the residual via conditional Mamba state-space module. Experiments on Jiangsu SWAN datasets demonstrate the improvements of our method over state-of-the-art methods, particularly in short-term forecasts.

AI.tldr: Two-stage coarse-to-fine VMU-Diff framework for precipitation nowcasting that fuses radar and multi-band satellite data: a deterministic coarse stage for global motion and a residual diffusion-based fine stage for details, achieving improved short-term forecasts on Jiangsu SWAN.
AI.motivation: Single-source radar with purely deterministic models yields blur; single diffusion models produce artifacts and are computationally heavy. Multi-source fusion and a coarse-to-fine strategy can better capture global dynamics and fine details.
AI.method: Stage 1 (coarse): input radar + multi-band satellite data; spatial-temporal attention and Vision Mamba state-space blocks fuse data and predict global echo dynamics. Stage 2 (fine): a residual conditional diffusion-based refine generator uses coarse predictions and ground truth to obtain spatio-temporal residuals, reconstructed via a conditional Mamba state-space module.
AI.result: Empirical results on Jiangsu SWAN dataset show improvements over state-of-the-art, particularly for short-term forecasts.
AI.conclusion: The VMU-Diff framework effectively combines multi-source fusion with a coarse-to-fine diffusion approach to improve precipitation nowcasting accuracy and efficiency, mitigating blurring and artifacts.

title: AIM-DDI: A Model-Agnostic Multimodal Integration Module for Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction
summary: Drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction is a critical task in computational biomedicine, as adverse interactions between co-administered drugs can cause severe side effects and clinical risks. A key challenge is unseen-drug generalization, where interactions must be predicted for drugs not observed during training. Although multimodal DDI models exploit diverse drug-related information, their fusion mechanisms are often tied to specific prediction architectures, limiting their reuse across models. To address this, we propose AIM-DDI, an architecture-independent multimodal integration module that represents heterogeneous modality information as tokens in a shared latent space. By modeling dependencies across modality tokens through a unified fusion module, AIM-DDI enables model-agnostic integration of structural, chemical, and semantic drug signals across different DDI prediction architectures. Extensive evaluations across diverse DDI models and DrugBank-based settings show that AIM-DDI consistently improves prediction performance, with the strongest gains under the most challenging both-unseen setting where neither drug in a test pair is observed during training. These results suggest that treating multimodal integration as a reusable module, rather than a model-specific fusion component, is an effective strategy for robust unseen-drug DDI prediction.

AI.tldr: A reusable, architecture-independent multimodal integration module (AIM-DDI) improves unseen-drug DDI prediction by representing diverse drug modalities as tokens in a shared latent space and fusing them with a unified module, enabling model-agnostic integration across architectures.
AI.motivation: Unseen-drug generalization in DDI remains challenging; existing multimodal fusion often ties to specific prediction architectures, hindering reuse and transfer across models.
AI.method: Introduce AIM-DDI: represent heterogeneous modalities (structural, chemical, semantic) as tokens in a shared latent space and apply a unified fusion module to model cross-modality dependencies, enabling architecture-agnostic integration across different DDI prediction models.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across diverse DDI models and DrugBank-based settings show consistent performance gains, with the largest improvements in the most challenging both-unseen scenario where neither drug in a test pair was observed during training.
AI.conclusion: Treating multimodal integration as a reusable module, rather than model-specific fusion components, yields robust unseen-drug DDI prediction and supports broader reuse of multimodal fusion across models.

title: VerbalValue: A Socially Intelligent Virtual Host for Sales-Driven Live Commerce
comment: Accepted to the CVPR 2026 HiGen Workshop
summary: A skilled live-commerce host is not merely a narrator, but a sales agent who converts viewer curiosity into purchase intent through expert product knowledge, emotionally intelligent response tactics, and entertainment that serves as a vehicle for product exposure. Yet no existing AI system replicates this: conversational recommenders treat recommendation as a terminal act, while general-purpose LLMs hallucinate product claims and default to generic promotional templates that fail to engage or persuade. We present VerbalValue, a sales-conversion-oriented virtual host that turns exceptional verbal ability into real commercial value, built on three contributions. First, we construct a domain knowledge base of product specifications and a curated sales terminology lexicon that anchor product-related responses in verified expertise. Second, we collect and annotate 1,475 live-commerce interactions spanning diverse viewer intents. Third, we fine-tune a large language model on this data to deliver empathetic, commercially oriented responses, adapting to viewer intent through empathetic amplification, evidence-backed rebuttal, and humor-mediated deflection. Experiments against GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and other baselines demonstrate gains of 23% on informativeness and 18% on factual correctness, with consistent advantages in tactfulness and viewer engagement.

AI.tldr: VerbalValue is a sales-conversion-oriented virtual host for live commerce, built with a domain knowledge base, a curated sales-lexicon, and a fine-tuned LLM trained on 1,475 real interactions to deliver empathetic, evidence-based, and entertaining responses that boost informativeness, factual correctness, tact, and viewer engagement, outperforming major LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: Current AI sales assistants fail to translate viewer curiosity into purchases: conversational recommenders treat recommendations as terminal actions, and general-purpose LLMs hallucinate or rely on generic promos. There is a need for a system that anchors product claims in verified expertise and engages viewers with empathetic, persuasive dialogue.
AI.method: 1) Construct a domain knowledge base of product specifications and a curated sales-terminology lexicon to anchor responses in verified expertise. 2) Collect and annotate 1,475 live-commerce interactions spanning diverse viewer intents. 3) Fine-tune a large language model on this data to deliver empathetic, commercially oriented responses, adapting to viewer intent via empathetic amplification, evidence-backed rebuttals, and humor-mediated deflection.
AI.result: Experiments against GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and other baselines show gains of 23% in informativeness and 18% in factual correctness, with consistent improvements in tactfulness and viewer engagement.
AI.conclusion: VerbalValue demonstrates that a sale-focused virtual host, grounded in verified product knowledge and trained on real interaction data, can outperform general LLM baselines in live-commerce settings by delivering more informative, accurate, and engaging conversations that drive conversion.

title: Towards Accurate Single Panoramic 3D Detection: A Semantic Gaussian Centric Approach
comment: Current has been accepted by ICME 2026
summary: Three-dimensional object detection in panoramic imagery is crucial for comprehensive scene understanding, yet accurately mapping 2D features to 3D remains a significant challenge. Prevailing methods often project 2D features onto discrete 3D grids, which break geometric continuity and limit representation efficiency. To overcome this limitation, this paper proposes PanoGSDet, a monocular panoramic 3D detection framework built upon continuous semantic 3D Gaussian representations. The proposed framework comprises a panoramic depth estimation component and a semantic Gaussian component. The panoramic depth estimation component extracts the equirectangular semantic and depth features from the monocular panorama input. The semantic Gaussian component includes a semantic Gaussian lifting module that projects spherical features into 3D semantic Gaussians, a semantic Gaussian optimization module that refines these semantic Gaussians, and a Gaussian guided prediction head that generates 3D bounding boxes from optimized Gaussian representations. Extensive experiments on the Structured3D dataset demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing methods.

AI.tldr: PanoGSDet introduces a monocular panoramic 3D detector using continuous semantic 3D Gaussians to avoid grid-based geometry discontinuities, combining panoramic depth estimation with a semantic Gaussian pipeline to predict 3D boxes, showing strong results on Structured3D.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch between 2D panoramic features and 3D representations. Grid-based projections break geometric continuity and efficiency; continuous Gaussian representations offer smoother, more compact 3D priors aligned with panoramic geometry.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) panoramic depth estimation with equirectangular semantic and depth features; (2) semantic Gaussian component comprising (a) semantic Gaussian lifting from spherical features to 3D Gaussians, (b) Gaussian optimization to refine these Gaussians, and (c) a Gaussian-guided prediction head that outputs 3D bounding boxes from optimized Gaussians.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Structured3D show significant improvement over existing methods, indicating the effectiveness of continuous semantic 3D Gaussians for monocular panoramic 3D detection.
AI.conclusion: Continuous semantic 3D Gaussian representations effectively bridge 2D panoramic features and 3D scene understanding for monocular panoramic 3D detection, with demonstrated gains on Structured3D; future work could explore broader datasets, ablations, and efficiency optimizations.

title: Nearest-Neighbor Radii under Dependent Sampling
summary: Nearest-neighbor methods are fundamental to classical and modern machine learning, yet their geometric properties are typically analyzed under independent sampling. In this paper, we study the nearest-neighbor radii under dependent sampling. We consider strong mixing dependent observations and ask whether dependence changes the scale of nearest-neighbor neighborhoods. We establish distribution-free almost sure convergence under polynomial mixing and sharp non-asymptotic moment bounds under geometric mixing. The moment bounds depend on the local intrinsic dimension rather than the ambient dimension, making the results applicable to high-dimensional data concentrated near lower-dimensional manifolds. Synthetic experiments and real-world time-series benchmarks support the theory, showing that nearest-neighbor geometry remains informative under dependence sampling.

AI.tldr: Nearest-neighbor radii under dependent sampling with strong mixing retain informative geometry: distribution-free almost sure convergence under polynomial mixing and sharp non-asymptotic moment bounds under geometric mixing, with bounds governed by local intrinsic dimension rather than ambient dimension.
AI.motivation: Address how dependence among samples (via mixing) affects nearest-neighbor geometry, extending NN theory beyond i.i.d. data and enabling analysis for high-dimensional data concentrated on low-dimensional manifolds.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of NN radii under strong mixing sequences; derive distribution-free almost sure convergence under polynomial mixing; obtain sharp non-asymptotic moment bounds under geometric mixing; emphasize bounds depending on local intrinsic dimension; support with synthetic and real-world time-series experiments.
AI.result: Established distribution-free almost sure convergence of NN radii under polynomial mixing; provided sharp non-asymptotic moment bounds under geometric mixing; bounds depend on local intrinsic dimension; experiments show NN geometry remains informative under dependence sampling.
AI.conclusion: Dependence modeled via mixing does not erase informative NN geometry. The results extend NN radius analysis to dependent data and manifold-like high-dimensional settings, with practical implications for NN-based methods on time-series and other dependent data.

title: Complacent, Not Sycophantic: Reframing Large Language Models and Designing AI Literacy for Complacent Machines
summary: Large language models are often described as sycophantic, in the sense that they appear to flatter users or mirror their beliefs. We argue that this label is conceptually misleading: sycophancy implies motives and strategic intent, which LLMs do not possess. Their behaviour is better understood as complacency, a structural tendency to agree with user input because training data, reward signals and design favour agreement and reinforcement over correction. We argue that this distinction matters. Whether developers act sycophantically or not, models themselves never are sycophants; they can only be made more or less complacent. This reframing locates agency in developers and institutions, not in the model. Because complacent models reinforce users' prior beliefs, we argue that AI literacy educational approaches should particularly focus on strategies to counter confirmation bias.

AI.tldr: Sycophancy is a misleading label for LLMs; their behavior is better understood as complacency—an outcome of training data, reward signals, and design that favor agreement over correction. Agency lies with developers and institutions, not the models. AI literacy should focus on countering confirmation bias.
AI.motivation: Clarify the conceptual misunderstanding of LLM behavior, reframe responsibility, and inform AI literacy and governance by distinguishing motives from structural incentives in model training.
AI.method: Conceptual/analytical argument: redefine key terms (sycophancy vs. complacency), critique common attributions of motive, and discuss implications for literacy and governance.
AI.result: A reframing of LLM behavior: models are not sycophants but complacent due to systemic incentives; reinforcing users' priors, which has normative consequences for education and policy.
AI.conclusion: Educational and policy focus should be on countering confirmation bias and on developer/institutional responsibility, rather than attributing agency or motive to the models themselves.

title: MambaRain: Multi-Scale Mamba-Attention Framework for 0-3 Hour Precipitation Nowcasting
summary: Accurate precipitation nowcasting over extended horizons (0-3 hours) is essential for disaster mitigation and operational decision-making, yet remains a critical challenge in the field. Existing deterministic approaches are predominantly constrained to shorter prediction windows (0-2 hours), exhibiting severe performance degradation beyond 90 minutes owing to their inherent difficulty in capturing long-range spatiotemporal dependencies from radar-derived observations. To address these fundamental limitations, we propose MambaRain, a novel multi-scale encoder-decoder architecture that synergistically integrates Mamba's linear-complexity long-range temporal modeling with self-attention mechanisms for explicit spatial correlation capture. The core innovation lies in a hybrid design paradigm wherein Mamba blocks leverage selective state space mechanisms to model global temporal dynamics across extended sequences with computational efficiency, while self-attention modules explicitly characterize spatial correlations within precipitation fields - a capability inherently absent in Mamba's sequential processing paradigm. This complementary synergy enables comprehensive spatiotemporal representation learning, effectively extending the viable forecasting horizon to 2-3 hours with substantial accuracy improvements. Furthermore, we introduce a spectral loss formulation to mitigate blurring artifacts characteristic of chaotic precipitation systems, thereby preserving fine-scale motion details critical for nowcasting accuracy. Experimental validation demonstrates that MambaRain substantially outperforms existing deterministic methodologies in 0-3 hour nowcasting tasks, with particularly pronounced performance gains in the challenging 2-3 hour prediction range.

AI.tldr: MambaRain extends nowcasting horizon to 2-3 hours by combining Mamba's linear-complexity long-range temporal modeling with self-attention for spatial correlations, augmented by a spectral loss to reduce blurring.
AI.motivation: Accurately forecasting precipitation beyond short windows (0-2 hours) is challenging due to long-range spatiotemporal dependencies and chaotic dynamics; improving horizon and preserving detail is critical for disaster mitigation and operations.
AI.method: A multi-scale encoder-decoder architecture where Mamba blocks model global temporal dynamics with selective state-space mechanisms for efficiency, while self-attention modules capture explicit spatial correlations in precipitation fields; a spectral loss term mitigates blurring artifacts.
AI.result: Experimental validation shows substantial performance gains over existing deterministic methods on 0-3 hour nowcasting, with particularly strong improvements in the 2-3 hour window.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid MambaRain design yields enhanced spatiotemporal representation, effectively extending the viable forecast horizon to 2-3 hours with notable accuracy improvements and preserved fine-scale motion details due to the spectral loss.

title: Exemplar Partitioning for Mechanistic Interpretability
summary:
We introduce Exemplar Partitioning (EP), an unsupervised method for constructing interpretable feature dictionaries from large language model activations with $\sim 10^{3}\times$ fewer tokens than comparable sparse autoencoders (SAEs). An EP dictionary is a Voronoi partition of activation space, built by leader-clustering streamed activations within a distance threshold. Each region is anchored by an observed exemplar that serves as both its membership criterion and intervention direction; dictionary size is not prespecified, but determined by the activation geometry at that threshold.
