AI.tldr: A framework for chaotic dynamics surrogates that jointly optimizes local Jacobians and long-term statistics by covering the attractor and aligning pushforward distributions with MMD; improves Jacobian accuracy and remains competitive with statistical methods.
AI.motivation: Chaotic systems exhibit sensitivity to initial conditions; exact long-term prediction is infeasible. A good surrogate should preserve local expansion/contraction (Jacobian) and match long-term statistics. Prior work typically emphasizes one axis; this work aims to bridge both.
AI.method: Construct a local covering of the chaotic attractor in phase space and analyze expansion/contraction of these coverings under the dynamics. Train the surrogate by minimizing the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between the pushforward distributions of the coverings under the surrogate and the ground-truth dynamics.
AI.result: Experiments show significant improvement in Jacobian accuracy and competitive performance with state-of-the-art statistically accurate dynamics learning methods.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a unified framework that bridges Jacobian- and statistics-focused dynamics learning, yielding surrogates with better local linearization and maintained global statistical fidelity; code available.

title: How Sensitive Are Radiomic AI Models to Acquisition Parameters?
summary: A main barrier for the deployment of AI radiomic systems in clinical routine is their drop in performance under heterogeneous multicentre acquisition protocols. This work presents a performance-oriented framework for quantifying scan parameter sensitivity of radiomic AI models, while identifying clinically significant parameter regions associated with improved cross-dataset robustness. We formulate a mixed-effects framework for quantifying the influence that clinically relevant acquisition parameters have on models performance, while accounting for subject-level random effects. We have applied our framework to lung cancer diagnosis in CT scans using two independent multicentre datasets (a public database and own-collected data) and several SoA architectures. To evaluate across-database reproducibility, CT parameters have been adjusted using the data collected and tested on the public set. The optimal configuration selected is the current of the X-ray tube >= 200 mA, spiral pitch <= 1.5, slice thickness <= 1.25 mm, which balances diagnostic quality with low radiation dose. These configuration push metrics from 0.79+-0.04 sensitivity, 0.47+-0.10 specificity in low quality scans to 0.90+-0.10 sensitivity, 0.79 +- 0.13 specificity in high quality ones.

AI.tldr: A performance-oriented framework quantifies scan parameter sensitivity in radiomic AI, identifies acquisition settings that improve cross-dataset robustness, and demonstrates improved diagnostic metrics across multicentre CT lung cancer datasets using a mixed-effects model.
AI.motivation: Heterogeneity in multicentre CT acquisitions causes radiomic model performance drop; need to understand parameter influence and derive robust, clinically feasible acquisition settings.
AI.method: Proposes a mixed-effects framework to quantify the impact of acquisition parameters on model performance, accounting for subject-level random effects; applies to two multicentre CT lung cancer datasets; evaluates multiple SOTA architectures; optimizes CT settings (tube current, pitch, slice thickness) and assesses cross-dataset reproducibility.
AI.result: Identifies optimal configuration (tube current >=200 mA, pitch <=1.5, slice thickness <=1.25 mm) yielding improved metrics from low-quality to high-quality scans: sensitivity 0.79±0.04 → 0.90±0.10; specificity 0.47±0.10 → 0.79±0.13.
AI.conclusion: A framework to inform acquisition protocol design that enhances radiomic robustness across datasets, facilitating more reliable multicentre deployment.

title: Beyond Instance-Level Self-Supervision in 3D Multi-Modal Medical Imaging
summary: Self-supervised pre-training methods in medical imaging typically treat each individual as an isolated instance, learning representations through augmentation-based objectives or masked reconstruction. They often do not adequately capitalize on a key characteristic of physiological features: anatomical structures maintain consistent spatial relationships across individuals (instances), such as the thalamus being medial to the basal ganglia, regardless of variations in brain size, shape, or pathology. We propose leveraging this cross-instance topological consistency as a supervisory signal. The challenge arises from the inherent variability in medical imaging, which can differ significantly across instances and modalities. To tackle this, we focus on two alignment regimes. (i) Intra-instance: with pixel-level correspondences available, a cross-modal triplet objective explicitly preserves local neighborhood topology. (ii) Inter-instance: without such supervision, we derive pseudo-correspondences to control partial neighborhood alignment and prevent topology collapse across modalities. We validate our approach across 7 downstream multi-modal tasks, achieving average improvements of 1.1% and 5.94% in segmentation and classification tasks, respectively, and demonstrating significantly better robustness when modalities are missing at test time.

AI.tldr: Proposes cross-instance topological consistency as supervision in self-supervised pretraining for multi-modal medical imaging; two alignment regimes (intra-instance with pixel-level correspondences; inter-instance with pseudo-correspondences); shows improvements on 7 down-stream tasks and robustness to missing modalities.
AI.motivation: Overcoming isolated instance learning in SSL and exploiting consistent anatomical topology across individuals and modalities to improve representations.
AI.method: Intra-instance: cross-modal triplet objective preserving local topology with pixel-level correspondences; Inter-instance: derive pseudo-correspondences to align partial neighborhoods and avoid topology collapse; evaluation on 7 tasks.
AI.result: Average improvements: 1.1% segmentation; 5.94% classification; improved robustness when modalities are missing at test time.
AI.conclusion: Cross-instance topological consistency is a viable supervisory signal for SSL in medical imaging, enabling better multi-modal representations and resilience to modality absence.

title: GeoViSTA: Geospatial Vision-Tabular Transformer for Multimodal Environment Representation
summary: Large-scale pretraining on Earth observation imagery has yielded powerful representations of the natural and built environment. However, most existing geospatial foundation models do not directly model the structured socioeconomic covariates typically stored in tabular form. This modality gap limits their ability to capture the complete total environment, which is critical for reasoning about complex environmental, social, and health-related outcomes. In this work, we propose GeoViSTA (Geospatial Vision-Tabular Transformer), a vision-tabular architecture that learns unified geospatial embeddings from co-registered gridded imagery and tabular data. GeoViSTA utilizes bilateral cross-attention to exchange spatial and semantic information across modalities, guided by a geography-aware attention mechanism that aligns continuous image patches with irregular census-tract tokens. We train GeoViSTA with a self-supervised joint masked-autoencoding objective, forcing it to recover missing image patches and tabular rows using local spatial context and cross-modal cues. Empirically, GeoViSTA's unified embeddings improve linear probing performance on high-impact downstream tasks, outperforming baselines in predicting disease-specific mortality and fire hazard frequency across held-out regions. These results demonstrate that jointly modeling the physical environment alongside structured socioeconomic context yields highly transferable representations for holistic geospatial inference.

AI.tldr: A vision-tabular transformer, GeoViSTA, unifies gridded imagery and census-tabular data via bilateral cross-attention and geography-aware patch-to-token alignment, trained with a self-supervised joint masked autoencoding objective, achieving better transferability for holistic geospatial inference.
AI.motivation: Tackling the modality gap between high-resolution geospatial imagery and structured socioeconomic tabular data to enable holistic reasoning about environmental, social, and health outcomes.
AI.method: Proposes GeoViSTA, a vision-tabular architecture that uses bilateral cross-attention to fuse modalities and a geography-aware attention mechanism that aligns continuous image patches with irregular census-tract tokens. Trained with a self-supervised joint masked autoencoding objective to recover missing image patches and tabular rows using local spatial context and cross-modal cues.
AI.result: Unified embeddings improve linear probing on downstream tasks and outperform baselines in predicting disease-specific mortality and fire hazard frequency across held-out regions, demonstrating transferability of holistic geospatial representations.
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling the physical environment with structured socioeconomic context yields highly transferable representations for holistic geospatial inference.

title: $π$-Bench: Evaluating Proactive Personal Assistant Agents in Long-Horizon Workflows
summary: The rise of personal assistant agents, e.g., OpenClaw, highlights the growing potential of large language models to support users across everyday life and work. A core challenge in these settings is proactive assistance, since users often begin with underspecified requests and leave important needs, constraints, or preferences unstated. However, existing benchmarks rarely evaluate whether agents can identify and act on such hidden intents before they are explicitly stated, especially in sustained multi-turn interactions where user needs emerge gradually. To address this gap, we introduce $π$-Bench, a benchmark for proactive assistance comprising 100 multi-turn tasks across 5 domain-specific user personas. By incorporating hidden user intents, inter-task dependencies, and cross-session continuity, $π$-Bench evaluates agents' ability to anticipate and address user needs over extended interactions, jointly measuring proactivity and task completion in long-horizon trajectories that better reflect real-world use. Experiments show (1) proactive assistance remains challenging, (2) a clear distinction between task completion and proactivity, and (3) the value of prior interaction for proactive intent resolution in later tasks.

AI.tldr: π-Bench is a benchmark to evaluate proactive, long-horizon assistance in large language model agents by simulating hidden user intents across 100 multi-turn tasks and 5 personas, measuring proactivity and task completion across extended trajectories.
AI.motivation: To address the gap where agents often receive underspecified requests and must infer hidden needs over sustained interactions; existing benchmarks inadequately test anticipatory behavior and cross-session continuity.
AI.method: Introduce π-Bench with 100 multi-turn tasks across 5 domain-specific personas, embedding hidden intents, inter-task dependencies, and cross-session continuity; evaluate agents on proactivity and task completion along long-horizon trajectories.
AI.result: Experiments indicate that proactive assistance remains challenging, proactivity is distinct from mere task completion, and prior interactions help resolve proactive intents in later tasks.
AI.conclusion: π-Bench provides a realistic, long-horizon benchmark for evaluating proactive AI assistants, highlights the importance of memory and anticipation, and suggests that leveraging prior interactions can improve proactive intent resolution.

title: MiVE: Multiscale Vision-language features for reference-guided video Editing
summary: Reference-guided video editing takes a source video, a text instruction, and a reference image as inputs, requiring the model to faithfully apply the instructed edits while preserving original motion and unedited content. Existing methods fall into two paradigms, each with inherent limitations: decoupled encoders suffer from modality gaps when processing instructions and visual content independently, while unified vision-language encoders lose fine-grained spatial details by relying solely on final-layer representations. We observe that VLM layers encode complementary information hierarchically -- early layers capture localized spatial details essential for precise editing, while deeper layers encode global semantics for instruction comprehension. Building on this insight, we present MiVE (Multiscale Vision-language features for reference-guided video Editing), a framework that repurposes VLMs as multiscale feature extractors. MiVE extracts hierarchical features from Qwen3-VL and integrates them into a unified self-attention Diffusion Transformer, eliminating the modality mismatch inherent in cross-attention designs. Experiments demonstrate that MiVE achieves state-of-the-art performance by ranking highest in human preference, outperforming both academic methods and commercial systems.

AI.tldr: MiVE introduces multiscale vision-language features for reference-guided video editing by extracting hierarchical features from Qwen3-VL and feeding them into a diffusion Transformer, achieving state-of-the-art results with high human preference.
AI.motivation: Address modality gaps in decoupled encoders and loss of spatial detail in unified vision-language encoders by leveraging hierarchical VLM representations to preserve both local details and global semantics.
AI.method: Repurpose Qwen3-VL as a multiscale feature extractor; integrate extracted features into a unified self-attention Diffusion Transformer to guide video editing without cross-attention modality mismatch.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance, ranking highest in human preference, and surpasses both academic methods and commercial systems.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the efficacy of hierarchical VLM feature integration for reference-guided video editing, enabling faithful edits with preserved motion and details.

title: MahaVar: OOD Detection via Class-wise Mahalanobis Distance Variance under Neural Collapse
summary: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is a critical component for ensuring the reliability of deep neural networks in safety-critical applications. In this work, we present a key empirical observation: for in-distribution (ID) samples, class-wise Mahalanobis distances exhibit a pronounced sharp minimum structure, where the distance to the nearest class is small while distances to all other classes remain large, resulting in high variance across classes. In contrast, OOD samples tend to exhibit a less pronounced sharp minimum structure, producing comparatively lower variance across classes. We further provide a theoretical analysis grounding this observation in Neural Collapse geometry: under relaxed Neural Collapse assumptions on within-class compactness and inter-class separation, ID samples are shown to structurally exhibit high class-wise distance variance, offering a theoretical basis for its use as an OOD score. Motivated by this observation and its theoretical backing, we propose MahaVar, a simple and effective post-hoc OOD detector that augments the Mahalanobis distance with a class-wise distance variance term. Following the OpenOOD v1.5 benchmark protocol, MahaVar achieves state-of-the-art performance on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet, with consistent improvements in both AUROC and FPR@95 over existing Mahalanobis-based methods across all benchmarks.

AI.tldr: MahaVar augments class-wise Mahalanobis distances with a class-wise distance variance term for post-hoc OOD detection; empirical observation ties high variance in in-distribution (ID) samples to a sharp minimum across class distances, while OOD samples show lower variance. The approach is theoretically grounded in Neural Collapse and achieves state-of-the-art results on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet under OpenOOD v1.5, improving AUROC and FPR@95 over previous Mahalanobis-based methods.
AI.motivation: Improve reliability of DNNs by robust OOD detection. Standard Mahalanobis-based detectors exploit class-wise distances but may underutilize variance structure. Observing that ID samples have high class-wise distance variance and OOD samples have lower variance, grounded in Neural Collapse, motivates an augmented score.
AI.method: Post-hoc detector MahaVar: compute class-wise Mahalanobis distances for a sample, compute the variance of these distances across classes, and combine them into a single score (e.g., distance plus a variance term) to detect OOD. The approach relies on Neural Collapse-inspired assumptions about within-class compactness and inter-class separation.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet under the OpenOOD v1.5 benchmark, with consistent improvements in AUROC and FPR@95 over existing Mahalanobis-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Variance-augmented Mahalanobis distance (MahaVar) provides a simple, effective post-hoc OOD detector with theoretical grounding and clear empirical gains on standard benchmarks.

title: On Strong Equivalence Notions in Logic Programming and Abstract Argumentation
summary: Strong equivalence between knowledge bases ensures the possibility of replacing one with the other without affecting reasoning outcomes, in any given context. This makes it a crucial property in nonmonotonic formalisms. In particular, the fields of logic programming and abstract argumentation provide primary examples in which this property has been subject to vast investigations. However, while (classes of) logic programs and abstract argumentation frameworks are known to be semantically equivalent in static settings, this alignment breaks in dynamic contexts due to differing notions of update. As a result, strong equivalence does not always carry over from one formalism to the other. In this paper, we carefully investigate this discrepancy and introduce a new notion of strong equivalence for logic programs. Our approach preserves strong equivalence under translation between certain classes of logic programs and both Dung-style and claim-augmented argumentation frameworks, thus restoring compatibility across these formalisms.

AI.tldr: Introduces a new strong equivalence notion for logic programs that remains invariant under translations to Dung-style and claim-augmented argumentation frameworks, aligning static and dynamic semantics across these formalisms.
AI.motivation: Strong equivalence is key for safe substitution in nonmonotonic reasoning. Static semantic equivalences between logic programs and AFs diverge under updates, creating cross-formalism incompatibility.
AI.method: Formalize a novel strong equivalence notion for logic programs and analyze translations to Dung-style and claim-augmented AFs. Prove preservation of the equivalence across the specified translations for certain program classes under dynamic update scenarios.
AI.result: A strong equivalence notion that is preserved under translations to both Dung-style and claim-augmented AFs, restoring compatibility between the two formalisms during updates.
AI.conclusion: The approach bridges logic programming and AFs in dynamic contexts, enabling robust cross-formalism reasoning during updates and guiding future work on broader nonmonotonic formalisms.

title: Are Candidate Models Really Needed for Active Learning?
comment: Accepted for publication in Computer Vision and Image Understanding (CVIU)
summary: Deep learning has profoundly impacted domains such as computer vision and natural language processing by uncovering complex patterns in vast datasets. However, the reliance on extensive labeled data poses significant challenges, including resource constraints and annotation errors, particularly in training Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and transformers due to a larger number of parameters. Active learning offers a promising solution to reduce labeling burdens by strategically selecting the most informative samples for annotation. However, the current active learning frameworks are time-intensive which select the samples iteratively with the help of initial candidate models. This study investigates the feasibility of using CNNs and transformers with randomly initialized weights, eliminating the need for initial candidate models while achieving results comparable to active learning frameworks that depend on such candidate models. We evaluate three confidence-based sampling strategies: high confidence (HC), low confidence (LC), and a combination of high confidence in the early stages of training and low confidence at later stages of training (HCLC). Among these, mostly LC demonstrated the best performance in our experiments, showcasing its effectiveness as an active learning strategy without the need for candidate models. Further, extensive experiments verify the robustness of the proposed active learning methods. By challenging traditional frameworks, the proposed work introduces a streamlined approach to active learning, advancing efficiency and flexibility across diverse datasets and domains.

AI.tldr: A simple, candidate-model-free active learning approach using randomly initialized CNNs/Transformers with confidence-based sampling (HC/LC/HCLC), where low-confidence sampling (LC) performs best and the method is robust across datasets.
AI.motivation: Reduce labeling costs and dependence on iterative candidate models in active learning by evaluating whether randomly initialized networks can achieve competitive performance with confidence-based sampling.
AI.method: Evaluate three sampling strategies—high confidence (HC), low confidence (LC), and a hybrid HCLC (high confidence early, low confidence later)—on CNNs and transformers trained from random initialization, and compare against traditional AL frameworks that rely on initial candidate models; assess robustness across datasets.
AI.result: LC generally yields the best performance among the strategies, indicating that active learning effectiveness can be achieved without pre-trained candidate models; the approach demonstrates robustness and potential efficiency gains.
AI.conclusion: Challenging conventional AL pipelines, the work presents a streamlined, flexible, and potentially more efficient active learning paradigm that can extend across different data domains; further work should explore theoretical underpinnings, broader datasets, and comparisons with more AL baselines.

title: What if Tomorrow is the World Cup Final? Counterfactual Time Series Forecasting with Textual Conditions
summary: Time series forecasting has become increasingly critical in real-world scenarios, where future sequences are influenced not only by historical patterns but also by forthcoming events. In this context, forecasting must dynamically adapt to complex and stochastic future conditions, which introduces fundamental challenges in both forecasting and evaluation. Traditional methods typically rely on historical data or factual future conditions, while overlooking counterfactual scenarios. Furthermore, many existing approaches are restricted to simple structured conditions, limiting their ability to generalize to the real-world complexities. To address these gaps, we introduce the task of counterfactual time series forecasting with textual conditions, enabling more flexible and condition-aware forecasting. We propose a comprehensive evaluation framework that encompasses both factual and counterfactual settings, even in the absence of ground truth time series. Additionally, we present a novel text-attribution mechanism that distinguishes mutable from immutable factors, thereby improving forecast accuracy under sophisticated and stochastic textual conditions. The project page is at https://seqml.github.io/TADiff/

AI.tldr: Proposes counterfactual time series forecasting with textual conditions, plus a comprehensive evaluation framework for factual and counterfactual settings and a text-attribution mechanism to separate mutable from immutable factors.
AI.motivation: Forecasting futures influenced by upcoming events requires handling counterfactual scenarios and complex textual conditions; existing methods rely on historical data or factual futures and struggle to generalize under realistic, stochastic conditions.
AI.method: Defines the task of counterfactual time series forecasting with textual conditions, develops an evaluation framework applicable to both factual and counterfactual settings (even without ground-truth series), and introduces a text-attribution mechanism to distinguish mutable versus immutable factors.
AI.result: Proposal introducing the task, framework, and attribution method; no empirical results stated in the abstract; project page provided for implementation details.
AI.conclusion: Enables more flexible, condition-aware forecasting under sophisticated textual conditions and stochastic future scenarios; broadens applicability of time series forecasting to counterfactual contexts.

title: Agentifying Patient Dynamics within LLMs through Interacting with Clinical World Model
summary: Sepsis management in the ICU requires sequential treatment decisions under rapidly evolving patient physiology. Although large language models (LLMs) encode broad clinical knowledge and can reason over guidelines, they are not inherently grounded in action-conditioned patient dynamics. We introduce SepsisAgent, a world model-augmented LLM agent for sepsis treatment recommendation. SepsisAgent uses a learned Clinical World Model to simulate patient responses under candidate fluid--vasopressor interventions, and follows a propose--simulate--refine workflow before committing to a prescription. We first show that world-model access alone yields inconsistent LLM decision performance, motivating agent-specific training. We then train SepsisAgent through a three-stage curriculum: patient-dynamics supervised fine-tuning, propose--simulate--refine behavior cloning, and world-model-based agentic reinforcement learning. On MIMIC-IV sepsis trajectories, SepsisAgent outperforms all traditional RL and LLM-based baselines in off-policy value while achieving the best safety profile under guideline adherence and unsafe-action metrics. Further analysis shows that repeated interaction with the Clinical World Model enables the agent to learn regularities in patient evolution, which remain useful even when simulator access is removed.

AI.tldr: SepsisAgent augments an LLM with a clinical world model to simulate patient responses to interventions, enabling a propose–simulate–refine workflow and achieving superior off-policy performance and safety on MIMIC-IV sepsis data.
AI.motivation: LLMs encode general clinical knowledge but lack grounding in action-conditioned patient dynamics; a world-model-augmented agent can reason about sequential treatments and patient physiology under uncertainty.
AI.method: Train a Clinical World Model to simulate responses to fluid and vasopressor interventions; implement a three-stage curriculum: patient-dynamics supervised fine-tuning; propose–simulate–refine behavior cloning; world-model-based agentic reinforcement learning; run on MIMIC-IV sepsis trajectories; compare to RL and LLM baselines.
AI.result: SepsisAgent outperforms baselines in off-policy value and exhibits best safety under guideline-adherence and unsafe-action metrics; additional analysis shows world-model interaction helps the agent learn regularities of patient evolution that persist after simulator access is removed.
AI.conclusion: Grounding LLM-based agents in learned clinical world models enables safer, more effective sepsis treatment recommendations; agentic training improves robustness and generalization beyond the simulator, with potential applicability to other time-evolving clinical conditions.

title: Collaborative Yet Personalized Policy Training: Single-Timescale Federated Actor-Critic
summary: Despite the popularity of the actor-critic method and the practical needs of collaborative policy training, existing works typically either overlook environmental heterogeneity or give up personalization altogether by training a single shared policy across all agents. We consider a federated actor-critic framework in which agents share a common linear subspace representation while maintaining personalized local policy components, and agents iteratively estimate the common subspace, local critic heads, and local policies (i.e., actors). Under canonical single-timescale updates with Markovian sampling, we establish finite-time convergence via a novel joint linear approximation framework. Specifically, we show that the critic error converges to zero at the rate of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/((1-γ)^4\sqrt{TK}))$, and the policy gradient norm converges to zero at the rate of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/((1-γ)^6\sqrt{TK}))$, where $T$ is the number of rounds, $K$ is the number of agents, and $γ\in (0,1)$ is the discount factor. These results demonstrate linear speedup with respect to the number of agents $K$, despite heterogeneous Markovian trajectories under distinct transition kernels and coupled learning dynamics. To address these challenges, we develop a new perturbation analysis for the projected subspace updates and QR decomposition steps, together with conditional mixing arguments for heterogeneous Markovian noise. Furthermore, to handle the additional complications induced by policy updates and temporal dependence, we establish fine-grained characterizations of the discrepancies between function evaluations under Markovian sampling and under temporally frozen policies. Experiments instantiate the framework within PPO on federated \texttt{Hopper-v5} action-map heterogeneity, showing gains over Single PPO and FedAvg PPO and downstream transfer from the learned shared trunk.

AI.tldr: Federated actor-critic with a shared linear subspace and personalized local policies; finite-time convergence with rates that scale favorably with the number of agents, showing linear speedup; novel perturbation and mixing analyses; PPO experiments on Hopper-v5 demonstrate gains.
AI.motivation: Address environmental heterogeneity and the need for personalization in multi-agent RL by combining federated learning concepts (shared trunk) with actor-critic methods, enabling efficient learning across heterogeneous agents.
AI.method: Agents share a common linear subspace representation while each agent maintains a local critic head and policy. They iteratively estimate the shared subspace, local critics, and local policies under single-timescale updates with Markovian sampling. The analysis introduces a joint linear-approximation framework, perturbation analysis for projected subspace updates and QR decomposition, conditional mixing arguments for heterogeneous Markovian noise, and fine-grained comparisons between Markovian sampling and temporally frozen policies.
AI.result: The critic error converges to zero at rate ~O~(1/((1-γ)^4 sqrt(TK))) and the policy-gradient norm converges at ~O~(1/((1-γ)^6 sqrt(TK))). This yields linear speedup in the number of agents K and handles heterogeneity in trajectories. The work provides new perturbation and mixing tools. Empirically, PPO on Hopper-v5 with action-map heterogeneity shows gains over Single PPO and FedAvg PPO and benefits from transferring the learned shared trunk.
AI.conclusion: A principled federated actor-critic framework with a shared subspace and personalized components achieves both theoretical convergence guarantees and practical gains in heterogeneous multi-agent RL, with scalable performance and transfer benefits.

title: XDomainBench: Diagnosing Reasoning Collapse in High-Dimensional Scientific Knowledge Composition
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed for knowledge synthesis, yet their capacity for compositional generalization in scientific knowledge remains under-characterized. Existing benchmarks primarily focus on single-turn restricted scenarios, failing to capture the capability boundaries exposed by real-world interactive scientific workflows. To address this, we introduce XDomainBench, a diagnostic benchmark for interactive interdisciplinary scientific reasoning. We formalize the composition order and mixture structure to enable systematic stress-testing from single-discipline to inter-disciplinary, comprising 8,598 interactive sessions across 20 domains and 4 task categories, with 8 realistic trajectory patterns covering difficulty and domain-mixture dynamics, simulating real AI4S scenarios. Large-scale evaluation of LLMs reveals a systematic reasoning collapse as composition order increases, stemming from two root causes: (i) direct difficulty increases induced by domain composition, and (ii) indirect interaction-amplified failures where trajectory patterns trigger error accumulation, reasoning breaks, and domain confusion, ultimately leading to session collapse.

AI.tldr: XDomainBench is a diagnostic benchmark for interactive interdisciplinary scientific reasoning. It reveals systematic reasoning collapse in LLMs as domain composition increases, demonstrated across 8,598 sessions in 20 domains with 8 trajectory patterns. Two root causes identified: direct difficulty from domain composition and indirect interaction-amplified failures leading to session collapse.
AI.motivation: To characterize and stress-test compositional generalization of LLMs in interactive scientific workflows, addressing the gap where existing benchmarks focus on single-turn, single-domain tasks.
AI.method: Introduce XDomainBench by formalizing composition order and mixture structure; construct 8,598 interactive sessions across 20 domains and 4 task categories, with 8 trajectory patterns capturing difficulty and domain-mixing dynamics; simulate real AI4S scenarios; conduct large-scale evaluation of LLMs.
AI.result: LLMs show systematic reasoning collapse as composition order increases, due to (i) direct difficulty from domain composition and (ii) indirect interaction-amplified failures (error accumulation, reasoning breakdown, domain confusion) triggered by trajectory patterns, culminating in session collapse.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark and evaluation framework illuminates limitations in contemporary LLMs for interactive interdisciplinary reasoning and motivates development of models and evaluation protocols that better handle cross-domain, multi-turn workflows and trajectory-dependent failures.

title: Generating HDR Video from SDR Video
summary: The high dynamic range (HDR) video ecosystem is approaching maturity, but the problem of upconverting legacy standard dynamic range (SDR) videos persists without a convincing solution. We propose a framework for HDR video synthesis from casual SDR footage by leveraging large-scale generative video models. We introduce a Multi-Exposure Video Model (MEVM) that can predict exposure-bracketed linear SDR video sequences from a single nonlinear SDR video input. We further propose a learnable Video Merging Model (VMM) that merges the predicted exposure-bracketed video into a high-quality HDR sequence while preserving detail in both shadows and highlights. Extensive experiments, quantitative and qualitative evaluation, and a user study demonstrate that our approach enables robust HDR conversion for in-the-wild examples from casual consumer videos and even iconic films. Finally, our model can support HDR synthesis pipelines built upon existing SDR generative video models. Output HDR videos can be viewed on our supplementary webpage: sdr2hdrvideo.github.io

AI.tldr: Introduces MEVM and VMM to synthesize HDR video from SDR input using large generative video models, enabling robust SDR-to-HDR upconversion and HDR-specific merging.
AI.motivation: HDR upconversion of legacy SDR video remains unsolved despite mature HDR ecosystems. The work aims to convert casual SDR footage into HDR reliably by leveraging large-scale generative video models.
AI.method: Proposes a two-component framework: (1) Multi-Exposure Video Model (MEVM) that predicts exposure-bracketed linear SDR sequences from a single nonlinear SDR input; (2) a learnable Video Merging Model (VMM) that fuses these sequences into a high-quality HDR video while preserving detail in shadows and highlights. The approach supports integration with existing SDR generative video models and is validated through extensive quantitative, qualitative, and user studies.
AI.result: Demonstrates robust HDR conversion on in-the-wild casual videos and iconic films. Outputs can be used in HDR synthesis pipelines, with results showcased on a supplementary webpage.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables practical HDR synthesis from SDR footage by combining exposure-bracket prediction and learnable merging, and it is compatible with current SDR generative video models, broadening HDR content recovery from legacy sources.

title: FrontierSmith: Synthesizing Open-Ended Coding Problems at Scale
summary: Many real-world coding challenges are open-ended and admit no known optimal solution. Yet, recent progress in LLM coding has focused on well-defined tasks such as feature implementation, bug fixing, and competitive programming. Open-ended coding remains a weak spot for LLMs, largely because open-ended training problems are scarce and expensive to construct. Our goal is to synthesize open-ended coding problems at scale to train stronger LLM coders. We introduce FrontierSmith, an automated system for iteratively evolving open-ended problems from existing closed-ended coding tasks. Starting from competitive programming problems, FrontierSmith generates candidate open-ended variants by changing the problems'goals, restricting outputs, and generalizing inputs. It then uses a quantitative idea divergence metric to select problems that elicit genuinely diverse approaches from different solvers. Agents then generate test cases and verifiers for the surviving candidates. On two open-ended coding benchmarks, training on our synthesized data yields substantial gains over the base models: Qwen3.5-9B improves by +8.82 score on FrontierCS and +306.36 (Elo-rating-based performance) on ALE-bench; Qwen3.5-27B improves by +12.12 and +309.12, respectively. The synthesized problems also make agents take more turns and use more tokens, similar to human-curated ones, suggesting that closed-ended seeds can be a practical starting point for long-horizon coding data.

AI.tldr: FrontierSmith automatically evolves open-ended coding problems from existing closed-ended tasks to train stronger LLM coders, showing substantial gains on two open-ended benchmarks by synthesizing problems, test cases, and verifiers.
AI.motivation: Open-ended coding tasks are scarce and expensive to construct; increasing the diversity and quantity of open-ended data is critical to improving LLM coding performance, especially for long-horizon reasoning.
AI.method: Starting from closed-ended competitive programming tasks, FrontierSmith iteratively generates open-ended variants by changing goals, output restrictions, and input generalization; it uses a quantitative idea-divergence metric to select diverse problems that elicit different solver approaches; agents create test cases and verifiers for the surviving problems; synthesized data is used to train state-of-the-art LLMs.
AI.result: On two open-ended benchmarks (FrontierCS and ALE-bench), models trained on synthesized data show strong gains: Qwen3.5-9B +8.82 FCS score and +306.36 Elo; Qwen3.5-27B +12.12 FCS score and +309.12 Elo; synthesized problems also drive longer solution trees and higher token usage, akin to human-curated datasets.
AI.conclusion: Closed-ended seeds can be a practical starting point for scalable open-ended coding data; FrontierSmith demonstrates effective data synthesis that improves open-ended coding performance, suggesting potential for broader applicability.

title: Probabilistic Verification of Recurrent Neural Networks for Single and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted at the 35th International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IJCAI) 2026
summary: History-dependent policies induced by recurrent neural networks (RNNs) rely on latent hidden state dynamics, making verification in partially observable reinforcement learning (RL) challenging. Existing RNN verification tools typically rely on restrictive modeling assumptions or coarse over-approximations of the hidden state space, which can lead to overly conservative or inconclusive results. We propose $\textbf{RNN}$ $\textbf{Pro}$babilistic $\textbf{Ve}$rification ($\texttt{RNN-ProVe}$), a probabilistic framework that $\textit{estimates the likelihood}$ of undesired behaviors in RNN-based policies. $\texttt{RNN-ProVe}$ uses policy-driven sampling to approximate the set of hidden states that are feasible under a trained policy, and derives statistical error bounds to produce bounded-error, high-confidence estimates of behavioral violations. Experiments on partially observable single-agent and cooperative multi-agent tasks show that $\texttt{RNN-ProVe}$ yields more quantitative, feasibility-aware probabilistic guarantees than existing tools, while scaling to recurrent and multi-agent settings.

AI.tldr: RNN-ProVe is a probabilistic verification framework for RNN-based policies in partially observable RL. It uses policy-driven sampling to estimate the likelihood of undesired behaviors with bounded error and high confidence, scalable to single-agent and multi-agent settings, and provides quantitative guarantees superior to existing tools.
AI.motivation: Verifying history-dependent policies (RNN-based) in partially observable RL is difficult due to latent hidden-state dynamics. Existing verification tools rely on restrictive assumptions or coarse over-approximations, yielding overly conservative or inconclusive results.
AI.method: RNN-ProVe employs policy-driven sampling to approximate the feasible hidden-state set under a trained policy and derives statistical error bounds to produce bounded-error, high-confidence estimates of behavioral violations.
AI.result: Experiments on partially observable single-agent and cooperative multi-agent tasks show that RNN-ProVe yields more quantitative, feasibility-aware probabilistic guarantees than existing tools and scales to recurrent and multi-agent settings.
AI.conclusion: RNN-ProVe offers a scalable probabilistic verification framework with bounded-error guarantees for RNN-based policies in partially observable environments, addressing limitations of prior tools and enabling more precise, actionable guarantees.

title: When Answers Stray from Questions: Hallucination Detection via Question-Answer Orthogonal Decomposition
summary: Hallucination detection in large language models (LLMs) requires balancing accu racy, efficiency, and robustness to distribution shift. Black-box consistency methods are effective but demand repeated inference; single-pass white-box probes are effi cient yet treat answer representations in isolation, often degrading sharply under domain shift. We propose QAOD (Question-Answer Orthogonal Decomposition), a single-pass framework that projects away the question-aligned direction from the answer representation to obtain a question-orthogonal component that suppresses domain-conditioned variation. To identify informative signals, QAOD further selects layers via diversity-penalized Fisher scoring and discriminative neurons via Fisher importance. To address both in-domain detection and cross-domain generalization, we design two complementary probing strategies: pairing the or thogonal component with question context yields a joint probe that maximizes in-domain discriminability, while using the orthogonal component alone preserves domain-agnostic factuality signals for robust transfer. QAOD's joint probe achieves the best in-domain AUROC across all evaluated model-dataset pairs, while the orthogonal-only probe delivers the strongest OOD transfer, surpassing the best white-box baseline by up to 21% on BioASQ at under 25% of generation cost.

AI.tldr: QAOD: a single-pass white-box probing framework for LLM hallucination detection that orthogonally projects the answer representation from the question, using Fisher-based layer and neuron selection; introduces two complementary probing strategies (joint with question context for strong in-domain AUROC; orthogonal-only for robust OOD transfer); achieves state-of-the-art in-domain performance and robust OOD transfer with lower computation than baselines (BioASQ gain up to 21% with <25% generation cost).
AI.motivation: Hallucination detection must trade off accuracy, efficiency, and robustness to distribution shift. Black-box methods are costly; white-box single-pass probes can be brittle under domain shift. Need a method that isolates question-aligned vs question-orthogonal information and selects informative layers/neurons.
AI.method: Compute answer representation, project out question-aligned direction to obtain question-orthogonal component. Use diversity-penalized Fisher scoring to select layers and Fisher importance to select discriminative neurons. Two probes: (A) joint probe combining the orthogonal component with question context to maximize in-domain discriminability; (B) orthogonal component alone to maintain domain-agnostic factual signals. Evaluate on multiple model-dataset pairs; compare to white-box baselines.
AI.result: Joint probe achieves best in-domain AUROC across evaluated pairs. Orthogonal-only probe delivers strongest OOD transfer, outperforming best white-box baseline by up to 21% on BioASQ, with generation cost under 25% of baseline.
AI.conclusion: QAOD offers a computationally efficient, robust single-pass probing framework for hallucination detection, with complementary probes for in-domain and cross-domain settings; promising for robust LLM evaluation with reduced inference cost.

title: AI Outperforms Humans in Personalized Image Aesthetics Assessment via LLM-Based Interviews and Semantic Feature Extraction
summary: Accurately predicting individual aesthetic evaluation for images is a fundamental challenge for AI. Various deep learning (DL)-based models have been proposed for this task, training on image evaluation data to extract objective low-level features. However, aesthetic preferences are inherently subjective and individual-dependent. Accurate prediction thus requires the extraction of high-level semantic features of images and the active collection of preference information from the target individual. To address this issue, we focus on the utility of Large Language Models (LLMs) pretrained on vast amounts of textual data, and develop an integrated DL-LLM system. The system actively elicits aesthetic preferences through LLM-based semi-structured interviews and predicts aesthetic evaluation by leveraging both low-level and high-level features. In our experiments, we compare the proposed system against conventional systems, human predictors, and the target individual's own re-evaluations after a certain time interval. Our results show that the proposed system outperforms all of them, with particularly strong performance on highly-rated images. Moreover, the prediction error of the proposed system is smaller than within-person variability, while human predictors show the largest error, likely due to the influence of their own aesthetic values. These results suggest that AI may be better positioned than others or one's future self to capture individual aesthetic preferences at a given point. This opens a new question of whether AI could serve as a deeper interpreter of human aesthetic sensibility than humans themselves.

AI.tldr: An integrated DL-LLM system predicts individual aesthetic evaluation by combining low/high-level visual features with LLM-driven, semi-structured interviews to elicit preferences, outperforming baselines and intra-person variability.
AI.motivation: Aesthetic preferences are subjective and variable; purely low-level DL features fail to capture high-level semantics and individual tastes. Leveraging LLMs trained on broad textual data can elicit and model personal preferences to improve prediction.
AI.method: Develop a DL-LLM pipeline that (1) uses DL to extract low-level image features and high-level semantic features; (2) conducts LLM-based semi-structured interviews to actively elicit target individual preferences; (3) fuses features to predict aesthetic evaluation; (4) evaluates against conventional systems, human predictors, and the target's own re-evaluations over time.
AI.result: The proposed system outperforms all baselines, with particularly strong performance on highly-rated images. Prediction error is smaller than within-person variability, while human predictors show the largest error due to their own aesthetic values.
AI.conclusion: AI may better capture an individual's aesthetic preferences at a given time than others or their future self, suggesting AI could serve as a deeper interpreter of human aesthetic sensibility and prompting further exploration of AI-based preference modeling.

title: Towards Continuous Sign Language Conversation from Isolated Signs
summary: Sign language is the primary language for many Deaf and Hard-of-Hearing (DHH) signers, yet most conversational AI systems still mediate interaction through spoken or written language. This spoken-language-centered interface can limit access for signers for whom spoken or written language is not the most accessible medium, motivating direct sign-to-sign conversational modeling. However, sentence-level sign video data are expensive to collect and annotate, leaving existing sign translation and production models with limited vocabulary coverage and weak open-domain generalization. We address this bottleneck by constructing continuous sign conversations from isolated signs: large-scale labeled isolated clips are collected as lexically grounded motion primitives and recomposed into sign-language-ordered utterances derived from existing dialogue corpora. We introduce SignaVox-W, which provides, to our knowledge, the largest labeled isolated-sign vocabulary to date, and SignaVox-U, a continuous 3D sign conversation dataset built from SignaVox-W. To bridge structural mismatch between spoken and signed languages, we use a retrieval-guided spoken-to-gloss translator; to bridge independently collected isolated clips, we propose BRAID, a diffusion Transformer that performs duration alignment and co-articulatory boundary inpainting. With the resulting data, we train SignaVox, a direct sign-to-sign conversational model that generates 3D body, hand, and facial motion responses from prior signing context without spoken-language text or externally provided glosses at inference time. Quantitative and qualitative evaluations show improved isolated-to-continuous motion quality, stronger response-level semantic alignment, and scalable signer-centered interaction that better supports visual-spatial articulation.

AI.tldr: Introduces SignaVox-W (large labeled isolated-sign vocabulary) and SignaVox-U (continuous 3D sign conversations) and a full pipeline to build continuous sign dialogue from isolated signs, enabling direct sign-to-sign conversation without spoken text or glosses.
AI.motivation: To overcome the data bottleneck and limited vocabulary of sign-language datasets: sentence-level sign video data are scarce and expensive, hindering open-domain, signer-centered sign language models.
AI.method: Construct continuous sign conversations from isolated signs by compiling large-scale labeled sign primitives and recombining them into utterances from dialogue corpora. Introduce SignaVox-W and SignaVox-U. Use a retrieval-guided spoken-to-gloss translator to bridge spoken language and gloss, and BRAID, a diffusion Transformer for duration alignment and co-articulatory boundary inpainting to align independently collected clips. Train SignaVox, a direct sign-to-sign model that generates 3D motion (body, hands, face) from prior signing context without text or glosses at inference.
AI.result: Quantitative and qualitative evaluations show improved isolated-to-continuous motion quality, stronger semantic alignment at the response level, and scalable signer-centered interaction. Demonstrates the feasibility of end-to-end sign-to-sign conversation with large vocabulary and open-domain potential.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a scalable pipeline and datasets enabling direct sign-to-sign dialogue, addressing data scarcity and reducing reliance on spoken text or glosses, with strong implications for natural, signer-centered ASL/I sign-language interfaces.

title: LiSA: Lifelong Safety Adaptation via Conservative Policy Induction
summary: As AI agents move from chat interfaces to systems that read private data, call tools, and execute multi-step workflows, guardrails become a last line of defense against concrete deployment harms. In these settings, guardrail failures are no longer merely answer-quality errors: they can leak secrets, authorize unsafe actions, or block legitimate work. The hardest failures are often contextual: whether an action is acceptable depends on local privacy norms, organizational policies, and user expectations that resist pre-deployment specification. This creates a practical gap: guardrails must adapt to their own operating environments, yet deployment feedback is typically limited to sparse, noisy user-reported failures, and repeated fine-tuning is often impractical. To address this gap, we propose LiSA (Lifelong Safety Adaptation), a conservative policy induction framework that improves a fixed base guardrail through structured memory. LiSA converts occasional failures into reusable policy abstractions so that sparse reports can generalize beyond individual cases, adds conflict-aware local rules to prevent overgeneralization in mixed-label contexts, and applies evidence-aware confidence gating via a posterior lower bound, so that memory reuse scales with accumulated evidence rather than empirical accuracy alone. Across PrivacyLens+, ConFaide+, and AgentHarm, LiSA consistently outperforms strong memory-based baselines under sparse feedback, remains robust under noisy user feedback even at 20% label-flip rates, and pushes the latency--performance frontier beyond backbone model scaling. Ultimately, LiSA offers a practical path to secure AI agents against the unpredictable long tail of real-world edge risks.

AI.tldr: LiSA is a lifelong safety adaptation framework that enhances a fixed guardrail with memory-based policy abstractions, conflict-aware local rules, and evidence-aware confidence gating to adapt to deployment environments under sparse or noisy feedback.
AI.motivation: Guardrails must adapt to local privacy norms, organizational policies, and user expectations; deployment feedback is sparse and noisy, making continuous fine-tuning impractical; conventional memory-based baselines struggle with long-tail, edge-case failures.
AI.method: LiSA induces conservative policy updates by converting failures into reusable policy abstractions stored in structured memory, adds conflict-aware local rules to prevent overgeneralization in mixed-label contexts, and uses posterior lower-bound-based confidence gating to control memory reuse based on accumulated evidence; evaluated on PrivacyLens+, ConFaide+, and AgentHarm.
AI.result: LiSA consistently outperforms strong memory-based baselines under sparse feedback, remains robust under up to 20% label-flip noise, and advances the latency–performance frontier as backbone model scale increases.
AI.conclusion: LiSA offers a practical, scalable path to securing AI agents against long-tail, real-world edge risks by enabling adaptive, evidence-driven guardrails that evolve with deployment environments.

title: MediaClaw: Multimodal Intelligent-Agent Platform Technical Report
summary: MediaClaw is a multimodal agent platform built on the OpenClaw ecosystem. Its core design follows a three-layer architecture of unified abstraction, pluginized extension, and workflow orchestration. The system is intended to address practical deployment pain points in AIGC adoption, including fragmented capabilities, heterogeneous interfaces, disconnected production processes, and limited reuse of high-quality production workflows. \system{} abstracts full-category AIGC capabilities into a unified invocation model, uses plugins to support hot-pluggable capability expansion, and uses task-oriented Skills to turn complex production processes into reusable workflow assets. This report focuses on the architectural design philosophy of MediaClaw, the design logic of its core capability model, and the key engineering trade-offs in implementation. It aims to provide reusable practical reference for building multimodal capability platforms.

AI.tldr: MediaClaw proposes a three-layer multimodal platform (unified invocation, plugin extension, and task-oriented Skills) to address AIGC deployment pain points by unifying capabilities, enabling hot-pluggable extensions, and turning complex workflows into reusable assets; the paper provides architectural principles and trade-offs to guide building such platforms.
AI.motivation: Fragmented AIGC capabilities, heterogeneous interfaces, disconnected production processes, and limited reuse of production workflows hinder practical AIGC adoption. A unified, extensible platform could streamline deployment, integration, and reuse across teams.
AI.method: Propose a three-layer architecture: (1) unified invocation model that abstracts full-category AIGC capabilities, (2) plugin-based extension for hot-pluggable capability expansion, and (3) task-oriented Skills to convert production processes into reusable workflow assets. The report discusses the design philosophy, core capability model, and engineering trade-offs, offering implementation guidance and practical considerations for building multimodal capability platforms.
AI.result: A reusable practical reference for constructing multimodal capability platforms, demonstrated through the MediaClaw design as a blueprint for integration, extensibility, and workflow reuse.
AI.conclusion: The architecture provides a robust, extensible framework to integrate diverse AIGC capabilities, with a focus on reducing deployment pain points and enabling reusable workflows; it aims to serve as a practical reference for practitioners building multimodal capability platforms.

title: StyleTextGen: Style-Conditioned Multilingual Scene Text Generation
comment: This paper has been accepted to CVPR 2026
summary: Style-conditioned scene text generation faces unique challenges in extracting precise text styles from complex backgrounds and maintaining fine-grained style consistency across characters, especially for multilingual scripts. We propose StyleTextGen, a novel framework that learns to perceive and replicate visual text styles across different languages and writing systems. Our approach features three key contributions: First, we introduce a dual-branch style encoder dedicated to style modeling, yielding robust multilingual text style representations in complex real-world scenes. Second, we design a text style consistency loss that enhances style coherence and improves overall visual quality. Third, we develop a mask-guided inference strategy that ensures precise style alignment between generated and reference text. To facilitate systematic evaluation, we construct StyleText-CE, a bilingual scene text style benchmark covering both monolingual and cross-lingual settings. Extensive experiments demonstrate that StyleTextGen significantly outperforms existing methods in style consistency and cross-lingual generalization, establishing new state-of-the-art performance in multilingual style-conditioned text generation.

AI.tldr: A multilingual style-conditioned scene text generation framework (StyleTextGen) with a dual-branch style encoder, a style-consistency loss, and a mask-guided inference strategy, plus a bilingual StyleText-CE benchmark, achieving state-of-the-art cross-lingual performance.
AI.motivation: To accurately extract and replicate visual text styles from complex real-world scenes across languages, addressing the challenge of maintaining fine-grained, cross-lingual style consistency in scene text generation.
AI.method: Proposes StyleTextGen with (1) a dual-branch style encoder for robust multilingual style representations, (2) a text style consistency loss to enforce coherence across characters, and (3) a mask-guided inference strategy for precise style alignment. Additionally, introduces StyleText-CE, a bilingual benchmark for monolingual and cross-lingual evaluation.
AI.result: Experimental results show StyleTextGen outperforms baselines in style consistency and cross-lingual generalization, achieving new state-of-the-art performance on multilingual style-conditioned text generation.
AI.conclusion: StyleTextGen advances multilingual scene text generation by improving style representation, coherence, and cross-lingual transfer, supported by a dedicated bilingual benchmark that facilitates systematic evaluation.

title: Focused PU learning from imbalanced data
summary: We propose a new method of learning from positive and unlabeled (PU) examples in highly imbalanced datasets. Many real-world problems, such as disease gene identification, targeted marketing, fraud detection, and recommender systems, are hard to address with machine learning methods, due to limited labeled data. Often, training data comprises positive and unlabeled instances, the latter typically being dominated by negative, but including also several positive instances. While PU learning is well-studied, few methods address imbalanced settings or hard-to-detect positive examples that resemble negative ones. Our approach uses a focused empirical risk estimator, incorporating both positive and unlabeled examples to train binary classifiers. Empirical evaluations demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on imbalanced datasets under two labeling mechanisms - selecting positives completely at random (SCAR) and selecting at random (SAR). Beyond these controlled experiments, we demonstrate the value of the proposed method in the real-world application of financial misstatement detection.

AI.tldr: A new PU learning method for imbalanced data using a focused empirical risk estimator that leverages positive and unlabeled data under SCAR and SAR, achieving state-of-the-art results and applied to financial misstatement detection.
AI.motivation: Imbalanced positive-unlabeled (PU) problems where positives are rare and can resemble negatives; existing PU approaches struggle with severe imbalance and hard-to-detect positives, necessitating robust risk estimation that leverages unlabeled data.
AI.method: A focused empirical risk estimator that uses both labeled positives and unlabeled data to train a binary classifier, suitable for PU settings under SCAR and SAR labeling mechanisms.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on imbalanced PU benchmarks under SCAR and SAR; demonstrates practical value in financial misstatement detection.
AI.conclusion: The method effectively addresses PU with severe imbalance and hard positives, improving detection performance in real-world applications such as finance.

title: Identifying Culprits Through Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient Deep Learning Investigation
summary: In the world of AI and advanced technologies investigation aspects identification of a crime or criminal plays a major problem. In this research we focus on a Conventional ways of implicating criminal investigations usually rely on limited data analysis. Finding an optimal and efficient method that will effectively identify criminals from complex datasets and minimise false positives and false negatives is the considered as a challenge. The main novelty approach of this work is based on the deep learning algorithm Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) is presented in this paper. We train the DDPG model with a dataset of crime scene material, witness statements and suspect profiles. The algorithm uses features to maximise the likelihood of identifying the offender while minimising the noise impact and irrelevant data. We show the efficacy of the proposed method, where DDPG identified criminals with an amazing accuracy of 95% than other several existing methods.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) approach to identify criminals from crime data with claimed 95% accuracy, but methodological details are insufficient.
AI.motivation: Improve accuracy and reduce false positives/negatives in criminal identification beyond conventional data analysis.
AI.method: Train a DDPG model on features from crime scene materials, witness statements, suspect profiles; aims to maximize offender identification likelihood while minimizing noise/irrelevance.
AI.result: Reported accuracy of 95% outperforming several existing methods.
AI.conclusion: While results are promising, the abstract lacks critical methodological and evaluation details; scientific rigor and ethical considerations require clarification.

title: Breaking Dual Bottlenecks: Evolving Unified Multimodal Models into Self-Adaptive Interleaved Visual Reasoners
summary: Recent unified models integrate multimodal understanding and generation within a single framework. However, an "understanding-generation gap" persists, where models can capture user intent but often fail to translate this semantic knowledge into precise pixel-level manipulation. This gap results in two bottlenecks in anything-to-image task (X2I): the attention entanglement bottleneck, where blind planning struggles with complex prompts, and the visual refinement bottleneck, where unstructured feedback fails to correct imperfections efficiently. In this paper, we propose a novel framework that empowers unified models to autonomously switch between generation strategies based on instruction complexity and model capability. To achieve this, we construct a hierarchical data pipeline that constructs execution paths across three adaptive modes: direct generation for simple cases, self-reflection for quality refinement, and multi-step planning for decomposing complex scenarios. Building on this pipeline, we contribute a high-quality dataset with over 50,000 samples and implement a two-stage training strategy comprising SFT and RL. Specifically, we design step-wise reasoning rewards to ensure logical consistency and intra-group complexity penalty to prevent redundant computational overhead. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms existing baselines on X2I, achieving superior generation fidelity among simple-to-complex instructions. The code is released at https://github.com/WeChatCV/Interleaved_Visual_Reasoner.

AI.tldr: We propose an adaptive, hierarchical framework to close the understanding-generation gap in anything-to-image (X2I) tasks by switching among direct generation, self-reflection, and multi-step planning; introducing a hierarchical data pipeline and a 50k+ sample dataset, trained via SFT and RL with step-wise reasoning rewards and intra-group complexity penalties, achieving improved fidelity.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between semantic understanding and pixel-level manipulation in unified multimodal models, addressing attention entanglement bottlenecks and inefficient visual refinement in X2I tasks.
AI.method: Build a hierarchical data pipeline that enables three adaptive execution modes (direct generation, self-reflection, multi-step planning) and create a high-quality dataset (>50k samples). Train with a two-stage strategy: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by reinforcement learning (RL). Employ step-wise reasoning rewards to ensure logical consistency and intra-group complexity penalties to limit redundant computation.
AI.result: Empirical results show the proposed method outperforms baselines on X2I, achieving higher generation fidelity across simple-to-complex instructions; code is released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive switching among generation strategies, guided by instruction complexity and model capability, alongside a hierarchical data pipeline, enhances alignment from intent to pixel-level output and improves fidelity in X2I tasks.

title: Test-Time Learning with an Evolving Library
summary: We introduce EvoLib, a test-time learning framework that enables large language models to accumulate, reuse, and evolve knowledge across problem instances without parameter updates or external supervision. Instead of adapting model parameters, our approach maintains a shared library of knowledge abstractions, including modular skills and reflective insights, automatically extracted from the model's own inference trajectories. To support continual improvement, we introduce a principled weighting and consolidation mechanism that jointly optimizes for immediate utility and long-term value. This allows simple, instance-specific abstractions to evolve into more general and reusable ones over time. Across challenging benchmarks in mathematical reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn agentic environments, EvoLib improves substantially over the top test-time scaling and learning methods without ground-truth feedback.

AI.tldr: EvoLib enables test-time learning for LLMs by building a shared library of knowledge abstractions (modular skills, reflective insights) extracted from inference trajectories, allowing accumulation and evolution of knowledge without parameter updates or external supervision, and improves performance across math, code, and multi-turn tasks with no ground-truth feedback.
AI.motivation: To enable continual improvement and reuse of knowledge by LLMs at test time without costly parameter updates or supervised data, leveraging the model's own inference traces to extract reusable abstractions.
AI.method: Construct EvoLib, a test-time learning framework that maintains a centralized library of abstractions derived from the model’s inference trajectories. It includes modular skills and reflective insights and uses a principled weighting and consolidation mechanism that optimizes for immediate utility and long-term value, enabling instance-level abstractions to evolve into general, reusable concepts.
AI.result: EvoLib substantially outperforms top test-time scaling and learning methods on benchmarks in mathematical reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn agentic environments, without ground-truth feedback.
AI.conclusion: EvoLib demonstrates that non-parameter updates, guided by a knowledge consolidation strategy, can yield significant and transferable improvements by evolving simple, instance-specific abstractions into general, reusable knowledge over time.

title: A Heterogeneous Temporal Memory Governance Framework for Long-Term LLM Persona Consistency
comment: 23 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables. Preprint version. Code for ARPM v4.0 is available at: https://github.com/Spirtxiaoqi7/ARPM
summary: Large language models often suffer from fact loss, timeline confusion, persona drift, and reduced stability during long-range interaction, especially under high-noise knowledge bases, context clearing, and cross-model transfer. To address these issues, we introduce ARPM, an external temporal memory governance framework for long-term dialogue. ARPM separates static knowledge memory from dynamic dialogue experience memory and combines vector retrieval, BM25, RRF fusion, dual-temporal reranking, chronological evidence reading, and a controlled analysis protocol for evidence verification and answer binding. Unlike approaches that encode persona consistency into model weights or rely only on long context, ARPM treats continuity as a traceable, auditable, and transferable governance problem. Using engineering logs, we conduct three experiments. First, in a 50-round question-answering setting, we compare signal-to-noise ratios of 1:5 and 1:200+, and distinguish CSV auto-judgment from manual review. Under 1:5, CSV recall accuracy is 54.0%, while manual review raises it to 100.0%. Under 1:200+, the values are 44.0% and 80.0%. These results show that automatic rules can underestimate recall after supporting evidence enters the prompt. Second, ablation results show that dialogue history retrieval is necessary for recent continuity: disabling it reduces strict accuracy from 100% to 66.7%, and disabling BM25 reduces it to 80.0%, indicating that pure semantic retrieval is insufficient for correction and tracing. Third, under a 5.1-million-character noise substrate, periodic context clearing, and multi-model handoff, ARPM maintains semantic continuity, boundary continuity, and persona consistency, while exposing limits caused by weak protocol compliance. These findings show that long-term persona consistency can be decomposed into governable components and evaluated in a white-box manner.

AI.tldr: ARPM is a governance framework for long-term dialogue memory that separates static knowledge memory from dynamic dialogue experience memory and uses multi-faceted retrieval and verification (vector retrieval, BM25, RRF, dual-temporal reranking, chronological evidence reading) to maintain semantic and persona continuity under noise; three experiments show partial success and reveal limitations, supporting governance-as-auditability approach over weight-encoded persona consistency.
AI.motivation: To solve fact loss, timeline confusion, persona drift, and instability in long-range interactions when knowledge bases are noisy or when context is cleared or cross-model transfer occurs.
AI.method: ARPM architecture separates static knowledge memory from dynamic dialogue experience memory. It employs vector retrieval, BM25, and RRF fusion, dual-temporal reranking, chronological evidence reading, and a controlled analysis protocol for evidence verification and answer binding. Evaluation uses engineering logs with three experiments: (1) 50-round QA with varying signal-to-noise ratios (1:5 and 1:200+), comparing CSV auto-judgment versus manual review; (2) ablation on dialogue history retrieval and BM25; (3) stress test with 5.1M-character noise substrate, periodic context clearing, and multi-model handoffs; outcomes are analyzed in terms of semantic continuity, boundary continuity, and persona consistency.
AI.result: CSV recall under 1:5 noise is 54.0%; manual review raises it to 100.0%. Under 1:200+ noise, CSV is 44.0% and manual 80.0%. Ablations show history retrieval is critical (100% to 66.7% strict accuracy when disabled) and BM25 also contributes (80.0% when disabled). ARPM maintains continuity under heavy noise, but reveals limits linked to protocol compliance. Overall, long-term persona consistency can be decomposed into governable, auditable components rather than embedded in model weights.
AI.conclusion: Long-term persona consistency can be treated as a governance problem with auditable, transferable components. External memory governance (not just model weights or long context) can maintain continuity and reveal system limits, enabling white-box evaluation of sustained persona and semantic continuity.

title: Vision-Core Guided Contrastive Learning for Balanced Multi-modal Prognosis Prediction of Stroke
summary: Deep learning and multi-modal fusion have demonstrated transformative potential in medical diagnosis by integrating diverse data sources. However, accurate prognosis for ischemic stroke remains challenging due to limitations in existing multi-modal approaches. First, current methods are predominantly confined to dual-modal fusion, lacking a framework that effectively integrates the trifecta of medical images, structured clinical data, and unstructured text. Second, they often fail to establish deep bidirectional interactions between modalities; To address these critical gaps, this paper proposes a novel tri-modal fusion model for ischemic stroke prognosis. Our approach first enriches the data representation by employing a Large Language Model (LLM) to automatically generate semi-structured diagnostic text from brain MRIs. This process not only addresses the scarcity of expert annotations but also serves as a regularized semantic enhancement, improving multimodal fusion robustness. Furthermore, we design a core component termed the Vision-Conditioned Dual Alignment Fusion Module (VDAFM), which strategically uses visual features as a conditional prior to guide fine-grained interaction with the generated text. This module achieves a dynamic and profound fusion through a dual semantic alignment loss, effectively mitigating modal heterogeneity. Extensive experiments on a real-world clinical dataset demonstrate that our model achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: Tri-modal fusion for ischemic stroke prognosis using images, structured data, and LLM-generated semi-structured text, with a Vision-Conditioned Dual Alignment Fusion Module (VDAFM) and dual semantic alignment loss achieving state-of-the-art results on real-world data.
AI.motivation: Existing prognostic methods are mostly limited to dual-modal fusion and lack deep, bidirectional interactions among images, structured clinical data, and unstructured text. There is also a scarcity of expert-annotated data, hindering robust multimodal learning in ischemic stroke prognosis.
AI.method: 1) Use a Large Language Model (LLM) to generate semi-structured diagnostic text from brain MRIs to augment annotations and regularize semantics. 2) Introduce Vision-Conditioned Dual Alignment Fusion Module (VDAFM) that uses visual features as a conditional prior to guide fine-grained interaction with the generated text. 3) Apply a dual semantic alignment loss to mitigate modal heterogeneity and enable dynamic tri-modal fusion.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on a real-world clinical dataset show state-of-the-art performance for ischemic stroke prognosis using the proposed tri-modal fusion framework.
AI.conclusion: A tri-modal fusion framework that integrates MRI-derived text, images, and structured data, coupled with LLM-generated diagnostic text and VDAFM, yields robust prognosis and surpasses existing dual-modal approaches.

title: A Novel Schur-Decomposition-Based Weight Projection Method for Stable State-Space Neural-Network Architectures
comment: 32 pages, 13 figures. Source code at https://codeberg.org/sergiovaneg/SchurSS
summary: Building black-box models for dynamical systems from data is a challenging problem in machine learning, especially when asymptotic stability guarantees are required. In this paper, we introduce a novel stability-ensuring and backpropagation-compatible projection scheme based on the Schur decomposition for the state matrix of linear discrete-time state-space layers, as well as an alternative pre-factorized formulation of the methodology. The proposed methods dynamically project the quasi-triangular factor of the state matrix's real Schur decomposition onto its nearest stable peer, ensuring stable dynamics with minimal overparameterization. Experiments on synthetic linear systems demonstrate that the method achieves accuracy and convergence rates comparable to those of state-of-the-art stable-system identification techniques, despite a marginal increase in computational complexity.
