AI.result: On both camera-space and world-space benchmarks, FactorizedHMR remains competitive with strong baselines, with the clearest gains in occlusion-heavy recovery and drift-sensitive world-space metrics.
AI.conclusion: FactorizedHMR shows that decomposing the problem into a deterministic torso-root/anchor estimation and a probabilistic distal-articulation completion yields improved robustness to occlusion and drift, leveraging specialized supervision and a synthetic data pipeline to support diverse viewpoints.

title: Slower Generalization, Faster Memorization: A Sweet Spot in Algorithmic Learning
summary: Critical-data-size accounts of grokking suggest a natural post-threshold intuition: once training data is sufficient to identify the underlying rule, additional data should accelerate validation convergence. We show that this intuition can fail in a controlled structured-output task. In Needleman--Wunsch (NW) matrix generation, small Transformers reach high validation exact-match accuracy fastest at an intermediate dataset size, not at the largest one. Past this dataset-size sweet spot, generalization remains achievable but requires more gradient updates. Conversely, in the regime where partial validation competence first appears, larger datasets can require fewer updates to reach high training accuracy, suggesting that emerging rule structure can accelerate fitting beyond example-wise memorization. A multiplication baseline does not show the same post-threshold slowdown. These results separate the critical data size for the onset of generalization from the dataset size that optimizes update-based convergence, and identify structured-output tasks where learning the rule and completing exact-fitting can diverge.

AI.tldr: In a structured-output task (NW matrix generation) there is a nonmonotonic data-size effect: an intermediate dataset size yields the fastest validation exact-match, while larger datasets slow update-based convergence even though generalization remains possible. Conversely, when partial competence appears, larger datasets can reduce updates needed for high training accuracy. A simple multiplication baseline lacks this post-threshold slowdown. This shows that the onset of generalization can be decoupled from update efficiency, and rule-learning can diverge from exact-fitting.
AI.motivation: Test whether critical-data-size/grokking concepts apply to structured-output tasks and whether the onset of generalization can be separated from update-based convergence dynamics.
AI.method: Train small Transformers on Needleman–Wunsch (NW) matrix generation, vary dataset size, measure validation exact-match accuracy and the number of gradient updates to reach thresholds; compare to a multiplication baseline that controls for memorization.
AI.result: Validation exact-match is fastest at an intermediate dataset size; beyond that size, generalization is still possible but requires more updates. In the regime where partial validation competence appears, larger datasets can reduce the number of updates to reach high training accuracy. The multiplication baseline does not show the same post-threshold slowdown.
AI.conclusion: Critical data size for the onset of generalization is separable from the dataset size that optimizes update-based convergence. Structured-output tasks can exhibit a divergence between learning the rule and achieving exact-fitting, with data efficiency and training dynamics influenced by the underlying rule structure.

title: LPH-VTON: Resolving the Structure-Texture Dilemma of Virtual Try-On via Latent Process Handover
summary: Virtual Try-On (VTON) aims to synthesize photorealistic images of garments precisely aligned with a person's body and pose. Current diffusion-based methods, however, face a fundamental trade-off between structural integrity and textural fidelity. In this paper, we formalize this challenge as a consequence of complementary inductive biases inherent in prevailing architectures: models heavily reliant on spatial constraints naturally favor geometric alignment but often suppress textures, whereas models dominated by unconstrained generative priors excel at vibrant detail rendering but are prone to structural drift. Based on this diagnosis, we propose LPH-VTON, a new synergistic framework that resolves this tension within a single, continuous denoising process. LPH-VTON strategically decomposes the generation, leveraging a structure-biased model to establish a geometrically consistent latent scaffold in the early stages, before handing over control to a texture-biased model for high-fidelity detail rendering. Extensive experiments validate our approach. Our model achieves a superior Pareto-optimal balance, establishing new benchmarks in perceptual faithfulness while maintaining highly competitive structural alignment across the standard dataset VITON-HD, proving the efficacy of temporal architectural decoupling.

AI.tldr: LPH-VTON proposes a single diffusion-based VTON pipeline that decouples structure and texture generation temporally: a structure-biased stage forms a geometric scaffold early, followed by a texture-biased stage for high-fidelity details, yielding a Pareto-optimal balance on VITON-HD.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based VTON methods suffer a fundamental trade-off between structural integrity (geometric alignment) and textural fidelity (textures) due to complementary inductive biases in prevailing architectures. A unified approach that reconciles these biases is needed to improve perceptual faithfulness without sacrificing alignment.
AI.method: Within a single continuous denoising process, LPH-VTON uses a two-phase, temporally decoupled architecture: an early stage with a structure-biased model establishes a geometrically consistent latent scaffold; a later stage with a texture-biased model refines high-frequency textures. The handover occurs within the diffusion steps, achieving temporal architectural decoupling of structure and texture.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show a superior Pareto-optimal trade-off between structure and texture. The method achieves new benchmarks in perceptual faithfulness and maintains competitive structural alignment on the VITON-HD dataset, supporting the effectiveness of temporal architectural decoupling in diffusion-based VTON.
AI.conclusion: Temporal architectural decoupling within a single diffusion process can resolve the structure–texture trade-off in virtual try-on, delivering improved perceptual fidelity with preserved geometric alignment and offering potential applicability to other conditional generative tasks.

title: AQKA: Active Quantum Kernel Acquisition Under a Shot Budget
summary: Estimating an $N \times N$ quantum kernel from circuit fidelities requires $Θ(N^2 S)$ measurement shots, the dominant bottleneck for deployment on near-term hardware. Existing budget-saving methods (Nyström-QKE, ShoFaR, kernel-target alignment) sub-sample \emph{which} entries to measure but allocate shots \emph{uniformly} within their chosen subset, ignoring how much each entry drives the downstream classifier. We close this gap with two contributions. \textbf{First, a complete regime decomposition} for shot-budgeted quantum kernel learning: a principled menu of when each allocator wins. Our method, \emph{AQKA}, dominates the budget-limited regime ($B \lesssim 16 n_{\mathrm{pairs}}$) on sparse-sensitivity KRR, with the gap \emph{growing} from $+8$ to $+25$ pts over uniform as $N$ scales $225{\to}1000$ and reaching $+26$--$32$ pts on an \texttt{ibm\_pittsburgh} (156-qubit Heron) hardware kernel; Nyström-QKE wins at saturating budgets on planted-sparse via low-rank reconstruction; ShoFaR is competitive only at extreme low budgets. \textbf{Second, a closed-form pair-level acquisition theory}: $s_{ij}^{\star} \propto |g_{ij}|\sqrt{K_{ij}(1-K_{ij})}$ with explicit gradient $g_{ij}$ for KRR (Lemma~1, $|β_iα_j+β_jα_i|\sqrt{K_{ij}(1-K_{ij})}$) and SVM via the envelope theorem ($|η_i^*η_j^*|\sqrt{K_{ij}(1-K_{ij})}$); a \emph{corrected} sparsity-aware Cauchy--Schwarz rate $ρ\le 2m/N$ matching empirics (vs.\ the naive $m^2/N^2$); an explicit-constant plug-in regret bound (Theorem~2); and a tighter SVM ceiling $ρ^{\mathrm{SVM}} \le m_{\mathrm{sv}}^2/N^2$. We close with the first multi-seed live online adaptive shot allocation on quantum hardware: $+17.0 \pm 4.8$ pts at $N{=}20$ on \texttt{ibm\_aachen} ($3.5σ$, 5 seeds), with the advantage holding at $N{=}30$ at higher budget on \texttt{ibm\_berlin} ($+14.0 \pm 8.5$ pts, 5 seeds).

AI.tldr: AQKA delivers a regime-aware, budget-constrained acquisition framework for quantum kernel estimation, with a closed-form pairwise allocation rule and theoretical guarantees, yielding clear performance gains over Nyström-QKE and ShoFaR in the budgeted regime and validated by multi-seed hardware experiments.
AI.motivation: Estimating an N×N quantum kernel requires Θ(N^2 S) measurement shots; existing budget-saving methods sub-sample which entries to measure but allocate shots uniformly within the subset, ignoring how much each entry drives downstream classifier.
AI.method: Two contributions: (i) complete regime decomposition and AQKA, optimized for budget-limited regime; (ii) closed-form pair-level acquisition theory: s_ij^* ∝ |g_ij| sqrt(K_ij(1-K_ij)); gradient expressions for KRR and SVM; corrected sparsity-aware rate ρ ≤ 2m/N; plug-in regret bound (Theorem 2); tighter SVM ceiling; and a first multi-seed live online adaptive shot allocation on hardware.
AI.result: AQKA dominates budget-limited regime on sparse-sensitivity KRR (gap grows with N; 225→1000; +8 to +25 pts vs uniform; +26–32 pts on ibm_pittsburgh); Nyström-QKE wins at saturating budgets on planted-sparse; ShoFaR is competitive only at extreme low budgets; hardware experiments show +17.0 ± 4.8 pts at N=20 on ibm_aachen and +14.0 ± 8.5 pts at N=30 on ibm_berlin, with multiple seeds).
AI.conclusion: Provides principled, theory-backed, budget-aware quantum kernel learning with regime-aware allocator guidance and hardware-validated gains; clarifies when each budget allocator is advantageous and delivers closed-form acquisition strategies with provable guarantees.

title: Unlocking Complex Visual Generation via Closed-Loop Verified Reasoning
summary:
Despite rapid advancements, current text-to-image (T2I) models predominantly rely on a single-step generation paradigm, which struggles with complex semantics and faces diminishing returns from parameter scaling.
  While recent multi-step reasoning approaches show promise, they are hindered by ungrounded planning hallucinations lacking verification, monolithic post-hoc reflection, long-context optimization instabilities, and prohibitive inference latency. To overcome these bottlenecks, we propose the Closed-Loop Visual Reasoning (CLVR) framework, a comprehensive system that deeply couples visual-language logical planning with pixel-level diffusion generation. CLVR introduces an automated data engine with step-level visual verification to synthesize reliable reasoning trajectories, and proposes Proxy Prompt Reinforcement Learning (PPRL) to resolve long-context optimization instabilities by distilling interleaved multimodal histories into explicit reward signals for accurate causal attribution. Furthermore, to mitigate the severe latency bottleneck caused by iterative denoising, we propose $Δ$-Space Weight Merge (DSWM), a theoretically grounded method that fuses alignment weights with off-the-shelf distillation priors, reducing the per-step inference cost to just 4 NFEs without requiring expensive re-distillation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CLVR outperforms existing open-source baselines across multiple benchmarks and approaches the performance of proprietary commercial models, unlocking general test-time scaling capabilities for complex visual generation.

AI.tldr: A novel closed-loop framework (CLVR) for text-to-image generation that tightly couples visual-language planning with diffusion generation, featuring an automated step-level verification data engine, Proxy Prompt Reinforcement Learning (PPRL) for long-context optimization, and Δ-Space Weight Merge (DSWM) to dramatically cut per-step inference cost.
AI.motivation: Current T2I models rely on a single-step generation paradigm and struggle with complex semantics; multi-step reasoning methods suffer from hallucinations, ungrounded planning, long-context instabilities, and prohibitive latency. A unified, verified, and efficient closed-loop system is needed to improve reliability and scalability.
AI.method: (CLVR) integrates: (1) an automated data engine with step-level visual verification to synthesize reliable reasoning trajectories; (2) Proxy Prompt Reinforcement Learning (PPRL) to distill interleaved multimodal histories into explicit reward signals for stable long-context optimization; (3) Δ-Space Weight Merge (DSWM) to fuse alignment weights with distillation priors, reducing per-step inference to 4 NFEs without re-distillation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show CLVR outperforms open-source baselines and approaches proprietary models, enabling test-time scaling for complex visual generation and reducing latency.
AI.conclusion: CLVR offers a comprehensive framework that tightly couples planning and generation with verification and efficient inference, delivering reliability and scalability for challenging visual reasoning tasks; it holds promise for broader impact in open-source and commercial settings.

title: Spontaneous symmetry breaking and Goldstone modes for deep information propagation
summary: In physical systems, whenever a continuous symmetry is spontaneously broken, the system possesses excitations called Goldstone modes, which allow coherent information propagation over long distances and times. In this work, we study deep neural networks whose internal layers are equivariant under a continuous symmetry and may therefore support analogous Goldstone-like degrees of freedom. We demonstrate, both analytically and empirically, that these degrees of freedom enable coherent signal propagation across depth and recurrent iterations, providing a mechanism for stable information flow without relying on architectural stabilizers such as residual connections or normalization. In feedforward networks, this results in improved trainability and representational diversity across layers. In recurrent settings, we demonstrate the same mechanism is valuable for long-term memory by propagating information over recurrent iterations, thereby improving performance of RNNs and GRUs on long-sequence modeling tasks.

AI.tldr: Continuous-symmetry equivariant neural networks host Goldstone-like degrees of freedom that enable stable, long-range information propagation across depth and time, improving trainability and long-sequence memory without residuals/normalization.
AI.motivation: Borrow the concept of Goldstone modes from spontaneously broken continuous symmetries to address information flow and trainability in deep nets; extend to both feedforward and recurrent architectures.
AI.method: Analytical and empirical investigation; design of layers equivariant under continuous symmetry; identification of Goldstone-like degrees of freedom; experiments on deep feedforward networks and RNNs/GRUs evaluating long-sequence tasks; comparison with baseline stabilizers.
AI.result: Demonstrates coherent signal propagation across depth/iterations; improved trainability and richer representations in feedforward; enhanced long-term memory and performance on long-sequence tasks for RNNs/GRUs.
AI.conclusion: Goldstone-like mechanisms from symmetry considerations provide a generic route to stable information flow in deep models, potentially reducing reliance on architectural stabilizers and enabling better long-range modeling.

title: HeatKV: Head-tuned KV-cache Compression for Visual Autoregressive Modeling
comment: 18 pages total including appendix; 6 main-paper figures, 2 appendix figures; 4 tables
summary: Visual Autoregressive (VAR) models have recently demonstrated impressive image generation quality while maintaining low latency. However, they suffer from severe KV-cache memory constraints, often requiring gigabytes of memory per generated image. We introduce HeatKV, a novel compression method that adapts cache allocation in each head based on its attention to previously generated scales. Using a small offline calibration set, the attention heads are ranked according to their attention scores over prior scales. Based on this ranking, we construct a static pruning schedule tailored to a given memory budget. Applied to the Infinity-2B model, HeatKV achieves $2 \times$ higher compression ratio in memory allocation for KV cache compared to existing methods, while maintaining similar or better image fidelity, prompt alignment and human perception score. Our method achieves a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) for VAR model KV-cache compression, showcasing the effectiveness of fine-grained, head-specific cache allocation.

AI.tldr: HeatKV proposes head-aware cache compression for Visual Autoregressive models, doubling KV-cache memory efficiency while preserving quality, via offline-calibrated per-head pruning.
AI.motivation: Visual Autoregressive models deliver high-quality images but require massive KV-cache memory; there is a need to reduce memory footprint without sacrificing fidelity, alignment, or perceptual quality.
AI.method: Compute per-head attention to previously generated scales on a small offline calibration set, rank heads by attention scores, and construct a static pruning schedule under a given memory budget; apply this head-specific cache allocation to Infinity-2B.
AI.result: Achieves ~2x higher KV-cache memory compression compared with existing methods, with similar or better image fidelity, prompt alignment, and human perceptual scores; sets a new state-of-the-art for VAR model KV-cache compression.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained, head-specific cache allocation is effective for KV-cache compression in VARs, enabling significantly reduced memory footprint with preserved generation quality.

title: ReMIA: a Powerful and Efficient Alternative to Membership Inference Attacks against Synthetic Data Generators
summary: Tabular data sharing under privacy constraints is increasingly important for research and collaboration. Synthetic data generators (SDGs) are a promising solution, but synthetic data remains vulnerable to attacks, such as membership inference attacks (MIAs), which aim to determine whether a specific record was part of the training data. State-of-the-art MIAs are powerful but impractical: they rely on shadow modeling, requiring hundreds of SDG training runs, and need auxiliary data several times larger than the original training set. Fast proxy metrics like distance to closest record (DCR) are efficient but have limited sensitivity to MIA risk. We introduce ReMIA (Relative Membership Inference Attack), a practical privacy metric that requires only two SDG training runs and additional data no larger than the original training set. Rather than predicting whether a record was in the training set, ReMIA generates two synthetic datasets from two source datasets and measures whether a classifier can identify which source a record came from. Experiments across multiple tabular datasets and SDGs show that ReMIA has a sensitivity comparable to state-of-the-art MIAs while being substantially more practical. We further observe that SDGs can achieve privacy-utility trade-offs that traditional noise-based anonymization methods do not match. Code is available at https://github.com/aindo-com/remia.

AI.tldr: ReMIA provides a practical two-run membership inference risk metric for tabular SDGs by training a classifier to distinguish which of two source datasets a record came from in two synthetic datasets, matching state-of-the-art MIA sensitivity with far less compute.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art MIAs are powerful but impractical due to shadow modeling (hundreds of SDG trainings) and large auxiliary data. Fast proxies like DCR exist but have limited sensitivity. A practical privacy metric tailored for SDGs is needed to assess privacy risk without prohibitive cost.
AI.method: Generate two synthetic datasets from two source datasets using SDGs, then train a classifier to predict the source dataset of a given record. The attack's success (ability to identify the origin) serves as a privacy risk proxy. Requires only two SDG training runs and auxiliary data no larger than the original training set.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple tabular datasets and SDGs shows ReMIA attains sensitivity comparable to state-of-the-art MIAs while being substantially more practical in terms of training requirements and data needs.
AI.conclusion: SDGs can achieve privacy-utility trade-offs not matched by traditional noise-based anonymization; ReMIA offers a practical, effective means to audit MIA risk for synthetic data sharing; code available at the provided GitHub URL.

title: Denoising-GS: Gaussian Splatting with Spatial-aware Denoising
summary: Recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have achieved remarkable success in high-fidelity Novel View Synthesis (NVS), yet the optimization process inevitably introduces noisy Gaussian primitives due to the sparse and incomplete initialization from Structure-from-Motion (SfM) point clouds. Most existing methods focus solely on adjusting the positions of primitives during optimization, while neglecting the underlying spatial structure. To this end, we introduce a new perspective by formulating the optimization of 3DGS as a primitive denoising process and propose Denoising-GS, a spatial-aware denoising framework for Gaussian primitives by taking both the positions and spatial structure into consideration. Specifically, we design an optimizer that preserves the spatial optimization flow of primitives, facilitating coherent and directed denoising rather than random perturbations. Building upon this, the Spatial Gradient-based Denoising strategy jointly considers the spatial supports of primitives to ensure gradient-consistent updates. Furthermore, the Uncertainty-based Denoising module estimates primitive-wise uncertainty to prune redundant or noisy primitives, while the Spatial Coherence Refinement strategy selectively splits primitives in sparse regions to maintain structural completeness. Experiments conducted on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that Denoising-GS consistently enhances NVS fidelity while maintaining representation compactness, achieving state-of-the-art performance across all benchmarks. Source code and models will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: Denoising-GS reframes 3D Gaussian Splatting optimization as a spatially-aware denoising task to suppress noise, preserve structure, and prune/ refine primitives, achieving state-of-the-art NVS while keeping compact representations.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D Gaussian Splatting optimization often only adjusts primitive positions, neglecting spatial structure, leading to noisy artifacts from SfM initialization. A spatially informed denoising framework aims to preserve spatial coherence and prune redundant noise-prone primitives for better fidelity and efficiency.
AI.method: Propose Denoising-GS with: (1) an optimizer that preserves the spatial optimization flow; (2) Spatial Gradient-based Denoising using the spatial supports of primitives for gradient-consistent updates; (3) Uncertainty-based Denoising to estimate primitive-wise uncertainty and prune noisy/redundant primitives; (4) Spatial Coherence Refinement that selectively splits primitives in sparse regions to retain structural completeness; end-to-end training/inference with public code release.
AI.result: Experiments on three benchmark datasets show consistent improvements in Novel View Synthesis fidelity while maintaining compact representations, achieving state-of-the-art performance across all benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Framing 3DGS optimization as a denoising problem with spatially aware mechanisms yields coherent refinement of Gaussian primitives, improved NVS quality, and efficient representations; the approach promises practical gains and will be accompanied by publicly released code and models.

title: The Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity Tradeoff in SAEs
summary: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) that can accurately reconstruct their input (minimizing distortion) by making efficient use of few features (minimizing the rate) often fail to learn monosemantic representations (highly interpretable), limiting their usefulness for mechanistic interpretability. In this paper, we characterise this tension in learning faithful, efficient, and interpretable explanations, introducing the Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity tradeoff in SAEs. Under toy-modeling assumptions, we theoretically and empirically show that restricting the SAE to be monosemantic necessarily comes with an increase in rate and distortion. Assuming a generative model behind the input observations, we further demonstrate that the degree of polysemanticity of optimal SAEs is determined by the training data distribution, especially by the probability of features to co-occur. Finally, we extend the analysis to real-world settings by deriving necessary conditions that a polysemanticity measure should satisfy when the data-generating process is unknown, and we benchmark existing proxy metrics on SAEs trained on Large Language Models. Taken together, our findings show that polysemanticity is a data problem that should be accounted for when addressing it at the architectural and optimization level.

AI.tldr: Formalizes a Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity tradeoff in Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs); constraining SAEs to be monosemantic increases rate and distortion; polysemanticity is data-dependent, shaped by feature co-occurrence; proposes conditions for measuring polysemanticity and benchmarks proxy metrics on LLM-derived data.
AI.motivation: SAEs can reconstruct with low distortion yet yield entangled, hard-to-interpret features. The work investigates why interpretability (monosemanticity) conflicts with efficiency (rate) and faithfulness, and how data distribution governs polysemanticity.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis under toy-models to derive the tradeoff; empirical validation on SAEs trained on data generated to resemble LLM feature statistics; modeling of data generation and feature co-occurrence; formulation of necessary conditions for polysemanticity measures when the data-generating process is unknown; benchmarking existing proxy metrics on real data.
AI.result: Proving that monosemantic constraints incur increased rate and distortion for SAEs; showing the degree of polysemanticity in optimal SAEs is dictated by data distribution (co-occurrence probabilities); deriving conditions a polysemanticity measure should meet; empirical benchmarks of proxy metrics on LLM-derived SAEs.
AI.conclusion: Polysemanticity is predominantly a data problem; addressing interpretability requires incorporating data distribution considerations into architecture and optimization; the results guide both evaluation (proxy metrics) and design choices for SAEs in practice.

title: Masked Next-Scale Prediction for Self-supervised Scene Text Recognition
comment: Accepted to the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) 2026 Findings Track.10 pages, 4 figures
summary: Scene Text Recognition requires modeling visual structures that evolve from coarse layouts to fine-grained character strokes. Training such models relies on large amounts of annotated data. Recent self-supervised approaches, such as Masked Image Modeling (MIM), alleviate this dependency by leveraging large-scale unlabeled data. Yet most existing MIM methods operate at a single spatial scale and fail to capture the hierarchical nature of scene text. In this work, we introduce Masked Next-Scale Prediction (MNSP), a unified self-supervised framework designed to explicitly model cross-scale structural evolution. The framework incorporates Next-Scale Prediction (NSP), which learns hierarchical representations by predicting higher-resolution features from lower-resolution contexts. Naive scale prediction, however, tends to produce spatially diffuse attention, directing the model toward background regions rather than textual structures. MNSP resolves this limitation by jointly learning cross-scale prediction and masked image reconstruction. NSP captures global layout priors across resolutions, while masked reconstruction imposes strong local constraints that guide attention toward informative text regions. A Multi-scale Linguistic Alignment module further maintains semantic consistency across different resolutions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MNSP achieves state-of-the-art performance, reaching 86.2\% average accuracy on the challenging Union14M benchmark and 96.7\% across six standard datasets. Additional analyses show that our method improves robustness under extreme scale and layout variations. Code is available at https://github.com/CzhczhcHczh/MNSP

AI.tldr: Introduces Masked Next-Scale Prediction (MNSP) for self-supervised scene text recognition to model cross-scale structure, combining next-scale feature prediction with masked image reconstruction and a cross-scale linguistic alignment to achieve state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Scene Text Recognition requires hierarchical visual representations; existing MIM methods operate at a single scale and misdirect attention to background; need to exploit unlabeled data and cross-scale structure.
AI.method: Proposes MNSP framework: Next-Scale Prediction (NSP) to predict higher-resolution features from lower-resolution context, masked image reconstruction to enforce local constraints and guide attention to text regions, and a Multi-scale Linguistic Alignment module to maintain semantic consistency across resolutions.
AI.result: Empirical results show 86.2% avg accuracy on Union14M and 96.7% across six standard datasets; improved robustness under extreme scale/layout variations; code released.
AI.conclusion: MNSP effectively models cross-scale textual evolution, combining global cross-scale priors with local reconstruction and alignment to achieve state-of-the-art performance in scene text recognition.

title: NeuroAtlas: Benchmarking Foundation Models for Clinical EEG and Brain-Computer Interfaces
summary: Foundation models (FMs) promise to extract unified representations that generalize across downstream tasks. They have emerged across fields, including electroencephalography (EEG), but it is less clear how effective they are in this particular field. Published evaluations differ in datasets, in the EEG-specific preprocessing that might influence reported results, and in the reported metrics, frequently obscuring the clinical relevance in EEG. We introduce NeuroAtlas, the largest EEG benchmark to date: 42 datasets and 260k hours covering clinical EEG (epilepsy, sleep medicine, brain age estimation) and brain-computer interfaces, and include multiple datasets per task along with bespoke clinical evaluation metrics. Besides evaluating EEG-FMs with respect to supervised baselines, we present results from generic time-series FMs. We report three findings. First, EEG-specific FMs do not consistently outperform time-series FMs, which have neither EEG-focused architectures nor been pretrained on EEG. Second, standard machine learning metrics are insufficient to assess clinical utility: thus, we thoroughly evaluate more appropriate measures such as the quality of event-level decision-making, hypnogram-derived features, and the brain-age gap in the domains of epilepsy, sleep, and brain age, respectively. Third, model rankings and performance can vary substantially within domains. We conclude that pretrained models perform largely on par, with only narrow advantages for a few, and that current models do not yet deliver on the promise of an out-of-the-box unified EEG model. NeuroAtlas exposes this gap and provides the datasets and metrics for the next generation of unified EEG FMs.

AI.tldr: NeuroAtlas benchmark: 42 EEG datasets (260k hours) across clinical EEG and BCIs; EEG-specific FMs vs generic time-series FMs; clinical metrics reveal limited advantages and variable performance; no out-of-the-box unified EEG model yet.
AI.motivation: Current EEG foundation-model evaluations are inconsistent and often rely on standard ML metrics that don't reflect clinical utility; there's a need for a large, task-diverse benchmark with clinically meaningful evaluation to guide the development of unified EEG models.
AI.method: Assemble NeuroAtlas: 42 datasets, 260k hours; multiple datasets per task; compare EEG-specific FMs against generic time-series FMs; use bespoke clinical evaluation metrics (event-level decision quality, hypnogram features, brain-age gap) in epilepsy, sleep, brain age; assess supervised baselines as well.
AI.result: EEG-specific FMs do not consistently outperform time-series FMs; standard metrics are insufficient for clinical utility; clinical metrics reveal nuanced results; model rankings vary within domains; pretrained models largely comparable with narrow advantages; no unified EEG model yet.
AI.conclusion: NeuroAtlas exposes the gap and provides datasets/metrics to drive the next generation of unified EEG FMs; sets a benchmark for evaluating clinical relevance and generalization across EEG tasks.

title: TAPIOCA: Why Task- Aware Pruning Improves OOD model Capability
summary: Recent work has promoted task-aware layer pruning as a way to improve model performance on particular tasks, as shown by TALE. In this paper, we investigate when such improvements occur and why. We show first that, across controlled polynomial regression tasks and large language models, such pruning yields no benefit on in-distribution (ID) data but consistently improves out-of-distribution (OOD) accuracy. We further show empirically that OOD inputs induce layerwise norm and pairwise-distance profiles that deviate from the corresponding ID profiles. This leads to a geometric explanation of task-aware pruning: each task induces a task-adapted geometry, characterized empirically by the representation profiles observed on ID inputs. OOD inputs can introduce a distorted version of the task-adapted geometry. Task-aware pruning identifies layers that create or amplify this distortion; by removing them, it shifts OOD representational norms and pairwise distances toward those observed on the adapted distribution. This realigns OOD inputs with the model's task-adapted geometry and improves performance. We provide causal evidence through controlled distribution shifts and residual-scaling interventions, and demonstrate consistent behavior across model scales.

AI.tldr: Task-aware layer pruning improves out-of-distribution (OOD) accuracy without benefiting in-distribution (ID) performance by removing layers that amplify distortion to a task-adapted geometry; this is demonstrated across polynomial regression tasks and large language models and explained via a geometry-based, causal mechanism that aligns OOD representations with the task geometry across model scales.
AI.motivation: To understand when and why task-aware pruning helps, moving beyond empirical gains to a geometric and causal account of layer-wise contributions under distribution shifts.
AI.method: Empirically analyze layerwise norms and pairwise distances for ID vs OOD inputs across controlled polynomial regression tasks and large language models; characterize task-adapted geometry from ID representations; apply pruning to remove layers contributing to distortion; test causality with residual-scaling interventions and controlled distribution shifts; verify robustness across model scales.
AI.result: No ID gains from pruning; consistent OOD accuracy improvements. OOD inputs distort layerwise norms and pairwise distances relative to ID profiles; pruning removes distortion-causing layers, shifting OOD representations toward the task-adapted geometry and improving performance. Causal evidence via distribution shifts and residual scaling, with consistent behavior across model sizes.
AI.conclusion: Task-aware pruning improves OOD performance by aligning representations with the task-adapted geometry under distribution shifts; the mechanism is geometric and causal and persists across scales.

title: Hierarchical Image Tokenization for Multi-Scale Image Super Resolution
comment: Accepted for publication at ICML 2026. *Joint first authorship (alphabetical order). arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2506.04990
summary: We introduce a multi-scale Image Super Resolution (ISR) method building on recent advances in Visual Auto-Regressive (VAR) modeling. VAR models break image tokenization into additive, gradually increasing scales, using Residual Quantization (RQ), an approach that aligns perfectly with our target ISR task. Previous works taking advantage of this synergy suffer from two main shortcomings. First, due to the limitations in RQ, they only generate images at a predefined fixed scale, failing to map intermediate outputs to the corresponding image scales. They also rely on large backbones or a large corpus of annotated data to achieve better performance. To address both shortcomings, we introduce two novel components to the VAR training for ISR, aiming at increasing its flexibility and reducing its complexity. In particular, we introduce a) a \textbf{Hierarchical Image Tokenization (HIT)} approach that progressively represents images at different scales while enforcing token overlap across scales, and b) a \textbf{Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) regularization term} that, relying solely on the (LR,HR) pair, encourages the transformer to produce the latter over the former. Our proposed HIT acts as a strong inductive bias for the VAR training, resulting in a small model (300M params vs 1B params of VARSR), that achieves state-of-the-art results without external training data, and that delivers multi-scale outputs with a single forward pass.

AI.tldr: A multi-scale ISR method using Visual Auto-Regressive modeling with Hierarchical Image Tokenization and Direct Preference Optimization to achieve scalable outputs and reduced model size without external data.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of Residual Quantization in VAR-based ISR: fixed scale outputs and need for large models/data; enable flexible multi-scale outputs and efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce HIT to progressively represent images at multiple scales with token overlap; introduce DPO regularization using LR-HR pairs to bias the transformer toward HR outputs; train a relatively small model (~300M params) capable of multi-scale outputs in a single forward pass.
AI.result: Claims state-of-the-art ISR performance without external training data; reduced model size (300M vs 1B); multi-scale outputs produced in one forward pass.
AI.conclusion: HIT and DPO provide strong inductive biases that improve flexibility and efficiency for VAR-based ISR, enabling scalable, scalable outputs with smaller models; validates approach without external data; may generalize to other multi-scale generative tasks.

title: Selective Safety Steering via Value-Filtered Decoding
summary: While large language models (LLMs) are trained to align with human values, their generations may still violate safety constraints. A growing line of work addresses this problem by modifying the model's sampling policy at decoding time using a safety reward. However, existing decoding-time steering methods often intervene unnecessarily, modifying generations that would have been safe under the base model. Such unnecessary interventions are undesirable, as they can distort key properties of the base model such as helpfulness, fluency, style, and coherence. We propose a new test-time steering method designed to reduce such unnecessary interventions while improving the safety of unsafe responses. Our approach filters tokens using a value-based safety criterion and provides an explicit bound on the probability of false interventions. A single threshold hyperparameter controls this bound, allowing practitioners to trade off higher rates of unnecessary intervention for better output safety. Across multiple datasets and experiments, we show that our value-filtered decoding method outperforms existing baselines, achieving better trade-offs between safety, helpfulness, and similarity to the base model.

AI.tldr: Proposes a test-time safety filtering for LLM decoding that reduces unnecessary interventions by using a value-based safety criterion with a bound on false interventions via a single threshold, improving safety while preserving base-model qualities.
AI.motivation: Current decoding-time safety methods often over-correct, harming helpfulness, fluency, and style by modifying safe outputs. A principled, tunable filter aims to minimize unnecessary interventions while enhancing safety.
AI.method: Introduce a value-filtered decoding approach that filters tokens via a safety criterion; derive explicit bound on probability of false interventions; use a single threshold hyperparameter; evaluate across datasets showing improved trade-offs.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines on datasets, achieving better safety-helpfulness-similarity to base model; provides controllable balance between safety and fidelity.
AI.conclusion: A practical and tunable decoding-time safety method that reduces unnecessary interventions without sacrificing safety; threshold parameter offers explicit control; applicable to various LLM settings.

title: Your CLIP has 164 dimensions of noise: Exploring the embeddings covariance eigenspectrum of contrastively pretrained vision-language transformers
summary: Contrastively pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) serve as powerful feature extractors. Yet, their shared latent spaces are prone to structural anomalies and act as repositories for non-semantic, multi-modal noise. To address this phenomenon, we employ spectral decomposition of covariance matrices to decompose the VLM latent space into a multi-modal semantic signal component and a shared noise subspace. We observe that this noise geometry exhibits strong subgroup invariance across distinct data subsets. Crucially, pruning these shared noise dimensions is mainly harmless, preserving or actively improving downstream task performance. By isolating true semantic signals from artifactual noise, this work provides new mechanistic insights into the representational structure of modern VLMs, suggesting that a substantial fraction of their latent geometry is governed by shared, architecture-level noise rather than task-relevant semantics alone.

AI.tldr: Spectral pruning of VLM latent spaces reveals a large shared noise subspace; removing it preserves or improves downstream performance.
AI.motivation: To understand why VLM latent spaces exhibit non-semantic, multimodal noise that undermines interpretability and possibly task performance.
AI.method: Apply spectral decomposition of covariance matrices across latent representations from contrastively pre-trained VLMs to separate semantic signal from shared noise; identify invariant noise subspace; prune the shared noise dimensions.
AI.result: Noise geometry shows strong subgroup invariance across data subsets; pruning shared noise is largely harmless and can improve downstream performance; semantic signals remain after pruning.
AI.conclusion: A substantial portion of VLM latent structure is governed by architecture-level noise rather than task-relevant semantics; isolating semantic signal via spectral pruning provides mechanistic insights into VLM representations.

title: Cognitive-Uncertainty Guided Knowledge Distillation for Accurate Classification of Student Misconceptions
summary: Accurately identifying student misconceptions is crucial for personalized education but faces three challenges: (1) data scarcity with long-tail distribution, where authentic student reasoning is difficult to synthesize; (2) fuzzy boundaries between error categories with high annotation noise; (3) deployment parado-large models overlook unconventional approaches due to pretraining bias and cannot be deployed on edge, while small models overfit to noise. Unlike traditional methods that increase diversity through large-scale data synthesis, we propose a two-stage knowledge distillation framework that mines high-value samples from existing data. The first stage performs standard distillation to transfer task capabilities. The second stage introduces a dual-layer marginal selection mechanism based on cognitive uncertainty, identifying four types of critical samples based on teacher model uncertainty and confidence differences. For different data subsets, we design difficulty-adaptive mechanism to balance hard/soft label contributions, enabling student models to inherit inter-class relationships from teacher soft labels while distinguishing ambiguous error types. Experiments show that with augmented training on only 10.30% of filtered samples, we achieve MAP@3 of 0.9585 (+17.8%) on the MAP-Charting dataset, and using only a 4B parameter model, we attain 84.38% accuracy on cross-topic tests of middle school algebra misconception benchmarks, significantly outperforming sota LLM (67.73%) and standard fine-tuned 72B models (81.25%). Our code is available at https://github.com/RoschildRui/acl2026_map.

AI.tldr: Two-stage knowledge distillation with cognitive-uncertainty-based sample mining enables small models to outperform larger models on student-misconception tasks using only a fraction of data.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity (long-tail), noisy error-category boundaries, and deployment constraints by leveraging existing data more efficiently and enabling edge-friendly models.
AI.method: Stage 1: standard distillation to transfer task capabilities. Stage 2: dual-layer marginal sample selection using teacher uncertainty and confidence gaps to identify four sample types. Across data subsets, apply a difficulty-adaptive weighting of hard/soft labels, allowing student to inherit inter-class relationships while distinguishing ambiguous error types.
AI.result: On MAP-Charting, filtered 10.30% of samples yields MAP@3 0.9585 (+17.8%). A 4B-model achieves 84.38% accuracy on cross-topic algebra misconception benchmarks, beating SOTA LLM (67.73%) and 72B fine-tuned model (81.25%).
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-stage distillation with uncertainty-guided sample selection improves data efficiency and edge-deployable performance; demonstrates strong cross-topic generalization with a compact model; implies potential applicability to other educational AI tasks.

title: SEDiT: Mask-Free Video Subtitle Erasure via One-step Diffusion Transformer
summary: Recent breakthroughs in video diffusion models have significantly accelerated the development of video editing techniques. However, existing methods often rely on inpainting video frames based on masked input, which requires extracting the target video mask in advance, and the precision of the segmentation directly affects the quality of the completion. In this paper, we present SEDiT, a novel one-stage video Subtitle Erasure approach via One-step Diffusion Transformer. We introduce a mask-free inference approach that enables direct erasure of the targeted subtitle. The proposed one-stage framework mitigates the sub-optimality inherent in the two-stage processing of prior models. Since subtitle removal is a localized editing task in which most pixels remain unchanged, the underlying distribution shift is minimal, making it well-suited to one-step generation under rectified flow. We empirically validate the reliability of one-step denoising and further provide a formal theoretical justification. Under the localized-editing structure of subtitle removal, the conditional optimal transport (OT) map and its induced rectified flow velocity field are Lipschitz continuous with respect to the latent variable, which underpins the theoretical feasibility of one-step sampling. To address the challenge of long-term temporal consistency, we adopt a hybrid training strategy by occasionally conditioning the model with a clean first-frame latent. This facilitates temporal continuity, allowing each segment during inference to leverage the output of its predecessor. To avoid visible seams caused by cropping and reinserting processed targets, particularly in scenarios involving substantial motion, we feed the original video directly into SEDiT. Thanks to one-step and chunk-wise streaming inference, our method can efficiently handle native 1440p video with infinite length.

AI.tldr: A mask-free, one-stage video subtitle erasure method (SEDiT) using diffusion transformers, enabling efficient long-video editing with improved temporal consistency.
AI.motivation: Current video editing relies on two-stage inpainting with explicit masks; dependence on segmentation quality and two-stage sub-optimal; need mask-free, one-step technique for localized edits like subtitles.
AI.method: One-stage diffusion transformer; mask-free inference; rectified flow; local-editing view; conditional optimal transport map ensures Lipschitz continuity; hybrid training with occasional conditioning on first-frame latent; feed original video to avoid seams; chunk-wise streaming inference enabling 1440p long videos.
AI.result: Empirical validation shows reliability of one-step denoising; formal theoretical justification via OT maps Lipschitz continuity; improved temporal continuity; able to process long/high-res video with streaming and reduced seams.
AI.conclusion: SEDiT provides efficient, mask-free, one-stage editing for subtitles with robust temporal consistency, suitable for long-form video editing.

title: Crys-JEPA: Accelerating Crystal Discovery via Embedding Screening and Generative Refinement
summary: De novo crystal generation seeks to discover materials that are not merely realistic, but also stable and novel. However, most existing generative models are trained to maximize the likelihood of observed crystals, which encourages samples to stay close to known materials yet not necessarily align with the criteria that matter in discovery. Through an empirical investigation, we show that current crystal generative models are caught in a pronounced stability--novelty trade-off: moving toward the observed distribution preserves stability but limits novelty, whereas moving away from it quickly destroys stability. This suggests that the useful region for discovering crystals that are both stable and novel is extremely narrow. To escape the trade-off, we introduce Crys-JEPA, a joint embedding predictive architecture for crystals that learns an energy-aware latent space preserving formation-energy differences. In this space, stability assessment can be reformulated as an embedding-based comparison against accessible training crystals, reducing the reliance on expensive energy evaluation and task-specific external references. Building on Crys-JEPA, we further develop a screening-and-refinement pipeline that identifies promising generated crystals and reintroduces them to refine the generative model. On MP-20 and Alex-MP-20 datasets, we achieve improvements over baselines up to 81.4% and 82.6% on V.S.U.N metric, respectively.

AI.tldr: Proposes Crys-JEPA, an energy-aware latent embedding for crystalline materials to balance stability and novelty; introduces a joint embedding predictive architecture, reduces need for energy evaluations, and shows improvements on MP-20 and Alex-MP-20 datasets using the V.S.U.N metric.
AI.motivation: Current crystal generative models optimized for likelihood concentrate samples near known materials, which preserves stability but restricts novelty; the stability–novelty trade-off is sharp, suggesting a narrow discovery region.
AI.method: Crys-JEPA learns an energy-aware latent space via a joint embedding predictive architecture that preserves formation-energy differences; uses embedding-based stability assessment against training crystals; introduces a screening-and-refinement workflow to identify and re-train on promising generations.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines on the V.S.U.N metric, achieving up to 81.4% improvement on MP-20 and 82.6% on Alex-MP-20.
AI.conclusion: Energy-aware embeddings combined with iterative screening/refinement can mitigate the stability–novelty trade-off in crystal generation, enabling more stable and novel material discovery with reduced energy evaluations.

title: MemLens: Benchmarking Multimodal Long-Term Memory in Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Memory is essential for large vision-language models (LVLMs) to handle long, multimodal interactions, with two method directions providing this capability: long-context LVLMs and memory-augmented agents. However, no existing benchmark conducts a systematic comparison of the two on questions that genuinely require multimodal evidence. To close this gap, we introduce MEMLENS, a comprehensive benchmark for memory in multimodal multi-session conversations, comprising 789 questions across five memory abilities (information extraction, multi-session reasoning, temporal reasoning, knowledge update, and answer refusal) at four standard context lengths (32K-256K tokens) under a cross-modal token-counting scheme. An image-ablation study confirms that solving MEMLENS requires visual evidence: removing evidence images drops two frontier LVLMs below 2% accuracy on the 80.4% of questions whose evidence includes images. Evaluating 27 LVLMs and 7 memory-augmented agents, we find that long-context LVLMs achieve high short-context accuracy through direct visual grounding but degrade as conversations grow, whereas memory agents are length-stable but lose visual fidelity under storage-time compression. Multi-session reasoning caps most systems below 30%, and neither approach alone solves the task. These results motivate hybrid architectures that combine long-context attention with structured multimodal retrieval. Our code is available at https://github.com/xrenaf/MEMLENS.

AI.tldr: MEMLENS is a new benchmark for memory in multimodal, multi-session conversations, enabling a direct comparison of long-context LVLMs and memory-augmented agents across 789 questions and 32K-256K token contexts; results indicate that neither approach alone suffices, motivating hybrid architectures.
AI.motivation: Addresses a gap in systematically evaluating how long-context LVLMs and memory-augmented agents handle memory and multimodal evidence across multi-session conversations, highlighting trade-offs in accuracy, retention, and visual fidelity.
AI.method: MEMLENS includes 789 questions spanning five memory abilities (information extraction, multi-session reasoning, temporal reasoning, knowledge update, answer refusal) at four context lengths (32K–256K tokens) using a cross-modal token-counting scheme. An image-ablation study shows reliance on visual evidence. Evaluated 27 LVLMs and 7 memory-augmented agents.
AI.result: Image evidence is essential: removing evidence images reduces accuracy sharply; 80.4% of questions rely on image evidence. Long-context LVLMs achieve high short-context accuracy via direct grounding but degrade as conversations lengthen; memory-augmented agents remain length-stable but suffer from visual fidelity loss under storage-time compression. Multi-session reasoning performances are mostly below 30%. Neither approach alone solves MEMLENS.
AI.conclusion: Results motivate hybrid architectures combining long-context attention with structured multimodal retrieval, to leverage the strengths of both approaches. Code is available at the provided GitHub URL.

title: Compositional Sparsity as an Inductive Bias for Neural Architecture Design
summary: Identifying the structural priors that enable Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) to overcome the curse of dimensionality is a fundamental challenge in machine learning theory. Existing literature suggests that effective high-dimensional learning is driven by compositional sparsity, where target functions decompose into constituents supported on low-dimensional variable subsets. To investigate this hypothesis, we combine Information Filtering Networks (IFNs), which extract sparse dependency structures via constrained information maximisation, with Homological Neural Networks (HNNs), which map the inferred topology into fixed-wiring sparse neural graphs. We formalise the design principles underlying this construction and present an interpretable pipeline in which abstraction emerges through hierarchical composition. HNNs are orders of magnitude sparser than standard DNNs and require only minimal hyperparameter tuning. On synthetic tasks with known sparse hierarchies, HNNs recover the underlying compositional structure and remain stable in regimes where dense alternatives degrade as dimensionality increases. Across a broad suite of real-world datasets, HNNs consistently match or outperform dense baselines while using far fewer parameters, exhibiting lower variance and showing reduced sensitivity to hyperparameters.

AI.tldr: A sparse, topology-informed pipeline (IFN + HNN) yields interpretable, highly sparse neural networks that capture compositional sparsity, achieving competitive performance with far fewer parameters and strong robustness to dimensionality.
AI.motivation: To identify and exploit structural priors—specifically compositional sparsity—as a mechanism to beat the curse of dimensionality in high-dimensional learning, by linking sparse dependency discovery with topology-aware fixed-wiring graphs.
AI.method: Combine Information Filtering Networks (IFNs) to extract sparse dependency structures via constrained information maximisation, with Homological Neural Networks (HNNs) that map the inferred topology into fixed-wiring sparse neural graphs; formalize design principles and present an interpretable, hierarchical abstraction pipeline.
AI.result: HNNs are orders of magnitude sparser than dense DNNs; on synthetic tasks they recover the underlying compositional structure and remain stable as dimensionality grows; on real-world datasets they match or outperform dense baselines while using far fewer parameters, with lower variance and reduced hyperparameter sensitivity.
AI.conclusion: The study supports the efficacy of structural priors—notably compositional sparsity—in enabling scalable, interpretable high-dimensional learning via a sparse, topology-informed neural architecture; HNNs offer a robust, low-parameter alternative to conventional dense networks.

title: SteerSeg: Attention Steering for Reasoning Video Segmentation
summary: Video reasoning segmentation requires localizing objects across video frames from natural language expressions, often involving spatial reasoning and implicit references. Recent approaches leverage frozen large vision-language models (LVLMs) by extracting attention maps and using them as spatial priors for segmentation, enabling training-free grounding. However, these attention maps are optimized for text generation rather than spatial localization, often resulting in diffuse and ambiguous grounding signals. In this work, we introduce SteerSeg, a lightweight framework that identifies attention misalignment as the key bottleneck in attention-based grounding and proposes to steer attention at its source through input-level conditioning. SteerSeg combines learnable soft prompts with reasoning-guided Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting. The soft prompts reshape the attention distribution to produce more spatially concentrated maps, while CoT-derived attributes resolve ambiguity among similar objects by guiding attention toward the correct instance. The resulting attention maps are converted into point prompts across keyframes to guide a segmentation model, while candidate tracklets are ranked and selected using correlation-based scoring. Our approach freezes the LVLM and segmentation model parameters and learns only a small set of soft prompts, preserving the model's pretrained reasoning capabilities while significantly improving grounding. Despite being trained only on Ref-YouTube-VOS, SteerSeg generalizes well across diverse benchmarks, significantly improving the spatial grounding capability of LVLMs. Project page: https://steerseg.github.io

AI.tldr: SteerSeg mitigates attention misalignment in LVLM-based video grounding by input-level conditioning using learnable soft prompts and reasoning-guided Chain-of-Thought; yields spatially concentrated attention maps to drive a segmentation model, trained only on Ref-YouTube-VOS, with strong cross-benchmark generalization, while keeping LVLM and segmentation model frozen.
AI.motivation: LVLM attention maps are optimized for text generation, often diffuse for spatial localization; effective grounding in video requires accurate object localization across frames given natural-language expressions.
AI.method: Freeze LVLM and segmentation model; learn a small set of soft prompts; use reasoning-guided Chain-of-Thought prompts to derive attributes that resolve ambiguity; generate attention maps; convert to point prompts across keyframes; feed to segmentation model; rank candidate tracklets with correlation-based scoring.
AI.result: Notable improvements in spatial grounding accuracy, yielding more precise segmentation across benchmarks; strong generalization from Ref-YouTube-VOS to diverse datasets, while preserving pretrained reasoning capabilities; minimal trainable parameters.
AI.conclusion: Identifies attention misalignment as the bottleneck and demonstrates that input-level conditioning (soft prompts + CoT attributes) can steer attention effectively, enabling a lightweight, generalizable solution for LVLM-based video grounding.

title: Composable Crystals: Controllable Materials Discovery via Concept Learning
summary: De novo crystal generation, a central task in materials discovery, aims to generate crystals that are simultaneously valid, stable, unique, and novel. Existing methods mainly rely on black-box stochastic sampling, providing limited control over how generated structures move beyond the observed distribution. In this paper, we introduce a concept-based compositional framework for crystal generation. We train a vector-quantized variational autoencoder to automatically discover a shared set of reusable crystal concepts, which serve as building blocks for guided generation. These learned concepts naturally exhibit interpretability from both local atomic environments and global symmetry patterns, and generalize to crystals from different distributions. By recombining such concepts, our framework enables controllable exploration of novel crystals beyond the training distribution, rather than relying solely on unconstrained random sampling. To further improve composition efficiency, we introduce a composition generator and iteratively refine it using high-quality samples generated by the model itself. The resulting concept compositions are then used to condition downstream crystal generation. Numerical experiments on MP-20 and Alex-MP-20 show that compositing concepts separately increase base model up to 53.2% and 51.7% on V.S.U.N metric, with particular gains in novelty.

AI.tldr: A concept-based compositional framework for crystal generation using a vector-quantized VAE discovers reusable crystal concepts, enabling controllable, novel generation by recombining concepts and a composition generator that iteratively refines samples; demonstrated performance gains on MP-20 / Alex-MP-20 (V.S.U.N metric).
AI.motivation: Address the lack of controllability and interpretability in black-box stochastic crystal generation methods and enable guided exploration beyond the training distribution.
AI.method: Train a vector-quantized variational autoencoder to learn a shared set of reusable crystal concepts; leverage these concepts for interpretable, local/global pattern representations; recombine concepts to guide generation; introduce a composition generator and iteratively refine it with high-quality samples; condition downstream crystal generation on the resulting concept compositions.
AI.result: Empirical results on MP-20 and Alex-MP-20 show substantial gains in the base model performance (up to 53.2% and 51.7% on the V.S.U.N metric) with notable improvements in novelty; concept compositions generalize across distributions and improve controllability.
AI.conclusion: A concept-based compositional framework enables controllable exploration of novel crystals beyond the training distribution, with effectiveness amplified by iterative composition refinement and concept recombination.

title: Representative Attention For Vision Transformers
summary: Linear attention has emerged as a promising direction for scaling Vision Transformers beyond the quadratic cost of dense self-attention. A prevalent strategy is to compress spatial tokens into a compact set of intermediate proxies that mediate global information exchange. However, existing methods typically derive these proxy tokens from predefined spatial layouts, causing token compression to remain anchored to image coordinates rather than the semantic organization of visual content. To overcome this limitation, we propose Representative Attention (RPAttention), a linear global attention mechanism that performs token compression directly in representation space. Instead of constructing intermediate tokens from fixed spatial partitions, it dynamically forms a compact set of learned representative tokens to enable semantically related regions to communicate regardless of their spatial distance, by following a lightweight Gather-Interact-Distribute paradigm. Spatial tokens are first softly gathered into representative tokens through competitive similarity-based routing. The representatives then perform global interaction within a compact latent space, before broadcasting the refined information back to all spatial tokens via query-driven cross-attention. Via replacing coordinate-driven aggregation with representation-driven compression, RPAttention preserves global receptive fields while adaptively aligning token communication with the content structure of each input.RPAttention reduces the dominant token interaction complexity from quadratic to linear scaling with respect to the number of spatial tokens, while maintaining expressive global context modeling. Extensive experiments across diverse vision transformer backbones on image classification, object detection, and semantic segmentation demonstrate the effectiveness of our design.

AI.tldr: RPAttention introduces a linear-time global attention for Vision Transformers by compressing tokens in representation space using learnable representative tokens with a Gather-Interact-Distribute workflow, enabling semantically-aware global communication.
AI.motivation: Current self-attention in Vision Transformers is quadratic in the number of tokens and proxy-token methods rely on fixed spatial layouts, tying compression to image coordinates rather than content structure. A representation-space, learnable compression could preserve global context while adapting to visual semantics.
AI.method: A Gather-Interact-Distribute pipeline: spatial tokens are softly gathered into a small set of representative tokens via competitive similarity-based routing; representatives perform global interaction in a latent space; refined information is broadcast back to spatial tokens through query-driven cross-attention.
AI.result: The approach reduces the dominant interaction cost from quadratic to linear with respect to token count, while maintaining expressive global context. Extensive experiments across vision backbones on image classification, object detection, and semantic segmentation demonstrate effectiveness and transferability.
AI.conclusion: RPAttention achieves linear-scaling global context modeling by replacing coordinate-driven compression with representation-driven token aggregation, aligning communication with content structure and broadening the applicability of linear-attention mechanisms in vision tasks.

title: Beyond What to Select: A Plug-and-play Oscillatory Data-Volume Scheduling for Efficient Model Training
summary: Data selection accelerates training by identifying representative training data while preserving model performance. However, existing methods mainly focus on designing sample-importance criteria, i.e., deciding what to select, while typically fixing the selected data volume as the target ratio throughout training. Thus, they are often dynamic in sample identity but static in data volume. In this work, we revisit data selection from an optimization perspective and show that selected-data training induces an implicit regularization effect modulated by the instantaneous selection ratio. This reveals a key trade-off: lower ratios amplify selection-induced regularization, whereas higher ratios preserve data coverage and optimization fidelity. Motivated by this insight, we propose PODS, a Plug-and-play Oscillatory Data-volume Scheduling framework. Rather than introducing another sample-scoring metric, PODS serves as a lightweight module that dynamically schedules how much data to select over training. Under the target selection ratio, PODS alternates between low-ratio regularization phases and high-ratio recovery phases to exploit selection-induced regularization without sacrificing optimization stability. With its lightweight, ratio-level, and task-agnostic design, PODS is compatible with existing static and dynamic selection methods and broadly applicable across training paradigms. Experiments across various datasets, architectures, and tasks show that PODS consistently improves the efficiency-generalization trade-off, e.g., reducing ImageNet-1k training cost by 50% with improved accuracy and accelerating LLM instruction tuning by over 2x without performance degradation.

AI.tldr: PODS introduces a plug-and-play data-volume scheduling method that dynamically oscillates the amount of data selected during training to balance selection-induced regularization with optimization fidelity, improving efficiency and generalization across tasks.
AI.motivation: Data selection methods improve training efficiency by reducing data; however, they typically fix the target data volume, making the ratio static while sample identity is dynamic. The authors reveal an implicit regularization effect modulated by the instantaneous selection ratio and a trade-off between stronger regularization at low ratios and better data coverage at high ratios.
AI.method: Propose PODS (Plug-and-play Oscillatory Data-volume Scheduling), a lightweight scheduling module that operates at the data-volume level rather than changing sample-scoring metrics. Under a target selection ratio, PODS alternates between low-ratio regularization phases and high-ratio recovery phases, exploiting regularization without destabilizing optimization. It is compatible with existing static and dynamic selection methods and is task-agnostic.
AI.result: Empirical results show consistent improvements in the efficiency-generalization trade-off across datasets, architectures, and tasks: for example, 50% reduction in ImageNet-1k training cost with improved accuracy and more than 2x acceleration for LLM instruction tuning, without performance degradation.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic data-volume scheduling can harness the implicit regularization induced by data selection to improve training efficiency and generalization. PODS provides a lightweight, ratio-level, task-agnostic interface that complements existing selection methods and is broadly applicable across training paradigms.

title: SceneParser: Hierarchical Scene Parsing for Visual Semantics Understanding
comment: Preprint. Code, models, and dataset are provided in the manuscript
summary: General scene perception has progressed from object recognition toward open-vocabulary grounding, part localization, and affordance prediction. Yet these capabilities are often realized as isolated predictions that localize objects, parts, or interaction points without capturing the structured dependencies needed for interaction-oriented scene understanding. To address this gap, we introduce Hierarchical Scene Parsing, an interaction-oriented parsing task that represents physical scenes as explicit scene -> object -> part -> affordance hierarchies with cross-level bindings. We instantiate this task with SceneParser, a VLM-based parser trained for unified hierarchical generation with structural-completion pseudo labels and curriculum learning. To support training and evaluation, we construct SceneParser-Bench, a large-scale benchmark built with a scalable hierarchical data engine, containing 110K training images, a 5K validation split, 777K objects, 1.14M parts, 1.74M affordance annotations, and 1.74M valid object-part-affordance chain instances. We further introduce Level-1 to Level-3 conditional metrics and ParseRate to evaluate localization, cross-level binding, and hierarchical completeness. Experiments show that existing MLLMs and perception-stitching pipelines struggle with hierarchical parsing on our SceneParser-Bench, while SceneParser achieves stronger structure-aware performance. Besides, ablations, evaluations on COCO and AGD20K, and a downstream planning probe demonstrate that our SceneParser is compatible with conventional tasks and provides an actionable representation for visual understanding.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Scene Parsing introduces a task and model that capture explicit scene->object->part->affordance hierarchies, with a scalable benchmark and new metrics, showing improved structure-aware parsing over baselines and compatibility with conventional tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where perception outputs (objects, parts, affordances) are produced separately without modeling cross-level dependencies. Propose an interaction-oriented, hierarchical representation to support downstream planning and reasoning.
AI.method: Define the Hierarchical Scene Parsing task and instantiate SceneParser, a VLM-based parser trained with unified hierarchical generation, using structural-completion pseudo labels and curriculum learning. Build SceneParser-Bench with a scalable data engine: 110K training images, 5K validation, 777K objects, 1.14M parts, 1.74M affordances, and 1.74M valid object–part–affordance chains. Introduce Level-1 to Level-3 conditional metrics and ParseRate for localization, cross-level binding, and hierarchical completeness.
AI.result: Baseline methods (MLLMs and perception-stitching pipelines) struggle with hierarchical parsing on SceneParser-Bench, while SceneParser achieves stronger structure-aware performance. Additional ablations, evaluations on COCO and AGD20K, and a downstream planning probe demonstrate compatibility with conventional tasks and applicability to planning-oriented understanding.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a scalable, interaction-oriented hierarchical parsing framework and benchmark, with a model that leverages unified generation and curriculum learning to produce structure-aware scene representations suitable for downstream planning and tasks.

title: Peng's Q($λ$) for Conservative Value Estimation in Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: We propose a model-free offline multi-step reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm, Conservative Peng's Q($λ$) (CPQL). Our algorithm adapts the Peng's Q($λ$) (PQL) operator for conservative value estimation as an alternative to the Bellman operator. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work in offline RL to theoretically and empirically demonstrate the effectiveness of conservative value estimation with a \textit{multi-step} operator by fully leveraging offline trajectories. The fixed point of the PQL operator in offline RL lies closer to the value function of the behavior policy, thereby naturally inducing implicit behavior regularization. CPQL simultaneously mitigates over-pessimistic value estimation, achieves performance greater than (or equal to) that of the behavior policy, and provides near-optimal performance guarantees -- a milestone that previous conservative approaches could not achieve. Extensive numerical experiments on the D4RL benchmark demonstrate that CPQL consistently and significantly outperforms existing offline single-step baselines. In addition to the contributions of CPQL in offline RL, our proposed method also contributes to the offline-to-online learning framework. Using the Q-function pre-trained by CPQL in offline settings enables the online PQL agent to avoid the performance drop typically observed at the start of fine-tuning and to attain robust performance improvements. Our code is available at https://github.com/oh-lab/CPQL.

AI.tldr: CPQL: a conservative multi-step offline RL algorithm using Peng's Q(λ) operator; shows theoretical and empirical benefits, outperforms single-step baselines, and enables robust offline-to-online fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Offline RL suffers from distribution shift and overestimation; prior conservative methods were typically single-step and lacked strong guarantees. A multi-step, conservative operator could better leverage offline trajectories and provide tighter performance guarantees tied to the behavior policy.
AI.method: Introduce Conservative Peng’s Q(λ) (CPQL) by replacing the Bellman operator with a conservative Peng’s Q(λ) operator in offline RL. Analyze fixed points to show closeness to behavior policy value, yielding implicit behavior regularization. Demonstrate near-optimal guarantees, mitigate over-pessimism, and achieve returns at least as good as the behavior policy. Evaluate on D4RL; show strong gains over offline single-step baselines. Extend to offline-to-online learning by pretraining the Q-function for robust online fine-tuning.
AI.result: Empirically CPQL consistently outperforms existing offline single-step baselines on D4RL; addresses over-pessimism while delivering strong performance guarantees, and enables improved offline-to-online transitions via pretraining. Code released.
AI.conclusion: Multi-step conservative value estimation via CPQL is effective for offline RL, offering both strong theoretical guarantees and practical performance, and beneficial for offline-to-online deployment.

title: Road Maps as Free Geometric Priors: Weather-Invariant Drone Geo-Localization with GeoFuse
summary: Drone-view geo-localization aims to match a query drone image, often captured under adverse weather conditions (e.g., rain, snow, fog), against a gallery of geo-tagged satellite images. Weather-induced degradations in the drone view, such as noise, reduced visibility, and partial occlusions, severely exacerbate the intrinsic cross-view domain gap. While prior methods predominantly rely on weather-specific architectures or data augmentations, they have largely overlooked road map data, a readily available modality that provides strong, inherently weather-invariant geometric layout cues (e.g., road networks and building footprints) at negligible additional cost. We introduce GeoFuse, a cross-modal fusion framework that integrates precisely aligned road map tiles with satellite imagery to yield more discriminative and weather-resilient representations. We first augment the existing University-1652 and DenseUAV benchmarks with geo-aligned road maps, supplying structural priors robust to meteorological variations.
