Building on this, we propose a flexible fusion module that combines satellite and road map features via token-level and channel-level interactions, with a lightweight dynamic gating mechanism that adaptively weights modality contributions per instance. Finally, we employ class-level cross-view contrastive learning to promote robust alignment between weather-degraded drone features and the fused satellite-roadmap representations. Extensive experiments under diverse weather conditions show that GeoFuse consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving +3.46% and +23.18% Recall@1 accuracy on the University-1652 and DenseUAV benchmarks, respectively.

AI.tldr: GeoFuse is a cross-modal fusion method that augments satellite imagery with geo-aligned road maps to improve drone-view geo-localization under adverse weather, using token- and channel-level fusion with dynamic gating and cross-view contrastive learning; it achieves notable improvements on University-1652 and DenseUAV.
AI.motivation: Weather-induced degradation widens the cross-view gap between drone and satellite imagery. Road maps provide weather-invariant, high-level geometric priors (roads, footprints) that can be fused with satellite data to enhance discriminability without much extra cost.
AI.method: Integrate geo-aligned road map tiles with satellite imagery. A flexible fusion module performs token-level and channel-level interactions, guided by a lightweight dynamic gating mechanism that weighs modality contributions per instance. Train with class-level cross-view contrastive learning to align weather-degraded drone features with fused satellite-roadmap representations.
AI.result: GeFuse outperforms state-of-the-art methods under diverse weather conditions, achieving +3.46% Recall@1 on University-1652 and +23.18% Recall@1 on DenseUAV.
AI.conclusion: Road-map augmentation provides robust, weather-invariant geometric priors that, when fused with satellite imagery through adaptive token- and channel-level interactions and cross-view contrastive learning, yield more discriminative and weather-resilient drone-to-satellite geo-localization.

title: Understanding Imbalanced Forgetting in Rehearsal-Based Class-Incremental Learning
comment: 37 pages; 24 tables; 7 figures; submitted to a journal
summary: Neural networks suffer from catastrophic forgetting in class-incremental learning (CIL) settings. Rehearsal$\unicode{x2013}$replaying a subset of past samples$\unicode{x2013}$is a well-established mitigation strategy. However, recent results suggest that, despite balanced rehearsal allocation, some classes are forgotten substantially more than others. Despite its relevance, this imbalanced forgetting phenomenon remains underexplored. This work shows that imbalanced forgetting arises systematically and severely in rehearsal-based CIL and investigates it extensively. Specifically, we construct, from a principled analysis, three last-layer coefficients that capture different gradient-level sources of interference affecting each past class during an incremental step. We then demonstrate that, together, they reliably predict how past classes will rank in terms of forgetting at the end of that step. While predictive performance alone does not establish causality, these results support the interpretation of the coefficients as a plausible mechanistic account linking last-layer gradient-level interactions during training to class-level forgetting outcomes. Notably, one coefficient$\unicode{x2013}$capturing self-induced interference$\unicode{x2013}$emerges as the strongest predictor, with controlled experiments providing evidence consistent with this coefficient being influenced by the new-class interference coefficient. Overall, our findings provide valuable insights and suggest promising directions for mitigating imbalanced forgetting by reducing class-wise disparities in the identified sources of interference.

AI.tldr: Imbalanced forgetting in rehearsal-based class-incremental learning (CIL) can be predicted by three last-layer gradient-interference coefficients, with self-induced interference as strongest predictor; findings suggest targeting class-wise interference to mitigate forgetting.
AI.motivation: Address systematic, uneven forgetting of past classes in rehearsal-based CIL despite balanced rehearsal, and identify gradient-level factors that explain and potentially mitigate this phenomenon.
AI.method: The authors derive three last-layer coefficients from a principled gradient analysis that capture sources of interference on each past class during an incremental step; they evaluate the predictive power of these coefficients for ranking forgetfulness across past classes and perform controlled experiments to relate coefficients, especially self-induced interference, to forgetting.
AI.result: The three coefficients jointly predict the ranking of past-class forgetting at the end of each incremental step; self-induced interference is the strongest predictor; new-class interference can modulate the self-induced term; imbalanced forgetting is systemic and can be mitigated by reducing disparities in these interference sources.
AI.conclusion: Imbalanced forgetting in rehearsal-based CIL is mechanistically linked to gradient-level interferences captured by the proposed coefficients, offering a pathway to mitigation by reducing class-wise disparities in these sources of interference.

title: SCRWKV: Ultra-Compact Structure-Calibrated Vision-RWKV for Topological Crack Segmentation
summary: Achieving pixel-level accurate segmentation of structural cracks across diverse scenarios remains a formidable challenge. Existing methods face significant bottlenecks in balancing crack topology modeling with computational efficiency, often failing to reconcile high segmentation quality with low resource demands. To address these limitations, we propose the Ultra-Compact Structure-Calibrated Vision RWKV (SCRWKV), a network that achieves high-precision modeling via a novel Structure-Field Encoder (SFE) backbone while maintaining linear complexity. The SFE integrates the Adaptive Multi-scale Cascaded Modulator (AMCM) to enhance texture representation and utilizes the Structure-Calibrated Insight Unit (SCIU) as its core engine. Specifically, the SCIU employs the Geometry-guided Bidirectional Structure Transformation (GBST) to capture topological correlations and integrates the Dynamic Self-Calibrating Decay (DSCD) into Dy-WKV to suppress noise propagation. Furthermore, we introduce a lightweight Cross-Scale Harmonic Fusion (CSHF) decoder to achieve precise feature aggregation. Systematic evaluations on multiple benchmarks characterized by complex textures and severe interference demonstrate that SCRWKV, with only 1.22M parameters, significantly outperforms SOTA methods. Achieving an F1 score of 0.8428 and mIoU of 0.8512 on the TUT dataset, the model confirms its robust potential for efficient real-world deployment. The code is available at https://github.com/zhxhzy/SCRWKV.

AI.tldr: A compact, structure-aware crack segmentation network (SCRWKV) with 1.22M parameters achieves state-of-the-art-like performance on crack datasets, using a Structure-Field Encoder backbone with novel modules to model structure while maintaining linear complexity.
AI.motivation: Crack segmentation requires accurate topology modeling across diverse textures while minimizing computational resources; existing methods trade off segmentation quality against efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes Ultra-Compact Structure-Calibrated Vision RWKV (SCRWKV) with a Structure-Field Encoder (SFE) backbone. Key components include Adaptive Multi-scale Cascaded Modulator (AMCM) for texture representation, Structure-Calibrated Insight Unit (SCIU) with Geometry-guided Bidirectional Structure Transformation (GBST) to model topology, Dynamic Self-Calibrating Decay (DSCD) integrated into Dy-WKV to suppress noise, and a lightweight Cross-Scale Harmonic Fusion (CSHF) decoder for feature aggregation. Claims linear computational complexity.
AI.result: On benchmarks with complex textures and interference, SCRWKV (1.22M params) outperforms SOTA methods, achieving F1=0.8428 and mIoU=0.8512 on the TUT crack dataset.
AI.conclusion: SCRWKV demonstrates strong potential for efficient real-world deployment and provides code for reproducibility.

title: GFMate: Empowering Graph Foundation Models with Test-time Prompt Tuning
summary: Graph prompt tuning has shown great potential in graph learning by introducing trainable prompts to enhance the model performance in conventional single-domain scenarios. Recent research has extended graph prompts to improve Graph Foundation Models (GFMs) by few-shot tuning auxiliary prompts. Despite their progress, most existing methods embed source-domain information into prompts, which serve either as input to GFMs or encoded during model pre-training. Such prompt entanglement with specific source domains and GFM pre-training strategy restricts their generalisability to other domains and different GFMs. Furthermore, existing GFM prompts merely rely on few-shot tuning for adaptation, neglecting the rich information in unlabelled target domain test data. Motivated by these insights, this paper aims to empower GFMs with pre-training-agnostic test-time graph prompt tuning, named GFMate. GFMate introduces centroid and layer prompts applied after pre-training on target domains, avoiding entanglement with specific source domains and model pre-training. In addition, a test-time complementary learning objective is devised to exploit both labelled and unlabelled target domain data for effective test-time prompt tuning. Extensive experiments on 12 benchmark datasets demonstrate the superior performance and efficiency of GFMate, achieving improvements of up to 30.63%. Code is available at https://github.com/YanJiangJerry/GFMate.

AI.tldr: GFMate enables pre-training-agnostic test-time graph prompt tuning for Graph Foundation Models by introducing centroid and layer prompts after pre-training on target domains and a test-time complementary learning objective that leverages both labeled and unlabeled target data, achieving up to 30.63% improvements on 12 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To improve generalization of graph prompts across different GFMs and domains by removing dependence on source-domain pretraining, and to exploit unlabeled test data during adaptation instead of relying solely on few-shot labeled data.
AI.method: After pre-training, apply centroid prompts (global representations) and layer prompts (per-layer prompts) on the target-domain data, decoupled from source-domain pretraining. Introduce a test-time complementary learning objective that jointly utilizes labeled and unlabeled target data for prompt tuning at inference time.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 12 benchmark datasets shows superior performance and efficiency, with improvements up to 30.63% over baselines.
AI.conclusion: GFMate offers a pre-training-agnostic, test-time prompt-tuning framework for GFMs that leverages both labeled and unlabeled target data, enhancing generalization across domains and GFMs; code is released.

title: Multi-scale Coarse-to-fine Modeling for Test-time Human Motion Control
summary: We present MSCoT, a multi-scale, coarse-to-fine model for test-time human motion synthesis and control. Unlike recent approaches that rely on multiple iterative denoising/token-prediction steps, or modules tailored for specific control signals, MSCoT discretizes motion into a multi-scale hierarchical representation and predicts the entire token sequence at each temporal scale in a coarse-to-fine fashion. Building on this coarse-to-fine paradigm, we propose an efficient multi-scale token guidance strategy that overcomes the challenge of discrete sampling and steers the token distribution towards the control goals, allowing for fast and flexible control. To address the limitations of a discrete codebook, a lightweight token refiner further adds continuous residuals to the discrete token embeddings and allows differentiable test-time refinement optimization to ensure precise alignment with the control objectives. MSCoT is able to produce quality motions, consistent with the control constraints, while offering substantially faster sampling than diffusion-based approaches. Experiments on popular benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art controllable text-to-motion generation performance of MSCoT over existing baselines, with better motion quality (48% FID improvement), higher control accuracy (-61% avg error), and $10 \times$ faster inference speed on HumanML3D.

AI.tldr: MSCoT introduces a multi-scale coarse-to-fine framework for test-time motion synthesis that predicts complete token sequences at each scale, aided by a multi-scale token guidance mechanism and a lightweight token refiner with differentiable residuals. This yields fast, controllable motion generation with high quality, outperforming diffusion-based baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-motion approaches rely on iterative denoising or rigid discrete codes, which are slow and inflexible for diverse control signals. There is a need for fast, accurate, and controllable motion synthesis that can align precisely with control objectives.
AI.method: The model discretizes motion into a multi-scale hierarchical token representation and predicts whole token sequences at each temporal scale in a coarse-to-fine manner. A multi-scale token guidance strategy addresses discrete sampling and steers the token distribution toward control goals. A lightweight token refiner adds continuous residuals to discrete token embeddings, enabling differentiable test-time refinement to align with control objectives.
AI.result: MSCoT achieves state-of-the-art controllable text-to-motion generation on benchmarks such as HumanML3D, with 48% improvement in FID (quality) and 61% reduction in average control error, along with approximately 10× faster inference speed compared to diffusion-based methods.
AI.conclusion: MSCoT offers fast, high-quality controllable motion synthesis by combining multi-scale coarse-to-fine token prediction, guided sampling, and continuous residual refinements, delivering strong controllability and efficiency gains over prior approaches.

title: ToMAToMP: Robust and Multi-Parameter Topological Clustering
summary: Topological clustering, and its main algorithm ToMATo, is a clustering method from Topological Data Analysis (TDA) which has been applied successfully in several applications during the last few years. This is due to its high versatility, as clusters are detected from the persistent components in the sublevel sets of any user-defined function (gene expression, pixel values, etc), and efficiency, as topological clustering enjoys robustness guarantees. However, ToMATo is also limited in several ways. First, a graph on the data points needs to be provided as a hyper-parameter of the method (whose fine-tuning is left to the user). Second, ToMATo is known to be very sensitive to outlier values in the function range. Finally, and most importantly, ToMATo can only handle one function at a time, whereas it is critical to use several functions in various applications. In this article, we introduce ToMAToMP: the first topological clustering method able to handle several functions at the same time with theoretical guarantees. More specifically, we leverage a recent tool from multi-parameter persistent homology, called MMA decomposition, to design our clustering algorithm, and prove that it enjoys robustness properties. As corollaries, we show that it can be used to make ToMATo independent of graph tuning, and robust to outliers. Finally, we provide a set of numerical experiments showcasing the efficiency and quality of the clusterings produced by ToMAToMP, by showing strong improvement over non-topological and topological baselines for various datasets.

AI.tldr: Introduces ToMAToMP, a multi-function topological clustering method with theoretical guarantees, extending ToMATo via MMA decomposition to handle several functions, reducing graph sensitivity and improving robustness to outliers, with empirical validation.
AI.motivation: ToMATo has limitations: requires a user-defined graph, is sensitive to outliers, and handles only a single function. There is a need for a robust, multi-function clustering method with guarantees.
AI.method: Employs MMA decomposition from multi-parameter persistent homology to design a clustering algorithm that processes multiple functions concurrently. Establishes theoretical robustness guarantees and derives corollaries such as independence from graph tuning.
AI.result: The approach yields theoretical robustness guarantees; corollaries show independence from graph tuning and robustness to outliers. Numerical experiments demonstrate improved clustering quality over non-topological and topological baselines across various datasets.
AI.conclusion: First multi-function topological clustering method with guarantees; shows robustness and reduced parameter sensitivity, highlighting promising applications and directions for future work.

title: ACE-LoRA: Adaptive Orthogonal Decoupling for Continual Image Editing
summary: State-of-the-art diffusion models often rely on parameter-efficient fine-tuning to perform specialized image editing tasks. However, real-world applications require continual adaptation to new tasks while preserving previously learned knowledge. Despite the practical necessity, continual learning for image editing remains largely underexplored. We propose ACE-LoRA, a dynamic regularization framework for continual image editing that effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting. ACE-LoRA leverages Adaptive Orthogonal Decoupling to identify and orthogonalize task interference, and introduces a Rank-Invariant Historical Information Compression strategy to address scalability issues in continual updates. To facilitate continual learning in image editing and provide a standardized evaluation protocol, we introduce CIE-Bench, the first comprehensive benchmark in this domain. CIE-Bench encompasses diverse and practically relevant image editing scenarios with a balanced level of difficulty to effectively expose limitations of existing models while remaining compatible with parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms existing baselines in terms of instruction fidelity, visual realism, and robustness to forgetting, establishing a strong foundation for continual learning in image editing.

AI.tldr: ACE-LoRA introduces a dynamic regularization framework for continual image editing with diffusion models, using Adaptive Orthogonal Decoupling to mitigate task interference and Rank-Invariant Historical Information Compression for scalability; CIE-Bench provides a standardized evaluation suite. Empirically, it improves instruction fidelity, visual realism, and reduces forgetting over baselines.
AI.motivation: Continual learning for image editing is underexplored and essential for real-world deployment. Existing diffusion fine-tuning methods struggle to adapt to new tasks without catastrophic forgetting, especially under parameter-efficient fine-tuning constraints.
AI.method: ACE-LoRA combines Adaptive Orthogonal Decoupling to orthogonalize task interference across tasks and a Rank-Invariant Historical Information Compression mechanism to retain useful historical information while scaling updates. It is evaluated on CIE-Bench, a new benchmark designed for continual image editing with a protocol aligned to parameter-efficient fine-tuning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments claim consistent gains over baselines in instruction fidelity, visual realism, and robustness to forgetting across diverse editing tasks, demonstrating the method’s effectiveness and generalization within the continual image editing setting.
AI.conclusion: The work advances continual learning in image editing by (1) proposing a dynamic regularization framework with orthogonal decoupling and historical information compression, (2) introducing the first comprehensive CIE-Bench benchmark, and (3) validating improved performance and resilience to forgetting for diffusion-based image editing under PEFT constraints.

title: GenAI for Energy-Efficient and Interference-Aware Compressed Sensing of GNSS Signals on a Google Edge TPU
summary: Traditional methods for classifying global navigation satellite system (GNSS) jamming signals typically involve post-processing raw or spectral data streams, requiring complex and costly data transmission to cloud-based interference classification systems. In contrast, our proposed approach efficiently compresses GNSS data streams directly at the hardware receiver while simultaneously classifying jamming and spoofing attacks in real time. Given the growing prevalence of GNSS jamming, there is a critical need for real-time solutions suitable for power-constrained environments. This paper introduces a novel method for compressing and classifying GNSS jamming threats using generative artificial intelligence (GenAI), specifically variational autoencoders (VAEs), deployed on Google Edge tensor processing units (TPUs). The study evaluates various autoencoder (AE) architectures to compress and reconstruct GNSS signals, focusing on preserving interference characteristics while minimizing data size near the receiver hardware. The pipeline adapts large-scale AE models for Google Edge TPUs through 8-bit quantization to ensure energy-efficient deployment. Tests on raw in-phase and quadrature-phase (IQ) data, Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) data, and handcrafted features show the system achieves significant compression (>42x) and accurate classification of approximately 72 interference types on reconstructed signals (F2-score 0.915), closely matching the original signals (F2-score 0.923). The hardware-centric GenAI approach also substantially reduces jammer signal transmission costs, offering a practical solution for interference mitigation. Ablation studies on conditional and factorized VAEs (i.e., FactorVAE) explore latent feature disentanglement for data generation, enhancing model interpretability and fostering trust in machine learning (ML) solutions for sensitive interference applications.

AI.tldr: Compresses GNSS data at the receiver with VAEs on Google Edge TPUs to enable real-time jamming/spoofing classification and reduce data transmission. Achieves >42x compression and ~0.915 F2-score on reconstructed signals, closely matching the original (0.923).
AI.motivation: Address real-time GNSS interference mitigation in power-constrained environments without costly cloud-based processing, by performing compression and classification directly at the hardware receiver.
AI.method: Adapt large-scale variational autoencoder (VAE) models for Google Edge TPUs with 8-bit quantization. Evaluate multiple autoencoder architectures on raw IQ data, FFT-derived data, and handcrafted features. Perform ablations on conditional VAEs and FactorVAE for latent disentanglement. Deploy and test in a hardware-centric GenAI pipeline on Edge TPUs for real-time compression and interference classification.
AI.result: Demonstrates significant data compression (>42x) and accurate classification of ~72 interference types on reconstructed signals (F2-score 0.915) versus original signals (F2-score 0.923). Reduces jammer transmission costs and enables energy-efficient interference mitigation. Ablation studies suggest improved interpretability through latent disentanglement.
AI.conclusion: A hardware-centric GenAI approach using 8-bit-quantized VAEs on Edge TPUs can effectively compress GNSS signals and classify jamming/spoofing in real time, offering a practical solution for power-constrained receivers and reducing backhaul/data transmission needs; ablations indicate potential for improved interpretability via disentangled latent representations.

title: A CUBS-Compatible Ultrasound Morphology and Uncertainty-Aware Baseline for Carotid Intima-Media Segmentation and Preliminary Risk Prediction
summary: Carotid atherosclerosis is a major contributor to ischemic stroke and transient ischemic attack. Conventional ultrasound assessment is commonly based on intima-media thickness, plaque appearance, stenosis degree, and peak systolic velocity, but these morphology- and velocity-based indicators may not fully capture patient-specific vascular risk. This study presents AtheroFlow-XNet, a CUBS-compatible ultrasound morphology and uncertainty-aware learning baseline for carotid intima-media segmentation and preliminary risk prediction. Using the Carotid Ultrasound Boundary Study dataset, manual lumen-intima and media-adventitia boundary annotations were converted into dense intima-media masks for supervised segmentation. Clinical variables were incorporated into an auxiliary risk-prediction branch, and Monte Carlo dropout was used for uncertainty-aware inference. The model was evaluated using a patient-level train-validation-test split with 1,522 training images, 326 validation images, and 328 testing images. The proposed model achieved a Dice coefficient of 0.7930 for LI-MA mask segmentation, a segmentation loss of 0.2359, and an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.6910 for preliminary risk prediction. Qualitative results showed that predicted masks were generally aligned with manual annotations, while uncertainty maps highlighted ambiguous wall-boundary regions. These results suggest that ultrasound-derived carotid morphology can support automated wall analysis and uncertainty-aware interpretation. Since CUBS does not provide Doppler waveforms or CFD-derived hemodynamic biomarkers, this work should be interpreted as a reproducible morphology-driven baseline. Future work will incorporate Doppler-derived flow profiles, patient-specific vascular reconstruction, and CFD-based wall shear biomarkers.

AI.tldr: AtheroFlow-XNet is a morphology-based, uncertainty-aware ultrasound segmentation model for carotid IM segmentation with a preliminary risk predictor, evaluated on the Carotid Ultrasound Boundary Study dataset. It achieves Dice=0.793 for LI-MA segmentation and AUC=0.691 for risk, using MC dropout for uncertainty and an auxiliary risk branch; results show plausible mask alignment but limited by lack of Doppler/CFD data, framing as a reproducible morphology baseline with future hemodynamic integration.
AI.motivation: Conventional ultrasound metrics (thickness, plaque morphology, stenosis, flow velocity) may not capture patient-specific vascular risk. A morphology- and uncertainty-aware learning approach could provide more robust wall segmentation and a risk signal that complements clinical data, especially when Doppler/CFD data are unavailable.
AI.method: AtheroFlow-XNet: a morphology-focused segmentation network that produces dense LI-MA masks from ultrasound images. An auxiliary risk-prediction branch ingests clinical variables. Uncertainty in predictions is estimated via Monte Carlo dropout. Training uses a patient-level train/validation/test split (1,522/326/328 images) on the Carotid Ultrasound Boundary Study dataset with manual LI-MA boundaries converted to masks.
AI.result: Dice for LI-MA segmentation: 0.7930; segmentation loss: 0.2359; AUC for preliminary risk: 0.6910. Qualitative results indicate good mask alignment with manual annotations, and uncertainty maps highlight ambiguous wall-boundary regions.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that ultrasound-derived carotid morphology can support automated wall analysis with uncertainty-aware interpretation, serving as a reproducible morphology-driven baseline (CUBS limitation: no Doppler/CFD biomarkers). Future work should incorporate Doppler flow profiles, patient-specific vascular reconstruction, and CFD-based wall shear biomarkers to enhance risk prediction.

title: In-Context Learning for Data-Driven Censored Inventory Control
summary: We study inventory control with decision-dependent censoring, focusing on the censored or repeated newsvendor (R-NV), where each order quantity determines whether demand is fully observed or censored by sales. Existing approaches based on parametric Thompson sampling (TS) can be brittle under prior mismatch, while offline imputation methods need not transfer to online learning. Motivated by the predictive view of decision making, we combine these ideas by taking oracle actions on learned completions of latent demand. We propose in-context generative posterior sampling (ICGPS), which uses modern generative models that are meta-trained offline and deployed online by in-context autoregressive generation. Theoretically, we show that the Bayesian regret of ICGPS with a learned completion kernel is bounded by the Bayesian regret of a TS benchmark with the ideal completion kernel plus a deployment penalty scaling as $\sqrt{T}$ times the square root of the completion mismatch. This yields a plug-in template for operational problems with known TS regret bounds. For R-NV, we derive sublinear Bayesian regret by reducing censored feedback to bandit convex optimization feedback. We also show that, under reasonable coverage and stability assumptions, the online completion mismatch is controlled by the offline censored predictive mismatch, so offline predictive quality transfers to online performance. Practically, we instantiate ICGPS with ChronosFlow, which combines a frozen time-series transformer backbone with a trainable conditional normalizing-flow head for fast censoring-consistent sampling. In benchmark experiments, ChronosFlow-ICGPS matches correctly specified TS, outperforms myopic and UCB-style baselines, and is robust to prior mismatch and distribution shift. ChronosFlow-ICGPS also performs well for the real-world SuperStore dataset, especially under heavy censoring.

AI.tldr: ICGPS is a plug-in online learning framework for decision-dependent censoring (e.g., censored/repeated newsvendor). It combines offline predictive learning with in-context autoregressive generation to produce completions used by oracle actions, achieving sublinear Bayesian regret and robustness to prior mismatch; instantiated as ChronosFlow, it performs well in benchmarks and real data with heavy censoring.
AI.motivation: To overcome brittleness of parametric Thompson sampling under prior mismatch and limitations of offline imputation for online learning in decision-dependent censoring problems like the censored/newsvendor setting.
AI.method: Train generative models offline to learn completions of latent demand; deploy online via in-context autoregressive generation (ICGPS) to propose actions on sampled completions. Provide a theoretical regret bound: Bayesian regret of ICGPS with a learned completion kernel is bounded by TS regret with ideal kernel plus a deployment penalty scaling as sqrt(T) times the completion mismatch. Reduce censored feedback to bandit convex optimization feedback for R-NV; show online completion mismatch is controlled by offline predictive mismatch under coverage/stability assumptions. Implement ChronosFlow: a frozen time-series transformer backbone + trainable conditional normalizing-flow head for fast, censoring-consistent sampling.
AI.result: Sublinear Bayesian regret established for R-NV; offline predictive quality transfers to online performance under mild assumptions; ChronosFlow-ICGPS matches well-specified TS, outperforms myopic/UCB baselines, robust to prior mismatch and distribution shift; strong performance on real-world SuperStore dataset with heavy censoring.
AI.conclusion: ICGPS provides a practical plug-in framework leveraging offline meta-trained generative models for online decision-making under censoring, with theoretical regret guarantees and strong empirical performance; ChronosFlow makes the approach scalable and competitive in real data.

title: GPart: End-to-End Isometric Fine-Tuning via Global Parameter Partitioning
summary: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) has become the dominant paradigm for parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) of large language models (LLMs). However, its bilinear structure introduces a critical limitation: the mapping from trainable parameters to weight updates is not distance-preserving, distorting the optimization landscape. Methods that project a low-dimensional vector into LoRA's parameter space, such as Uni-LoRA, improve parameter efficiency, but the subsequent bilinear LoRA map breaks end-to-end isometry, leaving the core distance-preservation problem unresolved. We propose GPart (Global Partition fine-tuning), a highly parameter-efficient fine-tuning method which removes the low-rank bottleneck entirely. Our method uses a single isometric partition matrix to map a $d$-dimensional trainable vector directly into the full weight space of the model. The result is an extremely minimal fine-tuning pipeline: one random projection, end-to-end isometric, with a single clean hyperparameter ($d$) and storage cost of $d+1$ values (the trainable vector plus a random seed). GPart builds on the theoretical premise that effective fine-tuning can emerge from random low-dimensional subspaces of the full weight space, without imposing low-rank matrix structure. We empirically demonstrate the superior or comparable performance of GPart to existing PEFT methods on natural language understanding, computer vision tasks, and mathematical reasoning. Overall, GPart achieves state-of-the-art efficiency and performance by removing structural constraints, offering a straightforward and elegant path to PEFT.

AI.tldr: GPart removes the low-rank bottleneck of LoRA by using a single isometric partition matrix to map a small trainable vector directly into the full weight space, enabling end-to-end isometry with extremely little storage (d+1 values) via a random projection. It claims state-of-the-art efficiency and competitive performance across NLP, vision, and reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the non–distance-preserving bilinear LoRA mapping that distorts the optimization landscape and requires low-rank constraints. Seek parameter efficiency without low-rank factorization by leveraging end-to-end isometry through random low-dimensional subspace projections.
AI.method: Replace LoRA’s bilinear low-rank update with a single isometric partition matrix that directly maps a d-dimensional trainable vector to the full model weight space. Uses a random projection with a single hyperparameter d and a seed; no low-rank decomposition; extremely compact storage (d+1). Builds on the idea that effective fine-tuning can emerge from random subspaces of the full weight space.
AI.result: Empirically shows superior or comparable performance to existing PEFT methods on natural language understanding, computer vision tasks, and mathematical reasoning, with enhanced efficiency by removing structural constraints.
AI.conclusion: Removing the low-rank bottleneck and adopting an isometric projection yields a simple, highly parameter-efficient PEFT method with end-to-end isometry, broad applicability, and strong empirical performance.

title: H-OmniStereo: Zero-Shot Omnidirectional Stereo Matching with Heading-Aligned Normal Priors
summary: Stereo matching on top-bottom equirectangular images provides an effective framework for full-surround perception, as vertically aligned epipolar lines enable the use of advanced perspective stereo architectures that are largely driven by large-scale datasets and monocular priors. However, the performance of such adaptations is severely limited by the scarcity of omnidirectional stereo datasets and the degradation of perspective monocular priors under spherical distortions.To address these challenges, we propose H-OmniStereo, a zero-shot omnidirectional stereo matching framework. First, we construct high-quality synthetic dataset comprising over 2.8 million top-bottom equirectangular stereo pairs to scale up training. Second, we introduce an equirectangular monocular normal estimator, specifically operating in a heading-aligned coordinate system. Beyond providing distortion-robust and cross-view-consistent geometric priors for establishing reliable correspondences in stereo matching, this design boosts training efficiency and accommodates train-test FoV mismatches.Extensive experiments show that our approach achieves higher accuracy than existing methods on out-of-domain datasets and successfully generalizes to real-world consumer camera setups using a single model. Both the model and the dataset will be open-sourced.

AI.tldr: A zero-shot omnidirectional stereo method, H-OmniStereo, uses a large synthetic dataset of 2.8M top-bottom equirectangular pairs and an equirectangular monocular normal estimator in a heading-aligned frame to improve stereo matching on 360-degree images, achieving better cross-domain generalization; the dataset and model will be open-sourced.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of omnidirectional stereo datasets and deterioration of monocular priors under spherical distortions hinder applying  perspective stereo architectures to 360-degree imagery. A synthetic, well-structured training pipeline and distortion-robust priors are needed to enable reliable correspondences and generalization.
AI.method: 1) Create a high-quality synthetic dataset with over 2.8 million top-bottom equirectangular stereo pairs. 2) Introduce an equirectangular monocular normal estimator operating in a heading-aligned coordinate system to supply geometric priors and improve correspondence estimation in stereo. 3) Build a zero-shot framework that leverages these priors for robust and cross-view-consistent stereo matching, accommodating FoV mismatches.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show higher accuracy than existing methods on out-of-domain datasets and successful generalization to real-world consumer camera setups using a single model.
AI.conclusion: The authors will open-source both the model and the dataset. The approach promises distortion-robust, cross-view-consistent priors that improve omni-stereo matching and training efficiency, with good cross-domain generalization.

title: MHSA: A Lightweight Framework for Mitigating Hallucinations via Steered Attention in LVLMs
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have achieved remarkable performance across diverse multimodal tasks, yet they continue to suffer from hallucinations, generating content that is inconsistent with the visual input. Prior work DHCP (Detecting Hallucinations by Cross-modal Attention Pattern) has explored hallucination detection from the perspective of cross-modal attention, but does not address hallucination mitigation. In this paper, we propose MHSA (Mitigating Hallucinations via Steered Attention), a lightweight framework that mitigates hallucinations by learning to correct cross-modal attention patterns in LVLMs. MHSA trains a simple three-layer MLP generator to produce corrected attention, guided by supervisory signals from the DHCP discriminator and the LVLM itself. During inference, MHSA mitigates both discriminative and generative hallucinations across various datasets and LVLMs by simply replacing the original cross-modal attention with the corrected one, without modifying any LVLM parameters. By extending cross-modal attention mechanisms from hallucination detection to hallucination mitigation, MHSA offers a novel perspective on hallucination research in LVLMs and helps enhance their reliability.

AI.tldr: MHSA introduces a lightweight cross-modal attention correction module that mitigates hallucinations in LVLMs by replacing attention patterns during inference, without retraining LVLMs.
AI.motivation: LVLMs still suffer from hallucinations; while prior work DHCP detects hallucinations via cross-modal attention, mitigation is needed to improve reliability.
AI.method: Train a three-layer MLP generator to produce corrected cross-modal attention, supervised by the DHCP discriminator and cues from the LVLM. At inference time, replace the LVLM’s original cross-modal attention with the corrected attention; no LVLM parameter updates.
AI.result: The approach mitigates both discriminative and generative hallucinations across multiple datasets and LVLMs, in a plug-and-play fashion.
AI.conclusion: Extends cross-modal attention from detection to mitigation, offering a lightweight, model-agnostic mechanism to enhance LVLM reliability without modifying base models.

title: Exploitation of Hidden Context in Dynamic Movement Forecasting: A Neural Network Journey from Recurrent to Graph Neural Networks and General Purpose Transformers
summary: Forecasting within signal processing pipelines is crucial for mitigating delays, particularly in predicting the dynamic movements of objects such as NBA players. This task poses significant challenges due to the inherently interactive and unpredictable nature of sports, where abrupt changes in velocity and direction are prevalent. Traditional approaches, including (S)ARIMA(X), Kalman filters (KF), and Particle filters (PF), often struggle to model the non-linear dynamics present in such scenarios. Machine learning (ML) methods, such as long short-term memory (LSTM) networks, graph neural networks (GNNs), and Transformers, offer greater flexibility and accuracy but frequently fail to explicitly capture the interplay between temporal dependencies and contextual interactions, which are critical in chaotic sports environments. In this paper, we evaluate these models and assess their strengths and weaknesses. Experimental results reveal key performance trade-offs across input history length, generalizability, and the ability to incorporate contextual information. ML-based methods demonstrated substantial improvements over linear models across forecast horizons of up to 2s. Among the tested architectures, our hybrid LSTM augmented with contextual information achieved the lowest final displacement error (FDE) of 1.51m, outperforming temporal convolutional neural network (TCNN), graph attention network (GAT), and Transformers, while also requiring less data and training time compared to GAT and Transformers. Our findings indicate that no single architecture excels across all metrics, emphasizing the need for task-specific considerations in trajectory prediction for fast-paced, dynamic environments such as NBA gameplay.

AI.tldr: Hybrid LSTM with contextual information achieves the best final displacement error among tested models for NBA trajectory forecasting, outperforming linear models up to 2s, with less data/training time than GAT/Transformers; no single architecture dominates across all metrics.
AI.motivation: Forecasting in dynamic, interactive sports environments is essential to mitigate delays and account for non-linear, context-dependent trajectories; traditional linear models struggle with abrupt velocity/direction changes, while ML models must capture temporal-context interactions.
AI.method: Evaluate traditional time-series models (SARIMA(X), KF, PF) and ML approaches (LSTM, GNNs, Transformers); compare TCNN, GAT, and Transformers; propose a hybrid LSTM augmented with contextual information; assess performance across input history length, generalizability, and context integration.
AI.result: ML methods substantially outperform linear models for forecast horizons up to 2s. The hybrid LSTM with contextual info achieves lowest final displacement error (FDE) of 1.51m, better than TCNN, GAT, and Transformers, and requires less data and training time than GAT/Transformers.
AI.conclusion: No single architecture is universally best; trajectory prediction in fast-paced environments benefits from task-specific design, balancing accuracy, data efficiency, and computation; contextual augmentation and hybrid models can offer superior performance in practical NBA-style scenarios.

title: MicroscopyMatching: Towards a Ready-to-use Framework for Microscopy Image Analysis in Diverse Conditions
summary: Analyzing microscopy images to extract biological object properties (e.g., their morphological organization, temporal dynamics, and population density) is fundamental to various biomedical research. Yet conducting this manually is costly and time-consuming. Though deep learning-based approaches have been explored to automate this process, the substantial diversity of microscopy analysis settings in practice (including variations of biological object types, sample processing protocols, imaging equipment, and analysis tasks, etc.) often renders them ineffective. As a result, these approaches typically require extensive adaptation for different settings, which, however, can impose burdens that are often practically unsustainable for laboratories, forcing biomedical researchers to still commonly rely on manual analysis, thereby severely bottlenecking the pace of biomedical research progress. This situation has created a pressing and long-standing need for a reliable and broadly applicable microscopy image analysis tool, yet such a tool is still missing. To address this gap, we present the first ready-to-use microscopy image analysis framework, MicroscopyMatching, that can reliably perform key analysis tasks (including segmentation, tracking, and counting) across diverse microscopy analysis settings. From a fundamentally different perspective, MicroscopyMatching reformulates diverse microscopy image analysis tasks as a unified matching problem, effectively handling this problem by exploiting the robust matching capability from pre-trained latent diffusion models.

AI.tldr: A novel ready-to-use framework, MicroscopyMatching, enables segmentation, tracking, and counting across diverse microscopy settings by reframing analysis tasks as a unified matching problem that leverages pre-trained latent diffusion models.
AI.motivation: There is a longstanding need for a reliable, broadly applicable microscopy image analysis tool due to the diversity of object types, processing protocols, imaging equipment, and tasks, which makes current deep learning methods ineffective without extensive adaptation.
AI.method: Formulates microscopy analysis tasks (segmentation, tracking, counting) as a unified matching problem and utilizes robust matching from pre-trained latent diffusion models to handle cross-setting variability.
AI.result: Introduces MicroscopyMatching as the first ready-to-use framework capable of performing key analysis tasks across diverse microscopy settings, highlighting a unified matching approach and leveraging latent diffusion models to achieve robust performance.
AI.conclusion: MicroscopyMatching addresses the bottleneck in microscopy image analysis by providing a general, ready-to-use solution that unifies tasks under a matching framework and leverages powerful pretrained diffusion models.

title: REALM: Retrospective Encoder Alignment for LFP Modeling
summary: Spike activity has been the dominant neural signal for behavior decoding due to its high spatial and temporal resolution. However, as brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) move toward high channel counts and wireless operation, the high sampling frequency of spike signals becomes a bottleneck due to high power and bandwidth requirements. Local field potentials (LFPs) represent a different spatial-temporal scale of brain activity compared to spikes, offering key advantages including improved long-term stability, reduced energy consumption, and lower bandwidth requirement. Despite these benefits, LFP-based decoding models typically show reduced accuracy and often rely on non-causal architectures that are unsuitable for real-time deployment. To address these challenges, we propose REALM: a retrospective distillation framework that enables causal LFP decoding. Inspired by offline-to-online distillation strategies in speech recognition, REALM transfers representational knowledge from a pretrained multi-session bidirectional LFP model to a causal version for real-time deployment. We first pretrain a bidirectional Mamba-2 teacher model using a masked autoencoding objective. We then distill this teacher model into a compact student model via a combined objective of representation alignment and task supervision. REALM consistently outperforms both causal and non-causal LFP-based SOTA methods for behavior decoding. Notably, our REALM improves decoding performance while achieving a $2\times$ reduction in parameter count and a $10\times$ reduction in training time. These results demonstrate that retrospective distillation effectively bridges the gap between offline and real-time neural decoding. REALM shows that LFP-only models can achieve competitive decoding performance without reliance on spike signals, offering a practical and scalable alternative for next-generation wireless implantable BCIs.

AI.tldr: REALM is a retrospective distillation framework that enables causal LFP decoding by transferring knowledge from a pretrained bidirectional LFP teacher to a causal student. It achieves competitive decoding performance with ~2× fewer parameters and ~10× faster training, addressing real-time, energy-efficient BCI deployment without spikes.
AI.motivation: BCIs using spike signals face high power and bandwidth demands at high channel counts. LFPs offer better long-term stability and lower energy/bandwidth requirements, but LFP-based decoders often underperform and rely on non-causal architectures unsuited for real-time use. This work aims to bridge offline-online performance gaps and enable real-time LFP decoding.
AI.method: Pretrain a bidirectional Mamba-2 LFP model as a teacher using a masked autoencoding objective across multi-session data. Distill to a causal student via a combined objective: representation alignment (feature-level alignment with the teacher) and task supervision, enabling online, causal decoding. This offline-to-online distillation is inspired by similar strategies in speech recognition.
AI.result: REALM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art LFP-based methods for behavior decoding, including both causal and non-causal approaches. It achieves ~2× reduction in parameter count and ~10× reduction in training time while maintaining or improving decoding accuracy, demonstrating competitive LFP-only performance without spikes.
AI.conclusion: Retrospective distillation effectively bridges the offline-online gap in neural decoding, enabling practical, scalable, real-time LFP-based BCIs for wireless implants without relying on spike signals.

title: Compositional Video Generation via Inference-Time Guidance
summary: Text-to-video diffusion models generate realistic videos, but often fail on prompts requiring fine-grained compositional understanding, such as relations between entities, attributes, actions, and motion directions. We hypothesize that these failures need not be addressed by retraining the generator, but can instead be mitigated by steering the denoising process using the model's own internal grounding signals. We propose \textbf{CVG}, an inference-time guidance method for improving compositional faithfulness in frozen text-to-video models. Our key observation is that cross-attention maps already encode how prompt concepts are grounded across space and time. We train a lightweight compositional classifier on these attention features and use its gradients during early denoising steps to steer the latent trajectory toward the desired composition. Built on a frozen VLM backbone, the classifier transfers across semantically related composition labels rather than relying only on narrow category-specific features. CVG improves compositional generation without modifying the model architecture, fine-tuning the generator, or requiring layouts, boxes, or other user-supplied controls. Experiments on compositional text-to-video benchmarks show improved prompt faithfulness while preserving the visual quality of the underlying generator.

AI.tldr: CVG introduces inference-time guidance for compositional fidelity in frozen text-to-video models by training a lightweight classifier on cross-attention features and using its gradients to steer early denoising steps.
AI.motivation: Address failures of T2V models to accurately compose entities, attributes, actions, and motion directions without retraining or modifying the generator.
AI.method: Train a small compositional classifier on cross-attention maps from the frozen T2V backbone. During early diffusion steps, backpropagate through this classifier to steer the latent trajectory toward the target composition; no architecture changes, no fine-tuning of the generator, and no user-supplied layouts.
AI.result: CVG yields improved prompt faithfulness on compositional T2V benchmarks while preserving the visual quality of the base generator.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time, model-agnostic compositional steering via attention-grounded gradients provides scalable fidelity gains for T2V models without retraining.

title: AIMing for Standardised Explainability Evaluation in GNNs: A Framework and Case Study on Graph Kernel Networks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have advanced significantly in handling graph-structured data, but a comprehensive framework for evaluating explainability remains lacking. Existing evaluation frameworks primarily involve post-hoc explanations, and operate in the setting where multiple methods generate a suite of explanations for a single model. This makes comparison of explanations across models difficult. Evaluation of inherently interpretable models often targets a specific aspect of interpretability relevant to the model, but remains underdeveloped in terms of generating insight across a suite of measures. We introduce AIM, a comprehensive framework that addresses these limitations by measuring Accuracy, Instance-level explanations, and Model-level explanations. AIM is formulated with minimal constraints to enhance flexibility and facilitate broad applicability. Here, we use AIM in a pipeline, extracting explanations from inherently interpretable GNNs such as graph kernel networks (GKNs) and prototype networks (PNs), evaluating these explanations with AIM, identifying their limitations and obtaining insights to their characteristics. Taking GKNs as a case study, we show how the insights obtained from AIM can be used to develop an updated model, xGKN, that maintains high accuracy while demonstrating improved explainability. Our approach aims to advance the field of Explainable AI (XAI) for GNNs, providing more robust and practical solutions for understanding and improving complex models.

AI.tldr: AIM provides a flexible framework to evaluate accuracy and explanations at instance and model levels for GNNs, enabling cross-model explainability comparisons and guiding improvements, demonstrated on GKNs and PN, leading to xGKN.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of GNN explainability is fragmented, often post-hoc and model-specific; there is a need for a unified, flexible framework to assess multiple explainability aspects across different models.
AI.method: Define AIM with three axes: Accuracy, Instance-level explanations, Model-level explanations; apply to inherently interpretable GNNs (GKNs, Prototypes); use AIM to diagnose limitations and inform development of xGKN.
AI.result: AIM-based evaluation reveals insights into explainability characteristics of GKNs and PNs; xGKN achieves high accuracy with improved explainability; demonstrates practicality of AIM for XAI in GNNs.
AI.conclusion: AIM advances XAI evaluation for GNNs through a flexible, multi-aspect framework that supports both analysis and model improvement.

title: Characterizing the visual representation of objects from the child's view
summary: Children acquire object category representations from their everyday experiences in the first few years of life. What do the inputs to this learning process look like? We analyzed first-person videos of young children's visual experience at home from the BabyView dataset ($N$ = 31 participants, 868 hours, ages 5--36 months), using a supervised object detection model to extract common object categories from more than 3 million frames. We found that children's object category exposure was highly skewed: a few categories (e.g., cups, chairs) dominated children's visual experiences while most categories appeared rarely, replicating previous findings from a more restricted set of contexts. Category exemplars were highly variable: children encountered objects from unusual angles, in highly cluttered scenes, and partially occluded views; many categories (especially animals) were most frequently viewed as depictions. Surprisingly, despite this variability, detected categories (e.g., giraffes, apples) showed stronger groupings within superordinate categories (e.g., animals, food) relative to groupings derived from canonical photographs of these categories. We found this same pattern when using high-dimensional embeddings from both self-supervised visual and multimodal models; this effect was also recapitulated in densely sampled data from individual children. Understanding the robustness and efficiency of visual category learning will require the development of models that can exploit strong superordinate structure and learn from non-canonical, sparse, and variable exemplars.

AI.tldr: Children’s object-category learning is shaped by highly skewed, variable real-world input; strong superordinate structure guides representations despite non-canonical, sparse exemplars, as seen in both children's data and model embeddings.
AI.motivation: To understand what visual inputs drive early object-category learning and how representations arise from naturalistic experience rather than curated stimuli.
AI.method: Analyzed BabyView, a home-first-person video dataset (N=31; 868 hours; ages 5–36 months). Used supervised object detection to extract common object categories from >3 million frames. Assessed exposure skew, exemplar variability (angles, clutter, occlusion), and prevalence of depictions. Examined category groupings within superordinate categories, and replicated patterns in self-supervised visual and multimodal embeddings; validated with densely sampled data from individual children.
AI.result: Exposure to object categories is highly skewed: a few categories dominate; many categories appear rarely. Exemplars are highly variable, often non-canonical and cluttered; animals frequently appear as depictions. Detected categories show stronger intra-superordinate grouping (e.g., animals, food) than groupings derived from canonical photographs. This pattern also appears in high-dimensional embeddings from self-supervised and multimodal models and in densely sampled child data.
AI.conclusion: Robust visual-category learning may rely on exploiting strong superordinate structure and learning from sparse, non-canonical exemplars. Future models should handle skewed input and variable views, leveraging superordinate priors to achieve efficient learning in early development.

title: Critic-Driven Voronoi-Quantization for Distilling Deep RL Policies to Explainable Models
summary: Despite many successful attempts at explaining Deep Reinforcement Learning policies using distillation, it remains difficult to balance the performance-interpretability trade-off and select a fitting surrogate model. In addition to this, traditional distillation only minimizes the distance between the behavior of the original and the surrogate policy while other RL-specific components such as action value are disregarded. To solve this, we introduce a new model-agnostic method called Critic-Driven Voronoi State Partitioning, which partitions a black box control policy into regions where a simple class of model can be optimized using gradient descent. By exploiting the critic value network of the original policy, we iteratively introduce new subpolicies in regions with insufficient value, standing in for a measure of policy complexity. The partitioning, a Voronoi quantizer, uses nearest neighbor lookups to assign a linear function to each point in the state space resulting in a cell-like diagram. We validate our approach on several well known benchmarks and proof that this distillation approaches the original policy using a reasonable sized set of linear functions.

AI.tldr: A model-agnostic, critic-guided Voronoi partitioning method distills a deep RL policy into a compact set of linear subpolicies, improving interpretability while preserving performance.
AI.motivation: Balance performance and interpretability in distillation; current methods ignore RL-specific components like value; need region-based representations guided by the critic to identify regions requiring richer subpolicies.
AI.method: Construct a Voronoi partition of the state space; use the critic value network to identify regions with insufficient value; iteratively add linear subpolicies optimized by gradient descent; assign each state to the nearest subpolicy; evaluate and update until performance closely matches the original policy.
AI.result: Empirical validation on standard benchmarks showing the distilled policy with a reasonable number of linear functions closely matches the original policy's performance.
AI.conclusion: Provides a scalable, model-agnostic path to interpretable distillation in DRL; demonstrates that a finite, piecewise-linear policy can approximate a deep policy; future work includes refining partitions and extending to nonlinear subpolicies or other architectures.

title: Predicting Response to Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy in Ovarian Cancer from CT Baseline Using Multi-Loss Deep Learning
summary: Ovarian cancer is the most lethal gynecologic malignancy: around 60% of patients are diagnosed at an advanced stage, with an associated 5-year survival rate of about 30%. Early identification of non-responders to neoadjuvant chemotherapy remains a key unmet need, as it could prevent ineffective therapy and avoid delays in optimal surgical management. This work proposes a non-invasive deep learning framework to predict neoadjuvant chemotherapy response from pre-treatment contrast-enhanced CT by leveraging automatically derived 3D lesion masks. The approach encodes axial slices with a partially fine-tuned pretrained image encoder and aggregates slice-level representations into a volumetric embedding through an attention-based module. Training combines classification loss with supervised contrastive regularization and hard-negative mining to improve separation between ambiguous responders and non-responders. The method was developed on a retrospective single-center cohort from the European Institute of Oncology (Milan, IT), including 280 eligible patients (147 responder, 133 non-responder). On the test cohort, the model achieved a ROC-AUC of 0.73 (95% CI: 0.58-0.86) and an F1-score of 0.70 (95% CI: 0.56-0.82). Overall, these results suggest that the proposed architecture learns clinically relevant predictive patterns and provides a robust foundation for an imaging-based stratification tool.

AI.tldr: Non-invasive deep learning approach predicting NAC response in ovarian cancer from pre-treatment CT using automatically derived 3D lesion masks; leverages slice-level encodings and attention-based aggregation with supervised contrastive learning; retrospective single-center study (N=280) achieving ROC-AUC 0.73 and F1 0.70 on test set.
AI.motivation: Early identification of non-responders to neoadjuvant chemotherapy to avoid ineffective therapy and delays in optimal surgery; ovarian cancer often diagnosed at advanced stage with poor survival.
AI.method: Automatically derived 3D lesion masks; partially fine-tuned pretrained image encoder to encode axial slices; aggregates slice representations into volumetric embedding via attention-based module; training with classification loss + supervised contrastive regularization + hard-negative mining.
AI.result: Test cohort ROC-AUC 0.73 (95% CI 0.58-0.86); F1-score 0.70 (95% CI 0.56-0.82); retrospective single-center Milan cohort; N=280 (147 responders, 133 non-responders).
AI.conclusion: Proposed architecture learns clinically relevant predictive patterns and provides a robust foundation for imaging-based stratification; external validation and multicenter studies are needed to confirm generalizability.

title: TILBench: A Systematic Benchmark for Tabular Imbalanced Learning Across Data Regimes
summary: Imbalanced learning remains a fundamental challenge in tabular data applications. Despite decades of research and numerous proposed algorithms, a systematic empirical understanding of how different imbalanced learning methods behave across diverse data characteristics is still lacking. In particular, it remains unclear how different method families compare in predictive performance, robustness under varying data characteristics, and computational scalability. In this work, we present Tabular Imbalanced Learning Benchmark (TILBench), a large-scale empirical benchmark for tabular imbalanced learning. TILBench evaluates more than 40 representative algorithms across 57 diverse tabular datasets, resulting in over 200000 controlled experiments across a wide range of data characteristics. Our findings show that no single method consistently dominates across all settings; instead, the effectiveness of imbalanced learning methods depends strongly on dataset characteristics and computational constraints. Based on these findings, we provide practical recommendations for selecting appropriate methods in real-world applications.

AI.tldr: Tabular Imbalanced Learning Benchmark (TILBench) shows no universal winner: algorithm performance varies with data characteristics and resources across 40+ methods and 57 datasets.
AI.motivation: Despite decades of work, there is limited systematic empirical understanding of how imbalanced learning methods perform under diverse data conditions; need guidance for selecting methods in real-world, resource-constrained tabular problems.
AI.method: Large-scale empirical benchmark: 40+ imbalanced-learning algorithms, 57 tabular datasets, 200k+ controlled experiments, varying data characteristics (e.g., class imbalance ratio, noise, feature distribution, correlation, sample size), measures predictive performance, robustness, and scalability; across settings.
AI.result: No single method dominates across all settings; method effectiveness depends strongly on dataset characteristics and computational constraints; some classes (e.g., resampling, cost-sensitive learning, algorithm-level approaches) perform better under certain regimes; performance-metrics vary; general recommendations derived.
AI.conclusion: Provide practical guidance for practitioners to select methods based on data characteristics and computational budgets; emphasize dataset-aware approach rather than one-size-fits-all; benchmark can inform method choice and future research directions.

title: 3D Skew-Normal Splatting
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a leading representation for real-time novel view synthesis and been widely adopted in various downstream applications. The core strength of 3DGS lies in its efficient kernel-based scene representation, where Gaussian primitives provide favorable mathematical and computational properties. However, under a finite primitive budget, the symmetric shape of each primitive directly affects representation compactness, especially near asymmetric structures such as object boundaries and one-sided surfaces. Recent works have explored more complex kernel distributions, yet they either remain within the elliptical family or rely on hard truncation, which limits continuous shape control and introduces distributional discontinuities. In this paper, we propose Skew-Normal Splatting (SNS), which adopts the Azzalini Skew-Normal distribution as the fundamental primitive. By introducing a learnable and bounded skewness parameter, SNS can continuously interpolate between symmetric Gaussians and Half-Gaussian-like shapes, enabling flexible modeling of both sharp boundaries and interior regions. Moremover, SNS preserves analytical tractability under affine transformations and marginalization. This property allows seamless integration into existing Gaussian Splatting rasterization pipelines.Furthermore, to address the strong coupling between scale, rotation, and skewness parameters, we introduce a decoupled parameterization and a block-wise optimization strategy to enhance training stability and accuracy. Extensive experiments on standard novel-view synthesis benchmarks show that SNS consistently improves reconstruction quality over Gaussian and recent non-Gaussian kernels, with clearer benefits on sharp boundaries and thin or one-sided structures.

AI.tldr: Skew-Normal Splatting (SNS) introduces the Azzalini Skew-Normal as the primitive in 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling continuous interpolation between symmetric Gaussians and skewed shapes to better model sharp boundaries and thin structures. It preserves affine-marginal tractability and uses a decoupled, block-wise optimization to stabilize training.
AI.motivation: Standard 3D Gaussian splatting uses symmetric primitives; under a finite primitive budget, this symmetry limits representation compactness, especially at object boundaries and asymmetric regions. Existing non-Gaussian kernels are either still elliptical or rely on hard truncation, causing discontinuities and limited continuous control.
AI.method: Adopt the Azzalini Skew-Normal distribution as the primitive. Introduce a learnable and bounded skewness parameter to continuously interpolate between Gaussian and Half-Gaussian-like shapes. Ensure analytical tractability under affine transformations and marginalization. Propose a decoupled parameterization and block-wise optimization to reduce coupling among scale, rotation, and skewness.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard novel-view synthesis benchmarks show SNS consistently improves reconstruction quality over Gaussian and recent non-Gaussian kernels, with clearer benefits on sharp boundaries and thin or one-sided structures.
AI.conclusion: SNS provides a flexible, controllable primitive for 3D Gaussian Splatting that integrates into existing pipelines, offering better handling of asymmetric regions and improved reconstruction quality, while maintaining tractable optimization.

title: A Mutual Information Lower Bound for Multimodal Regression Active Learning
summary: Active learning for continuous regression has lacked an acquisition function that targets epistemic uncertainty when the predictive distribution is multimodal: variance misses modal disagreement, and information-theoretic targets like BALD are designed for discrete outputs. We introduce a Two-Index framework that makes this separation explicit: one stochastic index selects among competing model hypotheses (epistemic source), while a second governs within-hypothesis randomness (aleatoric source). An entropy decomposition within the framework identifies the mutual information between the output and the epistemic index as a principled acquisition objective, and we prove this quantity vanishes as the model is trained on growing datasets, confirming that it captures exactly the uncertainty data can resolve. Because this mutual information is intractable for continuous outputs, we derive the Mutual Information Lower Bound (MI-LB) acquisition function, a closed-form approximation for Mixture Density Network ensembles. On benchmarks featuring multimodal systems, MI-LB matches or beats every baseline evaluated and is the only method to do so consistently -- geometric and Fisher-based baselines compete only when the input space already encodes the multimodality, and collapse otherwise.

AI.tldr: Introduces a Two-Index framework to target epistemic uncertainty in multimodal continuous regression; derives an MI-based acquisition objective and a tractable MI-LB surrogate for MDN ensembles; MI-LB achieves state-of-the-art performance on multimodal benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Epistemic uncertainty in continuous regression with multimodal outputs is not well captured by variance or BALD-like criteria; need a method that separates epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty and yields a principled acquisition objective.
AI.method: Proposes a Two-Index framework: one stochastic index selects among competing model hypotheses (epistemic), another governs within-hypothesis randomness (aleatoric). Performs an entropy decomposition to identify mutual information between the output and the epistemic index. Derives a Mutual Information Lower Bound (MI-LB) as a closed-form surrogate for MI in continuous outputs, specialized to Mixture Density Network ensembles.
AI.result: On multimodal benchmarks, MI-LB matches or outperforms all baselines and is the sole method with consistent superiority. Geometric and Fisher-based baselines succeed only when the input already encodes multimodality and otherwise collapse.
AI.conclusion: The mutual information between output and the epistemic index provides a principled acquisition objective for continuous regression under multimodality. MI-LB offers a tractable, accurate surrogate enabling effective active learning for multimodal systems; performance advantage arises from explicitly separating epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty.

title: HiSem: Hierarchical Semantic Disentangling for Remote Sensing Image Change Captioning
summary: Remote sensing image change captioning (RSICC) aims to achieve high-level semantic understanding of genuine changes occurring between bi-temporal images. Despite notable progress, existing methods are fundamentally limited by a shared modeling assumption: changed and unchanged image pairs, which have intrinsically different semantic granularities, are processed under a unified modeling strategy. This modeling inconsistency leads to semantic entanglement between coarse-grained change existence judgment and fine-grained semantic understanding.To address the above limitation, we propose a novel hierarchical semantic disentangling network (HiSem) that explicitly disentangles semantic representations of different granularities. Specifically, we first introduce the Bidirectional Differential Attention Modulation (BDAM) module that leverages discrepancy-aware attention to enhance cross-temporal interactions, thereby amplifying true change signals while suppressing irrelevant variations. Building upon this, we design a Hierarchical Adaptive Semantic Disentanglement (HASD) module that performs adaptive routing at two hierarchical levels: a coarse-grained image-level routing mechanism distinguishes changed and unchanged image pairs, while a fine-grained token-level Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) block models diverse and heterogeneous change semantics for changed samples. Extensive experiments on two benchmark datasets demonstrate that HiSem outperfoms previous methods, achieving a significant improvement of +7.52\% BLEU-4 on the WHU-CDC dataset. More importantly, our approach provides a structured perspective for RSICC by explicitly aligning model design with the intrinsic semantic heterogeneity of bi-temporal scenes. The code will be available at https://github.com/Man-Wang-star/HiSem

AI.tldr: Introduces HiSem, a hierarchical semantic disentangling network for RSICC that separates coarse change existence from fine-grained change semantics using BDAM and HASD, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Existing RSICC methods process bi-temporal pairs with a unified modeling strategy, causing semantic entanglement between coarse change existence and fine-grained change semantics due to different granularities. A disentangled, multi-granularity approach is needed.
AI.method: BDAM module for discrepancy-aware cross-temporal attention to amplify true change signals; HASD module with two-level adaptive routing: (1) image-level coarse-grained routing to distinguish changed vs unchanged; (2) token-level Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) to model diverse change semantics for changed samples.
AI.result: Outperforms prior methods, achieving a +7.52 BLEU-4 improvement on the WHU-CDC dataset; validated on two benchmark datasets; code to be released.
AI.conclusion: The HiSem framework provides a structured perspective for RSICC by aligning model design with the intrinsic semantic heterogeneity of bi-temporal scenes, explicitly disentangling multi-granularity semantics and delivering substantial performance gains.

title: Learning with Shallow Neural Networks on Cluster-Structured Features
summary: The success of deep learning in high-dimensional settings is often attributed to the presence of low-dimensional structure in real-world data. While standard theoretical models typically assume that this structure lies in the target function, projecting unstructured inputs onto a low-dimensional subspace, data such as images, text or genomic sequences exhibit strong spatial correlations within the input space itself. In this paper, we propose a tractable model to study how these correlations affect the sample complexity of learning with gradient descent on shallow neural networks. Specifically, we consider targets that depend on a small number of latent Boolean variables, and input features grouped into clusters and correlated with the latent variables. Under an identifiability assumption, we show that for a layerwise gradient-descent variant, the sample complexity scales with the number of hidden variables and, when the signal-to-noise ratio is sufficiently high, is independent of the input dimension, up to logarithmic terms. We empirically test our theoretical findings on both synthetic and real data.

AI.tldr: Under input correlations, shallow nets trained with a layerwise gradient-descent variant achieve sample complexity that scales with the number of latent variables and, at sufficiently high SNR, is independent of input dimension (up to logarithmic factors).
AI.motivation: Real-world data (images, text, genomes) exhibit strong spatial correlations in the input space. Standard models often place structure in the target function; this work studies how input correlations affect learning sample complexity with gradient descent.
AI.method: Propose a tractable model where targets depend on a small set of latent Boolean variables. Input features are clustered and correlated with latent variables. Under an identifiability assumption, analyze a layerwise gradient-descent training dynamics for a shallow network to derive sample complexity scaling.
AI.result: The sample complexity scales with the number of hidden variables. When the signal-to-noise ratio is sufficiently high, it becomes independent of the input dimension up to logarithmic factors.
AI.conclusion: Theoretical predictions are validated empirically on synthetic and real data. The results indicate that input correlations can reduce effective learning complexity, enabling dimension-free performance in high-SNR regimes for shallow networks under the proposed model.

title: EverAnimate: Minute-Scale Human Animation via Latent Flow Restoration
summary: We propose EverAnimate, an efficient post-training method for long-horizon animated video generation that preserves visual quality and character identity. Long-form animation remains challenging because highly dynamic human motion must be synthesized against relatively static environments, making chunk-based generation prone to accumulated drift: (i) low-level quality drift, such as progressive degradation of static backgrounds, and (ii) high-level semantic drift, such as inconsistent character identity and view-dependent attributes.
