To address this issue, EverAnimate restores drifted flow trajectories by anchoring generation to a persistent latent context memory, consisting of two complementary mechanisms. (i) Persistent Latent Propagation maintains a context memory across chunks to propagate identity and motion in latent space while mitigating temporal forgetting. (ii) Restorative Flow Matching introduces an implicit restoration objective during sampling through velocity adjustment, improving within-chunk fidelity. With only lightweight LoRA tuning, EverAnimate outperforms state-of-the-art long-animation methods in both short- and long-horizon settings: at 10 seconds, it improves PSNR/SSIM by 8%/7% and reduces LPIPS/FID by 22%/11%; at 90 seconds, the gains increase to 15%/15% and 32%/27%, respectively.

AI.tldr: EverAnimate presents a post-training method for long-horizon animated video generation that preserves visual quality and character identity by using a persistent latent context memory and a restorative flow matching objective, achieved with lightweight LoRA tuning. It yields consistent quality gains over short and long horizons.
AI.motivation: Long-form animation struggles with drift when chunk-based generation is stitched across segments. Visual quality degrades and character identity/view-dependent attributes become inconsistent due to accumulated drift between chunks.
AI.method: Introduce a persistent latent context memory with two mechanisms: (1) Persistent Latent Propagation to carry identity and motion information across chunks in latent space, mitigating temporal forgetting; (2) Restorative Flow Matching that applies an implicit restoration objective during sampling via velocity adjustment to improve within-chunk fidelity. The approach uses lightweight LoRA tuning for parameter efficiency.
AI.result: Quantitative gains reported: at 10 seconds, PSNR up by 8%, SSIM up by 7%, LPIPS down by 22%, FID down by 11%. At 90 seconds, PSNR up by 15%, SSIM up by 15%, LPIPS down by 32%, FID down by 27%. It outperforms state-of-the-art long-animation methods in both short- and long-horizon settings.
AI.conclusion: EverAnimate effectively combats both low-level and high-level drift in long-horizon animation with a memory-based latent propagation and an implicit restoration objective, achieving notable quality improvements with efficient, parameter-light tuning.

title: A Hardware-Aware, Per-Layer Methodology for Post-Training Quantization of Large Language Models
summary: Scaled Outer Product (SOP) is a post-training quantization methodology for large language model weights, designed to deliver near-lossless fidelity at 4.5--6 bits per weight on hardware with per-layer LUT decode. The methodology combines per-layer search of fixed and dynamic codebook pairs selected by a per-block selection bit, signed per-block scales, activation-weighted cosine selection, and multiple-choice knapsack promotion of sensitive layers with outlier and sparse-residual correction. Fixed codebooks include NF4, BOF4, Split87, and SH4; per-layer optimized codebooks (DD4) are hosted in LUT SRAM. A new hardware-efficient LUT output format (HIF) is proposed to improve performance, energy, and cost. Across six open model families, the recommended FP6 operating point (E2M3sUE4M4, 6.5 bpw) achieves lower weight reconstruction error than the conventional per-layer-POT FP8 baseline (E4M3, 8.0 bpw) at 1.5 bpw lower storage cost, demonstrating that block-scaled small atoms with carefully chosen scale precision can replace conventionally-deployed FP8. Full evaluation across the 4.5--6 bpw range, including layer promotion and sparse residual correction, is reported in a companion paper.

AI.tldr: Scaled Outer Product (SOP) post-training quantization achieves near-lossless weight fidelity at 4.5–6 bits/weight using per-layer fixed/dynamic codebooks, per-block selection, signed per-block scales, activation-weighted cosine selection, and knapsack-based layer promotion plus sparse-residual correction; introduces a hardware-friendly LUT format (HIF). FP6 (E2M3sUE4M4, 6.5 bpw) outperforms the FP8 baseline (E4M3, 8.0 bpw) with 1.5 bpw storage savings across six open-model families; full 4.5–6 bpw evaluation and companion results are reported separately.
AI.motivation: Reduce storage, energy, and latency for large language model weights via post-training quantization that remains near-lossless, leveraging hardware-friendly LUT-based decoding and targeted handling of outliers and sparsity.
AI.method: Per-layer search over fixed and dynamic codebooks; per-block selection bit; signed per-block scales; activation-weighted cosine-based codebook selection; multiple-choice knapsack promotion to protect sensitive layers; outlier correction and sparse residual correction. Fixed codebooks: NF4, BOF4, Split87, SH4; per-layer optimized codebooks (DD4) stored in LUT SRAM; new LUT output format (HIF) to improve performance, energy, and cost.
AI.result: FP6 operating point at 6.5 bpw (E2M3sUE4M4) yields lower weight reconstruction error than FP8 baseline at 8.0 bpw (E4M3) and reduces storage by 1.5 bpw, demonstrated across six open-model families. Full evaluation over 4.5–6 bpw with layer promotion and sparse residual correction is provided in a companion paper.
AI.conclusion: Block-scaled, fine-grained codebooks with carefully chosen scale precision and LUT-based decoding can replace conventional FP8 in post-training quantization, delivering lower reconstruction error and storage with hardware-friendly properties; the approach shows promise but relies on hardware support for per-layer LUTs and codebook management; comprehensive results are detailed in the companion work.

title: LATERN: Test-Time Context-Aware Explainable Video Anomaly Detection
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for video anomaly detection (VAD) due to their strong visual reasoning ability and natural language-based explainability. In this paper, we aim to address a key limitation of such pipelines, which perform segment-level inference independently owing to token constraints and reason without structured temporal context, allowing VLMs to interpret anomalies as deviations from evolving video dynamics rather than producing fragmented predictions and explanations. To specify, we propose a context-aware framework named LATERN, which reformulates VAD as a temporal evidence aggregation process. LATERN consists of two complementary modules: Context-Aware Anomaly Scoring (CEA) and Recursive Evidence Aggregation (REA). CEA introduces a novel image-grounded memory mechanism, which selectively chooses historical content via frame diversity and visual-textual alignment as expanded context to help generate reliable anomaly scores. Building upon these scores, REA performs recursive temporal aggregation to identify coherent anomaly intervals and produce event-level decisions and explanations grounded in visual-textual evidence. Extensive experiments on challenging benchmarks, including UCF-Crime and XD-Violence, show that LATERN enhances detection accuracy and explanation consistency for frozen VLMs during test time, while generating temporally coherent and semantically grounded event-level explanations.

AI.tldr: LATERN reframes video anomaly detection with a context-aware, temporal evidence aggregation framework for vision-language models, enabling coherent event-level decisions and explanations via CEA and REA.
AI.motivation: VAD using vision-language models often performs segment-level inferences independently due to token constraints and lacks structured temporal context, leading to fragmented predictions and explanations.
AI.method: LATERN comprises two modules: (1) Context-Aware Anomaly Scoring (CEA) with an image-grounded memory that selectively retrieves diverse historical frames through visual-textual alignment to provide expanded context for reliable anomaly scoring; (2) Recursive Evidence Aggregation (REA) that recursively aggregates anomaly scores over time to identify coherent anomaly intervals and generate event-level decisions and explanations grounded in visual-textual evidence.
AI.result: On challenging benchmarks (UCF-Crime, XD-Violence), LATERN improves detection accuracy and explanation consistency for frozen VLMs at test time, and yields temporally coherent, semantically grounded event-level explanations.
AI.conclusion: LATERN reframes VAD as a temporal evidence aggregation problem, delivering improved performance and explainability for VLM-based VAD and enabling robust event-level reasoning with temporally coherent explanations.

title: Computational Imaging Priors for Wireless Capsule Endoscopy: Monte Carlo-Guided Hemoglobin Mapping for Rare-Anomaly Detection
comment: 24 pages, 6 figures, 3 tables. Code and trained-model checkpoints at https://github.com/integritynoble/GI_Multi_Task . 6-seed (seeds 41, 42, 43, 44, 45, 47) mean +/- SD ablation as the headline; per-class single-seed=42 analyses in Appendix A
summary:
Background. RGB-trained capsule-endoscopy classifiers underperform on small-vessel vascular findings by
  conflating hemoglobin contrast with bile and illumination falloff. Thus, here we test whether a Monte
  Carlo-inspired analytic model can compute hemoglobin from RGB signal built upon extracted classifier.
  Methods. On Kvasir-Capsule (47,238 frames, video-level 70/15/15 split, 11 evaluable classes) we evaluate two
  software-only configurations against RGB-only EfficientNet-B0 across 6 seeds: (i) a prior P_blood =
  sigma(alpha * (H_norm - 0.5)) * Phi(r) fused as 2 zero-init auxiliary channels; (ii) a distillation head
  training a 3-channel RGB backbone to predict P_blood. Significance: paired DeLong, McNemar, bootstrap CIs
  with Bonferroni correction.
  Results. Across 6 seeds (n=6,423), the analytic prior provides a small but direction-consistent macro-AUC
  improvement: RGB-only 0.760 +/- 0.027, input-fusion 0.783 +/- 0.024 (paired Delta = +0.023, sign-positive on
  5/6 seeds), distillation 0.773 +/- 0.028. The largest robust per-class lift is on Lymphangiectasia, where AUC
  rises from RGB 0.238 +/- 0.057 to input-fusion 0.337 +/- 0.019, sign-consistent across all 6 seeds. On rare
  focal-vascular classes (Angiectasia, Blood - fresh) the prior's per-seed effects are bimodal: seed=42 reaches
  Angiectasia AUC 0.528 -> 0.916, but the cross-seed mean is 0.646 -> 0.608 with sigma_PI = 0.23 - reported as
  a high-variance per-seed exemplar.
  Conclusion. A Monte Carlo-inspired analytic prior provides a small, direction-consistent macro-AUC
  improvement on Kvasir-Capsule across 6 seeds with the largest robust per-class lift on Lymphangiectasia; the
  distillation variant runs on plain 3-channel RGB and yields a free interpretability heatmap.

AI.tldr: A Monte Carlo-inspired analytic prior to estimate hemoglobin from RGB signals yields small but consistent macro-AUC gains over RGB-only CNN baselines in capsule endoscopy, with the largest robust improvement on Lymphangiectasia; a distillation variant provides interpretability heatmaps but shows variable per-class effects for rare vascular findings.
AI.motivation: RGB capsule-endoscopy classifiers are confounded by non-hemoglobin factors (bile, illumination) that degrade detection of small-vessel vascular findings. Introducing a hemoglobin-aware prior/inference pathway aims to disentangle hemoglobin signal from confounders and improve macro-level performance.
AI.method: Evaluate two software-only configurations against an RGB-only EfficientNet-B0 baseline on Kvasir-Capsule (47,238 frames; 11 classes; video-level 70/15/15 split) across 6 seeds. (i) An analytic prior P_blood fused as two auxiliary channels with 0-init (input fusion). (ii) A distillation head training a 3-channel RGB backbone to predict P_blood. Statistical tests include paired DeLong, McNemar, and bootstrap CIs with Bonferroni correction.
AI.result: Macro-AUC improved from RGB 0.760±0.027 to 0.783±0.024 with input fusion (n=6 seeds, Δ=+0.023, positive on 5/6 seeds). Distillation: 0.773±0.028. Largest robust per-class gain on Lymphangiectasia (0.238±0.057 → 0.337±0.019). Rare focal-vascular classes showed high seed-to-seed variability (e.g., Angiectasia: seed 42 0.528→0.916 vs cross-seed mean 0.646→0.608, σ_PI=0.23).
AI.conclusion: A Monte Carlo-inspired analytic prior yields a small, directionally consistent macro-AUC improvement across seeds, with the most robust per-class improvement for Lymphangiectasia; the distillation variant provides interpretability heatmaps while maintaining competitive performance.

title: Octopus: History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization for Continual Learning in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Continual learning in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) aims to sequentially acquire knowledge while mitigating catastrophic forgetting, yet existing methods face inherent limitations: architecture-based approaches incur additional computational overhead and often generalize poorly to new tasks, rehearsal-based methods rely on storing historical data, raising privacy and storage concerns, and conventional regularization-based strategies alone are insufficient to fully prevent parameter interference. We propose Octopus, a two-stage continual learning framework based on History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization (HiFGO), which enforces gradient-level orthogonality without historical task data. Our proposed two-stage finetuning strategy decouples task adaptation from regularization, achieving a principled balance between plasticity and stability. Experiments on UCIT show that Octopus establishes state-of-the-art performance, surpassing prior SOTA by 2.14% and 6.82% in terms of Avg and Last.

AI.tldr: Octopus introduces HiFGO, a history-free gradient-orthogonal continual learning framework for multimodal LLMs, featuring a two-stage finetuning that decouples task adaptation from regularization. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on UCIT, surpassing prior SOTA by 2.14% in Avg and 6.82% in Last.
AI.motivation: Continual learning in multimodal LLMs faces significant limitations: (1) architecture-based methods incur extra compute and often generalize poorly to new tasks; (2) rehearsal-based methods require storing historical data, raising privacy and storage concerns; (3) regularization-only strategies fail to fully prevent parameter interference. A memory-free approach that preserves past knowledge while enabling adaptation is needed.
AI.method: Propose Octopus, a two-stage continual learning framework leveraging History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization (HiFGO). It enforces gradient-level orthogonality to past tasks without using historical task data. The framework adopts a two-stage finetuning strategy that decouples task adaptation from regularization, balancing plasticity and stability.
AI.result: Empirical results on UCIT show Octopus achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing prior SOTA by 2.14% (Avg) and 6.82% (Last).
AI.conclusion: Octopus provides a memory-free, gradient-orthogonal continual learning solution for multimodal LLMs, delivering strong empirical gains on UCIT and addressing key privacy/computational concerns associated with rehearsal and architecture-based methods.

title: On the Cultural Anachronism and Temporal Reasoning in Vision Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are increasingly applied to cultural heritage materials, from digital archives to educational platforms. This work identifies a fundamental issue in how these models interpret historical artifacts. We define this phenomenon as cultural anachronism, the tendency to misinterpret historical objects using temporally inappropriate concepts, materials, or cultural frameworks. To quantify this phenomenon, we introduce the Temporal Anachronism Benchmark for Vision-Language Models (TAB-VLM), a dataset of 600 questions across six categories, designed to evaluate temporal reasoning on 1,600 Indian cultural artifacts spanning prehistoric to modern periods. Systematic evaluations of ten state-of-the-art models reveal significant deficiencies on our benchmark, and even the best model (GPT-5.2) achieves only 58.7% overall accuracy. The performance gap persists across varying architectures and scales, suggesting that cultural anachronism represents a significant limitation in visual AI systems, regardless of model size. These findings highlight the disparity between current VLM capabilities and the requirements for accurately interpreting cultural heritage materials, particularly for non-Western visual cultures underrepresented in training data. Our benchmark provides a foundation for enhancing temporal cognition in multimodal AI systems that interact with historical artifacts. The dataset and code are available in our project page.

AI.tldr: Defines cultural anachronism in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and introduces TAB-VLM, a benchmark with 600 questions over 1,600 Indian artifacts to test temporal reasoning; ten models show persistent deficiencies (best ~58.7% accuracy); highlights non-Western cultural underrepresentation and provides dataset/code for advancing temporal cognition in multimodal AI.
AI.motivation: Address a core gap where VLMs misinterpret historical artifacts by applying temporally incongruent concepts, materials, or cultural frameworks; emphasize the need for robust temporal-cognition, especially for underrepresented cultures.
AI.method: Define cultural anachronism; construct TAB-VLM dataset with 600 questions across six categories; curate 1,600 Indian artifacts spanning prehistoric to modern periods; evaluate ten state-of-the-art VLMs (across architectures and scales) on the benchmark; report overall accuracy.
AI.result: Models show significant deficiencies in temporal reasoning; best performer (GPT-5.2) achieves 58.7% overall accuracy; performance gap persists across architectures and scales, indicating a fundamental limitation.
AI.conclusion: Cultural anachronism is a substantial limitation for visual AI in cultural heritage tasks. TAB-VLM provides a baseline to enhance temporal cognition in multimodal systems. The dataset and code are released for broader use and future improvement, with a focus on non-Western visual cultures.

title: Not All Symbols Are Equal: Importance-Aware Constellation Design for Semantic Communication
comment: Submitted to IEEE GLOBECOM 2026. 6 pages, 8 figures
summary: Semantic communication systems for goal-oriented transmission must protect task-relevant information not only through source compression but also via physical layer mapping. Existing approaches decouple constellation design and semantic encoding, exposing critical symbols to channel errors at the same rate as irrelevant ones. Contrary to this, in this paper, a joint semantic-physical layer framework is proposed, which is composed of a vector quantized-variational autoencoder that extracts discrete latent concepts, a semantic criticality indicator (SCI) that scores each concept by task relevance, and a deep reinforcement learning agent that dynamically selects the transmission subset based on instantaneous channel conditions. At the physical layer, a learned semantic-aware M -QAM constellation assigns symbol positions according to joint co-occurrence statistics and SCI scores, departing from the uniform spacing and Gray coding of standard M -QAM which minimizes average BER without regard for semantic content. We introduce a novel semantic symbol vulnerability (SSV) metric and a semantic protection probability (SPP) to quantify the exposure of task-critical symbols to decoding errors, and prove that any Gray-coded constellation is strictly suboptimal in SCI-Weighted SSV whenever the source exhibits non-uniform semantic importance and co-occurrence statistics. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed constellation achieves near 100% SPP across modulation orders from 4-QAM to 1024-QAM versus 50% for standard constellations at high spectral efficiency, a 21:1 compression ratio with semantic quality above 0.9, generalizing across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and FSDD without modification.

AI.tldr: Joint semantic-physical layer design for goal-oriented transmission that protects task-relevant information via a learned, adaptive constellation and transmission subset selection; introduces semantic symbol vulnerability (SSV) and semantic protection probability (SPP); shows Gray-coded constellations are suboptimal under semantic weighting; achieves near-100% SPP and high compression across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, FSDD.
AI.motivation: Protect task-relevant information beyond source compression by integrating semantic encoding with physical layer mapping and adaptive transmission, addressing the shortcomings of decoupled constellation design that treats all symbols equally.
AI.method: A vector-quantized-variational autoencoder extracts discrete latent concepts; a semantic criticality indicator (SCI) scores concepts by task relevance; a deep reinforcement learning agent selects the transmission subset based on instantaneous channel conditions; at the physical layer, a learned semantic-aware M-QAM constellation places symbols according to co-occurrence statistics and SCI; introduces SSV and SPP metrics and proves Gray-coded constellations are strictly suboptimal in SCI-Weighted SSV when semantic importance/co-occurrence are non-uniform.
AI.result: Simulation results show the proposed constellation achieves near 100% SPP across modulation orders from 4-QAM to 1024-QAM (vs ~50% for standard constellations at high spectral efficiency); achieves ~21:1 compression with semantic quality >0.9; generalizes across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and FSDD without modification.
AI.conclusion: The joint semantic-physical framework substantially improves protection of task-critical information in goal-oriented transmission, outperforming conventional constellations under non-uniform semantic importance and data statistics, with demonstrated generalization; practical considerations (complexity, training data, real-time adaptation) warrant further study.

title: SAGE3D: Soft-guided attention and graph excitation for 3D point cloud corner detection
summary: We present SAGE3D, a hybrid Transformer-based model for corner detection in airborne LiDAR point clouds. We propose a multi-stage solution built on a hierarchical encoder-decoder architecture that progressively downsamples point clouds through Set Abstraction layers and recovers per-point predictions via Feature Propagation. We introduce two innovations: Soft-Guided Attention, which injects ground-truth corner labels as a log-prior into attention logits during training to improve precision; then an Excitatory Graph Neural Network positioned at strategic resolutions in the hierarchy, employing positive-only message passing where high-confidence corners reinforce predictions through learned boosting, optimizing for recall. The hierarchical design enables multi-scale feature extraction while our guided attention and excitatory modules ensure corner signals are amplified rather than diluted across scales.

AI.tldr: Hybrid Transformer-based, multi-stage SAGE3D for corner detection in airborne LiDAR; uses Soft-Guided Attention and an Excitatory Graph Neural Network within a hierarchical encoder–decoder to boost precision and recall across scales.
AI.motivation: Address the sparse, multi-scale nature of airborne LiDAR corners and preserve/amplify corner signals across a hierarchical feature extraction pipeline.
AI.method: Encoder–decoder with Set Abstraction downsampling and Feature Propagation; Soft-Guided Attention injects ground-truth corner log-priors into attention logits during training; Excitatory Graph Neural Network with positive-only message passing at strategic resolutions to boost high-confidence corners.
AI.result: Abstract provides architectural and methodological contributions without reported quantitative results; claims improved precision and recall via the proposed modules.
AI.conclusion: SAGE3D advances corner detection by integrating guided attention and excitatory GNNs into a hierarchical Transformer-based framework, enabling multi-scale corner amplification.

title: Efficient Online Conformal Selection with Limited Feedback
summary: We address the problem of conformal selection, where an agent must select a minimal subset of options to ensure that at least one ``success'' is identified with a pre-specified target probability $φ$. While traditional online conformal prediction focuses on maintaining validity for the observed sequence, minimizing the resource cost (efficiency) of such selections, especially under limited feedback, remains a significant challenge. In this work, we consider settings with the most limited ``bandit'' feedback, and demonstrate that the simple Adaptive Conformal Inference (ACI) update rule, when applied to the appropriate control parameter or dual variable, is both adversarially valid, ensuring the success target is met on average for any input sequence (and hence under distribution shifts), and stochastically efficient, achieving sublinear efficiency regret for $i.i.d.$ inputs against an appropriate stochastic benchmark. We show such guarantees under canonical models capturing bandit and semi-bandit feedback to the agent via a unifying algorithmic technique, and analytic framework involving Lyapunov functions. Our approach handles more complex settings than prior work, while requiring significantly less feedback, and our results provide a new theoretical bridge between efficient online learning with limited feedback and distribution-free uncertainty quantification.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Conformal Inference (ACI) with a properly tuned control/dual variable achieves adversarial validity and sublinear efficiency regret in bandit/semi-bandit conformal selection, via a Lyapunov-function framework.
AI.motivation: Address the resource-cost challenge in online conformal prediction under extremely limited feedback. Extend conformal selection to bandit/semi-bandit settings while maintaining distribution-free validity and improving efficiency, even under distribution shifts.
AI.method: Apply the ACI update rule to a chosen control parameter or dual variable within a unifying algorithmic framework. Use Lyapunov-function analysis to prove adversarial validity (target success probability met on average for any sequence) and stochastic efficiency (sublinear efficiency regret for i.i.d. inputs) under canonical bandit and semi-bandit feedback models.
AI.result: The analysis yields guarantees that the target success probability is met on average for arbitrary sequences and that efficiency regret grows sublinearly under i.i.d. inputs. It demonstrates the method's applicability to more complex feedback models with significantly less feedback, linking efficient online learning with distribution-free uncertainty quantification.
AI.conclusion: The work broadens the scope of efficient online learning with limited feedback by providing a unified theoretical framework that ensures validity and efficiency in conformal selection, even under distribution shifts, thereby bridging practical conformal prediction with robust, low-feedback online learning.

title: CoralLite: μCT Reconstruction of Coral Colonies from Individual Corallites
summary: The life history of an individual coral is archived within the accreting skeleton of the colony. While reef-forming coral colonies (e.g. massive \emph{Porites} sp.) may live for hundreds of years and deposit calcareous structures many metres in height and width, their living tissue is a thin outer surface layer comprised of asexually-dividing polyps that only survive a few years. To understand the rate and timing of polyp division and the consequences for colony skeletal growth, scientists need to track the skeletal corallite deposited around each polyp. Here we propose CoralLite, an annotated μCT scan dataset of entire calcareous skeletons and an associated, first corallite deep learning reconstruction baseline. CoralLite combines fully quantified volumetric segmentations with cross-slice linking for visualisations of 3D models for each corallite up to colony scale. For segmentation, we propose and evaluate in detail a hybrid V-Trans-UNet architecture applicable to segmenting tiled μCT virtual slabs of \emph{Porites} sp. colonies. The model is pre-trained on weakly annotated data and topology-aware fine-tuned using fully annotated slice sections with 8k+ manual corallite region annotations. On unseen slices of the same colony, the resulting model reaches 0.94 topological accuracy at mean Dice scores of 0.77 on the same colony and projection axis, and 0.63 mean Dice scores on a different, biologically unrelated specimen. Whilst our experiments are limited in scale and context, our results show for the first time that visual machine learning can effectively support full 3D individual corallite modelling from μCT scans of coral skeletons alone. For reproducibility and as a baseline for future research we publish our full dataset of 697 μCT slices, 37 partial or full slice annotations, and all network weights and source code with this paper.

AI.tldr: CoralLite: an annotated μCT dataset for Porites skeletal material with a baseline 3D corallite reconstruction using a hybrid V-Trans-UNet. The study demonstrates weakly supervised pretraining with topology-aware fine-tuning to achieve high topological accuracy and competitive Dice scores, and provides a reproducible dataset and code for 3D corallite modelling.
AI.motivation: To quantify polyp division timing and skeletal growth by tracking corallite deposition around each polyp, enabling 3D reconstruction of individual corallites from coral skeletons.
AI.method: Hybrid V-Trans-UNet trained on tiled μCT slabs; pre-trained on weakly annotated data; topology-aware fine-tuning using 8k+ manually annotated corallite regions (slice-level); cross-slice linking for 3D corallite visualization; 697 μCT slices; 37 annotated slices; fully annotated weights and source code released.
AI.result: Topological accuracy of 0.94 with mean Dice 0.77 on the same colony and projection axis; Dice 0.63 on a biologically unrelated specimen; demonstrates feasibility of full 3D corallite modelling from μCT without tissue data; provides a reproducible baseline for future studies.
AI.conclusion: First demonstration that visual machine learning can support 3D corallite reconstruction from coral skeletons; dataset and model provide a baseline for future work, though current experiments are limited in scale and context.

title: InfoSFT: Learn More and Forget Less with Information-Aware Token Weighting
summary:
Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) provides the standard approach for teaching LLMs new behaviors from offline expert demonstrations. However, standard SFT uniformly fits all samples -- including those with low likelihood under the base model -- which can disproportionately drive training updates toward overfitting specific samples rather than learning the target behavior. Moreover, adapting to these unlikely samples induces substantial policy shifts that degrade prior capabilities. Existing methods mitigate this by filtering, regenerating, or down-weighting low-likelihood data. In doing so, they often suppress precisely the novel behaviors the base model has yet to learn.
  We propose InfoSFT, a principled weighting scheme for the SFT objective that concentrates learning signals on maximally informative, medium-confidence tokens -- those neither overly familiar to the base model nor too unlikely to cause instability. Requiring only a one-line modification to the standard token-wise loss, InfoSFT demonstrably improves generalization over vanilla SFT and likelihood-weighted baselines across math, code, and chain-of-thought tasks with diverse model families, while better preserving pre-existing capabilities.

AI.tldr: InfoSFT introduces a simple one-line token-weighting modification to SFT that emphasizes medium-confidence, informative tokens, improving generalization while preserving base capabilities.
AI.motivation: Standard SFT overfits on low-likelihood samples and can degrade pre-existing capabilities. Existing down-weighting or filtering can suppress novel behaviors. A weighting scheme targeting informative tokens should improve learning efficiency without sacrificing previously learned skills.
AI.method: Apply a one-line modification to the token-wise loss that reweights each token's contribution to the SFT objective to emphasize medium-confidence tokens (neither too familiar nor too unlikely). This yields InfoSFT weighting scheme.
AI.result: Across math, code, and chain-of-thought tasks and across diverse model families, InfoSFT improves generalization compared to vanilla SFT and likelihood-weighted baselines and better preserves pre-existing capabilities.
AI.conclusion: InfoSFT provides a simple, effective, and broadly applicable improvement to supervised fine-tuning by focusing learning signals on maximally informative tokens, with minimal change to training code.

title: DriveCtrl: Conditioned Sim-to-Real Driving Video Generation
summary: Large-scale labelled driving video data is essential for training autonomous driving systems. Although simulation offers scalable and fully annotated data, the domain gap between synthetic and real-world driving videos significantly limits its utility for downstream deployment. Existing video generation methods are not well-suited for this task, as they fail to simultaneously preserve scene structure, object dynamics, temporal consistency, and visual realism, all of which are critical for maintaining annotation validity in generated data. In this paper, we present DriveCtrl, a depth-conditioned controllable sim-to-real video generation framework for realistic driving video synthesis. Built upon a pretrained video foundation model, DriveCtrl introduces a structure-aware adapter that enables depth-guided generation while preserving the scene layout and motion patterns of the source simulation, producing temporally coherent driving videos that remain aligned with the original simulated sequences. We further introduce a scalable data generation pipeline that transforms simulator videos into realistic driving footage matching the visual style of a target real-world dataset. The pipeline supports three conditioning signals: structural depth, reference-dataset style, and text prompts, while preserving frame-level annotations for downstream perception tasks. To better assess this task, we propose a driving-domain-specific knowledge-informed evaluation metric called Driving Video Realism Score (DVRS) that assesses the realism of generated videos. Experiments demonstrate that DriveCtrl consistently outperforms the base model and competing alternatives in realism, temporal quality, and perception task performance, substantially narrowing the sim-to-real gap for driving video generation.

AI.tldr: Depth-conditioned, structure-aware sim-to-real video generator DriveCtrl preserves scene layout and motion from simulations while achieving realism and annotation fidelity, using a three-signal conditioning pipeline and a Driving Video Realism Score (DVRS).
AI.motivation: The demand for large-scale, annotated driving video data for autonomous systems is hampered by the sim-to-real gap. Existing generative methods struggle to jointly maintain structure, object dynamics, temporal coherence, and visual realism, risking annotation validity.
AI.method: DriveCtrl augments a pretrained video foundation model with a structure-aware adapter for depth-conditioned generation that preserves the source simulator's layout and motion. A scalable data-generation pipeline converts simulator videos into realism-matched footage in a target real-world style. Conditioning signals include structural depth, reference-dataset style, and text prompts, with frame-level annotations preserved. Evaluation is conducted using Driving Video Realism Score (DVRS).
AI.result: DriveCtrl consistently outperforms the base model and competing methods in realism, temporal quality, and perception-task performance, substantially narrowing the sim-to-real gap for driving video generation.
AI.conclusion: DriveCtrl enables scalable, realistic, annotation-preserving driving video synthesis and introduces DVRS as a domain-specific realism metric, demonstrating practical applicability for downstream perception tasks.

title: Distance-Matrix Wasserstein Statistics for Scalable Gromov--Wasserstein Learning
summary: Gromov--Wasserstein (GW) distances compare graphs, shapes, and point clouds through internal distances, without requiring a common coordinate system. This invariance is powerful, but discrete GW is a nonconvex quadratic optimal transport problem and is difficult to estimate at scale. We propose \emph{Distance-Matrix Wasserstein} (DMW), a hierarchy of Wasserstein statistics comparing laws of random finite distance matrices. Rather than optimizing a global point-level alignment, DMW samples $n$ points from each space, records their pairwise distances, and transports the resulting matrix laws. We prove that DMW is a relaxation and lower bound of GW, and establish a reverse approximation inequality: the GW--DMW gap is controlled by the Wasserstein error of approximating each original measure with $n$ samples. Hence population DMW converges to GW as sampled subspaces become dense. We further give finite-sample bounds, including intrinsic-dimensional rates that depend on the data manifold rather than the ambient matrix dimension $\binom n2$. For scalable computation, we introduce sliced and multi-scale DMW; for $p=1$, the sliced multi-scale dissimilarity yields positive-definite exponential kernels. Experiments on synthetic metric spaces, scalability benchmarks, graph classification, and two-sample testing validate the theory and demonstrate an interpretable GW-style proxy for structural comparison.

AI.tldr: Introduces Distance-Matrix Wasserstein (DMW), a scalable proxy to Gromov-Wasserstein (GW) that matches laws of random distance matrices; provides theoretical guarantees (relaxation, lower bound, reverse inequality, convergence, finite-sample/intrinsic-dimensional rates) and scalable variants (sliced/multi-scale); validated on synthetic/graph tasks with interpretability.
AI.motivation: GW captures geometry-invariant relationships between metric spaces but is a nonconvex, computationally hard optimal transport problem at scale. A scalable, interpretable proxy with provable guarantees is needed for large graphs, shapes, and point clouds.
AI.method: Sample n points from each space; form their pairwise distance matrices; treat the pairwise distance matrices as random objects and transport their laws using Wasserstein distance (DMW). Prove DMW is a relaxation and a lower bound of GW; establish a reverse inequality linking the GW–DMW gap to the sampling error of empirical measures; show population DMW converges to GW as samples become dense; derive finite-sample bounds with intrinsic-dimensional rates; develop scalable variants (sliced and multi-scale DMW) producing positive-definite kernels for p=1; validate with experiments.
AI.result: DMW is shown to relax GW and provide a computable, scalable surrogate. The GW–DMW gap is controlled by the Wasserstein error in empirical approximation; convergence from finite-sample DMW to GW is established; finite-sample rates depend on intrinsic dimension rather than ambient matrix size; sliced/multi-scale variants yield kernels; experiments demonstrate practical effectiveness for graph classification, two-sample testing, and scalability benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DMW offers a principled, scalable GW proxy with strong theoretical guarantees and practical variants that enable efficient structural comparison across graphs, shapes, and point clouds while maintaining interpretability.

title: MemEye: A Visual-Centric Evaluation Framework for Multimodal Agent Memory
summary: Long-term agent memory is increasingly multimodal, yet existing evaluations rarely test whether agents preserve the visual evidence needed for later reasoning. In prior work, many visually grounded questions can be answered using only captions or textual traces, allowing answers to be inferred without preserving the fine-grained visual evidence. Meanwhile, harder cases that require reasoning over changing visual states are largely absent. Therefore, we introduce MemEye, a framework that evaluates memory capabilities from two dimensions: one measures the granularity of decisive visual evidence (from scene-level to pixel-level evidence), and the other measures how retrieved evidence must be used (from single evidence to evolutionary synthesis). Under this framework, we construct a new benchmark across 8 life-scenario tasks, with ablation-driven validation gates for assessing answerability, shortcut resistance, visual necessity, and reasoning structure. By evaluating 13 memory methods across 4 VLM backbones, we show that current architectures still struggle to preserve fine-grained visual details and reason about state changes over time. Our findings show that long-term multimodal memory depends on evidence routing, temporal tracking, and detail extraction.

AI.tldr: MemEye provides a two-dimensional evaluation framework for long-term multimodal memory: (1) granularity of decisive visual evidence (scene-level to pixel-level) and (2) how evidence is used (single vs evolutionary synthesis). It introduces an 8-task benchmark and validates 13 memory methods across 4 VLM backbones, revealing current models struggle to preserve fine-grained visual details and reason about state changes over time.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks allow answers to be inferred from captions or textual traces, neglecting preservation of fine-grained visual evidence and dynamic state reasoning over time. A robust evaluation is needed to assess long-term multimodal memory beyond surface text cues.
AI.method: Proposes MemEye framework with two evaluation dimensions; constructs a new benchmark across 8 life-scenario tasks with ablation-driven gates for answerability, shortcut resistance, visual necessity, and reasoning structure. Evaluates 13 memory methods across 4 visual-language model (VLM) backbones.
AI.result: Across 13 memory methods and 4 VLM backbones, current architectures underperform in preserving fine-grained visual details and reasoning about state changes. The study highlights the importance of evidence routing, temporal tracking, and detail extraction for effective long-term multimodal memory.
AI.conclusion: Long-term multimodal memory performance hinges on effective evidence routing, robust temporal tracking, and precise detail extraction. The MemEye framework reveals substantial gaps in current models and provides a pathway for evaluating and improving memory architectures for dynamic visual reasoning.

title: Second-Order Actor-Critic Methods for Discounted MDPs via Policy Hessian Decomposition
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures including Appendix with Detailed proofs
summary: We address the discounted reward setting in reinforcement learning (RL). To mitigate the value approximation challenges in policy gradient methods, actor-critic approaches have been developed and are known to converge to stationary points under suitable assumptions. However, these methods rely on first-order updates. In contrast, second-order optimization provides principled curvature-aware updates that are proven to accelerate convergence, but its application in RL is limited by the computational complexity of Hessian estimation. In this work, we analyze second-order approximations for the actor update that leverage the full curvature information of the objective as much as possible. A stable approximation requires treating the action-value function as locally constant with respect to policy parameters, which does not generally hold in policy gradient methods. We show that this approximation becomes well-justified under a two-timescale actor-critic framework, where the critic evolves on a faster timescale and can be treated as quasi-stationary during actor updates. Building on this insight, we formulate a second-order actor-critic method for the discounted reward setting that leverages Hessian-vector product (HVP) computations, resulting in a computationally efficient and stable second-order update.

AI.tldr: Second-order actor-critic using Hessian-vector products in a two-timescale setting for discounted RL; achieves stable, efficient curvature-aware updates by treating the critic as quasi-stationary.
AI.motivation: To overcome slow/unstable first-order policy gradient updates and computational challenges of Hessians; unlock curvature information in RL with practical complexity.
AI.method: Develop second-order actor update leveraging Hessian information and HVPs; justify local constancy of Q under two-timescale critic; algorithmic framework for discounted reward; efficient computation.
AI.result: A practical second-order actor-critic formulation with HVP-based updates; demonstrated stability and computational efficiency (as per abstract).
AI.conclusion: Two-timescale critic enables valid curvature-based updates; the approach offers a viable path to incorporate second-order optimization in RL without prohibitive cost.

title: Causal Forcing++: Scalable Few-Step Autoregressive Diffusion Distillation for Real-Time Interactive Video Generation
summary: Real-time interactive video generation requires low-latency, streaming, and controllable rollout. Existing autoregressive (AR) diffusion distillation methods have achieved strong results in the chunk-wise 4-step regime by distilling bidirectional base models into few-step AR students, but they remain limited by coarse response granularity and non-negligible sampling latency. In this paper, we study a more aggressive setting: frame-wise autoregression with only 1--2 sampling steps. In this regime, we identify the initialization of a few-step AR student as the key bottleneck: existing strategies are either target-misaligned, incapable of few-step generation, or too costly to scale. We propose \textbf{Causal Forcing++}, a principled and scalable pipeline that uses \emph{causal consistency distillation} (causal CD) for few-step AR initialization. The core idea is that causal CD learns the same AR-conditional flow map as causal ODE distillation, but obtains supervision from a single online teacher ODE step between adjacent timesteps, avoiding the need to precompute and store full PF-ODE trajectories. This makes the initialization both more efficient and easier to optimize. The resulting pipeline, \ours, surpasses the SOTA 4-step chunk-wise Causal Forcing under the \textit{\textbf{frame-wise 2-step setting}} by 0.1 in VBench Total, 0.3 in VBench Quality, and 0.335 in VisionReward, while reducing first-frame latency by 50\% and Stage 2 training cost by $\sim$$4\times$. We further extend the pipeline to action-conditioned world model generation in the spirit of Genie3. Project Page: https://github.com/thu-ml/Causal-Forcing and https://github.com/shengshu-ai/minWM .

AI.tldr: Introduces Causal Forcing++ with causal consistency distillation for frame-wise 1–2 step autoregressive diffusion in real-time video generation, achieving state-of-the-art-like performance with half first-frame latency and substantially lower training cost, and extending to action-conditioned world modeling.
AI.motivation: Real-time interactive video generation requires low latency, streaming, and controllable rollout. Frame-wise 1–2 step autoregression is a highly aggressive setting, but initialization of few-step AR models is a bottleneck. Existing strategies are misaligned, unable to few-step generate, or costly to scale.
AI.method: Propose Causal Forcing++, a scalable pipeline that uses causal consistency distillation (causal CD) for few-step AR initialization. Causal CD learns the same AR-conditional flow map as causal ODE distillation but uses supervision from a single online teacher ODE step between adjacent timesteps, avoiding precomputation/storage of full PF-ODE trajectories. This yields an initialization that is efficient and easier to optimize. The pipeline targets frame-wise 2-step generation and is extended to action-conditioned world model generation (minWM/Genie3).
AI.result: In the frame-wise 2-step setting, the proposed method surpasses the 4-step chunk-wise Causal Forcing on key metrics: +0.1 in VBench Total, +0.3 in VBench Quality, and +0.335 in VisionReward. It also halves the first-frame latency and reduces Stage 2 training cost by ~4×.
AI.conclusion: Causal consistency distillation provides an effective, scalable solution for initializing few-step autoregressive diffusion in real-time video generation, enabling lower latency and training efficiency while preserving or improving quality; the approach generalizes to action-conditioned world modeling.

title: DeepTokenEEG Enhancing Mild Cognitive Impairment and Alzheimers Classification via Tokenized EEG Features
summary: The detection of Alzheimers disease (AD) is considered crucial, as timely intervention can improve patient outcomes. Electroencephalogram (EEG)-based diagnosis has been recognized as a non-invasive, accessible, and cost-effective approach for AD detection; however, it faces challenges related to data availability, accuracy of modern deep learning methods, and the time-consuming nature of expert-based interpretation. In this study, a novel lightweight and high-performance model, DeepTokenEEG, was designed for the diagnosis of AD and the classification of EEG signals from AD patients, individuals with other neurological conditions, and healthy subjects. Unlike traditional heavy-weight models, DeepTokenEEG ultilizes spatial and temporal tokenizer that effectively captures AD-related biomarkers in both temporal and frequency domain with only 0.29 million paramaters. Trained in a combined dataset of 274 subjects, including 180 AD cases, and 94 healthy controls, the proposed method achieves a maximum recorded accuracy of 100% on specific frequency bands, representing an improvement of 1.41-15.35% over state-of-the-art methods on the same dataset. These results indicate the potential of DeepTokenEEG for early detection and screening of AD, with promising applicability for deployment due to its compact size.

AI.tldr: A lightweight transformer-like EEG classifier DeepTokenEEG uses spatial-temporal tokenizers to detect Alzheimer's; 0.29M params; trained on 274 subjects; up to 100% accuracy on certain bands with 1.41-15.35% gain over SOTA.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity, computational cost, and need for accurate, easy-to-deploy AD EEG diagnostic tools by designing a compact, effective model that exploits temporal and frequency biomarkers.
AI.method: Develop a lightweight model with spatial and temporal tokenizers to capture AD biomarkers in temporal and frequency domains; train on a combined dataset (AD, other neurological conditions, healthy controls); evaluate across frequency bands; compare to state-of-the-art.
AI.result: Maximum accuracy of 100% on specific frequency bands; 1.41-15.35% improvement over SOTA on the same dataset; dataset: 274 subjects (180 AD, 94 healthy).
AI.conclusion: DeepTokenEEG demonstrates potential for early AD detection with a compact architecture suitable for deployment; suggests practical clinical utility due to non-invasive EEG.

title: Does Synthetic Layered Design Data Benefit Layered Design Decomposition?
comment: 22 pages, 10 figures. Code is available at https://github.com/YangHaolin0526/SynLayers
summary: Recent advances in image generation have made it easy to produce high-quality images. However, these outputs are inherently flattened, entangling foreground elements, background, and text within a fixed canvas. As a result, flexible post-generation editing remains challenging, revealing a clear last-mile gap toward practical usability. Existing approaches either rely on scarce proprietary layered assets or construct partially synthetic data from limited structural priors. However, both strategies face fundamental challenges in scalability. In this work, we investigate whether pure synthetic layered data can improve graphic design decomposition. We make the assumption that, in graphic design, effective decomposition does not require modeling inter-layer dependencies as precisely as in natural-image composition, since design elements are often intentionally arranged as modular and semantically separable components. Concretely, we conduct a data-centric study based on CLD baseline, which is a state-of-the-art layer decomposition framework. Based on the baseline, we construct our own synthetic dataset, SynLayers, generate textual supervision using vision language models, and automate inference inputs with VLM-predicted bounding boxes. Our study reveals three key findings: (1) even training with purely synthetic data can outperform non-scalable alternatives such as the widely used PrismLayersPro dataset, demonstrating its viability as a scalable and effective substitute; (2) performance consistently improves with increased training data scale, while gains begin to saturate at around 50K samples; and (3) synthetic data enables balanced control over layer-count distributions, avoiding the layer-count imbalance commonly observed in real-world datasets. We hope this data-centric study encourages broader adoption of synthetic data as a practical foundation for layered design editing systems.

AI.tldr: Synthetic layered data (SynLayers) can train a CLD-based layer decomposition model to outperform non-scalable real-data baselines; performance scales with data up to ~50k samples; synthetic data enables balanced layer-count distributions, supporting broader adoption for layered design editing.
AI.motivation: Image generation yields flattened outputs; flexible post-editing is hard due to lack of scalable layered data. The authors hypothesize that for graphic design, inter-layer dependencies are less strict, enabling effective learning from purely synthetic, modular design elements.
AI.method: 1) Use a CLD baseline for layer decomposition. 2) Construct SynLayers, a purely synthetic dataset of layered designs. 3) Generate textual supervision via vision-language models. 4) Automate inference inputs using VLM-predicted bounding boxes.
AI.result: Training with purely synthetic data can outperform non-scalable datasets (e.g., PrismLayersPro). Performance improves with larger training data, saturating around 50K samples. Synthetic data also provides balanced control over layer-count distributions, addressing common real-world dataset imbalances.
AI.conclusion: A data-centric, synthetic-data approach is viable and scalable for layered design editing systems, and the study advocates broader adoption of synthetic data for such tasks.

title: Boosting Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards via Randomly Selected Few-Shot Guidance
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has achieved great success in developing Large Language Models (LLMs) with chain-of-thought rollouts for many tasks such as math and coding. Nevertheless, RLVR struggles with sample efficiency on difficult problems where correct rollouts are hard to generate. Prior works propose to address this issue via demonstration-guided RLVR, i.e., to conduct Supervised FineTuning (SFT) when RL fails; however, SFT often requires a lot of data, which can be expensive to acquire. In this paper, we propose FEST, a FEw-ShoT demonstration-guided RLVR algorithm. It attains compelling results with only 128 demonstrations randomly selected from an SFT dataset. We find that three components are vital for the success: supervised signal, on-policy signal, and decaying weights on the few-shot SFT dataset to prevent overfitting from multiple-epoch training. On several benchmarks, FEST outperforms baselines with magnitudes less SFT data, even matching their performance with full dataset.

AI.tldr: FEST enables data-efficient RL-based verification-guided RL for LLMs by using very few-shot demonstrations (128) with a triad of supervision, on-policy signals, and decayed dataset weights, achieving competitive results with much less SFT data.
AI.motivation: RLVR struggles with sample inefficiency on hard tasks; demonstrations for guidance help but require large SFT data; aim to drastically reduce data needs while preserving performance.
AI.method: Train with a few-shot demonstration set (128 demos) selected randomly from an SFT dataset, leveraging three components: (1) supervised signal from the SFT data, (2) on-policy RL signal, (3) decaying weights on the SFT data to mitigate overfitting during multi-epoch training.
AI.result: On multiple benchmarks, FEST outperforms baselines with far less SFT data and can match the performance of models trained with the full SFT dataset.
AI.conclusion: Data-efficient RLVR is attainable by combining supervised and on-policy signals with a decaying-weight strategy on the limited demonstrations; this approach reduces labeling costs while maintaining competitive performance.

title: Evidential Reasoning Advances Interpretable Real-World Disease Screening
summary: Disease screening is critical for early detection and timely intervention in clinical practice. However, most current screening models for medical images suffer from limited interpretability and suboptimal performance. They often lack effective mechanisms to reference historical cases or provide transparent reasoning pathways. To address these challenges, we introduce EviScreen, an evidential reasoning framework for disease screening that leverages region-level evidence from historical cases. The proposed EviScreen offers retrospection interpretability through regional evidence retrieved from dual knowledge banks. Using this evidential mechanism, the subsequent evidence-aware reasoning module makes predictions using both the current case and evidence from historical cases, thereby enhancing disease screening performance. Furthermore, rather than relying on post-hoc saliency maps, EviScreen enhances localization interpretability by leveraging abnormality maps derived from contrastive retrieval. Our method achieves superior performance on our carefully established benchmarks for real-world disease screening, yielding notably higher specificity at clinical-level recall. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/DopamineLcy/EviScreen.

AI.tldr: EviScreen uses evidential regional evidence from historical cases to enable evidence-aware reasoning for disease screening, improving specificity at clinical recall and providing region-based localization via abnormality maps from contrastive retrieval, with dual knowledge banks.
AI.motivation: Current medical image screening models often lack interpretable reasoning and fail to reference historical cases, relying on opaque predictions and post-hoc saliency maps.
AI.method: EviScreen retrieves region-level evidence from dual knowledge banks to support an evidence-aware reasoning module that combines the current case with historical evidence; localization uses abnormality maps derived from contrastive retrieval rather than post-hoc saliency.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines on established benchmarks for real-world disease screening, achieving notably higher specificity at clinical-level recall; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates retrospection-based interpretability and improved screening performance through evidential reasoning over historical cases, with explicit regional evidence and contrastive-based abnormality localization.

title: Generalized Priority-Aware Shapley Value
summary: Shapley value and its priority-aware extensions are widely used for valuation in machine learning, but existing methods require pairwise priority to be binary and acyclic, a restriction spectacularly violated in real-data examples such as aggregated human preferences and multi-criterion comparisons. We introduce the generalized priority-aware Shapley value (GPASV), a random order value defined on arbitrary directed weighted priority graphs, in which pairwise edges penalize rather than forbid order violations. GPASV covers a range of classical models as boundary cases. We establish GPASV through an axiomatic characterization, develop the associated computational methods, and introduce a priority sweeping diagnostic extending PASV's. We apply GPASV to LLM ensemble valuation on the cyclic Chatbot Arena preference graph, illustrating that priority-aware valuation is not a one-button operation: different balances of pairwise graph priority versus individual soft priority produce substantively different valuations of the same data.

AI.tldr: GPASV generalizes Shapley-based valuation to arbitrary directed weighted priority graphs with penalties for order violations; axiomatic foundation, computation, and a diagnostic; applied to LLM ensemble valuation in a cyclic preference graph, showing valuations depend on the balance between graph priority and individual soft priority.
AI.motivation: Existing priority-aware Shapley methods require binary, acyclic pairwise priority, which fails in real-world data with cycles and soft priorities. A generalized, flexible framework is needed to capture complex priority structures and produce interpretable valuations.
AI.method: Define GPASV as a random-order value on arbitrary directed weighted priority graphs where edges penalize (not forbid) order violations. Provide axiomatic characterization, derive representation, develop computational methods, and introduce a priority sweeping diagnostic; boundary cases recover classical models such as PASV and Shapley-type values.
AI.result: GPASV extends to boundary cases; computational methods enable use; diagnostic helps understand priority influence; empirical validation on LLM ensemble valuation in the cyclic Chatbot Arena shows that varying the balance between graph-based priority and individual soft priority yields substantively different valuations of the same data.
AI.conclusion: GPASV offers a principled, flexible valuation framework for complex priority structures, enabling richer modeling of preferences and ensembles; encourages careful calibration of graph priors and suggests directions for further efficiency and empirical validation.

title: SANA-WM: Efficient Minute-Scale World Modeling with Hybrid Linear Diffusion Transformer
summary: We introduce SANA-WM, an efficient 2.6B-parameter open-source world model natively trained for one-minute generation, synthesizing high-fidelity, 720p, minute-scale videos with precise camera control. SANA-WM achieves visual quality comparable to large-scale industrial baselines such as LingBot-World and HY-WorldPlay, while significantly improving efficiency. Four core designs drive our architecture: (1) Hybrid Linear Attention combines frame-wise Gated DeltaNet (GDN) with softmax attention for memory-efficient long-context modeling. (2) Dual-Branch Camera Control ensures precise 6-DoF trajectory adherence. (3) Two-Stage Generation Pipeline applies a long-video refiner to stage-1 outputs, improving quality and consistency across sequences. (4) Robust Annotation Pipeline extracts accurate metric-scale 6-DoF camera poses from public videos to yield high-quality, spatiotemporally consistent action labels. Driven by these designs, SANA-WMdemonstrates remarkable efficiency across data, training compute, and inference hardware: it uses only $\sim$213K public video clips with metric-scale pose supervision, completes training in 15 days on 64 H100s, and generates each 60s clip on a single GPU; its distilled variant can be deployed on a single RTX 5090 with NVFP4 quantization to denoise a 60s 720p clip in 34s. On our one-minute world-model benchmark, SANA-WM demonstrates stronger action-following accuracy than prior open-source baselines and achieves comparable visual quality at $36\times$ higher throughput for scalable world modeling.

AI.tldr: A 2.6B-parameter open-source world model, SANA-WM, capable of one-minute (60s) 720p video generation with precise 6-DoF camera control. Introduces Hybrid Linear Attention (frame-wise GDN + softmax), Dual-Branch Camera Control, Two-Stage Generation with a long-video refiner, and Robust Annotation Pipeline for metric-scale camera poses. Achieves 36x higher throughput than prior open-source baselines, trains on ~213k public clips in 15 days on 64 H100s, and can generate each clip on a single GPU; distilled variant enables edge deployment with NVFP4 quantization. Demonstrates strong action-following vs baselines and comparable visual quality at high efficiency.
AI.motivation: To build an efficient, scalable, open-source world model for long-context video generation with accurate camera control, reducing data and compute while maintaining high fidelity, enabling practical deployment and benchmarking against industrial baselines.
AI.method: Core designs: (1) Hybrid Linear Attention combining frame-wise Gated DeltaNet with softmax attention for memory-efficient long-context modeling; (2) Dual-Branch Camera Control for precise 6-DoF trajectory adherence; (3) Two-Stage Generation Pipeline with a long-video refiner to improve quality and temporal consistency; (4) Robust Annotation Pipeline to extract metric-scale 6-DoF camera poses from public videos for high-quality spatiotemporal labels. Training uses ~213k public clips with metric-scale supervision, 15 days on 64 H100s; inference on one GPU per 60s clip; a distilled variant supports edge devices (RTX 5090) with NVFP4 quantization to denoise a 60s 720p clip in 34s.
AI.result: Efficiency and performance gains: lower data and compute requirements, faster training, and high-throughput inference. Clocked results include training on ~213k clips in 15 days on 64 H100s; generation of each 60s clip on a single GPU; 34s denoised 60s 720p clip on RTX 5090 with NVFP4. On a one-minute world-model benchmark, SANA-WM achieves stronger action-following accuracy than prior open-source baselines and comparable visual quality at 36× higher throughput.
AI.conclusion: SANA-WM demonstrates that a compact 2.6B world model can deliver high-quality minute-scale videos with precise 6-DoF camera control efficiently, outperforming open-source baselines in action-following while maintaining competitive visual fidelity and enabling scalable deployment across hardware via quantization and distillation.

title: TopoPrimer: The Missing Topological Context in Forecasting Models
summary:
We introduce TopoPrimer, a framework that makes the global topological structure of the series population an explicit input
  to any forecasting model. TopoPrimer improves accuracy across diverse domains, stabilizes forecasts under seasonal demand
  spikes, and closes the cold-start gap. Precomputed once per domain via persistent homology and spectral sheaf coordinates,
  TopoPrimer deploys per token for fully-trained models and as a lightweight adapter for pre-trained backbones. Of these two
  components, sheaf coordinates are the primary accuracy driver. Across four public benchmarks on Chronos and TimesFM,
  TopoPrimer consistently improves forecasting accuracy, with gains of up to 7.3% MSE on ECL. The topology advantage persists
  with near-identical magnitude across zero-shot and fine-tuned backbones, suggesting topology and per-series training
  capture complementary signals. The gains are most pronounced in difficult regimes. Under peak seasonal demand, classical
  and zero-shot models degrade by up to 50%, while TopoPrimer stays within 10%. At cold start with no item history,
  TopoPrimer reduces MAE by 27% over a topology-free baseline.

AI.tldr: TopoPrimer injects global topological structure into forecasting models via persistent homology and spectral sheaf coordinates, yielding robust accuracy gains, especially during seasonality spikes and cold-start, and works as per-token inputs or lightweight adapters.
AI.motivation: Forecasting performance deteriorates under seasonal spikes and cold-start; incorporating the global topology of the time-series population can provide additional signal.
AI.method: Precompute per domain (persistent homology and spectral sheaf coordinates). Deploy as per-token input for fully-trained models or as lightweight adapter for pre-trained backbones. The sheaf coordinates are the primary driver; may combine with per-series training.
AI.result: On Chronos and TimesFM benchmarks; up to 7.3% MSE improvement on ECL; topology gains nearly identical magnitude for zero-shot and fine-tuned backbones; strongest in hard regimes; under peak seasonal demand, competitors degrade by up to 50% while TopoPrimer stays within 10%; at cold-start with no history MAE reduced by 27% vs topology-free baseline.
AI.conclusion: Topological features via persistent homology and spectral sheaf coordinates provide robust, domain-agnostic gains; the approach is interpretable as per-token addition; topology and per-series training provide complementary signals; the method is practical (precompute once per domain).

title: From Plans to Pixels: Learning to Plan and Orchestrate for Open-Ended Image Editing
summary: Modern image editing models produce realistic results but struggle with abstract, multi step instructions (e.g., ``make this advertisement more vegetarian-friendly''). Prior agent based methods decompose such tasks but rely on handcrafted pipelines or teacher imitation, limiting flexibility and decoupling learning from actual editing outcomes. We propose an experiential framework for long-horizon image editing, where a planner generates structured atomic decompositions and an orchestrator selects tools and regions to execute each step. A vision language judge provides outcome-based rewards for instruction adherence and visual quality. The orchestrator is trained to maximize these rewards, and successful trajectories are used to refine the planner. By tightly coupling planning with reward driven execution, our approach yields more coherent and reliable edits than single-step or rule-based multistep baselines.

AI.tldr: An experiential RL framework for long-horizon image editing that couples planning and reward-driven execution via a planner, an orchestrator, and a vision-language judge to achieve coherent multi-step edits.
AI.motivation: To enable abstract, multi-step image editing tasks that current methods struggle with due to reliance on handcrafted pipelines or imitation learning and a disconnection between planning and actual editing outcomes.
AI.method: A planner generates structured atomic decompositions of tasks; an orchestrator selects tools and regions to execute each step; a vision-language judge provides outcome-based rewards assessing instruction adherence and visual quality; the orchestrator learns to maximize these rewards; successful trajectories refine the planner.
AI.result: The approach yields more coherent and reliable edits than single-step or rule-based multistep baselines, demonstrating improved long-horizon editing performance.
AI.conclusion: Tightly coupling planning with reward-driven execution and iterative planner refinement improves long-horizon image editing and aligns planning with actual editing outcomes.

title: Separating Intrinsic Ambiguity from Estimation Uncertainty in Deep Generative Models for Linear Inverse Problems
summary: Recently, deep generative models have been used for posterior inference in inverse problems, including high-stakes applications in medical imaging and scientific discovery, where the uncertainty of a prediction can matter as much as the prediction itself. However, posterior uncertainty is difficult to interpret because it can mix ambiguity inherent to the forward operator with uncertainty propagated through inference. We introduce a structural decomposition of posterior uncertainty that isolates intrinsic ambiguity. A cascade formulation makes this ambiguity accessible for calibration analysis, enabling qualitative diagnostics and simulation-based calibration tests that reveal failure modes that remain hidden when models are selected by reconstruction quality alone. We first validate the approach on a Gaussian example with analytical posterior structure, then illustrate the decomposition on accelerated magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and finally apply the calibration diagnostics to electroencephalography (EEG) source imaging.

AI.tldr: Introduces a cascade-based decomposition of posterior uncertainty to separate intrinsic ambiguity from forward-model induced uncertainty; provides calibration diagnostics and simulation-based calibration tests; validated on Gaussian models and demonstrated on MRI and EEG.
AI.motivation: Posterior uncertainty in inverse problems blends forward-model ambiguity with inference uncertainty, hindering interpretable uncertainty; a decomposition is needed to isolate intrinsic ambiguity and enable calibration.
AI.method: Structural decomposition of posterior uncertainty with a cascade formulation; qualitative diagnostics; simulation-based calibration tests; validation on a Gaussian analytic posterior; applications to accelerated MRI and EEG source imaging.
AI.result: Isolates intrinsic ambiguity; calibration diagnostics reveal failure modes unseen when relying on reconstruction quality alone; demonstrated with a Gaussian example and medical imaging datasets (MRI, EEG).
AI.conclusion: The framework enhances interpretability and reliability of posterior uncertainty in high-stakes inverse problems, enabling better model assessment and potential improvements in imaging workflows.

title: Warp-as-History: Generalizable Camera-Controlled Video Generation from One Training Video
summary: Camera-controlled video generation has made substantial progress, enabling generated videos to follow prescribed viewpoint trajectories. However, existing methods usually learn camera-specific conditioning through camera encoders, control branches, or attention and positional-encoding modifications, which often require post-training on large-scale camera-annotated videos. Training-free alternatives avoid such post-training, but often shift the cost to test-time optimization or extra denoising-time guidance. We propose Warp-as-History, a simple interface that turns camera-induced warps into camera-warped pseudo-history with target-frame positional alignment and visible-token selection. Given a target camera trajectory, we construct camera-warped pseudo-history from past observations and feed it through the model's visual-history pathway. Crucially, we align its positional encoding with the target frames being denoised and remove warped-history tokens without valid source observations. Without any training, architectural modification, or test-time optimization, this interface reveals a non-trivial zero-shot capability of a frozen video generation model to follow camera trajectories. Moreover, lightweight offline LoRA finetuning on only one camera-annotated video further improves this capability and generalizes to unseen videos, improving camera adherence, visual quality, and motion dynamics without test-time optimization or target-video adaptation. Extensive experiments on diverse datasets confirm the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: Warp-as-History enables training-free camera-trajectory following in video generation by turning camera-induced warps into a camera-warped pseudo-history that aligns with target frames, enabling zero-shot capabilities in a frozen model; lightweight LoRA fine-tuning on a single camera-annotated video further improves performance without test-time optimization.
AI.motivation: Eliminate dependence on camera-specific conditioning and post-training on large camera-annotated videos; achieve zero-shot camera-following with frozen models and minimal offline fine-tuning by leveraging a pseudo-history constructed from past observations.
AI.method: Construct camera-warped pseudo-history from past observations for a given target camera trajectory, feed it into the model's visual-history pathway, align positional encoding with target frames, and remove tokens without valid observations; no training or test-time optimization required; optionally perform lightweight offline LoRA finetuning on a single camera-annotated video to improve generalization.
AI.result: Demonstrates non-trivial zero-shot capability of following camera trajectories with a frozen video-generating model; improves camera adherence, visual quality, and motion dynamics; generalizes to unseen videos; effective on diverse datasets.
AI.conclusion: Warp-as-History provides a simple, training-free interface to enable camera-trajectory following in video generation, with additional gains from lightweight offline LoRA finetuning on a single annotated video, without test-time optimization.

title: An Interpretable Latency Model for Speculative Decoding in LLM Serving
summary: Speculative decoding (SD) accelerates large language model (LLM) inference by using a smaller draft model to propose multiple tokens that are verified by a larger target model in parallel. While prior work demonstrates substantial speedups in isolated or fixed-batch settings, the behavior of SD in production serving systems remains poorly understood: request load varies over time, and effective batch size emerges from the serving system rather than being directly controlled or observed. In this work, we develop a simple and interpretable latency model for SD in LLM serving. We infer effective batch size from request rate using Little's Law and decompose per-request demand into load-independent and load-dependent components for prefill, drafting, and verification. We validate our model using extensive measurements from vLLM across verifier and drafter model sizes, prefill and decode lengths, request rates, draft lengths, and acceptance probabilities. The model accurately describes observed latency, explains why speedups often diminish as server load increases, and characterizes how draft length, acceptance rate, and verifier-drafter size shape latency across serving conditions, with implications for configuring SD in deployed systems. We further show how the framework extends to mixture of experts models, where sparse expert activation changes the effective service costs across load regimes. Together, our results provide a structured framework for understanding SD in real LLM serving systems.

AI.tldr: A simple, interpretable latency model for speculative decoding (SD) in LLM serving is developed and validated. By using Little's Law to infer effective batch size and decomposing per-request demand into prefill, drafting, and verification, the work explains latency behavior under varying load, clarifies when speedups hold, and extends to mixture-of-experts settings for deployment guidance.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between SD's speedups in isolated/fixed-batch experiments and real production serving where load varies and batch size emerges from the system, complicating performance projections and configurations.
AI.method: Propose a latency model that partitions per-request demand into load-independent and load-dependent components for prefill, drafting, and verification. Infer effective batch size from request rate using Little's Law. Validate the model with extensive measurements on vLLM across various drafter/verifier sizes, prefill and decode lengths, request rates, draft lengths, and acceptance probabilities. Extend the framework to mixture-of-experts models where sparse activation alters service costs across load regimes.
