AI.result: The model accurately captures observed latency, explains why speedups diminish with increased server load, and characterizes how draft length, acceptance rate, and verifier-drafter sizes influence latency across conditions. Provides practical guidance for configuring SD in deployed systems and demonstrates applicability to mixture-of-experts architectures.
AI.conclusion: The study offers a structured and interpretable framework for understanding SD in real LLM serving systems, enabling better deployment decisions under time-varying load and supporting extensions to more complex architectures like mixtures of experts.

title: Quantitative Video World Model Evaluation for Geometric-Consistency
summary: Generative video models are increasingly studied as implicit world models, yet evaluating whether they produce physically plausible 3D structure and motion remains challenging. Most existing video evaluation pipelines rely heavily on human judgment or learned graders, which can be subjective and weakly diagnostic for geometric failures. We introduce PDI-Bench (Perspective Distortion Index), a quantitative framework for auditing geometric coherence in generated videos. Given a generated clip, we obtain object-centric observations via segmentation and point tracking (e.g., SAM 2, MegaSaM, and CoTracker3), lift them to 3D world-space coordinates via monocular reconstruction, and compute a set of projective-geometry residuals capturing three failure dimensions: scale-depth alignment, 3D motion consistency, and 3D structural rigidity. To support systematic evaluation, we build PDI-Dataset, covering diverse scenarios designed to stress these geometric constraints. Across state-of-the-art video generators, PDI reveals consistent geometry-specific failure modes that are not captured by common perceptual metrics, and provides a diagnostic signal for progress toward physically grounded video generation and physical world model. Our code and dataset can be found at https://pdi-bench.github.io/.

AI.tldr: PDI-Bench provides a quantitative, geometry-focused evaluation framework for generative video models. It audits 3D coherence by turning videos into object-centric observations, lifting them to 3D via monocular reconstruction, and computing projective-geometry residuals across scale-depth, 3D motion, and structural rigidity; paired with a stress-test PDI-Dataset. The approach reveals geometry-specific failures not captured by perceptual metrics, enabling diagnostic progress toward physically grounded video generation.
AI.motivation: Existing video evaluation often relies on perceptual judgments or learned graders, which can be subjective and weakly diagnostic for geometric failures. There is a need for objective, geometry-centric benchmarks to assess whether generative video models produce physically plausible 3D structure and motion.
AI.method: 1) Obtain object-centric observations from segmentation and tracking (e.g., SAM 2, MegaSaM, CoTracker3). 2) Lift observations to 3D world-space coordinates via monocular reconstruction. 3) Compute projective-geometry residuals capturing (a) scale-depth alignment, (b) 3D motion consistency, and (c) 3D structural rigidity. 4) Create PDI-Dataset with diverse, geometry-stressful scenarios. 5) Evaluate state-of-the-art video generators to diagnose geometry-specific failures and provide diagnostic signals.
AI.result: The framework uncovers consistent geometry-specific failure modes across modern video generators that perceptual metrics miss, offering a diagnostic signal for progress toward physically grounded video generation. The accompanying dataset supports systematic benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: PDI-Bench enables objective, geometry-centered evaluation of generative videos and highlights specific geometric deficiencies to drive development of physically plausible video models. Code and dataset are publicly available.

title: TFGN: Task-Free, Replay-Free Continual Pre-Training Without Catastrophic Forgetting at LLM Scale
summary:
Continually pre-training a large language model on heterogeneous text domains, without replay or task labels, has remained an unsolved architectural problem at LLM scale. Existing methods rely on replay buffers, task identifiers, regularization penalties that scale poorly, or sentence-classification-scale evaluation.
  We introduce TFGN, an architectural overlay for transformer language models that produces input-conditioned, parameter-efficient updates while leaving the rest of the transformer unchanged. On six heterogeneous text domains (Prose, Python, Math, Biomedical, Chinese, JavaScript) at 1B tokens per phase across three model scales (~398M, ~739M, ~9B) and two regimes (From-Scratch and Retrofit), TFGN achieves backward transfer of -0.007 at LLaMA 3.1 8B Retrofit, HellaSwag retention 0.506/0.504/0.510, and >=99.59% L2-orthogonal gradient separation between domain pairs - with no replay, no task IDs, no Fisher penalty.
  The same matrices show positive cross-domain forward transfer: held-out JavaScript PPL drops 26.8% at LLaMA-8B Retrofit and 62.0% at GPT-2 Medium From-Scratch purely from Python training. Two extensions on the same substrate close further open problems. A closed-loop meta-control layer (Extension A) reduces forgetting by an additional 81% at ~398M, mapping onto the System A and System M roles of Dupoux et al. (arXiv:2603.15381). An operator-level plan vector (Extension B) reshapes forward-pass behavior at 99.96% cosine fidelity over 30 source->target pairs.
  The architectural insight is a Read/Write decomposition: the forward pass is fully dense, while cross-domain parameter updates are structured so prior-domain subspaces are not written to. To our knowledge, TFGN is the first architecture that simultaneously closes catastrophic forgetting at LLM scale, realizes a closed-loop autonomous-learning meta-controller, and carries an operator-level latent planner.

AI.tldr: TFGN: an architectural overlay for LLMs enabling input-conditioned, parameter-efficient updates to prevent forgetting across heterogeneous domains without replay or task labels; achieves both backward/forward transfer, with extensions for meta-control and operator-level planning.
AI.motivation: Address continual pre-training of LLMs across diverse text domains at scale without replay buffers, task identifiers, or costly regularization, to mitigate catastrophic forgetting and enable cross-domain transfer.
AI.method: Read/Write decomposition: forward pass remains dense; updates are structured to avoid writing to prior-domain subspaces. Architectural overlay (TFGN) applied to transformers; tested on six domains (Prose, Python, Math, Biomedical, Chinese, JavaScript) at 1B tokens per phase, across scales ~398M, ~739M, ~9B, in From-Scratch and Retrofit regimes. Two extensions: Extension A (closed-loop meta-control) and Extension B (operator-level plan vector). No replay, no task IDs, no Fisher penalties.
AI.result: Backward transfer of -0.007 on LLaMA 3.1 8B Retrofit; HellaSwag retention ~0.506/0.504/0.510; over 99.59% L2-orthogonal gradient separation between domain pairs; forward transfer: JavaScript PPL drops 26.8% at LLaMA-8B Retrofit and 62.0% at GPT-2 Medium From-Scratch. Extension A reduces forgetting further by ~81% at ~398M; Extension B yields 99.96% cosine fidelity of cross-domain policy across 30 source→target pairs.
AI.conclusion: TFGN is the first architecture to simultaneously close catastrophic forgetting at LLM scale, enable a closed-loop autonomous-learning meta-controller, and provide an operator-level latent planner, via a Read/Write decomposition that isolates prior-domain subspaces from updates.

title: VGGT-Edit: Feed-forward Native 3D Scene Editing with Residual Field Prediction
summary: High-quality 3D scene reconstruction has recently advanced toward generalizable feed-forward architectures, enabling the generation of complex environments in a single forward pass. However, despite their strong performance in static scene perception, these models remain limited in responding to dynamic human instructions, which restricts their use in interactive applications. Existing editing methods typically rely on a 2D-lifting strategy, where individual views are edited independently and then lifted back into 3D space. This indirect pipeline often leads to blurry textures and inconsistent geometry, as 2D editors lack the spatial awareness required to preserve structure across viewpoints. To address these limitations, we propose VGGT-Edit, a feed-forward framework for text-conditioned native 3D scene editing. VGGT-Edit introduces depth-synchronized text injection to align semantic guidance with the backbone's spatial poses, ensuring stable instruction grounding. This semantic signal is then processed by a residual transformation head, which directly predicts 3D geometric displacements to deform the scene while preserving background stability. To ensure high-fidelity results, we supervise the framework with a multi-term objective function that enforces geometric accuracy and cross-view consistency. We also construct the DeltaScene Dataset, a large-scale dataset generated through an automated pipeline with 3D agreement filtering to ensure ground-truth quality. Experiments show that VGGT-Edit substantially outperforms 2D-lifting baselines, producing sharper object details, stronger multi-view consistency, and near-instant inference speed.

AI.tldr: VGGT-Edit provides a text-conditioned, native 3D scene editing framework with depth-synchronized guidance and a residual transformation head, trained with a multi-term objective, plus a large DeltaScene dataset; it outperforms 2D-lifting baselines with sharper details and better multi-view consistency, while enabling near-instant inference.
AI.motivation: Current 3D editing methods are strong for static scenes but struggle with dynamic instructions. 2D-lifting edits cause texture blur and geometric inconsistency across views. A native, geometry-aware, fast editing approach is needed for interactive applications.
AI.method: Introduce depth-synchronized text injection to align semantic guidance with the backbone's spatial poses; a residual transformation head predicts 3D geometric displacements to deform the scene; supervised by a multi-term objective enforcing geometric accuracy and cross-view consistency; DeltaScene Dataset created via automated pipeline with 3D agreement filtering to ensure ground-truth quality.
AI.result: VGGT-Edit substantially outperforms 2D-lifting baselines, yielding sharper object details, stronger multi-view consistency, and near-instant inference speed.
AI.conclusion: Proposes an effective end-to-end 3D editing framework and companion dataset, enabling high-quality, fast, text-guided edits directly in 3D with stable grounding across views.

title: DiffusionOPD: A Unified Perspective of On-Policy Distillation in Diffusion Models
summary: Reinforcement learning has emerged as a powerful tool for improving diffusion-based text-to-image models, but existing methods are largely limited to single-task optimization. Extending RL to multiple tasks is challenging: joint optimization suffers from cross-task interference and imbalance, while cascade RL is cumbersome and prone to catastrophic forgetting. We propose DiffusionOPD, a new multi-task training paradigm for diffusion models based on Online Policy Distillation (OPD). DiffusionOPD first trains task-specific teachers independently, then distills their capabilities into a unified student along the student own rollout trajectories. This decouples single-task exploration from multi-task integration and avoids the optimization burden of solving all tasks jointly from scratch. Theoretically, we lift the OPD framework from discrete tokens to continuous-state Markov processes, deriving a closed-form per-step KL objective that unifies both stochastic SDE and deterministic ODE refinement via mean-matching. We formally and empirically demonstrate that this analytic gradient provides lower variance and better generality compared to conventional PPO-style policy gradients. Extensive experiments show that DiffusionOPD consistently surpasses both multi-reward RL and cascade RL baselines in training efficiency and final performance, while achieving state-of-the-art results on all evaluated benchmarks.

AI.tldr: DiffusionOPD introduces Online Policy Distillation for multi-task diffusion models, training independent task teachers then distilling into a single student along the student’s rollout trajectories; it derives an analytic per-step KL gradient for continuous-state processes, reducing variance and unifying stochastic SDE and deterministic ODE refinement; it outperforms multi-task RL and cascade RL baselines and achieves state-of-the-art results on evaluated benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Extend RL to multi-task learning for diffusion-based text-to-image models, addressing cross-task interference, imbalance, and forgetting that arise with joint optimization or cascade RL.
AI.method: Train task-specific teachers independently, then distill their capabilities into a unified student using the student’s own rollout trajectories. The approach lifts Online Policy Distillation to continuous-state Markov processes, deriving a closed-form per-step KL objective that unifies stochastic SDE and deterministic ODE refinement via mean-matching.
AI.result: Analytic gradient proposes lower variance and better generality than PPO-style policy gradients. Empirically, DiffusionOPD surpasses multi-reward RL and cascade RL baselines in training efficiency and final performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on all evaluated benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DiffusionOPD provides a scalable, efficient multi-task RL framework for diffusion models by decoupling single-task exploration from multi-task integration through teacher-student distillation, with strong empirical evidence of improved efficiency and performance.

title: RAVEN: Real-time Autoregressive Video Extrapolation with Consistency-model GRPO
summary: Causal autoregressive video diffusion models support real-time streaming generation by extrapolating future chunks from previously generated content. Distilling such generators from high-fidelity bidirectional teachers yields competitive few-step models, yet a persistent gap between the history distributions encountered during training and those arising at inference constrains generation quality over long horizons. We introduce the Real-time Autoregressive Video Extrapolation Network (RAVEN), a training-time test framework that repacks each self rollout into an interleaved sequence of clean historical endpoints and noisy denoising states. This formulation aligns training attention with inference-time extrapolation and allows downstream chunk losses to supervise the history representations on which future predictions depend. We further propose Consistency-model Group Relative Policy Optimization (CM-GRPO), which reformulates a consistency sampling step as a conditional Gaussian transition and applies online Reinforcement Learning (RL) directly to this kernel, avoiding the Euler-Maruyama auxiliary process adopted in prior flow-model RL formulations. Experiments demonstrate that RAVEN surpasses recent causal video distillation baselines across quality, semantic, and dynamic degree evaluations, and that CM-GRPO provides further gains when combined with RAVEN.

AI.tldr: RAVEN repacks self-rollouts into alternating clean histories and noisy denoising states to align training with inference for real-time causal video extrapolation; CM-GRPO reframes consistency sampling as a conditional Gaussian and applies online RL to improve stability, yielding improvements over baselines.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between training history distributions and inference history in causal autoregressive video diffusion to maintain high quality over long horizons.
AI.method: RAVEN: a training-time test framework that repacks self rollouts into interleaved sequences of clean historical endpoints and noisy denoising states, enabling chunk losses to supervise history representations for future predictions. CM-GRPO: reformulates a consistency sampling step as a conditional Gaussian transition and applies online reinforcement learning to this kernel, avoiding the Euler-Maruyama auxiliary process used in prior flow-model RL.
AI.result: RAVEN surpasses recent causal video distillation baselines across quality, semantic, and dynamic degree evaluations; CM-GRPO provides further gains when combined with RAVEN.
AI.conclusion: The approaches align training with inference dynamics in real-time video extrapolation, improving quality and stability for causal autoregressive diffusion models and enabling stronger performance through the combined use of RAVEN and CM-GRPO.

title: Aligning Latent Geometry for Spherical Flow Matching in Image Generation
summary: Latent flow matching for image generation usually transports Gaussian noise to variational autoencoder latents along linear paths. Both endpoints, however, concentrate in thin spherical shells, and a Euclidean chord leaves those shells even when preprocessing aligns their radii. By decomposing each latent token into radial and angular components, we show through component-swap probes that decoded perceptual and semantic content is carried predominantly by direction, with radius contributing much less. We therefore project data latents onto a fixed token radius, use the radial projection of Gaussian noise as the spherical prior, finetune the decoder with the encoder frozen, and replace linear interpolation with spherical linear interpolation. The resulting geodesic paths stay on the sphere at every timestep, and their velocity targets are purely angular by construction. Under matched training, the method consistently improves class-conditional ImageNet-256 FID across different image tokenizers, leaves the diffusion architecture unchanged, and requires no auxiliary encoder or representation-alignment objective.

AI.tldr: Latent diffusion with spherical geometry: constrain latent tokens to a fixed radius, use spherical prior, and replace linear interpolation with spherical interpolation to produce on-sphere geodesics; improves ImageNet-256 FID without extra encoders or objectives.
AI.motivation: Latent tokens in latent diffusion often lie on thin spherical shells; Euclidean chords can exit these shells even after radius alignment. The method seeks to separate directional (content) from radial (size) information, ensuring generative trajectories stay on the sphere.
AI.method: Decompose latent tokens into radial and angular components; project latents to a fixed radius; use radial projection of Gaussian noise as the spherical prior; fine-tune the decoder with the encoder frozen; replace linear interpolation with spherical linear interpolation (slerp); ensure geodesic paths on the sphere with angular-only velocity targets.
AI.result: Under matched training, the method consistently improves class-conditional ImageNet-256 FID across different image tokenizers; diffusion architecture unchanged; no auxiliary encoder or representation-alignment objective required.
AI.conclusion: Latent content is predominantly carried by direction; enforcing a fixed radius and on-sphere geodesic interpolation yields angular velocity targets and improved generation quality without modifying the diffusion model architecture.

title: Proposal and study of statistical features for string similarity computation and classification
summary: Adaptations of features commonly applied in the field of visual computing, co-occurrence matrix (COM) and run-length matrix (RLM), are proposed for the similarity computation of strings in general (words, phrases, codes and texts). The proposed features are not sensitive to language related information. These are purely statistical and can be used in any context with any language or grammatical structure. Other statistical measures that are commonly employed in the field such as longest common subsequence, maximal consecutive longest common subsequence, mutual information and edit distances are evaluated and compared. In the first synthetic set of experiments, the COM and RLM features outperform the remaining state-of-the-art statistical features. In 3 out of 4 cases, the RLM and COM features were statistically more significant than the second best group based on distances (P-value < 0.001). When it comes to a real text plagiarism dataset, the RLM features obtained the best results.

AI.tldr: Co-occurrence and run-length features from visual texture analysis are adapted to string similarity, outperforming traditional statistics in synthetic tests and delivering best results on real plagiarism data; language-agnostic and purely statistical.
AI.motivation: Provide language-agnostic, statistical string similarity measures that do not rely on grammar or semantics, addressing limitations of edit-distance-based metrics across languages and domains.
AI.method: Apply co-occurrence matrix (COM) and run-length matrix (RLM) features to strings (words/phrases/codes/texts), compare with longest common subsequence, maximal LCS, mutual information, and edit distance; evaluate on synthetic datasets and real plagiarism corpus.
AI.result: In synthetic experiments, COM and RLM outperform other statistics; in 3 of 4 cases, they are significantly better (P<0.001). On a real plagiarism dataset, RLM yields the best results.
AI.conclusion: COM and RLM provide effective, language-agnostic statistical features for string similarity, with strong empirical performance; suitable for plagiarism detection and similar tasks, though details on computation and generalization remain to be explored.

title: VGGT-$Ω$
summary: Recent feed-forward reconstruction models, such as VGGT, have proven competitive with traditional optimization-based reconstructors while also providing geometry-aware features useful for other tasks. Here, we show that the quality of these models scales predictably with model and data size. We do so by introducing VGGT-$Ω$, which substantially improves reconstruction accuracy, efficiency, and capabilities for both static and dynamic scenes. To enable training this model at an unprecedented scale, we introduce architectural changes that improve training efficiency, a high-quality data annotation pipeline that supports dynamic scenes, and a self-supervised learning protocol. We simplify VGGT's architecture by using a single dense prediction head with multi-task supervision and removing the expensive high-resolution convolutional layers. We also use registers to aggregate scene information into a compact representation and introduce register attention, which restricts inter-frame information exchange to these registers, in part replacing global attention. In this way, during training, VGGT-$Ω$ uses only about 30% of the GPU memory of its predecessor, allowing us to train with 15x more supervised data than prior work and to leverage vast amounts of unlabeled video data. VGGT-$Ω$ achieves strong results for reconstruction of static and dynamic scenes across multiple benchmarks, for example, improving over the previous best camera estimation accuracy on Sintel by 77%. We also show that the learned registers can improve vision-language-action models and support alignment with language, suggesting that reconstruction can be a powerful and scalable proxy task for spatial understanding. Project Page: http://vggt-omega.github.io/

AI.tldr: VGGT-Ω is a memory-efficient, scaled-up feed-forward reconstruction model that surpasses prior methods on static and dynamic scenes, enabling 15x more supervised data via self-supervised training and register-based attention.
AI.motivation: Address scaling limits of feed-forward reconstruction models by increasing model/data size, enabling training with large unlabeled video data, and handling dynamic scenes while maintaining efficiency.
AI.method: Architectural simplifications (single dense prediction head, multi-task supervision, removal of high-res convolutions), introduction of registers to aggregate scene information into a compact representation, and register attention to limit inter-frame exchange. Also includes high-quality dynamic-scene data annotation and a self-supervised training protocol to leverage unlabeled video data; memory-efficient training (~30% of previous GPU memory).
AI.result: VGGT-Ω achieves improved reconstruction accuracy for static and dynamic scenes across benchmarks, including a 77% improvement in camera estimation accuracy on Sintel over the previous best. Learned registers also enhance vision-language-action models and enable alignment with language.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that reconstruction can be a scalable proxy task for spatial understanding when paired with memory-efficient architecture and register-based attention; the approach enables large-scale supervised and self-supervised training and may generalize to multi-modal tasks.

title: Learning from Language Feedback via Variational Policy Distillation
summary: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) suffers from sparse outcome signals, creating severe exploration bottlenecks on complex reasoning tasks. Recent on-policy self-distillation methods attempt to address this by utilizing language feedback to generate dense, token-level supervision. However, these approaches rely on a fixed, passive teacher to interpret the feedback. As the student policy improves, the teacher's zero-shot assessment capabilities plateau, ultimately halting further learning. To overcome this, we propose Variational Policy Distillation (VPD), a framework that formalizes learning from language feedback as a Variational Expectation-Maximization (EM) problem. VPD co-evolves both policies: in the E-step, the teacher is actively refined on trajectory outcomes via an adaptive trust-region update, translating textual feedback into a dynamically improved target token distribution. In the M-step, the student internalizes this dense distributional guidance on its own on-policy rollouts. By continuously improving the teacher's ability to extract actionable signals from textual critique, VPD overcomes the limitations of passive distillation. Evaluated across diverse sources of diagnostic feedback on scientific reasoning and code generation tasks, VPD consistently outperforms both standard RLVR and existing self-distillation baselines. Finally, by stress-testing our framework on rigid mathematical reasoning and cold-start regimes, we illuminate the fundamental bounds of feedback-driven self-distillation compared to pure environment-driven RL.

AI.tldr: VPD formalizes learning from language feedback as a Variational EM problem where a dynamically refined teacher and a student co-evolve; E-step adaptively improves the teacher's target token distribution via trajectory outcomes, M-step updates the student on-policy to match this distribution; yields richer supervision than fixed self-distillation and overcomes sparse RL rewards.
AI.motivation: RL with verifiable rewards suffers from sparse signals; fixed passive teachers plateau as the student improves; dynamic teacher refinement is needed to continuously extract actionable signals from textual feedback.
AI.method: Formulate as Variational EM. In E-step, refine the teacher using trajectory outcomes with an adaptive trust-region update to translate feedback into a better target distribution. In M-step, the student learns on-policy to imitate the dense distributional guidance. The teacher and student co-evolve to improve feedback utilization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across diverse diagnostic feedback on scientific reasoning and code generation shows VPD outperforms standard RLVR and self-distillation baselines.
AI.conclusion: VPD overcomes limitations of passive distillation by continuously improving the teacher's ability to extract signals from textual critique; stress-testing reveals bounds of feedback-driven self-distillation versus environment-driven RL, highlighting both the potential and limits of this approach.

title: RefDecoder: Enhancing Visual Generation with Conditional Video Decoding
summary: Video generation powers a vast array of downstream applications. However, while the de facto standard, i.e., latent diffusion models, typically employ heavily conditioned denoising networks, their decoders often remain unconditional. We observe that this architectural asymmetry leads to significant loss of detail and inconsistency relative to the input image. To address this, we argue that the decoder requires equal conditioning to preserve structural integrity. We introduce RefDecoder, a reference-conditioned video VAE decoder by injecting high-fidelity reference image signal directly into the decoding process via reference attention. Specifically, a lightweight image encoder maps the reference frame into the detail-rich high-dimensional tokens, which are co-processed with the denoised video latent tokens at each decoder up-sampling stage. We demonstrate consistent improvements across several distinct decoder backbones (e.g., Wan 2.1 and VideoVAE+), achieving up to +2.1dB PSNR over the unconditional baselines on the Inter4K, WebVid, and Large Motion reconstruction benchmarks. Notably, RefDecoder can be directly swapped into existing video generation systems without additional fine-tuning, and we report across-the-board improvements in subject consistency, background consistency, and overall quality scores on the VBench I2V benchmark. Beyond I2V, RefDecoder generalizes well to a wide range of visual generation tasks such as style transfer and video editing refinement.

AI.tldr: Introduces RefDecoder, a reference-conditioned video VAE decoder that injects high-fidelity reference image tokens into the decoder via reference attention to improve detail, consistency, and quality across video generation backbones, achieving PSNR gains and generalization to other tasks.
AI.motivation: Current video generation with latent diffusion models uses heavily conditioned denoisers but often uncouples the decoder, causing loss of detail and inconsistency relative to the input image.
AI.method: A lightweight image encoder maps a reference frame into high-dimensional detail tokens, which are integrated at each decoder up-sampling stage via reference attention, co-processing with the denoised video latent tokens. Works across decoders (e.g., Wan 2.1, VideoVAE+).
AI.result: Up to +2.1 dB PSNR over unconditional baselines on Inter4K, WebVid, and Large Motion reconstruction benchmarks; improved subject and background consistency and overall quality on VBench I2V; can be swapped into existing video generation systems without additional fine-tuning; generalizes to style transfer and video editing refinement.
AI.conclusion: Reference conditioning through RefDecoder enhances fidelity and consistency in video generation and is broadly applicable to other visual generation tasks, with ready compatibility to existing pipelines.

title: Natural Synthesis: Outperforming Reactive Synthesis Tools with Large Reasoning Models
summary: Reactive synthesis, the problem of automatically constructing a hardware circuit from a logical specification, is a long-standing challenge in formal verification. It is elusive for two reasons: It is algorithmically hard, and writing formal specifications by hand is notoriously difficult. In this paper, we tackle both sides of the problem. For the algorithmic side, we present a neuro-symbolic approach to reactive synthesis that couples large reasoning models with model checkers to iteratively repair a synthesized Verilog implementation via sound symbolic feedback. Our approach solves more benchmarks than the best dedicated tools in the annual synthesis competition and extends to constructing parameterized systems, a problem known to be undecidable. On the specification side, we introduce an autoformalization step that shifts the specification task from temporal logic to natural language by introducing a hand-authored dataset of natural-language specifications for evaluation. We demonstrate performance comparable to that of starting from formal specifications, establishing natural synthesis as a viable end-to-end workflow.

AI.tldr: Neuro-symbolic reactive synthesis paired with autoformalization of specs. The approach improves benchmark performance over dedicated tools, extends to parameterized systems, and demonstrates an end-to-end natural-language–to–formal-spec workflow.
AI.motivation: Address two central bottlenecks in reactive synthesis: (1) the intrinsic algorithmic hardness of constructing correct hardware from logical specs, and (2) the human difficulty of writing precise formal specifications.
AI.method: (1) A neuro-symbolic loop that couples large reasoning models with model checkers to iteratively repair a Verilog implementation via sound symbolic feedback. (2) An autoformalization pipeline that shifts spec writing from temporal logic to natural language, supported by a hand-authored NL specification dataset for evaluation.
AI.result: The approach solves more benchmarks than the best dedicated synthesis tools in the annual synthesis competition and extends to parameterized systems (undecidable in general). The NL-to-formal evaluation shows performance comparable to starting from formal specifications, supporting end-to-end natural-language–to–formal synthesis.
AI.conclusion: The work provides evidence for the viability of neuro-symbolic reactive synthesis and end-to-end NL-to-formal specification workflows, broadening the practical applicability of reactive synthesis, including for parameterized systems.

title: ATLAS: Agentic or Latent Visual Reasoning? One Word is Enough for Both
summary: Visual reasoning, often interleaved with intermediate visual states, has emerged as a promising direction in the field. A straightforward approach is to directly generate images via unified models during reasoning, but this is computationally expensive and architecturally non-trivial. Recent alternatives include agentic reasoning through code or tool calls, and latent reasoning with learnable hidden embeddings. However, agentic methods incur context-switching latency from external execution, while latent methods lack task generalization and are difficult to train with autoregressive parallelization. To combine their strengths while mitigating their limitations, we propose ATLAS, a framework in which a single discrete 'word', termed as a functional token, serves both as an agentic operation and a latent visual reasoning unit. Each functional token is associated with an internalized visual operation, yet requires no visual supervision and remains a standard token in the tokenizer vocabulary, which can be generated via next-token prediction. This design avoids verbose intermediate visual content generation, while preserving compatibility with the vanilla scalable SFT and RL training, without architectural or methodological modifications. To further address the sparsity of functional tokens during RL, we introduce Latent-Anchored GRPO (LA-GRPO), which stabilizes the training by anchoring functional tokens with a statically weighted auxiliary objective, providing stronger gradient updates. Extensive experiments and analyses demonstrate that ATLAS achieves superior performance on challenging benchmarks while maintaining clear interpretability. We hope ATLAS offers a new paradigm inspiring future visual reasoning research.

AI.tldr: ATLAS introduces a single discrete functional token that serves both as an agentic operation and a latent visual reasoning unit, avoiding explicit intermediate visual content generation while remaining compatible with standard tokenizers and training pipelines. It is paired with LA-GRPO, an auxiliary objective to stabilize RL training by anchoring tokens.
AI.motivation: To synthesize the strengths of agentic reasoning (code/tool calls) and latent visual reasoning (hidden embeddings) while mitigating their drawbacks—latency and lack of generalization—without incurring architectural changes or heavy visual content generation.
AI.method: Define functional tokens that map to internalized visual operations and live as standard vocabulary tokens, enabling next-token generation. Introduce Latent-Anchored GRPO (LA-GRPO) as a statically weighted auxiliary objective to alleviate token sparsity and stabilize RL updates. Train via vanilla SFT and RL without architectural modifications.
AI.result: Empirical demonstrations show ATLAS achieves superior performance on challenging benchmarks and preserves interpretability, validating the unify-and-accelerate approach.
AI.conclusion: ATLAS offers a new paradigm for visual reasoning by unifying agentic and latent reasoning through functional tokens and stable RL training, with broad compatibility and interpretability guiding future research.

title: Causal Foundation Models with Continuous Treatments
summary: Causal inference, estimating causal effects from observational data, is a fundamental tool in many disciplines. Of particular importance across a variety of domains is the continuous treatment setting, where the variable of intervention has a continuous range. This setting is far less explored and represents a substantial shift from the binary treatment setting, with models needing to represent effects across a continuum of treatment values. In this paper, we present the first causal foundation model for the continuous treatment setting. Our model meta-learns the ability to predict causal effects across a wide variety of unseen tasks without additional training or fine-tuning. First, we design a novel prior over data-generating processes with continuous treatment variables in order to generate a rich causal training corpus. We then train a transformer to reconstruct individual treatment-response curves given only observational data, leveraging in-context learning to amortize expensive Bayesian posterior inference. Our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on individual treatment-response curve reconstruction tasks compared to causal models which are trained specifically for those tasks.

AI.tldr: First foundation model for continuous treatment causal inference, meta-learning a prior and using a transformer with in-context learning to reconstruct individual treatment-response curves across unseen tasks.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap in continuous-treatment causal inference and enable zero-shot generalization to new tasks without retraining.
AI.method: Design a data-generating-process prior for continuous treatments; train a transformer to reconstruct individual treatment-response curves from observational data; leverage in-context learning to amortize Bayesian posterior inference.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on individual treatment-response curve reconstruction compared to task-specific causal models.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of a causal foundation model for continuous treatments with broad applicability and suggests directions for robustness and real-world evaluation.

title: EntityBench: Towards Entity-Consistent Long-Range Multi-Shot Video Generation
summary: Multi-shot video generation extends single-shot generation to coherent visual narratives, yet maintaining consistent characters, objects, and locations across shots remains a challenge over long sequences. Existing evaluations typically use independently generated prompt sets with limited entity coverage and simple consistency metrics, making standardized comparison difficult. We introduce EntityBench, a benchmark of 140 episodes (2,491 shots) derived from real narrative media, with explicit per-shot entity schedules tracking characters, objects, and locations simultaneously across easy / medium / hard tiers of up to 50 shots, 13 cross-shot characters, 8 cross-shot locations, 22 cross-shot objects, and recurrence gaps spanning up to 48 shots. It is paired with a three-pillar evaluation suite that disentangles intra-shot quality, prompt-following alignment, and cross-shot consistency, with a fidelity gate that admits only accurate entity appearances into cross-shot scoring. As a baseline, we propose EntityMem, a memory-augmented generation system that stores verified per-entity visual references in a persistent memory bank before generation begins. Experiments show that cross-shot entity consistency degrades sharply with recurrence distance in existing methods, and that explicit per-entity memory yields the highest character fidelity (Cohen's d = +2.33) and presence among methods evaluated. Code and data are available at https://github.com/Catherine-R-He/EntityBench/.

AI.tldr: EntityBench provides a large benchmark for evaluating cross-shot entity consistency in multi-shot video generation, and EntityMem demonstrates that memory-augmented generation with per-entity visual references improves character fidelity and presence, outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: To address the persistent challenge of maintaining coherent entities (characters, objects, locations) across long sequences in multi-shot video generation and to enable standardized, multifaceted evaluation beyond independent prompt setups.
AI.method: EntityBench comprises 140 episodes (2,491 shots) with explicit per-shot schedules for 13 cross-shot characters, 8 locations, and 22 objects, across easy/medium/hard tiers (up to 50 shots, recurrence gaps up to 48). It uses a three-pillar evaluation suite (intra-shot quality, prompt-following alignment, cross-shot consistency) with a fidelity gate for cross-shot scoring. EntityMem is a baseline that stores verified per-entity visual references in a persistent memory bank before generation, enabling memory-augmented generation.
AI.result: Cross-shot entity consistency degrades sharply with recurrence distance in existing methods. Explicit per-entity memory yields the highest character fidelity (Cohen's d = +2.33) and presence among evaluated methods.
AI.conclusion: The combination of a scalable, per-shot annotated benchmark and memory-augmented generation substantially improves cross-shot consistency and character fidelity in multi-shot video generation, providing a standardized evaluation framework and a practical memory-based baseline.

title: Training ML Models with Predictable Failures
summary: Estimating how often an ML model will fail at deployment scale is central to pre-deployment safety assessment, but a feasible evaluation set is rarely large enough to observe the failures that matter. Jones et al. (2025) address this by extrapolating from the largest k failure scores in an evaluation set to predict deployment-scale failure rates. We give a finite-k decomposition of this estimator's forecast error and show that it has a built-in bias toward over-prediction in the typical case, which is the safety-favorable direction. This bias is offset when the evaluation set misses a rare high-failure mode that the deployment set contains, leaving the forecast to under-predict at deployment scale. We propose a fine-tuning objective, the forecastability loss, that addresses this failure mode. In two proof-of-concept experiments, a language-model password game and an RL gridworld, fine-tuning substantially reduces held-out forecast error while preserving primary-task capability and achieving safety similar to that of supervised baselines.

AI.tldr: Finite-k extrapolation of deployment failure rates shows a bias toward over-prediction; a forecastability loss objective is proposed to handle rare high-failure modes; two proof-of-concept tasks show reduced forecast error with retained task performance and comparable safety to supervised baselines.
AI.motivation: Reliable pre-deployment risk estimation is hard when evaluation sets are too small to observe rare yet consequential failures; need methods that avoid unsafe over- or under-estimation.
AI.method: Finite-k decomposition of the top-k failure extrapolation error; analysis of bias toward over-prediction and the risk of under-prediction when a rare high-failure mode is present only in deployment; introduce forecastability loss as a fine-tuning objective; validate with two PoCs (language-model password game and RL gridworld) showing reduced forecast error while preserving primary-task performance and safety.
AI.result: The estimator exhibits a bias toward over-prediction in typical cases; however, missing rare high-failure modes in evaluation can lead to under-prediction at deployment scale; applying forecastability loss reduces held-out forecast error; in two experiments, fine-tuning preserves task capability and achieves safety comparable to supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: Forecastability loss mitigates the risk of mis-estimating deployment failures due to rare modes; finite-k extrapolation remains a viable approach when adjusted for bias, as demonstrated in two controlled experiments.

title: Forgetting That Sticks: Quantization-Permanent Unlearning via Circuit Attribution
summary: Standard unlearning evaluations measure behavioral suppression in full precision, immediately after training, despite every deployed language model being quantized first. Recent work has shown that 4-bit post-training quantization can reverse machine unlearning; we show this is not a tuning artefact but a systematic dual failure: gradient-based methods that achieve meaningful forgetting lose it under compression, while methods that survive quantization barely change the model. Both failures trace to the same root cause: across all baselines, per-parameter updates lie 47-828x below the NF4 quantization bin width; updates diffused across billions of parameters cannot clear quantization bin boundaries, a consequence we formalize as a sparsity-permanence tradeoff. We present MANSU (Mechanistic-Aligned Null-Space Unlearning), which resolves both modes by combining causal circuit attribution to isolate the minimal forget-set subgraph, circuit-restricted null-space projection with a diagonal-Fisher retain bound, and a per-parameter magnitude floor guaranteeing quantization survival by construction. We additionally introduce Circuit Attribution Divergence (CAD), a mechanistic verification metric distinguishing structural erasure from behavioral suppression, a distinction existing metrics cannot make. Across multiple model families and hazard benchmarks, MANSU is the first method to jointly satisfy all four properties with margin on each (meaningful forgetting, retain preservation, non-positive PTQ gap, and structural erasure), while gradient-based baselines recover up to +0.05 accuracy under compression.

AI.tldr: Unlearning evaluations often ignore quantization; 4-bit post-training quantization (PTQ) can reverse unlearning. The paper shows gradient-based forgetting fails under compression, while methods that survive barely change the model. Root cause: updates are far below quantization bin width and diffused over billions of parameters, making bin boundaries impervious—termed a sparsity-permanence tradeoff. Proposed MANSU (Mechanistic-Aligned Null-Space Unlearning) combines causal circuit attribution, circuit-restricted null-space projection with a diagonal-Fisher retain bound, and a per-parameter magnitude floor to guarantee quantization survival. Introduces CAD (Circuit Attribution Divergence) to verify mechanistically. Across model families, MANSU achieves four properties simultaneously (meaningful forgetting, retain preservation, non-positive PTQ gap, and structural erasure); gradient baselines can recover a small amount under compression.
AI.motivation: To address the discrepancy between unlearning performance and its persistence under quantization, and to provide a mechanistic verification framework that ensures forgetting survives post-training quantization rather than being masked by parameter quantization.
AI.method: Introduce MANSU: (1) causal circuit attribution to identify the minimal forget-set subgraph; (2) circuit-restricted null-space projection with a diagonal-Fisher retain bound; (3) per-parameter magnitude floor to guarantee surviving quantization; Also introduce CAD, a metric to distinguish structural erasure from behavioral suppression.
AI.result: MANSU satisfies all four desired properties with margin across multiple model families and hazard benchmarks; gradient-based baselines show only minor improvements under compression (up to about 0.05 accuracy). CAD provides mechanistic verification of true forgetting vs behavior-only suppression.
AI.conclusion: MANSU resolves dual failures of unlearning under quantization by aligning mechanics with quantization constraints; it is the first approach reported to jointly guarantee meaningful forgetting, preserve retained behavior, avoid PTQ-induced gaps, and demonstrate structural erasure; the work highlights the necessity of mechanistic verification for robust unlearning under deployment quantization.

title: Widening the Gap: Exploiting LLM Quantization via Outlier Injection
summary: LLM quantization has become essential for memory-efficient deployment. Recent work has shown that quantization schemes can pose critical security risks: an adversary may release a model that appears benign in full precision but exhibits malicious behavior once quantized by users. However, existing quantization-conditioned attacks have been limited to relatively simple quantization methods, where the attacker can estimate weight regions that remain invariant under the target quantization. Notably, prior attacks have consistently failed to compromise more popular and sophisticated schemes, limiting their practical impact. In this work, we introduce the first quantization-conditioned attack that consistently induces malicious behavior that can be triggered by a broad range of advanced quantization techniques, including AWQ, GPTQ, and GGUF I-quants. Our attack exploits a simple property shared by many modern quantization methods: large outliers can cause other weights to be rounded to zero. Consequently, by injecting outliers into specific weight blocks, an adversary can therefore induce a targeted, predictable weight collapse in the model. This effect can be used to craft seemingly benign full-precision models that exhibit a wide range of malicious behaviors after quantization. Through extensive evaluation across three attack scenarios and LLMs, we show that our attack achieves high success rates against a broad range of quantization methods on which prior attacks fail. Our results demonstrate, for the first time, that the security risks of quantization are not restricted to simpler schemes but are broadly relevant across complex, widely-used quantization methods.

AI.tldr: First quantization-conditioned attack that defeats modern quantizers by injecting outliers to trigger weight collapse, enabling malicious behavior post-quantization across AWQ, GPTQ, and GGUF I-quants.
AI.motivation: Quantization enables memory-efficient deployment of LLMs, but existing security analyses focused on simple quantizers. This work reveals that sophisticated quantization schemes are also vulnerable to controlled perturbations, broadening the threat model.
AI.method: Inject outliers into specific weight blocks to induce a cascade where large outliers cause other weights to be rounded to zero, leading to targeted weight collapse after quantization. This yields a benign full-precision model that behaves maliciously when quantized across multiple schemes (AWQ, GPTQ, GGUF I-quants).
AI.result: The attack achieves high success rates across three attack scenarios and multiple LLMs, demonstrating effectiveness against a broad range of quantization methods previously thought resistant to such manipulation.
AI.conclusion: Quantization security risks extend beyond simple schemes to widely-used, complex quantizers. The work calls for defense research and robust quantization pipelines to mitigate injected outlier-induced failures.

title: Self-Distilled Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a central paradigm for post-training LLM agents, yet its trajectory-level reward signal provides only coarse supervision for long-horizon interaction. On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) complements RL by introducing dense token-level guidance from a teacher branch augmented with privileged context. However, transferring OPSD to multi-turn agents proves problematic: compounding multi-turn instability destabilizes supervision, while skill-conditioned privileged guidance requires asymmetric treatment for negative teacher rejections may arise from imperfect skills retrieval or utilization. We introduce SDAR (Self-Distilled Agentic Reinforcement Learning), which treats OPSD as a gated auxiliary objective while keeping RL as the primary optimization backbone. SDAR maps detached token-level signals into a sigmoid gate, strengthening distillation on teacher-endorsed positive-gap tokens and softly attenuating negative teacher rejections. Across the Qwen2.5 and Qwen3 families on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-QA, SDAR substantially improves over GRPO (+9.4% on ALFWorld, +7.0% on Search-QA, +10.2% on WebShop-Acc), avoids the instability of naive GRPO+OPSD, and consistently outperforms hybrid RL--OPSD baselines across model scales.

AI.tldr: SDAR is a gated auxiliary objective that stabilizes on-policy self-distillation (OPSD) in multi-turn RL for LLM agents, improving performance over GRPO+OPSD and other hybrids across Qwen models and three domains.
AI.motivation: Trajectory-level rewards in RL are coarse for long-horizon tasks; OPSD provides token-level guidance but struggles with instability and asymmetric handling of negative guidance in multi-turn settings.
AI.method: Treat OPSD as a detached-token-level signal mapped through a sigmoid gate, integrated as a gated auxiliary objective with RL as the primary objective. The gate strengthens teacher-endorsed positive-gap tokens and softly attenuates negative teacher rejections, maintaining stability.
AI.result: Substantial improvements over baselines across Qwen2.5 and Qwen3 on ALFWorld (+9.4%), Search-QA (+7.0%), WebShop-Acc (+10.2%). SDAR avoids instability of naive GRPO+OPSD and outperforms hybrid RL–OPSD baselines across model scales.
AI.conclusion: SDAR effectively stabilizes multi-turn OPSD by gating auxiliary signals, delivering consistent performance gains and scalability across domains and model sizes.

title: Position: Behavioural Assurance Cannot Verify the Safety Claims Governance Now Demands
summary: This position paper argues that behavioural assurance, even when carefully designed, is being asked to carry safety claims it cannot verify. AI governance frameworks enacted between 2019 and early 2026 require reviewable evidence of properties such as the absence of hidden objectives, resistance to loss-of-control precursors, and bounded catastrophic capability; current assurance methodologies (primarily behavioural evaluations and red-teaming) are epistemically limited to observable model outputs and cannot verify the latent representations or long-horizon agentic behaviours these frameworks presume to regulate. We formalize this structural mismatch as the audit gap, the divergence between required and achievable verification access, and introduce the concept of fragile assurance to describe cases where the evidential structure does not support the asserted safety claim. Through an analysis of a 21-instrument inventory, we identify an incentive gradient where geopolitical and industrial pressures systematically reward surface-level behavioral proxies over deep structural verification. Finally, we propose a technical pivot: bounding the weight of behavioral evidence in legal text and extending voluntary pre-deployment access with mechanistic-evidence classes, specifically linear probes, activation patching, and before/after-training comparisons.

AI.tldr: Behavioral assurance is insufficient for validating safety claims; introduces audit gap and fragile assurance, analyzes incentives via a 21-instrument audit, and proposes mechanistic-evidence pivots (linear probes, activation patching, before/after-training comparisons) to strengthen governance.
AI.motivation: To close the gap between governance requirements for verifiable properties and current verification methods that only observe model outputs, not latent representations or long-horizon agentic behavior.
AI.method: Formal definitions of audit gap and fragile assurance; diagnostic analysis of a 21-instrument inventory; incentive-gradient analysis showing geopolitical/industrial pressures favor behavioral proxies; proposal of a mechanistic-evidence pivot with concrete techniques.
AI.result: A conceptual framework identifying verification gaps, an analysis of incentive structures, and concrete recommendations to incorporate mechanistic evidence alongside behavioral evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Advocate bounding the role of behavioral evidence in law and expanding access to mechanistic evidence to improve safety verification in AI governance.

title: Eradicating Negative Transfer in Multi-Physics Foundation Models via Sparse Mixture-of-Experts Routing
summary: Scaling Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) toward universal foundation models is bottlenecked by negative transfer: the simultaneous co-training of disparate partial differential equation (PDE) regimes can induce gradient conflict, unstable optimization, and plasticity loss in dense neural operators. In particular, broadband open-channel fluid dynamics and boundary-dominated porous media flows impose incompatible spectral and geometric demands on a single dense parameter path. We introduce Shodh-MoE, a sparse-activated latent transformer architecture for multi-physics transport. Shodh-MoE operates on compressed 16^3 physical latents produced by a physics-informed autoencoder with an intra-tokenizer Helmholtz-style velocity parameterization, restricting decoded states to divergence-free velocity manifolds. The model guarantees exact mass conservation, achieving a physically verifiable velocity divergence of ~2.8 x 10^-10 (evaluated post-hoc in FP64) on 128^3 grids. A Top-1 soft-semantic router dynamically assigns localized latent patches to expert subnetworks, enabling specialized parameter paths for distinct physical mechanisms while preserving shared experts for universal symmetries. In a 20,000-step distributed pretraining run over mixed three-dimensional physical tensors, routing telemetry shows autonomous domain bifurcation: held-out validation tokens from the open-channel domain route exclusively to Expert 0, while porous-media tokens route exclusively to Expert 1. The model converges simultaneously across both regimes, achieving latent validation MSEs of 2.46 x 10^-5 and 9.76 x 10^-6, and decoded physical MSEs of 2.48 x 10^-6 and 1.76 x 10^-6. These results support sparse expert routing as a practical architectural mechanism for mitigating multi-physics interference in universal neural operators.

AI.tldr: Sparse-activated latent transformer (Shodh-MoE) for multi-physics transport that routes latent patches to specialized experts, achieving mass conservation and reduced interference between open-channel and porous-media PDE regimes.
AI.motivation: Address negative transfer in scaling SciML toward universal foundation models when co-training disparate PDE regimes; need specialized pathways within a shared architecture while preserving universal symmetries.
AI.method: Physics-informed autoencoder compresses states into 16^3 latents with divergence-free velocity via Helmholtz-style parameterization; Top-1 soft semantic router assigns local latent patches to expert subnetworks; 3D tensors; 20k-step distributed pretraining; domain bifurcation observed; evaluation on 128^3 and 20k steps with FP64 verification.
AI.result: Mass conservation ensured with velocity divergence ~2.8e-10 (FP64) on 128^3 grids; latent validation MSEs of 2.46e-5 and 9.76e-6; decoded physical MSEs of 2.48e-6 and 1.76e-6; autonomous domain bifurcation: open-channel tokens route to Expert 0, porous-media tokens to Expert 1; simultaneous convergence across regimes.
AI.conclusion: Sparse expert routing mitigates multi-physics interference, enabling closer to universal neural operators and demonstrating domain specialization within a single shared model without sacrificing global symmetries.

title: When Are Two Networks the Same? Tensor Similarity for Mechanistic Interpretability
summary: Mechanistic interpretability aims to break models into meaningful parts; verifying that two such parts implement the same computation is a prerequisite. Existing similarity measures evaluate either empirical behaviour, leaving them blind to out-of-distribution mechanisms, or basis-dependent parameters, meaning they disregard weight-space symmetries. To address these issues for the class of tensor-based models, we introduce a weight-based metric, tensor similarity, that is invariant to such symmetries. This metric captures global functional equivalence and accounts for cross-layer mechanisms using an efficient recursive algorithm. Empirically, tensor similarity tracks functional training dynamics, such as grokking and backdoor insertion, with higher fidelity than existing metrics. This reduces measuring similarity and verifying faithfulness into a solved algebraic problem rather than one of empirical approximation.

AI.tldr: A weight-based, symmetry-invariant tensor similarity metric for tensor-based models that captures global functional equivalence and accounts for cross-layer interactions via a recursive algorithm; empirically aligns with training dynamics better than prior metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing similarity measures either rely on empirical behavior (susceptible to out-of-distribution mechanisms) or depend on basis-specific weight representations (ignoring weight-space symmetries). There is a need for a symmetry-invariant, globally faithful measure for mechanistic interpretability in tensor-based models.
AI.method: Introduce tensor similarity, a weight-based metric invariant to weight-space symmetries; develop an efficient recursive algorithm to incorporate cross-layer mechanics; designed for tensor-based architectures.
AI.result: Tensor similarity tracks functional training dynamics (e.g., grokking, backdoor insertion) with higher fidelity than existing metrics; reduces faithfulness verification to solving an algebraic problem rather than empirical approximation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed metric provides a principled, symmetry-invariant, algebraic approach to measuring model similarity and faithfulness, enabling robust mechanistic interpretability for tensor-based models.

title: FutureSim: Replaying World Events to Evaluate Adaptive Agents
summary: AI agents are being increasingly deployed in dynamic, open-ended environments that require adapting to new information as it arrives. To efficiently measure this capability for realistic use-cases, we propose building grounded simulations that replay real-world events in the order they occurred. We build FutureSim, where agents forecast world events beyond their knowledge cutoff while interacting with a chronological replay of the world: real news articles arriving and questions resolving over the simulated period. We evaluate frontier agents in their native harness, testing their ability to predict world events over a three-month period from January to March 2026. FutureSim reveals a clear separation in their capabilities, with the best agent's accuracy being 25%, and many having worse Brier skill score than making no prediction at all. Through careful ablations, we show how FutureSim offers a realistic setting to study emerging research directions like long-horizon test-time adaptation, search, memory, and reasoning about uncertainty. Overall, we hope our benchmark design paves the way to measure AI progress on open-ended adaptation spanning long time-horizons in the real world.

AI.tldr: FutureSim: a chronologically replayed benchmark to evaluate long-horizon world-event forecasting by AI agents in real-world news streams; initial results show strong performance gaps and potential to study memory, search, and uncertainty reasoning.
AI.motivation: Open-ended, dynamic environments demand adaptation to unfolding information; existing benchmarks miss temporal order and real-world complexity; need realistic long-horizon evaluation.
AI.method: Create FutureSim: a chronological replay where agents receive real news articles and answer questions while events unfold over Jan–Mar 2026; measure forecasting accuracy and Brier skill score; perform ablations to analyze contributing components.
AI.result: Best agent achieves 25% accuracy; many agents have worse Brier score than baseline (no prediction); clear separation in capabilities across frontier agents.
AI.conclusion: FutureSim offers a realistic testbed to study long-horizon test-time adaptation, memory, search, and uncertainty reasoning; provides a path to quantify AI progress on open-ended adaptation across extended time horizons.
